| **Environmental** | Poor Infrastructure | Medium | Public-Private Partnerships |
| **Environmental** | Regulatory Burden | Medium | Compliance automation |

---

## 3.4 Productivity Index Calculation (Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context:** Base year (2018) productivity = 2.5 units/hour. Current year (2023) productivity = 3.75 units/hour. Calculate the Productivity Index for 2023.

The Productivity Index formula (with Base = 100) is:

$$ P_{Index} = \frac{P_{current}}{P_{base}} \times 100 $$

Substituting the values:

$$ P_{Index} = \frac{3.75}{2.50} \times 100 $$

Performing the division:

$$ P_{Index} = 1.5 \times 100 = 150 $$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The firm is now **50% more productive** than the base year. A Productivity Index of 150 is considered excellent in most industrial benchmarks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Framework — The Six Pillars of Productivity

The following Mermaid block renders a **hierarchical block diagram** mapping the six major factor domains of productivity and their sub-components. This is a high-yield visual for KTU 14-mark theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PRODUCTIVITY"]:::root --> B["HUMAN FACTORS"]:::human
    A --> C["TECHNOLOGICAL FACTORS"]:::tech
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define productivity. Differentiate between labor productivity and machine productivity.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Productivity** is the ratio of output to input in a production system. **Labor productivity** measures output per unit of labor (man-hours), reflecting worker efficiency, while **machine productivity** measures output per unit of machine-time, reflecting equipment efficiency.

**Key Differences:**

- **Numerator:** Both use output, but denominators differ (labor-hours vs. machine-hours)
- **Application:** Labor productivity is used in labor-intensive industries (textiles, agriculture), while machine productivity is critical in capital-intensive sectors (automotive, steel)
- **Controllability:** Labor productivity is influenced by human factors; machine productivity depends on technology and maintenance

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any six factors that affect productivity in a manufacturing organization.**

**Model Answer:**

The six key factors are:

1. **Skill and training** of workers
2. **Level of automation** and technology
3. **Management practices** including planning and supervision
4. **Capital availability** for investment
5. **Quality and availability** of raw materials
6. **Government policies** and infrastructure support

> [!TIP]
> **Board Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect at least **6 well-explained points** for a 3-mark question. Avoid one-word answers — write short descriptive sentences for each point.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **human factors** affecting productivity. Discuss how **training, motivation, and working conditions** can be improved to enhance productivity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **technological factors** in improving productivity. Explain with examples how **automation, modernization, and R&D** contribute to productivity gains. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Human Factors Affecting Productivity (7 Marks)**

Human factors constitute the **most significant controllable variable** in productivity management. The key human factors are:

**1. Skill and Training (1.5 Marks):**
Skilled workers produce more output with less waste. Continuous training programs (on-the-job training, apprenticeships, 5S, Six Sigma certifications) enhance competence. Example: Toyota's extensive worker training program is a global benchmark.

**2. Motivation and Morale (1.5 Marks):**
Motivated workers exhibit higher output and quality. Theories like **Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs** and **Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory** explain how hygiene factors (salary, safety) and motivators (recognition, growth) drive productivity. Incentive schemes, performance bonuses, and participative management boost morale.

**3. Working Conditions (1 Mark):**
Proper lighting, ventilation, temperature control, noise reduction, and ergonomics directly impact worker efficiency. Poor conditions lead to fatigue, accidents, and absenteeism.

**4. Industrial Relations (1 Mark):**
Harmonious labor-management relations prevent strikes and lockouts. Collective bargaining, grievance redressal mechanisms, and trade union cooperation ensure smooth operations.

**5. Other Factors (2 Marks):**
- **Wage levels** — fair compensation attracts and retains talent
- **Health and welfare** — medical benefits, canteen, transport
- **Job satisfaction** — task variety, autonomy, and responsibility
- **Absenteeism and turnover** — disrupts workflow and training investment

#### **Part (b) — Technological Factors Affecting Productivity (7 Marks)**

Technology is the **engine of long-term productivity growth**. The major technological factors are:

**1. Automation (2 Marks):**
Automation replaces manual effort with machines, robots, and control systems. It increases speed, accuracy, and consistency. Example: Tesla's Gigafactory uses over 1,000 robots per production line, achieving unprecedented output per worker.

**2. Modernization of Machinery (1.5 Marks):**
Replacing old equipment with CNC, PLC-controlled, and IoT-enabled machines reduces downtime and improves quality. Modern machines have higher Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE).

**3. Research and Development (1.5 Marks):**
R&D leads to new products, processes, and materials. Companies investing 5–10% of revenue in R&D (e.g., Apple, Samsung) sustain long-term productivity advantages.

**4. Information Technology (1 Mark):**
ERP systems (SAP, Oracle), MES, and Industry 4.0 technologies enable real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and data-driven decision-making.

**5. Quality of Tools and Equipment (1 Mark):**
Precision jigs, fixtures, gauges, and testing instruments ensure first-time-right production, reducing rework and warranty costs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Productivity improvement requires a **balanced approach** — human factors deliver short-term gains, while technology drives sustainable long-term growth. Best results come from **integrating both dimensions**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Define productivity. Explain the various **types of productivity** with suitable formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **managerial and financial factors** affecting productivity. How can organizations mitigate the negative impact of these factors? **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Definition and Types of Productivity (7 Marks)**

**Definition (2 Marks):**
Productivity is the ratio of output produced to input consumed in a production or service system. It measures the efficiency of resource utilization.

**Types of Productivity (5 Marks):**

**1. Labor Productivity (1 Mark):**

$$ P_L = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Man-hours Worked}} $$

**2. Machine Productivity (1 Mark):**

$$ P_M = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Machine-hours Operated}} $$

**3. Capital Productivity (1 Mark):**

$$ P_C = \frac{\text{Output Value}}{\text{Capital Invested}} $$

**4. Material Productivity (1 Mark):**

$$ P_{Mat} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Raw Material Consumed}} $$

**5. Multifactor / Total Productivity (1 Mark):**

$$ P_{MFP} = \frac{\text{Total Output}}{L + M + C + E} $$

Where $L$ = Labor, $M$ = Material, $C$ = Capital, $E$ = Energy inputs.

#### **Part (b) — Managerial and Financial Factors (7 Marks)**

**Managerial Factors (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Planning and Scheduling** — Effective production planning using MRP, JIT, and ERP minimizes idle time and bottlenecks.
2. **Supervision Quality** — Skilled supervisors ensure adherence to standards, provide on-the-job coaching, and resolve conflicts.
3. **Organizational Structure** — Flat, agile structures enable faster decision-making and better communication.
4. **Performance Measurement** — KPIs, dashboards, and regular audits drive accountability.
5. **Coordination** — Cross-functional teams break silos and improve throughput.

**Mitigation Strategies for Managerial Factors:**
- Adopt **Lean Management** and **Agile methodologies**
- Implement **ERP/MRP-II** for integrated planning
- Conduct **management development programs** (MDPs)
- Use **Balanced Scorecard** for performance tracking

**Financial Factors (3.5 Marks):**

1. **Capital Availability** — Adequate funds enable modernization and expansion.
2. **Cost of Capital** — High interest rates discourage investment in productivity-enhancing technology.
3. **Wage Inflation** — Rising wages without productivity gains erode competitiveness.
4. **Tax Policies** — Depreciation allowances and investment tax credits incentivize capital investment.
5. **Market Conditions** — Demand fluctuations affect capacity utilization and unit costs.

**Mitigation Strategies for Financial Factors:**
- Avail **government subsidies** (e.g., PLI scheme in India)
- Use **leasing and hire-purchase** for capital equipment
- **Outsource non-core activities** to free up capital
- Adopt **financial hedging** for currency and interest rate risks
- Build **strategic cash reserves** for downturns

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** Managerial efficiency and financial discipline are **interdependent pillars** of sustainable productivity. Organizations must align their management systems with their financial strategies to unlock the full productivity potential.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Productivity Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing productivity with production** — Production is *absolute output*; productivity is *output per unit input*. Marks lost: 1–2
> 2. **Writing one-word answers in Part A** — Examiners expect 2–3 line descriptive answers, not just keywords. Marks lost: 1
> 3. **Skipping formulas in numerical questions** — Even if the calculation is correct, missing the formula statement leads to 1-mark deduction
> 4. **Not classifying factors properly** — Random listing without categorization (Human/Technological/Managerial/etc.) loses structure marks
> 5. **Ignoring units in productivity** — Always mention "units per labor-hour" or "₹/₹" — careless unit handling loses 0.5–1 mark
> 6. **Forgetting productivity improvement strategies** — A 14-mark question without strategy discussion is incomplete — loses 2–3 marks
> 7. **Confusing productivity index with inflation index** — Productivity Index = $P_{current}/P_{base} \times 100$, not a price index

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam!**

### **Core Definitions**
- ✅ **Productivity** = Output ÷ Input (a ratio, always dimensionless or units/hour)
- ✅ **Productivity Index** = $P_{current}/P_{base} \times 100$ (Base year = 100)
- ✅ **% Change in Productivity** = $\Delta P = (P_{new} - P_{old})/P_{old} \times 100$

### **Six Pillars of Factors (Memorize!)**
- ✅ **Human** — Skills, training, motivation, morale, wages, IR
- ✅ **Technological** — Automation, machinery, R&D, IT, tools
- ✅ **Managerial** — Planning, supervision, structure, KPIs
- ✅ **Financial** — Capital, taxes, interest, wages, market
- ✅ **Material** — Quality, supply, inventory, energy
- ✅ **Environmental** — Government, infrastructure, politics, trade

### **Critical Formulas (One-Liners)**
- ✅ Labor Productivity = Output / Man-hours
- ✅ Machine Productivity = Output / Machine-hours
- ✅ Capital Productivity = Output Value / Capital
- ✅ MFP = Output / (Labor + Material + Capital + Energy)

### **KTU Board Must-Know Points**
- ✅ Productivity ≠ Production (ratio vs. absolute)
- ✅ Productivity ≠ Efficiency (broader concept)
- ✅ Value Added concept in output measurement
- ✅ Productivity improves **standard of living**
- ✅ Productivity is the **root of wage growth**
- ✅ MFP is the **most comprehensive** productivity measure
- ✅ Productivity Index is always relative to a **base year**

### **Strategy Box (Use in 14-mark answers)**
- ✅ Always conclude with a **productivity improvement strategy**
- ✅ Reference **Lean, Six Sigma, TPM, ERP, Industry 4.0** for tech questions
- ✅ Reference **Maslow, Herzberg, McGregor** for human factor questions
- ✅ Mention **WTO, PLI, Make in India** for environmental/policy questions

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** A well-structured 14-mark answer should have — *Introduction (2 marks) + Classification (3 marks) + Detailed Explanation (6 marks) + Strategies & Conclusion (3 marks)*. This 2-3-6-3 framework is the gold standard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Productivity Ratios
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Productivity Ratios

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Productivity** is formally defined as the **ratio of output produced to the input consumed** in a production process, over a specified period of time. It is the **fundamental measure of efficiency** in any industrial or service operation, quantifying how well an organization converts its resources (inputs) into goods or services (outputs).

Mathematically, the generic productivity ratio is expressed as:

$$
\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}
$$

Where **output** is the quantity of goods or services produced (usually expressed in units, value, or service deliverables), and **input** is the sum total of resources consumed (labor hours, materials, capital, energy, etc.).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Mileage of Industry"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of productivity exactly like the mileage (km/litre) of a vehicle.** A car that travels 20 km on 1 litre of fuel is more "productive" than one that travels 12 km on 1 litre. Similarly, a factory that produces **100 units with 10 workers** is more productive than one that produces **80 units with the same 10 workers**. Productivity is the **"fuel efficiency" of an enterprise** — the more output you extract from each unit of input, the higher your productivity.

In everyday industrial parlance, productivity is often confused with **production** or **efficiency**. They are related but distinct:
- **Production** → Absolute volume of output (e.g., 10,000 units/day)
- **Productivity** → Output *per unit of input* (e.g., 10 units/worker-hour)
- **Efficiency** → Actual output *vs.* standard/expected output (a comparative ratio)

## 1.3 Classification of Productivity Ratios

Productivity is broadly classified into three hierarchical levels based on the **scope of inputs** considered:

| # | Type | Input Scope | Use Case |
|---|------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | **Partial Productivity** | A single input factor only (labor, material, capital, energy) | Quick benchmarking of individual resource efficiency |
| 2 | **Multifactor Productivity** | Two or more input factors combined (e.g., labor + material) | Departmental / process-level performance |
| 3 | **Total Productivity** | All input factors aggregated together | Firm-wide strategic performance measurement |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to *define, compute, and interpret* all three types of productivity ratios and explain the **factors affecting productivity** in a manufacturing or service context.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Behind Productivity Measurement

The rationale for measuring productivity rests on three industrial truths:

- **Truth 1 — Resources are scarce and costly.** Every rupee, every man-hour, and every kilowatt-hour has an opportunity cost. Productivity tells management whether these scarce resources are being converted optimally into value.
- **Truth 2 — Competitiveness is a productivity game.** Nations, industries, and firms that improve productivity faster than their competitors gain sustainable competitive advantage (lower cost, better quality, faster delivery).
- **Truth 3 — Productivity is the engine of economic growth.** Long-term wage increases, profit margins, and standards of living are mathematically tied to productivity growth. This is why the KTU 2024 Industrial Engineering syllabus places productivity ratios in Module 1.

The conceptual logic follows this step-by-step reasoning:

- **Step 1 → Identify the production system boundary** (a machine, a department, a factory, or a country).
- **Step 2 → Quantify the output** in consistent physical or monetary units over a defined period.
- **Step 3 → Quantify each input** in compatible units.
- **Step 4 → Compute the ratio** of output to input.
- **Step 5 → Benchmark** the ratio against past performance, industry standards, or competitors.
- **Step 6 → Take corrective action** to improve the ratio (process redesign, training, automation, waste reduction).

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of the Three Productivity Tiers

### 2.2.1 Partial Productivity Ratio
A partial productivity ratio isolates a **single input factor**, allowing managers to drill down on the efficiency of a specific resource. It is the most commonly used ratio in shop-floor reporting because it is simple and directly actionable.

$$
P_{\text{partial}} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Single Input Factor}}
$$

Common partial productivity measures used in industry:
- **Labor Productivity** = Units produced / Direct labor hours
- **Machine Productivity** = Units produced / Machine hours
- **Material Productivity** = Units produced / Quantity of raw material
- **Capital Productivity** = Output value / Capital invested
- **Energy Productivity** = Output / Energy consumed (kWh)

### 2.2.2 Multifactor Productivity Ratio
A multifactor productivity ratio combines **two or more input categories** to give a more holistic, though still focused, view of efficiency. It eliminates the limitation of partial productivity, where high labor productivity might mask the overuse of materials.

$$
P_{\text{multi}} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}_1 + \text{Input}_2 + \dots + \text{Input}_n}
$$

Typical combinations in practice:
- **Labor + Material Productivity** (most common in manufacturing)
- **Labor + Capital Productivity**
- **Labor + Material + Energy Productivity**

### 2.2.3 Total Productivity Ratio
The total productivity ratio considers **all input factors** simultaneously — labor, material, capital, energy, overheads, and every other resource consumed. It is the **gold standard** of productivity measurement, used at the strategic, firm-wide level.

$$
P_{\text{total}} = \frac{\text{Total Output Value}}{\text{Total Input Cost}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why total productivity is rarely reported on the shop floor:** It requires all inputs to be converted into a common monetary unit, which demands accurate cost accounting data. Hence, in practice, firms use a mix of partial and multifactor ratios for daily operations and total productivity for annual strategic reviews.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Ratio | Formula | Units / Interpretation |
|---|-------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Generic Productivity | $P = O / I$ | Output units per input unit |
| 2 | Labor Productivity (Physical) | $P_L = Q / H$ | Units produced per labor hour |
| 3 | Labor Productivity (Value) | $P_L = S / L$ | Sales value (₹) per labor cost (₹) |
| 4 | Machine Productivity | $P_M = Q / M_h$ | Units per machine hour |
| 5 | Material Productivity | $P_{\text{mat}} = Q / M$ | Units per kg or per ₹ of material |
| 6 | Capital Productivity | $P_C = S / C$ | Sales value per ₹ of capital |
| 7 | Energy Productivity | $P_E = Q / E$ | Units per kWh |
| 8 | Multifactor Productivity (Labor + Material) | $P_{\text{LM}} = Q / (L + M)$ | Output per combined labor + material |
| 9 | Total Productivity | $P_T = O_{\text{total}} / I_{\text{total}}$ | Total output value / total input cost |
| 10 | Productivity Index | $I = (P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{base}}) \times 100$ | Percentage change vs. base year |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $O$ = Output, $I$ = Input, $Q$ = Quantity produced, $H$ = Labor hours, $S$ = Sales value, $L$ = Labor cost, $M_h$ = Machine hours, $M$ = Material cost/quantity, $C$ = Capital, $E$ = Energy.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Productivity Ratios

- **Strategic Planning:** CEOs use total productivity trends (typically over 5–10 years) to decide on expansion, diversification, and capital investment.
- **Performance Benchmarking:** Industries compare their partial productivity ratios with national and international benchmarks (e.g., labour productivity in Indian auto industry vs. Japanese auto industry).
- **Wage Negotiations:** Many labour unions tie wage demands to productivity growth indices — a politically and economically significant application.
- **Process Improvement:** Six Sigma, Lean, and Kaizen practitioners use multifactor productivity as a North Star metric for continuous improvement projects.
- **Government Policy:** Nations publish productivity statistics (e.g., India’s National Productivity Council data) to shape industrial policy and skill development programs.
- **Cost Estimation:** Productivity ratios feed into standard costing systems, helping firms set realistic cost targets and detect inefficiencies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Productivity Index from Base Year

The productivity index is a normalized measure that shows the percentage change in productivity relative to a chosen base year. It is a direct derivative of the generic productivity ratio.

**Step 1 — Write the generic productivity ratio for the current year:**

$$
P_{\text{current}} = \frac{O_{\text{current}}}{I_{\text{current}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the generic productivity ratio for the base year (same system, earlier period):**

$$
P_{\text{base}} = \frac{O_{\text{base}}}{I_{\text{base}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio of the two productivities (dimensionless, since units cancel):**

$$
\text{Productivity Index} = \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 100 to express as a percentage (this is the conventional KTU/industrial reporting form):**

$$
I = \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation rule:**
- $I = 100$ → No change in productivity vs. base year
- $I > 100$ → Productivity has *improved* (e.g., $I = 115$ means a 15% gain)
- $I < 100$ → Productivity has *declined* (e.g., $I = 90$ means a 10% drop)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The productivity index is **dimensionless** because both numerator and denominator have identical units (output/input). This is what makes it universally comparable across industries, countries, and time periods.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A medium-scale manufacturing firm in Kerala produces **8,000 units of a product per month**. The following resource consumption data is recorded for one month:
- Direct labor: **4,000 hours** at a wage rate of **₹150 per hour**
- Direct material: **₹6,00,000**
- Machine hours: **2,000 hours**
- Capital employed: **₹50,00,000**
- Energy consumed: **10,000 kWh** at **₹8 per kWh**
- Selling price of the product: **₹250 per unit**

**Required:** Calculate (a) Labor productivity (in units/hour and in ₹/₹), (b) Machine productivity, (c) Material productivity, (d) Multifactor productivity (Labor + Material), (e) Total productivity, and (f) Productivity index if last year's total productivity was ₹2.20 of output per ₹1 of input.

---

### 3.2.1 Solution — Part (a): Labor Productivity

**Sub-part (a)(i) — Physical units form (units per labor hour):**

$$
P_L = \frac{Q}{H} = \frac{8000 \text{ units}}{4000 \text{ hours}}
$$

$$
P_L = 2.0 \text{ units per labor hour}
$$

**Sub-part (a)(ii) — Monetary value form (₹ of output per ₹ of labor input):**

*Step 1 — Compute the total output value in rupees:*

$$
S = Q \times \text{Selling Price} = 8000 \times 250
$$

$$
S = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ (i.e., ₹20 lakhs)}
$$

*Step 2 — Compute the total labor cost in rupees:*

$$
L = H \times \text{Wage Rate} = 4000 \times 150
$$

$$
L = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹ (i.e., ₹6 lakhs)}
$$

*Step 3 — Compute the ratio:*

$$
P_L^{\text{value}} = \frac{S}{L} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
P_L^{\text{value}} = 3.33 \text{ ₹ of output per ₹ of labor input}
$$

---

### 3.2.2 Solution — Part (b): Machine Productivity

$$
P_M = \frac{Q}{M_h} = \frac{8000 \text{ units}}{2000 \text{ machine hours}}
$$

$$
P_M = 4.0 \text{ units per machine hour}
$$

---

### 3.2.3 Solution — Part (c): Material Productivity

*Step 1 — Convert material to per-rupee basis (already given in rupees):*

$$
P_{\text{mat}} = \frac{Q}{M_{\text{cost}}} = \frac{8000}{6{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
P_{\text{mat}} = 0.01333 \text{ units per ₹ of material}
$$

*Step 2 — Alternatively, in monetary terms:*

$$
P_{\text{mat}}^{\text{value}} = \frac{S}{M_{\text{cost}}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{6{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
P_{\text{mat}}^{\text{value}} = 3.33 \text{ ₹ of output per ₹ of material}
$$

---

### 3.2.4 Solution — Part (d): Multifactor Productivity (Labor + Material)

*Step 1 — Sum the two input costs:*

$$
I_{\text{LM}} = L + M = 6{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 12{,}00{,}000
$$

*Step 2 — Compute the ratio using output value:*

$$
P_{\text{LM}} = \frac{S}{I_{\text{LM}}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
P_{\text{LM}} = 1.667 \text{ ₹ of output per ₹ of combined labor + material input}
$$

---

### 3.2.5 Solution — Part (e): Total Productivity

*Step 1 — Identify all input costs:*

$$
I_{\text{total}} = L + M + E + \text{Depreciation on capital}
$$

For this problem, energy cost is direct and capital depreciation can be approximated (assuming 12% annual depreciation on ₹50 lakh, monthly = ₹50,000):

$$
E = 10000 \times 8 = 80{,}000
$$

$$
D = 50{,}000
$$

*Step 2 — Sum all inputs:*

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 6{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 50{,}000
$$

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 13{,}30{,}000
$$

*Step 3 — Compute the ratio:*

$$
P_T = \frac{S}{I_{\text{total}}} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{13{,}30{,}000}
$$

$$
P_T = 1.504 \text{ ₹ of output per ₹ of total input}
$$

---

### 3.2.6 Solution — Part (f): Productivity Index

*Step 1 — Apply the index formula:*

$$
I = \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100 = \frac{1.504}{2.20} \times 100
$$

$$
I = 68.36
$$

*Step 2 — Interpretation:*

$$
I = 68.36 \Rightarrow \text{Productivity has } \textbf{declined by } 31.64\% \text{ vs. base year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error in Part (f):** Some students mistakenly interpret the index as a *percentage increase*. If the index is **below 100**, it is always a **decline**, not a gain. The drop is calculated as $(100 - I) = (100 - 68.36) = 31.64\%$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Productivity

| # | Factor Category | Specific Factors | Effect on Productivity |
|---|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Technology & Capital** | Automation, CNC, robotics, ERP systems | ↑ Direct (replaces manual effort) |
| 2 | **Human Factors** | Training, skill level, motivation, absenteeism | ↑ with training and engagement |
| 3 | **Methods & Processes** | Work study, standardization, lean tools | ↑ by eliminating waste |
| 4 | **Material Quality** | Scrap rate, defect rate, supplier quality | ↑ with better input quality |
| 5 | **Working Conditions** | Lighting, ventilation, ergonomics, safety | ↑ with better environment |
| 6 | **Management Systems** | Planning, scheduling, control systems | ↑ with better coordination |
| 7 | **External Factors** | Government policy, taxes, infrastructure | Indirect but significant |
| 8 | **Plant Layout & Material Handling** | Distance travelled, waiting time | ↑ with optimized layout |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight (Module 1, IE Context):** Most of the 14-mark questions in this module expect students to *link* productivity ratios to **work study, method study, and time study** (covered in later modules) and to recommend specific industrial engineering interventions to improve a low productivity ratio.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Productivity Measurement Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **end-to-end functional flow** of how an industrial enterprise measures and acts upon productivity ratios. It maps the interaction between the production system, data capture, ratio computation, benchmarking, and corrective action.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Production System] --> B[Step 1: Define System Boundary]
    B --> C[Step 2: Identify Output Q in Units or Value]
    B --> D[Step 3: Identify Inputs Labor, Material, Capital, Energy]
    C --> E[Step 4: Compute Partial Productivity Q per Single Input]
    C --> F[Step 4b: Compute Multifactor Productivity Q per Multiple Inputs]
    C --> G[Step 4c: Compute Total Productivity Output Value per Total Input Cost]
    D --> E
    D --> F
    D --> G
    E --> H[Step 5: Compute Productivity Index versus Base Year]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Step 6: Benchmark against Past Industry or International Standards]
    I --> J{Decision: Is Ratio Satisfactory?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Step 7a: Document and Sustain Best Practices]
    J -->|No| L[Step 7b: Diagnose Root Causes using Work Study and Pareto Analysis]
    L --> M[Step 8: Implement IE Tools Lean Six Sigma Kaizen Automation]
    M --> N[Step 9: Re-measure Productivity in Next Cycle]
    N --> H
    K --> O[End: Continuous Improvement Loop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for students:** The block labelled `Q` represents the *quantified output*, while the inputs branch into four parallel streams. The **feedback loop from Step 9 back to Step 5** is the **Deming Cycle (PDCA)** applied to productivity management — Plan, Do, Check, Act.

## 4.2 Hierarchy Diagram — Three Tiers of Productivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Productivity Output over Input] --> PP[Partial Productivity]
    P --> MP[Multifactor Productivity]
    P --> TP[Total Productivity]
    PP --> PP1[Labor Productivity]
    PP --> PP2[Machine Productivity]
    PP --> PP3[Material Productivity]
    PP --> PP4[Capital Productivity]
    PP --> PP5[Energy Productivity]
    MP --> MP1[Labor plus Material]
    MP --> MP2[Labor plus Capital]
    MP --> MP3[Material plus Energy]
    TP --> TP1[All Inputs Aggregated to Monetary Units]
    TP1 --> KPI[Strategic Firm Wide KPI]
    PP1 --> OPR1[Shop Floor Operational Metric]
    PP2 --> OPR1
    PP3 --> OPR1
    MP1 --> OPR2[Departmental Process Metric]
    MP2 --> OPR2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' observation:** Many KTU answer scripts confuse the *scope* of these three tiers. Use the **scope-of-inputs rule of thumb**: *one input = partial, two-to-few inputs = multifactor, all inputs = total*. Memorize this distinction — it is the most frequently tested differentiator.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define productivity. Differentiate between partial productivity and total productivity.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Productivity is the ratio of output obtained to the input consumed in a production process. Mathematically, $P = O / I$.
- **Partial Productivity (1 mark):** It considers only **one input factor** (e.g., labor, material, machine). It is simple, easily computed, and useful for shop-floor decisions. Example: units produced per labor hour.
- **Total Productivity (1 mark):** It considers **all input factors combined** — labor, material, capital, energy, and overheads — converted to monetary terms. It is comprehensive and used for firm-wide strategic evaluation. Example: total output value / total input cost.

---

### Q2. List any four factors affecting productivity in a manufacturing industry.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct factor with brief justification):**
1. **Technology and automation** — Modern CNC, robotics, and ERP reduce manual effort and errors, directly raising output per worker.
2. **Skill level and training of workers** — Better-trained workers produce more units per hour with lower defect rates.
3. **Working conditions and ergonomics** — Adequate lighting, ventilation, and safe layouts reduce fatigue and absenteeism.
4. **Material quality and supplier reliability** — High-quality raw materials reduce scrap and rework, raising effective output.

*(Acceptable alternatives: management systems, plant layout, government policy, motivation systems.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

> **A manufacturing company produced 12,000 units in a month. The inputs were: Labor = 6,000 hours at ₹120/hour; Material = ₹8,00,000; Machine hours = 3,000; Capital = ₹60,00,000 (assume 1% monthly depreciation); Energy = 8,000 kWh at ₹10/kWh. Selling price = ₹300 per unit. Last year, the company's total productivity was 1.80.**
> **(a)** Compute the **labor productivity** (in units/hour and ₹/₹), **material productivity**, and **multifactor productivity (Labor + Material)**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compute the **total productivity** and the **productivity index**. Comment on the trend and suggest **three industrial engineering interventions** to improve it. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Partial and Multifactor Productivity [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Labor productivity in physical units:**

$$
P_L = \frac{12000}{6000} = 2.0 \text{ units/hour} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Step 2 — Output value and labor cost:**

$$
S = 12000 \times 300 = 36{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{[0.5 mark]}
$$

$$
L = 6000 \times 120 = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{[0.5 mark]}
$$

**Step 3 — Labor productivity in monetary terms:**

$$
P_L^{\text{value}} = \frac{36{,}00{,}000}{7{,}20{,}000} = 5.0 \text{ ₹/₹} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Step 4 — Material productivity in monetary terms:**

$$
P_{\text{mat}} = \frac{S}{M} = \frac{36{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} = 4.5 \text{ ₹/₹} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Step 5 — Combined labor + material input cost:**

$$
I_{\text{LM}} = 7{,}20{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 = 15{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Step 6 — Multifactor productivity (Labor + Material):**

$$
P_{\text{LM}} = \frac{36{,}00{,}000}{15{,}20{,}000} = 2.368 \text{ ₹/₹} \quad \text{[2 marks]}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — Total Productivity, Index, and Recommendations [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify all additional input costs:**

$$
E = 8000 \times 10 = 80{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
D = 60{,}00{,}000 \times 0.01 = 60{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Total input cost:**

$$
I_{\text{total}} = 7{,}20{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 = 16{,}60{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Step 3 — Total productivity:**

$$
P_T = \frac{36{,}00{,}000}{16{,}60{,}000} = 2.169 \text{ ₹/₹} \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Step 4 — Productivity index:**

$$
I = \frac{2.169}{1.80} \times 100 = 120.5 \quad \text{[1 mark]}
$$

**Step 5 — Trend comment (1 mark):**

The index of 120.5 indicates a **20.5% improvement in total productivity** over the previous year — a healthy gain.

**Step 6 — Three IE interventions (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Work Study and Method Study:** Conduct method study on the bottleneck operation to eliminate unnecessary motions, combine operations, and rearrange the workplace to reduce cycle time.
2. **Time Study and Performance Benchmarking:** Establish standard times for each operation; identify and train slow-pace workers; introduce a fair incentive wage scheme linked to standard hours.
3. **Lean Tools and 5S Implementation:** Deploy 5S to reduce searching and waiting time; introduce SMED and pull-based production to reduce work-in-process inventory; apply Kaizen workshops for continuous incremental improvement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **omit unit conversion** for energy and capital. Energy must be in **₹ (kWh × rate)**, and capital depreciation must be **amortized to the relevant time period** (monthly depreciation = annual rate / 12). Failure to do so will be marked down by **1 to 2 marks** per error, depending on the valuation key.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

> **(a)** Define the three types of productivity ratios with one numerical example each. Discuss why **partial productivity ratios may give a misleading picture** of overall efficiency. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A firm has the following data for two consecutive years. Compute the **productivity index** for each input factor and overall. Suggest **two methods to improve capital productivity**. **(7 marks)**
>
> | Input / Output | Year 1 | Year 2 |
> |---|---|---|
> | Output (units) | 10,000 | 14,000 |
> | Labor hours | 5,000 | 6,000 |
> | Material (kg) | 2,000 | 2,500 |
> | Capital (₹ lakhs) | 20 | 22 |
> | Selling price (₹/unit) | 200 | 210 |

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Definitions, Examples, and Limitation of Partial Productivity [7 marks]

**1. Partial Productivity (1.5 marks):**
Ratio of output to a *single* input.
*Example:* If 10,000 units are produced by 5,000 labor hours, labor productivity = 2 units/hour.

**2. Multifactor Productivity (1.5 marks):**
Ratio of output to *two or more combined* inputs.
*Example:* If 10,000 units are produced using ₹5,00,000 labor and ₹3,00,000 material, multifactor productivity (L+M) = 10,000 / 8,00,000 = 0.0125 units per rupee.

**3. Total Productivity (1.5 marks):**
Ratio of total output value to *total input cost* across all factors.
*Example:* Output value = ₹20,00,000; total input cost = ₹15,00,000; total productivity = 1.33 ₹/₹.

**4. Why partial ratios can mislead (2.5 marks):**
- A manager may boost **labor productivity** by switching to a cheaper, lower-quality material that causes higher scrap — output rises, but quality falls and customer returns rise.
- A factory may record excellent **machine productivity** by running machines 24×7 without maintenance — short-term gain, long-term breakdown and total productivity collapse.
- High **material productivity** might mask excess labor cost, and vice versa — partial ratios are **silo metrics** that ignore substitution effects between inputs.
- Hence, partial ratios must always be **read alongside multifactor or total productivity** for a true picture.

---

#### Part (b) — Productivity Index and Capital Productivity Improvement [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Year 1 productivities (2 marks):**

$$
P_{L1} = \frac{10000}{5000} = 2.0 \text{ units/hour}
$$

$$
P_{M1} = \frac{10000}{2000} = 5.0 \text{ units/kg}
$$

$$
P_{C1} = \frac{10000 \times 200}{20{,}00{,}000} = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} = 1.0 \text{ ₹/₹}
$$

**Step 2 — Year 2 productivities (2 marks):**

$$
P_{L2} = \frac{14000}{6000} = 2.33 \text{ units/hour}
$$

$$
P_{M2} = \frac{14000}{2500} = 5.6 \text{ units/kg}
$$

$$
P_{C2} = \frac{14000 \times 210}{22{,}00{,}000} = \frac{29{,}40{,}000}{22{,}00{,}000} = 1.336 \text{ ₹/₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Productivity indices (1 mark):**

$$
I_L = \frac{2.33}{2.0} \times 100 = 116.5 \quad (16.5\% \text{ gain})
$$

$$
I_M = \frac{5.6}{5.0} \times 100 = 112.0 \quad (12\% \text{ gain})
$$

$$
I_C = \frac{1.336}{1.0} \times 100 = 133.6 \quad (33.6\% \text{ gain})
$$

**Step 4 — Two methods to improve capital productivity (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Increase capacity utilization** through better production planning, reduced changeover time (SMED), and running the plant at higher OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) — this extracts more output from the same capital base.
2. **Outsource or lease non-core assets** rather than owning them, and replace low-ROI machinery with shared/contract-manufactured capacity — this lowers the capital denominator, directly raising capital productivity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In **Part (b) of Question B**, students often forget to multiply the output (units) by the **selling price** when computing capital productivity, because capital is in monetary (₹) units. The correct numerator is **monetary output value (₹), not physical units (units)**. This single error can cost up to **2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Productivity Ratios (Module 1)**

- **Core equation (memorize first):** $P = O / I$ — everything else is a variation of this.
- **Three tiers — one rule of thumb:** *1 input = Partial, 2+ inputs = Multifactor, ALL inputs = Total.*
- **Units must be compatible** within each ratio (units/units, ₹/₹). Never divide units by ₹ in a single ratio.
- **Productivity Index formula:** $I = (P_{\text{current}} / P_{\text{base}}) \times 100$. Below 100 = decline, above 100 = improvement.
- **Partial ratios can mislead** because they ignore input substitution effects — always validate with multifactor or total ratios.
- **Monetary form vs. physical form:** Physical form uses units, monetary form uses ₹ for both numerator and denominator — both are valid, but be consistent.
- **Energy cost formula:** $\text{Energy cost} = \text{kWh consumed} \times \text{Tariff (₹/kWh)}$.
- **Capital depreciation:** Always amortize to the relevant period (monthly or yearly), not the full capital value.
- **Top 5 factors affecting productivity** (in exam order): *Technology, Human skills, Work methods, Material quality, Working conditions.*
- **Three IE interventions to boost productivity** (safe exam answer for 14-mark questions): *Work study + method study, Time study + incentive wage, Lean / 5S / Kaizen.*
- **Commonly confused terms:** Productivity ≠ Production ≠ Efficiency. Productivity = Output/Input; Production = absolute output volume; Efficiency = Actual/Standard.
- **Examiner's golden rule:** *Always show the formula, substitute the values, and quote the final answer with units.* A formula written explicitly at the start of every numerical earns easy partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Productivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Productivity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Productivity** is formally defined as the **ratio of output produced to the input consumed** in a production process. It is a dimensionless indicator of the efficiency with which an organization converts its resources (inputs) into goods or services (outputs). Mathematically, the most fundamental expression is: $$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$ In the context of **Industrial Engineering**, productivity measurement is a *systematic, quantitative evaluation* of how effectively an enterprise uses its resources — including **Labor**, **Capital**, **Materials**, **Energy**, and **Time** — to generate economic value. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy (The Lemonade Stand):** > Imagine two children, *Aravind* and *Balu*, each running a lemonade stand. > - **Aravind** uses **10 lemons, 5 kg of sugar, and 4 hours** of effort to make **40 glasses** of lemonade. > - **Balu** uses the **same 10 lemons and 5 kg of sugar** but works only **2 hours** to make **40 glasses**. > > Although both produce the *same output* (40 glasses), **Balu is more productive** because he achieved the same output with *less input* (specifically, less time). This intuitive concept of "getting more from less" is the very heart of productivity measurement. ### 1.3 Types of Productivity — KTU Syllabus Classification The KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes productivity measurement into three principal types, each offering a different lens through which efficiency is evaluated: | # | Type | What it Measures | Common KTU Examples | |---|------|------------------|---------------------| | 1 | **Partial Productivity** | One specific input vs. output | Labor productivity, Machine productivity | | 2 | **Multifactor Productivity** | A group of related inputs vs. output | Labor + Material productivity, Capital productivity | | 3 | **Total Productivity** | **All** inputs combined vs. output | Organization-wide productivity index | > [!TIP] > **Key Takeaway:** A single productivity ratio is rarely sufficient. Engineers use a *suite* of partial, multifactor, and total productivity indices to get a complete picture of organizational efficiency. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Three Pillars #### 2.1.1 Partial Productivity (Single-Factor Productivity) - Considers the output in relation to **only one class of input**. - Useful for diagnosing bottlenecks in a *specific* resource category. - **Limitation:** A high partial productivity in one factor may mask inefficiencies in another. #### 2.1.2 Multifactor Productivity (Multi-Factor Productivity / MFP) - Combines **two or more input categories** into a single denominator. - Provides a more *balanced* and *holistic* view than partial productivity. - Widely used by national statistical agencies (e.g., Bureau of Labor Statistics uses **labor + capital** multifactor productivity). #### 2.1.3 Total Productivity (Total Factor Productivity / TFP) - Considers **the total output** divided by **the sum of all measurable inputs**. - Represents the *overall* effectiveness of an organization. - Most comprehensive but most data-intensive to compute. ### 2.2 Productivity Index (P.I.) > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Productivity Index** is a normalized ratio that compares productivity over **two time periods** (typically the *current period* vs. a *base period*). It is the standard tool for tracking productivity growth. $$\text{Productivity Index} = \frac{\text{Productivity in Current Period}}{\text{Productivity in Base Period}} \times 100$$ A P.I. of **110** indicates a **10% improvement** in productivity over the base period. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use this high-yield table as a one-stop reference for any KTU numerical problem on productivity measurement. | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Basic Productivity | $\text{P} = \dfrac{O}{I}$ | Output $\div$ Input | | 2 | Partial Productivity (Labor) | $P_L = \dfrac{O}{L}$ | Output per man-hour or per worker | | 3 | Partial Productivity (Machine) | $P_M = \dfrac{O}{M_h}$ | Output per machine-hour | | 4 | Partial Productivity (Material) | $P_{Mat} = \dfrac{O}{M}$ | Output per kg / per unit of material | | 5 | Multifactor Productivity | $P_{MF} = \dfrac{O}{I_1 + I_2 + \cdots}$ | Inputs must be in **same monetary unit** | | 6 | Total Productivity | $P_T = \dfrac{O}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i}$ | All input categories summed | | 7 | Productivity Index (P.I.) | $P.I. = \dfrac{P_{current}}{P_{base}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless percentage | | 8 | % Change in Productivity | $\Delta P\% = \dfrac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}} \times 100$ | For growth comparison | | 9 | Value-Added Productivity | $P_{VA} = \dfrac{\text{Value Added}}{\text{Total Input}}$ | Excludes purchased input cost | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing Plants:** Comparing the productivity of two shifts on the same machine. - **Service Industries (Banking, IT):** Measuring tickets resolved per employee-hour. - **National Policy:** TFP is a key indicator in India's **Economic Survey** and the **World Bank's** global competitiveness reports. - **Lean Manufacturing:** The foundational metric for **Kaizen** (continuous improvement) and **Six Sigma** projects. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Partial Productivity Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A manufacturing unit produces **2,000 units** of a product in a week. The unit employs **50 workers**, each working **40 hours** that week. Calculate the **Labor Productivity** in units per labor-hour. **Step 1 — Identify the Output (O).** $$O = 2000 \text{ units}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the Labor Input (L).** $$L = 50 \text{ workers} \times 40 \text{ hours/worker} = 2000 \text{ labor-hours}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the partial productivity formula.** $$P_L = \frac{O}{L} = \frac{2000 \text{ units}}{2000 \text{ labor-hours}}$$ $$\boxed{P_L = 1.0 \text{ unit/labor-hour}}$$ **Interpretation:** The workforce produces **1 unit of product per labor-hour**. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multifactor Productivity > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A factory produces **₹ 8,00,000 worth** of output in a month. The inputs are: > - **Labor cost:** ₹ 2,00,000 > - **Material cost:** ₹ 1,50,000 > - **Overhead cost:** ₹ 50,000 > > Calculate the **Multifactor Productivity** considering all three costs. **Step 1 — Sum the Input Costs (Total Input).** $$I_{total} = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 50{,}000$$ $$I_{total} = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ (in ₹)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Multifactor Productivity formula.** $$P_{MF} = \frac{O}{I_{total}} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{P_{MF} = 2.0}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Note:** When inputs are in **monetary (₹) units**, the resulting productivity ratio is also in **₹/₹ = dimensionless**. This is the standard convention in KTU numerical problems. **Interpretation:** For every **₹ 1** spent on combined inputs, the firm produces **₹ 2** worth of output. --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Productivity Index (P.I.) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** In 2023, a company produced **10,000 units** using **5,000 labor-hours** (Base Year). In 2024, the same company produced **13,200 units** using **6,000 labor-hours** (Current Year). Calculate the **Productivity Index** and the **% improvement**. **Step 1 — Compute Base Year Productivity (2023).** $$P_{base} = \frac{10{,}000 \text{ units}}{5{,}000 \text{ labor-hours}} = 2.0 \text{ units/labor-hour}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Current Year Productivity (2024).** $$P_{current} = \frac{13{,}200 \text{ units}}{6{,}000 \text{ labor-hours}} = 2.2 \text{ units/labor-hour}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the P.I. formula.** $$P.I. = \frac{P_{current}}{P_{base}} \times 100 = \frac{2.2}{2.0} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{P.I. = 110\%}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the % Change in Productivity.** $$\Delta P\% = \frac{P_{current} - P_{base}}{P_{base}} \times 100 = \frac{2.2 - 2.0}{2.0} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\Delta P\% = 10\%}$$ > [!TIP] > **Board Examiner's Insight:** Notice that *output* grew by **32%** but *productivity* (output per hour) grew by only **10%**. This is because labor-hours also increased. KTU loves this kind of two-step analysis to test conceptual clarity. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Total Productivity (Comprehensive) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** An automotive firm produced **1,000 cars** in a quarter. The resource consumption was: > - **Labor:** 20,000 hours @ ₹ 100/hour → ₹ 20,00,000 > - **Materials:** ₹ 80,00,000 > - **Capital (depreciation):** ₹ 15,00,000 > - **Energy:** ₹ 5,00,000 > > Total value of output: **₹ 1,50,00,000**. Calculate **Total Productivity**. **Step 1 — Sum all input costs.** $$\sum I_i = 20{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000$$ $$\sum I_i = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ (in ₹)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Total Productivity formula.** $$P_T = \frac{O}{\sum I_i} = \frac{1{,}50{,}00{,}000}{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}$$ $$\boxed{P_T = 1.25}$$ **Interpretation:** The firm generates **₹ 1.25 of output for every ₹ 1** of total input — a healthy productivity ratio for an automotive manufacturer. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Productivity Classification Flow > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block renders the **hierarchical classification** of productivity measurement types as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["PRODUCTIVITY MEASUREMENT"]:::root --> B["PARTIAL
PRODUCTIVITY"] A --> C["MULTIFACTOR
PRODUCTIVITY"] A --> D["TOTAL
PRODUCTIVITY"] B --> B1["LABOR
PRODUCTIVITY"] B --> B2["MACHINE
PRODUCTIVITY"] B --> B3["MATERIAL
PRODUCTIVITY"] B --> B4["ENERGY
PRODUCTIVITY"] C --> C1["LABOR + MATERIAL
PRODUCTIVITY"] C --> C2["LABOR + CAPITAL
PRODUCTIVITY"] C --> C3["LABOR + OVERHEAD
PRODUCTIVITY"] D --> D1["ORGANIZATION
LEVEL"] D --> D2["PLANT
LEVEL"] D --> D3["NATIONAL
LEVEL"] B1 --> E["P = Output
per labor-hour"] B2 --> F["P = Output
per machine-hour"] C1 --> G["P = Output
per total rupee cost"] D1 --> H["P = Total Output
per Total Input"] classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef child fill:#d9e1f2,stroke:#4472c4,color:#000000 classDef leaf fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Productivity Index Calculation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Collect Output
Data Current"] --> C["Productivity
Calculation Engine"] B["Collect Input
Data Current"] --> C D["Collect Output
Data Base"] --> E["Base Period
Productivity"] F["Collect Input
Data Base"] --> E C --> G["P.I. = Pcur / Pbase x 100"] E --> G G --> H["Percentage
Improvement"] G --> I["Benchmarking
Report"] classDef io fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#000000 classDef proc fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11,color:#000000 classDef out fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#000000 class A,B,D,F io class C,E,G proc class H,I out ``` ### 4.3 Productivity Improvement Strategy Map ```mermaid flowchart TD P["Measure Baseline
Productivity"]:::root --> Q["Identify Low
Productivity Area"] Q --> R1["Labor Issues"]:::lab Q --> R2["Material
Wastage"]:::lab Q --> R3["Machine
Downtime"]:::lab Q --> R4["Energy
Inefficiency"]:::lab R1 --> S1["Training
Kaizen
Motivation"] R2 --> S2["Lean
Inventory
Control"] R3 --> S3["TPM
Preventive
Maintenance"] R4 --> S4["Energy
