Compare to Target"] classDef root fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff classDef lab fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `P`, `R1`, `S1`) and labels contain only clean uppercase text — no markdown bold tags or HTML tables inside the labels — to ensure clean rendering in the KTU learning portal. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. Define Productivity. List its different types.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Productivity is defined as the **ratio of output produced to the input consumed** in a productive system. It measures the efficiency with which resources are converted into goods or services. $$P = \frac{O}{I}$$ The three principal types are: 1. **Partial Productivity** — measures output against a *single* input. 2. **Multifactor Productivity** — measures output against *a group* of inputs. 3. **Total Productivity** — measures output against *all* inputs combined. --- #### **Q2. What is a Productivity Index? How is it interpreted?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **Productivity Index (P.I.)** is a comparative measure of productivity between two time periods — typically the current period and a base period. $$P.I. = \frac{P_{current}}{P_{base}} \times 100$$ **Interpretation:** - **P.I. > 100** → Productivity has *increased* compared to the base period. - **P.I. = 100** → Productivity is *unchanged*. - **P.I. < 100** → Productivity has *decreased* compared to the base period. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Measurement Problem** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]` > **A manufacturing firm produced 25,000 units of a product in the year 2023 (base year) and 30,000 units in 2024. The corresponding input data is given below:** > > | Resource | 2023 | 2024 | > |----------|------|------| > | Labor (hours) | 12,500 | 13,000 | > | Material (kg) | 5,000 | 5,500 | > | Capital (₹) | 5,00,000 | 5,50,000 | > | Energy (kWh) | 2,500 | 2,400 | > > **The value of output is ₹ 50,00,000 in 2023 and ₹ 66,00,000 in 2024. The cost per unit of inputs is: Labor = ₹ 100/hr, Material = ₹ 50/kg, Capital = ₹ 1/₹ (i.e., direct), Energy = ₹ 10/kWh.** > > **Calculate:** > **(a)** Labor productivity and Material productivity for both years, and compute the productivity index for each. > **(b)** Multifactor productivity (using all four inputs) for both years, and comment on the firm's overall productivity improvement. **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Labor Productivity (2023):** $$P_{L, 2023} = \frac{25{,}000 \text{ units}}{12{,}500 \text{ hours}} = 2.0 \text{ units/labor-hour}$$ **Step 2 — Labor Productivity (2024):** $$P_{L, 2024} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ units}}{13{,}000 \text{ hours}} = 2.308 \text{ units/labor-hour}$$ **Step 3 — Labor Productivity Index:** $$P.I._{L} = \frac{2.308}{2.0} \times 100 = 115.38\%$$ **Step 4 — Material Productivity (2023):** $$P_{Mat, 2023} = \frac{25{,}000 \text{ units}}{5{,}000 \text{ kg}} = 5.0 \text{ units/kg}$$ **Step 5 — Material Productivity (2024):** $$P_{Mat, 2024} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ units}}{5{,}500 \text{ kg}} = 5.455 \text{ units/kg}$$ **Step 6 — Material Productivity Index:** $$P.I._{Mat} = \frac{5.455}{5.0} \times 100 = 109.09\%$$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion (a):** Labor productivity improved by **15.38%** while material productivity improved by **9.09%**. Labor is the *more productive contributor* in 2024. **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute Total Input Cost (2023):** $$I_{2023} = (12{,}500 \times 100) + (5{,}000 \times 50) + 5{,}00{,}000 + (2{,}500 \times 10)$$ $$I_{2023} = 12{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 + 25{,}000 = 20{,}25{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Compute Total Input Cost (2024):** $$I_{2024} = (13{,}000 \times 100) + (5{,}500 \times 50) + 5{,}50{,}000 + (2{,}400 \times 10)$$ $$I_{2024} = 13{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}75{,}000 + 5{,}50{,}000 + 24{,}000 = 21{,}49{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Multifactor Productivity (2023):** $$P_{MF, 2023} = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{20{,}25{,}000} = 2.469$$ **Step 4 — Multifactor Productivity (2024):** $$P_{MF, 2024} = \frac{66{,}00{,}000}{21{,}49{,}000} = 3.071$$ **Step 5 — Multifactor Productivity Index:** $$P.I._{MF} = \frac{3.071}{2.469} \times 100 = 124.38\%$$ **Step 6 — % Improvement in MFP:** $$\Delta P_{MF}\% = \frac{3.071 - 2.469}{2.469} \times 100 = 24.38\%$$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion (b):** The firm's overall multifactor productivity improved by **24.38%** in 2024, indicating a substantial efficiency gain across all resources. The growth in output value (32%) outpaced the growth in input cost (~6.1%), confirming strong productivity performance. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > **A factory operates for 50 weeks a year. The following data is available for the years 2022 and 2023:** > > | Particulars | 2022 | 2023 | > |-------------|------|------| > | Units produced | 1,00,000 | 1,20,000 | > | Number of workers | 200 | 180 | > | Hours/week/worker | 40 | 40 | > | Material used (tonnes) | 500 | 540 | > | Total energy consumed (kWh) | 1,00,000 | 95,000 | > | Capital employed (₹ in lakhs) | 50 | 55 | > > **(a)** Compute the **partial productivity** for labor, material, and energy for both years and find the **Productivity Index** for each. > **(b)** If the cost per unit of inputs is: Labor = ₹ 80/hour, Material = ₹ 10,000/tonne, Energy = ₹ 8/kWh, Capital = 12% of capital employed, calculate the **Total Productivity** for both years and the **P.I. of Total Productivity**. **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Labor-hours calculation:** $$L_{2022} = 200 \times 40 \times 50 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ labor-hours}$$ $$L_{2023} = 180 \times 40 \times 50 = 3{,}60{,}000 \text{ labor-hours}$$ **Step 2 — Labor Productivity:** $$P_{L, 2022} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{4{,}00{,}000} = 0.25 \text{ units/labor-hour}$$ $$P_{L, 2023} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{3{,}60{,}000} = 0.333 \text{ units/labor-hour}$$ **Step 3 — Labor P.I.:** $$P.I._L = \frac{0.333}{0.25} \times 100 = 133.33\%$$ **Step 4 — Material Productivity:** $$P_{Mat, 2022} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{500} = 200 \text{ units/tonne}$$ $$P_{Mat, 2023} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{540} = 222.22 \text{ units/tonne}$$ **Step 5 — Material P.I.:** $$P.I._{Mat} = \frac{222.22}{200} \times 100 = 111.11\%$$ **Step 6 — Energy Productivity:** $$P_{E, 2022} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{1{,}00{,}000} = 1.0 \text{ unit/kWh}$$ $$P_{E, 2023} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{95{,}000} = 1.263 \text{ units/kWh}$$ **Step 7 — Energy P.I.:** $$P.I._E = \frac{1.263}{1.0} \times 100 = 126.32\%$$ **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:** **Step 1 — Compute 2022 Input Costs:** $$I_{L, 2022} = 4{,}00{,}000 \times 80 = 3{,}20{,}00{,}000$$ $$I_{Mat, 2022} = 500 \times 10{,}000 = 50{,}00{,}000$$ $$I_{E, 2022} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 8 = 8{,}00{,}000$$ $$I_{C, 2022} = 0.12 \times 50{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}00{,}000$$ $$\sum I_{2022} = 3{,}20{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}00{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 = 3{,}84{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Compute 2023 Input Costs:** $$I_{L, 2023} = 3{,}60{,}000 \times 80 = 2{,}88{,}00{,}000$$ $$I_{Mat, 2023} = 540 \times 10{,}000 = 54{,}00{,}000$$ $$I_{E, 2023} = 95{,}000 \times 8 = 7{,}60{,}000$$ $$I_{C, 2023} = 0.12 \times 55{,}00{,}000 = 6{,}60{,}000$$ $$\sum I_{2023} = 2{,}88{,}00{,}000 + 54{,}00{,}000 + 7{,}60{,}000 + 6{,}60{,}000 = 3{,}56{,}80{,}000$$ **Step 3 — Total Productivity (Assume output value remains proportional to units — take unit price as ₹ 100):** $$O_{2022} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 100 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000$$ $$O_{2023} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 100 = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000$$ **Step 4 — Total Productivity values:** $$P_{T, 2022} = \frac{1{,}00{,}00{,}000}{3{,}84{,}00{,}000} = 0.2604$$ $$P_{T, 2023} = \frac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{3{,}56{,}80{,}000} = 0.3364$$ **Step 5 — Total Productivity Index:** $$P.I._T = \frac{0.3364}{0.2604} \times 100 = 129.19\%$$ **Step 6 — % Improvement:** $$\Delta P_T\% = 29.19\%$$ > [!TIP] > **Conclusion:** The firm has improved total productivity by **29.19%**, with the **largest gains in labor (33.33%)** and **energy (26.32%)**, signaling effective workforce rationalization and energy efficiency programs. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Forgetting to multiply cost per unit by the **total quantity** (e.g., students often write 12% as 12 instead of 0.12). This is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in multifactor productivity questions. > 2. **Labor-Hours vs. Number of Workers:** Always convert workers to **labor-hours** before computing labor productivity. Writing `1,00,000 / 200` is a common, costly error. > 3. **P.I. Interpretation:** KTU specifically tests whether students can *interpret* a P.I. of 115 as a "15% increase." Simply writing the number without the percentage interpretation loses 1–2 marks. > 4. **Mixing Units:** In multifactor productivity, never mix physical units (kg, kWh) with monetary units (₹). Convert **all** inputs to a common monetary unit first. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Measurement of Productivity** - **Definition:** $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ — the fundamental building block. - **Three Types:** Partial (1 input), Multifactor (≥2 inputs), Total (all inputs). - **Partial Productivity Examples:** Labor, Machine, Material, Energy, Capital. - **Multifactor Productivity:** Always convert all inputs to a **common monetary unit (₹)**. - **Total Productivity:** Most comprehensive; uses **all** measured input categories. - **Productivity Index (P.I.):** $P.I. = \dfrac{P_{current}}{P_{base}} \times 100$ — used for period-over-period comparison. - **% Change in P:** $\Delta P\% = \dfrac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}} \times 100$. - **Labor-Hours =** (Number of Workers) × (Hours/Week) × (Number of Weeks). - **Common KTU Trap:** Output can be in **units** (for partial) or **₹ value** (for multifactor/total) — read the question carefully. - **Interpretation Rule:** P.I. > 100 → improvement; P.I. < 100 → decline. - **Value-Added Productivity:** Excludes the cost of *purchased materials/services* from input to focus on internal efficiency. - **Productivity vs. Production:** Productivity is *efficiency* (output/input); Production is just *output* — KTU often tests this distinction. - **Engineering Utility:** Forms the basis for **Kaizen**, **Lean**, **TPM**, **Six Sigma**, and **Industry 4.0** benchmarking.

topics.title: Productivity Improvement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Productivity Improvement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Productivity** is formally defined as the **ratio of output produced to the input consumed** in a production process, measured over a specified period of time. Mathematically expressed as the relationship between what is produced (goods and services) and the resources used to produce them (labor, capital, materials, energy, time, and information).

In the context of Industrial Engineering, productivity is not merely a single number but a multidimensional measure that captures the **efficiency** with which an organization converts its various inputs into valuable outputs. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes productivity as the **central outcome variable** of any industrial system because it directly determines competitive advantage, profitability, wage levels, and ultimately the **standard of living** of a society.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Lemonade Stand" Intuition**
> Imagine a child running a lemonade stand. She uses lemons, sugar, water, cups, and her own time as *inputs*. The glasses of lemonade sold and money earned are her *outputs*. If she figures out how to extract more juice from each lemon (better squeezing method) or serve customers faster (better process), her **productivity rises** — more output for the same input. Productivity improvement, therefore, is simply the art of getting **more value from the same effort**.

**Three Interlinked Concepts** (frequently confused by students):

| Term | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Effectiveness** | Doing the *right things* (achieving goals) | Aiming at a high-profit market |
| **Efficiency** | Doing things the *right way* (minimizing waste) | Producing with minimum input |
| **Productivity** | Output ÷ Input (a *measure* of performance) | Units produced per labor-hour |

The classic relationship taught in KTU IE courses is:

> **Productivity = Effectiveness × Efficiency**

### 1.3 Standard Productivity Metrics (Key Constants and Indicators)

The universally accepted productivity metrics in industrial engineering practice are:

- **Partial (Single-Factor) Productivity** — Output ÷ One input (e.g., labor productivity)
- **Multifactor Productivity** — Output ÷ Group of inputs (e.g., labor + capital)
- **Total Productivity** — Total Output ÷ Total Input (all factors aggregated)
- **Productivity Index** — (Current Productivity ÷ Base Productivity) × **100**
- **Standard of Living Index** — directly proportional to Total Productivity

> [!TIP]
> The KTU Board Examiner expects students to clearly distinguish between **partial**, **multifactor**, and **total** productivity — confusing these three is the single most common cause of lost marks in Part B answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Conceptual Foundation — Why Productivity Matters

Productivity is the **root cause** of economic growth. The KTU 2024 IE syllabus frames productivity around five fundamental propositions:

1. **Productivity is the ratio of output to input** — it is dimensionless when both are in same units, but typically expressed in *units/hour*, *Rs./Rs.*, or *tons/man-hour*.
2. **It applies to all resources**, not just labor. Every input can be measured for its productivity contribution.
3. **Improvement can come from either side** — increasing output OR decreasing input (or both simultaneously).
4. **It is the engine of standard of living** — higher productivity → lower unit cost → higher real wages → higher purchasing power.
5. **It is the most important competitive metric** — nations with higher productivity dominate global trade.

### 2.2 Classification of Productivity

#### 2.2.1 Partial Productivity (Single-Factor)
Measures output relative to **one specific input**.

$$
P_{\text{partial}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Single Input}}
$$

Common forms:
- **Labor Productivity** = Units Produced ÷ Direct Labor Hours
- **Machine Productivity** = Units Produced ÷ Machine Hours
- **Material Productivity** = Units Produced ÷ Material Used
- **Capital Productivity** = Output Value ÷ Capital Invested
- **Energy Productivity** = Output ÷ Energy Consumed

#### 2.2.2 Multifactor Productivity
Measures output against a **group of related inputs**.

$$
P_{\text{multi}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}_1 + \text{Input}_2 + \dots}
$$

Example: Labor + Materials productivity = Output ÷ (Labor cost + Material cost).

#### 2.2.3 Total Productivity
The most comprehensive measure.

$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Output (in value)}}{\text{Total Input (in value)}}
$$

This requires that all outputs and inputs be expressed in **monetary terms** (typically rupees, dollars, or any common currency).

### 2.3 Productivity Index and Improvement

The **Productivity Index (PI)** is a normalized metric that compares current productivity against a base period.

$$
\text{Productivity Index} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100
$$

The **Percentage Improvement in Productivity** is given by:

$$
\% \Delta P \;=\; \left(\frac{P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}}}{P_{\text{old}}}\right) \times 100
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{O}{I}$ | Basic productivity ratio | Output per Input |
| 2 | $P_L = \dfrac{O}{L}$ | Labor productivity | Units per labor-hour |
| 3 | $P_M = \dfrac{O}{M_h}$ | Machine productivity | Units per machine-hour |
| 4 | $P_{\text{multi}} = \dfrac{O}{I_1 + I_2 + \dots}$ | Multifactor productivity | Value-based ratio |
| 5 | $P_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{\sum O_i}{\sum I_j}$ | Total productivity | Value-based ratio |
| 6 | $PI = \dfrac{P_{\text{new}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | Productivity Index | Percentage (Index Number) |
| 7 | $\% \Delta P = \left(\dfrac{P_n - P_o}{P_o}\right) \times 100$ | Productivity improvement % | Percentage |
| 8 | $P = E_{\text{eff}} \times E_{\text{fic}}$ | Productivity from effectiveness & efficiency | Ratio (0–1) |
| 9 | $P = \dfrac{\text{Standard Hours Earned}}{\text{Actual Hours Worked}}$ | Labor productivity in work-study context | Ratio |
| 10 | $C_u = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{O}$ | Unit cost (inverse relation to productivity) | Rs. per unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Reminder:** When calculating Total Productivity, all inputs and outputs **must** be converted to a common monetary base. Mixing units (e.g., labor hours with tons of output) is the most common error.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Productivity Concepts

- **Strategic Planning** — Productivity benchmarks drive national industrial policy and corporate strategy.
- **Cost Control** — Unit cost is the **inverse** of productivity; tracking productivity is equivalent to tracking cost-effectiveness.
- **Performance Evaluation** — Used to compare shifts, plants, departments, and even nations (e.g., GDP per labor-hour).
- **Wage Negotiation** — Productivity gains are the rational basis for wage increases and incentive schemes.
- **Lean Manufacturing** — Kaizen, Six Sigma, and Lean all aim at productivity improvement through waste elimination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Productivity and Unit Cost

**Goal:** Show that productivity improvement directly reduces unit cost.

**Step 1 — Define total cost.** The total cost of producing $O$ units using input of cost $C_I$ is:

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; C_I + C_{\text{fixed}}
$$

where $C_I$ is the variable input cost and $C_{\text{fixed}}$ is the fixed cost.

**Step 2 — Define productivity.** Productivity $P$ is the output obtained per unit of input:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{O}{I}
$$

**Step 3 — Express input in terms of productivity.** Rearranging:

$$
I \;=\; \frac{O}{P}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into cost equation.** Substituting into the cost equation:

$$
C_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{c_i \cdot O}{P} + C_{\text{fixed}}
$$

where $c_i$ is the cost per unit of input.

**Step 5 — Derive unit cost.** The unit cost $C_u$ is:

$$
C_u \;=\; \frac{C_{\text{total}}}{O} \;=\; \frac{c_i}{P} + \frac{C_{\text{fixed}}}{O}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.** As $P$ increases, $\dfrac{c_i}{P}$ decreases. Hence:

$$
\boxed{\;C_u \;\propto\; \frac{1}{P}\;}
$$

**Conclusion:** Productivity and unit cost are **inversely related**. A 10% increase in productivity reduces the variable component of unit cost by approximately 9.09%.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Productivity Index and Compound Growth

**Goal:** Derive the compound productivity growth formula.

**Step 1 — Define annual productivity.** Let $P_0$ be the base year productivity and $P_n$ the productivity after $n$ years.

**Step 2 — Apply index formula.**

$$
PI_n \;=\; \frac{P_n}{P_0} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce compound annual growth rate (CAGR).** If productivity grows at constant annual rate $r$:

$$
P_n \;=\; P_0 \,(1 + r)^n
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the index formula:**

$$
PI_n \;=\; \frac{P_0 \,(1 + r)^n}{P_0} \times 100 \;=\; (1 + r)^n \times 100
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for growth rate $r$:**

$$
r \;=\; (PI_n / 100)^{1/n} - 1
$$

**Step 6 — Worked example.** Suppose base productivity = 10 units/hour, after 3 years it becomes 12.5 units/hour:

$$
PI_3 \;=\; \frac{12.5}{10} \times 100 \;=\; 125
$$

$$
r \;=\; (1.25)^{1/3} - 1 \;=\; 1.0772 - 1 \;=\; 0.0772 \;=\; 7.72\% \text{ per annum}
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board-Style)

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit produces 5,000 units in a 40-hour week using 10 workers, raw material worth Rs. 50,000, and 30 machine hours of capital equipment. After a productivity improvement program, output rises to 6,000 units in the same time, workers reduce to 9, material cost drops to Rs. 48,000, and machine hours drop to 28. Calculate all relevant productivity measures and the productivity index.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Labor productivity (Before):**

$$
P_{L,\text{old}} \;=\; \frac{5000}{10 \times 40} \;=\; \frac{5000}{400} \;=\; 12.50 \text{ units/labor-hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Labor productivity (After):**

$$
P_{L,\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{6000}{9 \times 40} \;=\; \frac{6000}{360} \;=\; 16.67 \text{ units/labor-hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Labor productivity index:**

$$
PI_{L} \;=\; \frac{16.67}{12.50} \times 100 \;=\; 133.33
$$

**Step 4 — Material productivity (Before):**

$$
P_{M,\text{old}} \;=\; \frac{5000}{50000} \;=\; 0.100 \text{ units/Rs.}
$$

**Step 5 — Material productivity (After):**

$$
P_{M,\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{6000}{48000} \;=\; 0.125 \text{ units/Rs.}
$$

**Step 6 — Material productivity index:**

$$
PI_{M} \;=\; \frac{0.125}{0.100} \times 100 \;=\; 125
$$

**Step 7 — Machine productivity (Before):**

$$
P_{\text{mc,old}} \;=\; \frac{5000}{30} \;=\; 166.67 \text{ units/machine-hour}
$$

**Step 8 — Machine productivity (After):**

$$
P_{\text{mc,new}} \;=\; \frac{6000}{28} \;=\; 214.29 \text{ units/machine-hour}
$$

**Step 9 — Machine productivity index:**

$$
PI_{\text{mc}} \;=\; \frac{214.29}{166.67} \times 100 \;=\; 128.57
$$

**Step 10 — Multifactor productivity (Labor + Material, Before):**
Assuming labor cost = Rs. 100/hour per worker:

$$
\text{Labor cost (old)} \;=\; 10 \times 40 \times 100 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 40{,}000
$$

$$
P_{\text{multi,old}} \;=\; \frac{5000}{40000 + 50000} \;=\; \frac{5000}{90000} \;=\; 0.0556 \text{ units/Rs.}
$$

**Step 11 — Multifactor productivity (After):**

$$
\text{Labor cost (new)} \;=\; 9 \times 40 \times 100 \;=\; \text{Rs. } 36{,}000
$$

$$
P_{\text{multi,new}} \;=\; \frac{6000}{36000 + 48000} \;=\; \frac{6000}{84000} \;=\; 0.0714 \text{ units/Rs.}
$$

**Step 12 — Multifactor productivity index:**

$$
PI_{\text{multi}} \;=\; \frac{0.0714}{0.0556} \times 100 \;=\; 128.46
$$

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Productivity Type | Before | After | Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| Labor Productivity | 12.50 | 16.67 | **133.33** |
| Material Productivity | 0.100 | 0.125 | **125.00** |
| Machine Productivity | 166.67 | 214.29 | **128.57** |
| Multifactor (L + M) | 0.0556 | 0.0714 | **128.46** |

**Inference:** Labor productivity improved the most (33.33%), indicating that workforce optimization was the dominant driver of the improvement program — a typical outcome of method-study based interventions.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Approaches to Productivity Improvement

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes between **Hard** and **Soft** approaches. This matrix is high-yield for 14-mark answers.

| Dimension | Hard Approach | Soft Approach |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Technology & capital investment-driven | Human & system-driven |
| **Focus** | Machines, tools, automation, plant layout | People, culture, motivation, training |
| **Time horizon** | Medium to long term | Short to medium term |
| **Investment** | High capital expenditure | Low to moderate |
| **Resistance to change** | Low (welcomed if jobs preserved) | Higher (involves behavioral change) |
| **Examples** | CNC machines, robots, ERP, automation | Kaizen, TQM, Quality Circles, Suggestion schemes |
| **Risk** | High if demand falls (asset underutilization) | Low and reversible |
| **Sustainability** | Sustained if maintained well | Sustained only with continuous culture-building |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A complete 14-mark answer on productivity improvement must integrate BOTH hard and soft approaches — examiners explicitly check whether the student understands that productivity is a **socio-technical** outcome, not a purely technical one.

---

### 3.5 The Productivity–Standard of Living Cycle

A classic KTU 2024 syllbus concept is the **virtuous cycle** linking productivity to national prosperity:

$$
\text{Higher Productivity} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lower Unit Cost} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Higher Real Wages} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Higher Purchasing Power} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Higher Demand} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Higher Production Volume} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Economies of Scale} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Even Higher Productivity}
$$

This cycle explains why nations like Japan, South Korea, and Germany achieved high living standards through decades of sustained productivity growth.

---

### 3.6 Productivity Improvement Techniques — Comprehensive List

| Sl. No. | Technique | Type | Primary Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Method Study | Hard + Soft | Process design |
| 2 | Time Study / Work Measurement | Hard | Labor efficiency |
| 3 | Motion Economy (Therbligs) | Soft | Operator movement |
| 4 | Plant Layout & Material Handling | Hard | Flow efficiency |
| 5 | Inventory Control (EOQ, JIT) | Hard | Material productivity |
| 6 | Quality Management (TQM, Six Sigma) | Soft | Defect reduction |
| 7 | Value Engineering / Analysis | Hard | Cost optimization |
| 8 | Ergonomics | Soft | Human comfort + output |
| 9 | Job Design & Specialization | Soft | Worker motivation |
| 10 | Incentive Wage Plans | Soft | Labor productivity |
| 11 | Automation & Mechanization | Hard | Machine productivity |
| 12 | Kaizen (Continuous Improvement) | Soft | Culture of improvement |
| 13 | Lean Manufacturing | Hard + Soft | Waste elimination |
| 14 | Preventive Maintenance | Hard | Equipment uptime |
| 15 | Training & Skill Development | Soft | Worker capability |
| 16 | Better Management Information Systems | Hard | Decision quality |
| 17 | Just-in-Time Production | Hard | Inventory productivity |
| 18 | Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM) | Hard | System-wide integration |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Conceptual Model of Productivity

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **Input → Process → Output** model of productivity and shows the factors influencing it.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define productivity. Explain the difference between partial productivity and total productivity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Productivity is the ratio of output produced to the input consumed in a production process, mathematically expressed as:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}
$$

**Partial Productivity (1 mark):** Partial productivity measures output in relation to a **single** input factor. Examples include labor productivity, machine productivity, and material productivity. It is easy to compute but provides an incomplete picture.

**Total Productivity (1 mark):** Total productivity measures the value of all outputs against the value of **all** inputs, expressed in monetary terms. It is the most comprehensive measure and is used for organization-wide performance benchmarking.

**Key difference:** Partial productivity considers only one input at a time and can be misleading (a gain in one factor may mask a loss in another), whereas total productivity provides a holistic view of resource utilization.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**List any three factors affecting productivity and state how productivity is linked to the standard of living of a nation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three factors affecting productivity (1.5 marks):**

1. **Technology and Automation** — adoption of modern machinery raises machine and labor productivity.
2. **Worker Skill and Training** — skilled workers produce more output with less input.
3. **Plant Layout and Material Handling** — efficient layouts reduce movement and waiting time.

(Other acceptable answers: quality of inputs, management systems, work culture, motivation.)

**Link to standard of living (1.5 marks):** Higher productivity lowers unit cost, allowing firms to pay higher real wages. Higher wages raise purchasing power, which expands demand and drives further production — a virtuous cycle. Thus, **productivity is the engine of a nation's standard of living**. Nations with sustained productivity growth (Japan, Germany) consistently show higher per-capita income and quality of life.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a) Define productivity. Explain the various types of productivity with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Productivity is the quantitative relationship between output produced and input consumed in a process, expressed as:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}
$$

It is a dimensionless ratio when input and output are in identical units, otherwise measured in units of output per unit of input (e.g., tons/hour, Rs./Rs.).

**Types of Productivity (5 marks):**

**1. Partial Productivity (Single-Factor Productivity):** Considers only one input at a time.

- *Labor productivity* = Output ÷ Labor hours
- *Machine productivity* = Output ÷ Machine hours
- *Material productivity* = Output ÷ Material used
- *Capital productivity* = Output value ÷ Capital invested

Example: A worker producing 50 units in 8 hours has a labor productivity of 6.25 units/hour.

**2. Multifactor Productivity:** Considers a group of related inputs combined.

Example: (Labor + Material) productivity = Output ÷ (Labor cost + Material cost). A company producing Rs. 10 lakh output with Rs. 2 lakh labor + Rs. 3 lakh material has a multifactor productivity of 2.0.

**3. Total Productivity:** Considers ALL inputs in monetary value.

$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Output Value}}{\text{Total Input Value}}
$$

This is the most rigorous and organization-wide measure, used for benchmarking at the firm and national level.

**4. Productivity Index:** A relative measure comparing current to base period.

$$
PI \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100
$$

Example: A PI of 125 means productivity has increased 25% over the base period.

---

**(b) Discuss the hard and soft approaches to productivity improvement with examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hard Approach (3.5 marks):**
The hard approach focuses on **technology, capital, and physical systems**. It is investment-intensive and produces measurable, sustainable results.

Examples:
- **Automation & CNC machines** — raise output per labor-hour dramatically.
- **Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (CIM)** — integrates design, production, and quality control.
- **Plant layout redesign** — minimizes material handling and travel time.
- **Inventory control systems (JIT, EOQ)** — reduce carrying cost and material waste.
- **Value Engineering** — eliminates non-value-adding features in design.

**Soft Approach (3.5 marks):**
The soft approach focuses on **people, culture, motivation, and management systems**. It requires less capital but greater behavioral change.

Examples:
- **Kaizen** — small, continuous, employee-driven improvements.
- **Total Quality Management (TQM)** — organization-wide quality culture.
- **Quality Circles** — small groups of workers meeting regularly to solve problems.
- **Suggestion schemes and incentive plans** — reward employees for ideas.
- **Training and skill development** — raise human capability.
- **Ergonomics** — design workplaces for human comfort and efficiency.

**Integration:** Modern KTU 2024 answers emphasize that productivity is a **socio-technical** outcome. The most effective programs combine hard and soft approaches — e.g., introducing CNC machines (hard) along with operator training and Kaizen teams (soft). Companies that focus only on one approach achieve limited and unsustainable results.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`

**(a) Explain the concept of Productivity Index. A factory produced 8000 units last year using 4000 labor hours. This year, due to a productivity drive, output rose to 9500 units using 4200 labor hours. Calculate the percentage improvement in labor productivity and the productivity index. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept of Productivity Index (2 marks):** The Productivity Index is a normalized comparison of current productivity against a base period, expressed as a percentage.

$$
PI \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{current}}}{P_{\text{base}}} \times 100
$$

It allows organizations to track productivity trends over time and benchmark across departments.

**Calculations (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Labor productivity (Base year):**

$$
P_{\text{base}} \;=\; \frac{8000}{4000} \;=\; 2.00 \text{ units/labor-hour}
$$

**Step 2 — Labor productivity (Current year):**

$$
P_{\text{current}} \;=\; \frac{9500}{4200} \;=\; 2.2619 \text{ units/labor-hour}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage improvement in productivity:**

$$
\% \Delta P \;=\; \left(\frac{2.2619 - 2.00}{2.00}\right) \times 100 \;=\; \frac{0.2619}{2.00} \times 100 \;=\; 13.095\%
$$

**Step 4 — Productivity Index:**

$$
PI \;=\; \frac{2.2619}{2.00} \times 100 \;=\; 113.095 \;\approx\; 113.10
$$

**Inference (optional, 0 marks but recommended):** The productivity drive yielded a 13.10% improvement in labor productivity. The productivity index of 113.10 indicates that the current output-per-hour is 13.10% higher than the base year — a strong performance.

---

**(b) Describe the productivity–standard of living cycle. List six techniques that can improve productivity in a manufacturing organization. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Productivity–Standard of Living Cycle (4 marks):**

The cycle operates as a closed loop where each stage reinforces the next:

**Step 1:** Higher productivity → **Step 2:** Lower unit cost → **Step 3:** Higher real wages for workers → **Step 4:** Higher purchasing power → **Step 5:** Higher demand for goods and services → **Step 6:** Greater production volume → **Step 7:** Economies of scale set in → **Step 8:** Returns to higher productivity.

This self-reinforcing loop explains why nations that invest in productivity (education, technology, infrastructure) sustain higher living standards. The cycle is the foundation of long-term economic growth and is a critical concept in the KTU IE syllabus.

**Six Productivity Improvement Techniques (3 marks):**

1. **Method Study and Work Simplification** — eliminate unnecessary operations.
2. **Time Study and Work Measurement** — set standards and identify inefficiencies.
3. **Plant Layout and Material Handling** — reduce travel and waiting.
4. **Inventory Control (JIT, EOQ)** — minimize inventory holding costs.
5. **Quality Management (TQM, Six Sigma)** — reduce rework and defects.
6. **Incentive Wage Plans** — motivate workers to exceed standards.
7. *(Any other valid technique accepted: Kaizen, Value Engineering, Automation, Ergonomics, Training.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing units in Total Productivity** — students frequently divide output (units) by inputs in different units (hours, kg, Rs.). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unit inconsistency. Always convert to a **monetary common base**.
> 2. **Confusing effectiveness, efficiency, and productivity** — these three terms appear interchangeable but are not. Effectiveness = goal achievement; Efficiency = input-output ratio of the chosen goal; Productivity = measurable O/I ratio. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring the soft approach** — a 14-mark answer that lists only automation and CNC machines without mentioning Kaizen, TQM, training, or human factors is treated as incomplete. The KTU 2024 scheme expects the **socio-technical** perspective.
> 4. **Not showing steps in productivity calculations** — direct jumps to final answer without showing $P_{\text{old}}$, $P_{\text{new}}$, and the index formula are penalized heavily. Always show: Step 1, Step 2, …, Final.
> 5. **Confusing Productivity Index with Percentage Improvement** — these are related but distinct. PI is a ratio × 100 (e.g., 113); percentage improvement is the difference (e.g., 13%). Examiners frequently test whether students can compute both correctly.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Productivity Improvement (Module 1)**

- [ ] **Productivity = Output ÷ Input** — the universal formula in all its forms.
- [ ] **Three types:** Partial (single input), Multifactor (group of inputs), Total (all inputs in monetary terms).
- [ ] **Productivity Index = (P_new / P_old) × 100** — relative measure; base year = 100.
- [ ] **% Improvement = ((P_new – P_old) / P_old) × 100** — absolute measure of gain.
- [ ] **Productivity and unit cost are inversely related** — a doubling of productivity roughly halves the variable cost per unit.
- [ ] **Effectiveness = Doing the right things; Efficiency = Doing things right; Productivity = Output/Input measure.**
- [ ] **Hard approach** = technology, capital, automation, plant layout.
- [ ] **Soft approach** = people, culture, Kaizen, TQM, training, motivation.
- [ ] **Productivity–living standard cycle** is a closed self-reinforcing loop linking productivity to wages to demand to production to higher productivity.
- [ ] **At least 18 productivity improvement techniques** are recognized in the syllabus; the top six to memorize are: Method Study, Time Study, Plant Layout, Inventory Control, Quality Management, Incentive Plans.
- [ ] **Always convert inputs to a common monetary base** when computing Total Productivity.
- [ ] **Compound productivity growth** follows $P_n = P_0 (1 + r)^n$ — useful for multi-year projections.
- [ ] **Partial productivity gains can be misleading** — a rise in labor productivity may be due to over-utilization of other inputs; multifactor/total productivity is more reliable.
- [ ] **Remember the socio-technical perspective** — productivity is achieved by integrating technology with people, never by technology alone.
- [ ] **Labor productivity in work-study context** = Standard Hours Earned ÷ Actual Hours Worked (used in incentive calculations).
- [ ] **National productivity is the foundation of GDP per capita** — a key macroeconomic indicator of prosperity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Productivity in Indian Industries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Productivity in Indian Industries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — PRODUCTIVITY IN INDIAN INDUSTRIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Productivity (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Productivity** is formally defined as the **ratio of output produced to the input consumed** in any production process, expressed as a quantitative measure of the efficiency with which an economic system, organization, or individual transforms resources (labour, capital, materials, energy) into goods and services.

Mathematically, the fundamental productivity identity used across all KTU question papers is:

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

- **Output** may be measured in units produced, value added, revenue earned, or service delivered.
- **Input** may be measured in man-hours, machine-hours, capital invested, raw material consumed, or energy utilized.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water-Pump" Intuition

Imagine a water pump drawing water from a well. The **useful water delivered** at the outlet is the *output*, and the **electricity/fuel** consumed by the pump is the *input*. If two pumps deliver the same quantity of water but Pump A consumes less electricity, then **Pump A has higher productivity**. In the same way, a factory that produces more cars using fewer worker-hours, less steel, and less energy is more productive than its rival. **Productivity is, therefore, the "intelligence" of conversion — how smartly inputs become outputs.**

### 1.3 Productivity vs. Production vs. Efficiency (Critical Distinction)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. Memorize the three terms as separate concepts:
> - **Production** = absolute volume of goods made (no comparison with input).
> - **Productivity** = output **per unit of input** (efficiency-oriented).
> - **Efficiency** = actual output ÷ standard/desired output (often expressed as a percentage).

| Term | What it Measures | Example (Factory) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Production | Total quantity made | 1,000 cars/month |
| Productivity | Cars produced per worker-hour | 0.5 cars/man-hour |
| Efficiency | How close to best possible | 85% of theoretical maximum |

### 1.4 Classification of Productivity Measures

KTU 2024 Module 1 explicitly covers the following taxonomy of productivity:

1. **Partial (Single-Factor) Productivity** — output related to one input only.
2. **Total (Multi-Factor) Productivity** — output related to combined inputs.
3. **Total Productivity** — output related to *all* inputs.
4. **Value-Added Productivity** — value-added output per total input.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Productivity — Step-by-Step Logic

#### 2.1.1 Partial Productivity (Single-Factor Productivity)

Partial productivity isolates **one input** at a time. It is the most commonly used measure in Indian shop-floor reporting because data for a single factor (e.g., man-hours) is easier to capture than data for combined factors.

- **Why use it?** Simple, easy to compute, identifies which specific resource is under-performing.
- **How it works?** The output is divided by a single, identifiable input category.

The KTU-required partial productivity forms are:

1. **Labour Productivity** — output per labour unit.
2. **Capital Productivity** — output per unit of capital.
3. **Material Productivity** — output per unit of raw material.
4. **Energy Productivity** — output per unit of energy consumed.
5. **Machine Productivity** — output per machine-hour.

#### 2.1.2 Total Factor Productivity (TFP) — Multi-Factor Productivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Factor Productivity (TFP)** captures the *combined* effect of labour, capital, and intermediate inputs, plus the contribution of **technological progress, innovation, organizational learning, and economies of scale** — the residual that cannot be explained by measurable inputs alone.

TFP is the academic "gold standard" for cross-country and cross-industry comparisons used by the **Reserve Bank of India (RBI)** and the **National Productivity Council (NPC)**.

#### 2.1.3 Total Productivity

Total productivity uses **all** input factors (labour + capital + material + energy + miscellaneous) in the denominator. It is the broadest and most accurate measure of organizational efficiency.

#### 2.1.4 Value-Added Productivity

$$\text{Value-Added Productivity} = \frac{\text{Value of Output} - \text{Value of Bought-out Inputs}}{\text{Total Input (or Total Cost)}}$$

This measure is crucial for Indian industries because it strips out the cost of externally purchased components, isolating the **genuine contribution of the firm to the economy**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** All formulas below are recurring in KTU University Examinations. Master the units — KTU examiners frequently deduct marks for missing units in the final answer.

| # | Productivity Measure | Formula | Typical Units (India) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Labour Productivity | Output ÷ Direct Labour Hours | Tonnes/man-hour, ₹/worker |
| 2 | Capital Productivity | Output ÷ Capital Invested | ₹ output / ₹ capital |
| 3 | Material Productivity | Output ÷ Material Used | kg product / kg material |
| 4 | Energy Productivity | Output ÷ Energy Consumed | kWh-equivalent saved |
| 5 | Machine Productivity | Output ÷ Machine Hours | Units/machine-hour |
| 6 | Total Factor Productivity | Output ÷ (Labour + Capital Cost) | Index number |
| 7 | Total Productivity | Total Output ÷ Total Input | Ratio / Index |
| 8 | Value-Added Productivity | (Output − Bought Inputs) ÷ Total Input | ₹ / ₹ |
| 9 | Productivity Index | (Current Year Productivity ÷ Base Year Productivity) × 100 | Number |
| 10 | Capacity Utilization | (Actual Output ÷ Maximum Possible Output) × 100 | Percentage |

### 2.3 Productivity in the Indian Industrial Context — Why it Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India is the **5th largest economy** in the world (by nominal GDP, 2024) but its **labour productivity** is significantly lower than that of China, the USA, Japan, and Germany. This productivity gap is the central reason productivity studies are a compulsory Module 1 topic in KTU 2024 PCPET501.

**Key Indian Productivity Facts (Kerala / National Data):**

- India's manufacturing sector contributes roughly **14–17%** of GDP (target: 25% under the **National Manufacturing Policy**).
- The **services sector** has the highest labour productivity in India, followed by manufacturing, then agriculture.
- **Kerala** has one of the highest Human Development Indices (HDI) in India but a relatively lower industrial productivity base because the state is service-oriented (tourism, IT, remittances).
- The **National Productivity Council (NPC)**, established in **1958** under the Ministry of Commerce & Industry, is the apex body that measures and publishes India's annual productivity indices.

### 2.4 Factors Affecting Productivity in Indian Industries

KTU examiners test the categorization of these factors under three broad heads:

1. **Internal (Organizational) Factors** — technology adopted, worker skill, supervision quality, plant layout, inventory policy, work culture.
2. **External (Environmental) Factors** — government policy, taxation, infrastructure (roads, power, ports), political stability, market demand.
3. **Human Factors** — training, motivation, labour relations, absenteeism rate, industrial disputes.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Productivity metrics are the backbone of:
- **Capacity planning** in any Indian manufacturing plant.
- **ISO 9001 / Lean / Six Sigma** projects that KTU graduates will lead on day one of their employment.
- **Government policy** — schemes like **Make in India (2014)**, **Atmanirbhar Bharat (2020)**, and **PM Gati Shakti** are designed primarily to raise India's productivity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Labour Productivity

> **Question:** A factory in Coimbatore produces **12,000 units** in a month. The total direct labour hours worked during the month are **4,800 hours**. The factory employs **60 workers** working **8 hours/day** for **25 days**. Calculate:
> (a) Labour productivity per worker-hour.
> (b) Labour productivity per worker per month.
> (c) If the target productivity was **3 units/worker-hour**, what is the efficiency?

**Step 1 — Total Worker-Hours Available (cross-verification):**

$$\text{Total Worker-Hours} = 60 \text{ workers} \times 8 \text{ hours/day} \times 25 \text{ days} = 12{,}000 \text{ worker-hours}$$

**Step 2 — Labour Productivity per Worker-Hour:**

$$P_{\text{WH}} = \frac{\text{Total Output}}{\text{Total Worker-Hours}} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ units}}{12{,}000 \text{ worker-hours}} = 1.0 \text{ unit/worker-hour}$$

**Step 3 — Labour Productivity per Worker per Month:**

$$P_{\text{WM}} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ units}}{60 \text{ workers}} = 200 \text{ units/worker/month}$$

**Step 4 — Efficiency vs. Target:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Achieved Productivity}}{\text{Target Productivity}} \times 100 = \frac{1.0}{3.0} \times 100 = 33.33\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The answer 33.33% signals that the plant is operating at only one-third of its target capability. In a KTU 14-mark answer, a student who only states the formula but **omits the unit and the percentage** loses 1–2 marks. Always quote the unit *and* the comparison metric.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Multi-Factor Productivity

> **Question:** An automotive plant produces cars worth **₹50 crore** in a year. The input costs are:
> - Labour: ₹8 crore
> - Material: ₹20 crore
> - Capital (annualized): ₹12 crore
> - Energy: ₹5 crore
>
> Calculate (a) Total Productivity, (b) Multi-Factor Productivity (Labour + Capital only), (c) Value-Added Productivity.

**Step 1 — Total Input Cost:**

$$\text{Total Input} = 8 + 20 + 12 + 5 = 45 \text{ crore}$$

**Step 2 — Total Productivity:**

$$P_{\text{Total}} = \frac{50 \text{ crore}}{45 \text{ crore}} = 1.111$$

This means the plant generates **₹1.11 of output for every ₹1 of input** — a healthy productivity ratio above 1.0.

**Step 3 — Multi-Factor Productivity (Labour + Capital):**

$$P_{\text{MFP}} = \frac{50}{8 + 12} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5$$

So every ₹1 spent on labour and capital returns ₹2.5 of output.

**Step 4 — Value-Added Productivity:**

First, identify bought-out inputs. Material (₹20 crore) is largely bought out. Energy (₹5 crore) is also externally procured. So:

$$\text{Bought Inputs} = 20 + 5 = 25 \text{ crore}$$

$$\text{Value-Added Output} = 50 - 25 = 25 \text{ crore}$$

$$P_{\text{VA}} = \frac{25}{45} = 0.556$$

This means **55.6 paise of value is added per rupee of total input** — a key indicator for industrial policy review by NPC.

---

### 3.3 Productivity Index — Year-on-Year Comparison

> **Question:** A Kerala-based spice company had a labour productivity of **4 kg/worker-hour in 2020** and **5.5 kg/worker-hour in 2024**. Calculate the productivity index (base year = 2020).

**Step 1 — Apply the productivity index formula:**

$$P_{\text{Index}} = \frac{P_{\text{Current Year}}}{P_{\text{Base Year}}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$P_{\text{Index (2024)}} = \frac{5.5}{4.0} \times 100 = 137.5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Interpretation: The company's productivity in 2024 is **137.5% of the 2020 base**, i.e., a **37.5% improvement** over four years. This is roughly a 8.27% compounded annual growth rate (CAGR), which is a strong KTU 14-mark analytical discussion point.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Productivity Matrix — India vs. Major Economies (Illustrative)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is built using publicly available conceptual benchmarks typical of NPC-style reporting. KTU students must understand the *interpretation*, not memorize the exact values.

| Country | Approx. Labour Productivity (USD/hour worked) | Position vs. India |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| United States | ~70–75 | ~10× India |
| Germany | ~65 | ~9× India |
| Japan | ~50 | ~7× India |
| South Korea | ~45 | ~6× India |
| China | ~25 | ~3.5× India |
| **India** | **~7–9** | **Baseline (1×)** |
| Vietnam | ~6 | ~0.8× India |

### 3.5 Strategic Inference for KTU 14-Mark Answers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Indian productivity gap arises from:
> 1. **Low technology adoption** (especially in MSME sector).
> 2. **Skill gap** in workforce — addressed by the **Skill India / PMKVY** scheme.
> 3. **Infrastructure deficit** — power, logistics, port handling.
> 4. **High regulatory burden** (eased by **ease-of-doing-business** reforms).
> 5. **Low R&D intensity** — India spends ~0.65% of GDP on R&D vs. 2.5–4% in advanced economies.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Productivity Conceptual Framework (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inputs] --> B1[Labour]
    A --> B2[Capital]
    A --> B3[Material]
    A --> B4[Energy]
    A --> B5[Technology and Management]
    B1 --> C[Conversion Process]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    B5 --> C
    C --> D[Outputs]
    D --> E[Productivity Measured as Output per Input]
    E --> F1[Partial Productivity]
    E --> F2[Multi Factor Productivity]
    E --> F3[Total Productivity]
    E --> F4[Value Added Productivity]
    F1 --> G[Productivity Index and Benchmarking]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Strategic Decision Making]
```

### 4.2 Productivity Classification Tree (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Productivity Measures] --> P1[Partial Productivity]
    P --> P2[Total Factor Productivity]
    P --> P3[Total Productivity]
    P --> P4[Value Added Productivity]
    P1 --> P1A[Labour Productivity]
    P1 --> P1B[Capital Productivity]
    P1 --> P1C[Material Productivity]
    P1 --> P1D[Energy Productivity]
    P1 --> P1E[Machine Productivity]
    P2 --> P2A[TFP Includes Technology and Innovation]
    P3 --> P3A[All Inputs Aggregated]
    P4 --> P4A[Value Added Divided by Total Input]
```

### 4.3 Indian Industry Productivity Influencer Map (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Indian Industrial Productivity] --> F1[Internal Factors]
    IN --> F2[External Factors]
    IN --> F3[Human Factors]
    IN --> F4[Government Policy]
    F1 --> F1A[Plant Layout]
    F1 --> F1B[Technology Used]
    F1 --> F1C[Inventory Management]
    F2 --> F2A[Power Supply]
    F2 --> F2B[Logistics and Transport]
    F2 --> F2C[Market Demand]
    F3 --> F3A[Worker Skill Level]
    F3 --> F3B[Training and Motivation]
    F3 --> F3C[Industrial Relations]
    F4 --> F4A[Make in India]
    F4 --> F4B[Skill India]
    F4 --> F4C[Atmanirbhar Bharat]
    F4 --> F4D[PM Gati Shakti]
```

### 4.4 Productivity Improvement Cycle (PDCA-style Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Measure Current Productivity] --> S2[Step 2 Compare with Benchmark]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Identify Gaps and Causes]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Implement Improvement Plan]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Monitor and Review]
    S5 --> S1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define *productivity*. How is it different from *efficiency* and *production*? (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
- **Productivity** is the ratio of output produced to the input consumed in a process. (1 mark)
- **Production** is the absolute volume of goods/services produced, with no reference to input. (1 mark)
- **Efficiency** is the ratio of *actual* output to *standard/desired* output, usually expressed as a percentage. (1 mark)

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** List any **three partial productivity measures** used in Indian manufacturing industries. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Labour Productivity** — output per worker-hour. (1 mark)
2. **Capital Productivity** — output per unit of capital invested. (1 mark)
3. **Material Productivity** — output per kilogram of raw material. (1 mark)
*(Other acceptable answers: Energy productivity, Machine productivity.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question below carries the KTU "Module Internal Choice" structure. Students are expected to answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION A (14 MARKS) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Style]**

**Q (A).** **(a)** Define productivity. Explain the various types of productivity measures used in industrial engineering with suitable formulas. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting productivity in Indian industries**, with reference to government initiatives. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question A

**(a) Definition and Types of Productivity (7 marks)**

Productivity is the ratio of output produced to the input consumed. Mathematically:

$$P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

The main types of productivity measures are:

1. **Partial (Single-Factor) Productivity** — measures output against one input. Examples: labour productivity, capital productivity, material productivity, energy productivity, and machine productivity. (2 marks)
2. **Total Factor Productivity (TFP)** — output related to the combined cost of labour and capital. It captures the joint effect of inputs plus technological progress. (2 marks)
3. **Total Productivity** — output divided by the *sum of all* input costs. It is the broadest and most accurate measure. (1.5 marks)
4. **Value-Added Productivity** — value added by the firm (output minus bought-out inputs) divided by total input cost. (1.5 marks)

**(b) Factors Affecting Productivity in Indian Industries (7 marks)**

The factors can be grouped into four categories:

1. **Internal Factors (2 marks):** Plant layout, technology adopted, inventory management, machine maintenance, and process design.
2. **External Factors (2 marks):** Power and water supply, transport and logistics infrastructure, market demand cycles, and global competition.
3. **Human Factors (1.5 marks):** Skill level of workers, training, motivation schemes, labour relations, and absenteeism.
4. **Government Initiatives (1.5 marks):**
   - **Make in India (2014)** — boosts manufacturing output.
   - **Skill India / PMKVY** — addresses the workforce skill gap.
   - **Atmanirbhar Bharat (2020)** — reduces import dependence, improving capital productivity.
   - **PM Gati Shakti** — improves logistics productivity.
   - **National Productivity Council (NPC)** — measures and disseminates productivity data.

---

#### ❖ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample Bank Model]**

**Q (B).** **(a)** A manufacturing unit produces **20,000 units per month** with **80 workers** working **8 hours/day for 25 days**. The total material consumed is **10,000 kg** and the value of output is **₹40 lakh** against an input cost of **₹30 lakh** (Labour ₹8L, Material ₹15L, Capital ₹5L, Energy ₹2L). Calculate:
   (i) Labour productivity per worker-hour. (3 marks)
   (ii) Material productivity. (2 marks)
   (iii) Total productivity. (2 marks)
**(b)** A factory had a labour productivity of **6 units/worker-hour in 2020**, which rose to **8.4 units/worker-hour in 2024**. Calculate the productivity index and comment on the improvement. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL ANSWER — Question B

**(a) Productivity Calculations (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Total Worker-Hours:**

$$\text{WH} = 80 \times 8 \times 25 = 16{,}000 \text{ worker-hours}$$

**Step 2 — Labour Productivity per Worker-Hour:**

$$P_L = \frac{20{,}000}{16{,}000} = 1.25 \text{ units/worker-hour}$$

**Step 3 — Material Productivity:**

$$P_M = \frac{20{,}000 \text{ units}}{10{,}000 \text{ kg}} = 2.0 \text{ units/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Total Productivity:**

$$P_T = \frac{40 \text{ lakh}}{30 \text{ lakh}} = 1.333$$

(For each calculation, full marks only if **units are quoted** with the answer.)

---

**(b) Productivity Index (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Apply the productivity index formula:**

$$P_{\text{Index}} = \frac{8.4}{6.0} \times 100 = 140$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation (3 marks):**

The factory's 2024 productivity is **140% of the 2020 base**, indicating a **40% improvement** in labour productivity over four years.

**Step 3 — CAGR Calculation (optional, 1 mark):**

$$\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{8.4}{6.0}\right)^{\frac{1}{4}} - 1 = 0.0878 = 8.78\%$$

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**
A compound annual growth rate of ~8.8% in labour productivity is **above the global average for manufacturing** and indicates effective adoption of new technology, better worker training, and successful lean practices.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Productivity Questions:**
> 1. **Omitting units** — KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** per numerical answer if the unit (e.g., units/worker-hour, ₹/₹) is missing.
> 2. **Confusing Total Productivity with Total Factor Productivity** — these are *not* the same. Total Productivity includes ALL inputs; TFP typically includes only labour + capital.
> 3. **Wrong base year in productivity index** — the *base year* must be explicitly stated; otherwise, the answer is technically meaningless.
> 4. **Quoting percentage efficiency without showing the formula** — KTU 2024 scheme follows Bloom's "Apply" level for Part B numericals; raw numbers without derivation lose 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring government initiatives in descriptive questions** — failing to mention at least two initiatives (e.g., Make in India, Skill India) results in a 1–2 mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Productivity = Output / Input**, expressed with **explicit units**.
- **Partial productivity** isolates one input; **TFP** uses two or three; **Total productivity** uses all inputs; **Value-Added productivity** strips out bought-out costs.
- **Labour productivity** is the most tracked metric in Indian industry because labour data is most readily available.
- India's **labour productivity is roughly 1/10th of the USA** and 1/3rd of China's — a major policy concern.
- The **National Productivity Council (NPC), 1958** is the apex Indian body for productivity measurement and advocacy.
- **Key government schemes** linked to productivity improvement: Make in India, Skill India (PMKVY), Atmanirbhar Bharat, PM Gati Shakti, and the National Manufacturing Policy.
- **Factors affecting productivity** = Internal + External + Human + Government Policy (4 categories, frequently tested).
- **Productivity Index** = (Current ÷ Base) × 100, with **base year explicitly named**.
- **CAGR** in productivity growth above 6–8% is considered strong for Indian manufacturing.
- **Efficiency ≠ Productivity**: Efficiency compares actual to standard output; productivity compares output to input.
- For 14-mark answers, always pair **definition + formula + numerical illustration + Indian context (NPC / government scheme)** — this is the KTU 2024 valuation pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Productivity and Product Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Productivity and Product Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Productivity — Formal Academic Definition

**Productivity** is defined as the ratio of output produced to the input consumed in any industrial, economic, or service process. It is a dimensionless index (when output and input are in identical units) or measured in units of output per unit of input (e.g., kg/hour, units/day, ₹/₹).

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Productivity is the quantitative relationship between what is produced (output) and what is required to produce it (input). It is a measure of how efficiently an organization converts inputs into outputs."*

### 1.2 Productivity — Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a fisherman catching fish. If a fisherman uses **1 net and 4 hours** to catch **50 fish**, his productivity is:

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{50 \text{ fish}}{4 \text{ hours}} = 12.5 \text{ fish/hour}$$

If he switches to a better net and now catches **80 fish in 4 hours**, his productivity jumps to **20 fish/hour** — a **60% improvement** in productivity without working harder, just by working *smarter* (better tools = better input efficiency).

In an engineering factory:
- **Output** = Number of components produced / Revenue generated / Value added
- **Input** = Labor hours, Machine hours, Material, Capital, Energy

A higher productivity means the company gets **more value from the same resources**, leading to lower costs, higher profits, and competitive advantage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Productivity is NOT the same as production. Production measures *quantity*, while productivity measures *efficiency*.

### 1.3 Types of Productivity (KTU High-Yield Classification)

| # | Type | Formula Basis | Industrial Use |
|---|------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Labor Productivity** | Output / Labor hours | Workforce efficiency |
| 2 | **Capital Productivity** | Output / Capital invested | ROI in equipment |
| 3 | **Material Productivity** | Output / Material consumed | Raw material yield |
| 4 | **Energy Productivity** | Output / Energy consumed | Energy audits |
| 5 | **Machine Productivity** | Output / Machine hours | Equipment utilization |
| 6 | **Total (Multi-factor) Productivity** | Total Output / Total Input | Overall organizational efficiency |
| 7 | **Partial Productivity** | Output / One specific input | Targeted analysis |

### 1.4 Product Design Process — Formal Definition

**Product Design** is the systematic process of conceiving, defining, and developing a product that satisfies identified customer needs, is technically feasible, economically viable, and manufacturable within acceptable quality standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *"Product design is the totality of characteristics of an entity (product) that bear on its ability to satisfy stated or implied needs. It is an iterative decision-making process that transforms market opportunities into producible artifacts."*

### 1.5 Product Design — Intuitive Analogy

Think of designing a **water bottle for school children**:
- *Who is it for?* Kids aged 6–12 (Customer identification)
- *What do they need?* Easy to hold, doesn't leak, fun colors (Functional requirements)
- *What material?* BPA-free plastic, light, durable (Material selection)
- *How will it be made?* Injection molding (Process selection)
- *How much to sell?* ₹150 target (Costing)
- *How will it look?* Cartoon characters, ergonomic grip (Aesthetics)

This **entire journey from idea → prototype → mass production** is the **Product Design Process**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Productivity — Theoretical Framework

Productivity analysis rests on three pillars:

**Pillar 1: Measurement**
- Quantify output (units, ₹, kg, services delivered)
- Quantify input (hours, ₹, kg, kWh)
- Compute the ratio

**Pillar 2: Comparison**
- Compare against historical baseline (internal benchmarking)
- Compare against industry standards (external benchmarking)
- Compare against theoretical maximum (efficiency)

**Pillar 3: Improvement**
- Identify bottlenecks
- Apply techniques (Lean, Six Sigma, Kaizen, 5S, automation)
- Re-measure and verify

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Productivity)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \frac{O}{I}$ | Basic Productivity | Output/Input |
| 2 | $P_L = \frac{O}{L}$ | Labor Productivity | Units/Man-hour |
| 3 | $P_C = \frac{O}{C}$ | Capital Productivity | Output/₹ |
| 4 | $P_M = \frac{O}{M}$ | Material Productivity | Output/kg |
| 5 | $P_{total} = \frac{O_{total}}{I_{total}}$ | Total Factor Productivity | Output/Input (mixed) |
| 6 | $\text{P.I.} = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{standard}} \times 100$ | Productivity Index | Percentage (%) |
| 7 | $\Delta P\% = \frac{P_{new} - P_{old}}{P_{old}} \times 100$ | Productivity Improvement % | Percentage (%) |
| 8 | $\text{Production} = P \times I \times T$ | Output relationship | Units |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{P_{achieved}}{P_{max}} \times 100$ | Efficiency (Productivity-based) | Percentage (%) |

### 2.3 Productivity Improvement Techniques (KTU Favorites)

1. **Method Study & Work Measurement** — Scientifically streamlining operations.
2. **Time Study / Motion Study** — Taylor's principles.
3. **Value Engineering / Value Analysis** — Eliminating cost without sacrificing function.
4. **Quality Improvement (TQM, Six Sigma)** — Reducing rework and defects.
5. **Better Plant Layout & Ergonomics** — Reducing wasted motion.
6. **Investment in Better Equipment / Automation** — Higher throughput per input.
7. **Training & Motivation of Workers** — Skill enhancement and morale.
8. **Standardization & Simplification** — Reducing variability.
9. **Just-In-Time (JIT) Inventory** — Reducing inventory carrying cost.
10. **Preventive Maintenance** — Reducing downtime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Productivity metrics guide capacity planning, capital budgeting, wage policy, and even national economic planning. Countries with high labor productivity (e.g., Japan, Germany) dominate manufacturing exports.

### 2.4 Product Design Process — Theoretical Steps

The KTU 2024 scheme mandates understanding of the following 8-step design framework:

**Step 1: Need Identification (Voice of Customer)**
- Market research, customer surveys, problem reports, regulatory requirements.
- Output: Product Design Specification (PDS) document.

**Step 2: Concept Generation**
- Brainstorming, TRIZ, morphological analysis, biomimicry.
- Output: Multiple concept sketches.

**Step 3: Concept Screening & Selection**
- Pugh matrix, weighted scoring, feasibility filter.
- Output: 1–2 selected concepts.

**Step 4: Preliminary Design**
- Engineering sketches, basic calculations, material selection, DFM/DFA.
- Output: Preliminary engineering drawings.

**Step 5: Detailed Design**
- CAD modeling (SolidWorks, CATIA, Creo), tolerance stack-up, GD&T.
- Output: Production-ready drawings, BOM.

**Step 6: Prototyping**
- 3D printing, CNC machining, rapid tooling.
- Output: Functional prototype.

**Step 7: Testing & Validation**
- Functional testing, reliability testing (MTBF), user trials.
- Output: Test reports, design modifications.

**Step 8: Production Launch**
- Pilot run, tooling setup, SOP preparation, market release.

### 2.5 Key Design Considerations (KTU High-Yield)

| Consideration | Description | Example |
|--------------|-------------|---------|
| **Functionality** | Must perform intended task | Watch must show time accurately |
| **Reliability** | Must perform over time | Car engine 200,000 km life |
| **Manufacturability (DFM)** | Easy to produce | Avoiding complex undercuts |
| **Assemblability (DFA)** | Easy to assemble | Snap-fit over screws |
| **Cost** | Within target price | Smartphone under ₹15,000 |
| **Aesthetics** | Visually appealing | Apple iPhone design language |
| **Ergonomics** | Comfortable to use | Curved chair backrest |
| **Safety** | No harm to user | Child-proof medicine cap |
| **Sustainability** | Recyclable, low carbon | Tesla's recycled aluminum |
| **Maintainability** | Easy to repair | Modular smartphone batteries |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Productivity Index and Improvement Percentage

**Given:**
- Standard Productivity (from industrial engineering studies): $P_s$
- Actual Productivity (current achieved): $P_a$
- Old Productivity (before improvement): $P_o$
- New Productivity (after improvement): $P_n$

**Derivation:**

$$\text{Productivity Index (P.I.)} = \frac{\text{Actual Output Achieved}}{\text{Standard Output Expected}} \times 100$$

Rewriting in productivity terms (since output = productivity × input × time):

$$P.I. = \frac{P_a}{P_s} \times 100$$

**Improvement Percentage:**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{P_n - P_o}{P_o} \times 100$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem:** A manufacturing unit produces **1,200 units** of a product in **8 hours** using **5 workers** and **₹20,000** worth of materials.

**Calculate:**
1. Labor Productivity (units/worker-hour)
2. Material Productivity (units/₹)
3. Total Productivity if capital investment is ₹1,00,000
4. If the company improves production to **1,500 units** with the same inputs, find the % improvement.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Output $O = 1{,}200$ units
- Labor hours $L = 5 \times 8 = 40$ worker-hours
- Material cost $M = ₹20{,}000$
- Capital $C = ₹1{,}00{,}000$

**Step 1: Labor Productivity**

$$P_L = \frac{O}{L} = \frac{1{,}200 \text{ units}}{40 \text{ worker-hours}}$$

$$P_L = 30 \text{ units/worker-hour}$$

**Step 2: Material Productivity**

$$P_M = \frac{O}{M} = \frac{1{,}200 \text{ units}}{₹20{,}000}$$

$$P_M = 0.06 \text{ units/₹}$$

**Step 3: Total Productivity** (combining all inputs in monetary terms)

$$I_{total} = L + M + C = 40 \times \text{wage rate} + 20{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000$$

Assuming wage rate = ₹100/hour:

$$I_{total} = (40 \times 100) + 20{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000 = 4{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$I_{total} = ₹1{,}24{,}000$$

$$P_{total} = \frac{1{,}200}{1{,}24{,}000} = 0.00968 \text{ units/₹} \approx 0.97 \text{ paise/unit}$$

**Step 4: Productivity Improvement %**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{P_n - P_o}{P_o} \times 100$$

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{1{,}500 - 1{,}200}{1{,}200} \times 100 = \frac{300}{1{,}200} \times 100$$

$$\Delta P\% = 25\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 25% productivity gain from the same inputs means the company saved ₹1,24,000 worth of resources for 300 extra units — directly boosting profit margins.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Productivity vs. Production vs. Efficiency

| Parameter | Definition | What it Answers |
|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| **Production** | Total output in volume | *How much?* |
| **Productivity** | Output per unit input | *How efficiently?* |
| **Efficiency** | Actual output ÷ Standard output | *How close to ideal?* |
| **Effectiveness** | Goal achieved ÷ Goal set | *Did we achieve aim?* |
| **Utilization** | Time used ÷ Time available | *How busy is the resource?* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Relationship:**
> $$\text{Production} = \text{Productivity} \times \text{Input} \times \text{Time}$$
> $$\text{Production} = \eta \times \text{Standard Production Rate} \times \text{Time}$$

### 3.4 Product Design Cost Estimation Derivation

For a product, the total design cost is composed of:

$$C_{design} = C_{concept} + C_{CAD} + C_{prototype} + C_{testing} + C_{iteration}$$

The total unit cost becomes:

$$C_{unit} = \frac{C_{design}}{N} + C_{material} + C_{labor} + C_{overhead}$$

where $N$ is the production volume (higher $N$ → lower amortized design cost per unit → **economies of scale**).

For $N = 10{,}000$ units and $C_{design} = ₹5{,}00{,}000$:

$$C_{design/unit} = \frac{₹5{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = ₹50/unit$$

For $N = 50{,}000$ units:

$$C_{design/unit} = \frac{₹5{,}00{,}000}{50{,}000} = ₹10/unit$$

This 5× difference shows why **mass production of well-designed products is highly profitable**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Productivity Computation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Input Data] --> B[Input Classification]
    B --> B1[Labor Hours]
    B --> B2[Capital Invested]
    B --> B3[Material Cost]
    B --> B4[Energy Used]
    A --> C[Output Data]
    C --> C1[Units Produced]
    C --> C2[Revenue Earned]
    C --> C3[Services Delivered]
    B1 --> D[Productivity Engine]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D --> E[Productivity Ratios]
    E --> F[Labor Productivity]
    E --> G[Capital Productivity]
    E --> H[Material Productivity]
    E --> I[Total Productivity]
    F --> J[Performance Dashboard]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Decision Making]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Product Design Process (8-Stage Sequential Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Need Identification] --> S2[Stage 2: Concept Generation]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3: Concept Screening]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4: Preliminary Design]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5: Detailed CAD Design]
    S5 --> S6[Stage 6: Prototyping]
    S6 --> S7[Stage 7: Testing and Validation]
    S7 --> S8[Stage 8: Production Launch]
    S1 -.feedback.-> S2
    S4 -.iteration.-> S2
    S7 -.redesign.-> S5
    S8 --> STG[Market and Customer Feedback]
    STG -.continuous.-> S1
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram — Productivity Types in Industrial Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PR[Productivity Master Index] --> PART[Partial Productivity]
    PR --> TOTAL[Total Multi Factor Productivity]
    PART --> L1[Labor Productivity]
    PART --> L2[Capital Productivity]
    PART --> L3[Material Productivity]
    PART --> L4[Energy Productivity]
    PART --> L5[Machine Productivity]
    TOTAL --> T1[Output divided by all Combined Inputs]
    L1 --> M1[Units per Worker Hour]
    L2 --> M2[Output per Rupee of Capital]
    L3 --> M3[Output per Kg of Material]
    L4 --> M4[Output per kWh of Energy]
    L5 --> M5[Output per Machine Hour]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Concurrent Engineering vs. Traditional Sequential Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRAD[Traditional Sequential Design]
    A1[Design] --> A2[Manufacturing]
    A2 --> A3[Assembly]
    A3 --> A4[Service]
    A4 --> A5[Feedback After Months]
    end
    subgraph CONC[Concurrent Engineering Model]
    B1[Design] <--> B2[Manufacturing]
    B2 <--> B3[Assembly]
    B3 <--> B4[Service]
    B1 <--> B3
    B1 <--> B4
    B2 <--> B4
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Concurrent Engineering Matters:** Traditional design discovers manufacturing issues *after* the design is frozen (costly redesign). Concurrent engineering catches them in real-time, reducing time-to-market by **30–60%** and cost by **20–40%**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define productivity. Differentiate between **productivity** and **production**.

**Model Answer:**
- **Productivity** is the ratio of output to input, measuring the *efficiency* of resource utilization.
- **Production** is the absolute volume or quantity of goods produced in a given time.
- Production measures *quantity*; Productivity measures *efficiency*.
- Example: Producing 1000 units = production; producing 1000 units with half the labor = higher productivity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **four factors affecting productivity** in a manufacturing industry.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Technology & Automation level** — Modern CNC machines produce more than manual tools.
2. **Worker skill & training** — Skilled workers produce fewer defects.
3. **Quality of inputs (materials)** — Better raw material yields better output.
4. **Plant layout & workflow** — Efficient layouts reduce material handling time.
5. *(Bonus)* Management practices, motivation, and workplace safety.

---

### PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Productivity Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A factory employs **40 workers** working **8 hours/day** for **25 days/month**. Output is **12,000 units/month**. Material cost is **₹3,00,000/month** and capital investment is **₹50,00,000**.

Calculate:
1. Labor productivity (units/worker-hour)
2. Material productivity (units/₹)
3. Capital productivity (units/₹)
4. Total productivity (assuming all inputs in ₹)
