**(b) [7 Marks]** After process improvement, the same factory now produces **15,000 units/month** with the **same input cost**. Calculate the **productivity improvement %**. List any **five techniques** the factory may have adopted.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step:**

Total labor hours = 40 workers × 8 hours × 25 days = **8,000 worker-hours/month**

1. **Labor Productivity:**
$$P_L = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ units}}{8{,}000 \text{ worker-hours}} = 1.5 \text{ units/worker-hour}$$

2. **Material Productivity:**
$$P_M = \frac{12{,}000}{3{,}00{,}000} = 0.04 \text{ units/₹}$$

3. **Capital Productivity:**
$$P_C = \frac{12{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} = 0.0024 \text{ units/₹}$$

4. **Total Productivity:** Convert all inputs to monetary terms.
Assuming wage = ₹100/hour:
$$L_{cost} = 8{,}000 \times 100 = ₹8{,}00{,}000$$
$$I_{total} = 8{,}00{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 50{,}00{,}000 = ₹61{,}00{,}000$$
$$P_{total} = \frac{12{,}000}{61{,}00{,}000} = 0.00197 \text{ units/₹}$$

**(b) Productivity Improvement:**

$$\Delta P\% = \frac{15{,}000 - 12{,}000}{12{,}000} \times 100 = \frac{3{,}000}{12{,}000} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Five techniques:**
1. Method study & work simplification
2. Better machine maintenance (preventive maintenance)
3. Worker training and skill development
4. Implementation of TQM/Six Sigma to reduce defects
5. Improved plant layout and material handling

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Product Design Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **eight sequential steps of the Product Design Process** with a real-world example of designing a **manual mixer grinder**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the importance of **Design for Manufacturing (DFM)** and **Design for Assembly (DFA)**. List four benefits of **concurrent engineering** over traditional sequential design.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Eight Steps Applied to Manual Mixer Grinder:**

1. **Need Identification:** Households need an affordable, durable grinder for spices — target price ₹1,500.
2. **Concept Generation:** Multiple concepts — top-jar with handle, table-top compact design, cordless battery model.
3. **Concept Screening:** Use Pugh matrix — select top-jar design for best ergonomics and cost.
4. **Preliminary Design:** Choose 500W motor, stainless steel jars (3 sizes), ABS plastic body.
5. **Detailed Design:** CAD modeling in SolidWorks; tolerance ±0.05 mm; define gear ratios (1:3).
6. **Prototyping:** 3D print the housing; CNC the jar blade assembly.
7. **Testing & Validation:** Run for 100 hours continuous; check noise <80 dB, jar leak-proof, motor temp <60°C.
8. **Production Launch:** Set up injection molding tooling, prepare SOPs, launch in market with 6-month warranty.

**(b) DFM & DFA + Concurrent Engineering:**

- **DFM (Design for Manufacturing):** Designing parts so they can be produced easily, cheaply, and reliably using available manufacturing processes. Example: Avoid complex internal curves that need 5-axis CNC.
- **DFA (Design for Assembly):** Minimizing the number of parts and simplifying assembly. Example: Snap-fit joints instead of screws.

**Four Benefits of Concurrent Engineering:**

1. **Faster time-to-market** — Parallel work cuts project duration by 30–50%.
2. **Lower redesign cost** — Manufacturing issues caught early, not post-design.
3. **Higher product quality** — Cross-functional input reduces defects.
4. **Lower total cost** — Saves 20–40% in development cost.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - **For Productivity Questions:** Students commonly **forget to multiply worker count by hours and days** when computing labor-hours. Always write the full expansion.
> - **For Product Design Questions:** Students **list steps without the engineering methodology** (TRIZ, Pugh matrix, CAD, DFM/DFA). Mentioning these techniques scores bonus marks.
> - **Don't confuse "production" with "productivity"** — this is the #1 mistake in Part A.
> - **Always include units** in productivity answers (units/hour, ₹/₹, kg/kg, %).

---

### 🧠 TOPIC RECAP — RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Productivity** = Output / Input (ratio, dimensionless or with units)
- ✅ **Production** = absolute quantity; **Productivity** = efficiency ratio
- ✅ Types: Labor, Capital, Material, Energy, Machine, Total (Multi-factor)
- ✅ **Productivity Index (P.I.)** = (Actual / Standard) × 100
- ✅ **Improvement %** = [(New − Old) / Old] × 100
- ✅ Improvement Techniques: Method study, Value Engineering, TQM, Lean, JIT, Automation, Training
- ✅ Product Design = systematic process from idea to market
- ✅ 8 Steps: Need → Concept → Screening → Preliminary Design → Detailed CAD → Prototype → Test → Launch
- ✅ **DFM** = Design for Manufacturing (easy production)
- ✅ **DFA** = Design for Assembly (easy assembly, fewer parts)
- ✅ **Concurrent Engineering** = Parallel design-manufacturing-assembly-service design (reduces time & cost)
- ✅ **Product Design Specification (PDS)** = foundational document driving all design decisions
- ✅ Higher production volume (N) → lower per-unit design cost (₹₹₹ amortization)
- ✅ Productivity directly affects **profit, pricing, wages, and national GDP**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Role of Work Study in Improving Productivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Role of Work Study in Improving Productivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Work Study** is a systematic, scientific technique used to investigate, analyze, and improve working methods and to establish standard times for work operations. It is the most widely used and effective method of engineering analysis for improving productivity in manufacturing and service industries. As per the **British Standards Institution (BS 3138:1979)**, work study is defined as:

> *"A generic term for those techniques, particularly method study and work measurement, which are used in the examination of human work in all its contexts, and which lead systematically to the investigation of all the factors which affect the efficiency and economy of the situation being reviewed, in order to effect improvement."*

Work study comprises two major branches:
1. **Method Study (MS)** – Concerned with *how* work is done (the "one best way").
2. **Work Measurement (WM)** – Concerned with *how long* it should take (the standard time).

## 1.2 Productivity – The Core Metric

**Productivity** is formally defined as the ratio of *output produced* to the *input consumed*. In industrial engineering, productivity quantifies the efficiency with which an organization converts inputs (labor, materials, machines, energy) into outputs (goods and services).

$$\text{Productivity} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Kitchen vs. A Factory**
>
> Imagine a small restaurant where the chef (the only worker) makes 20 dishes per hour. Now, a management consultant observes the workflow: the chef walks 5 meters every time to fetch salt, washes the same plate twice due to a poorly placed sink, and chops vegetables with the wrong knife.
>
> - **Method Study** would rearrange the kitchen so the salt is within arm's reach, the sink is moved closer, and the right knife is selected — this is *"working smarter."*
> - **Work Measurement** would time the chef and announce: "Each dish should take exactly **3 minutes** under the new method" — this is *"setting a fair benchmark."*
>
> After implementing both, the chef now makes **35 dishes per hour** — productivity nearly doubled. This is precisely what Work Study does inside a factory.

## 1.4 The Big Picture: Why Work Study Matters for Productivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 2):**
> Work Study is the **primary productivity-improvement engine** in Industrial Engineering. It bridges the gap between *current performance* and *theoretical optimum* by removing waste, standardizing methods, and setting fair work standards.

The relationship can be summarized as:

> *"Without a standard, there is no logical basis for improvement."* — **H.W. Heinrich**

Work Study is the **means**, and improved **Productivity** is the **end**. Every technique within work study (motion study, time study, process charts, etc.) directly feeds into one of three productivity outcomes:
- **Higher output** for the same input,
- **Lower input** for the same output, or
- **Better quality** at the same cost.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Productivity Improvement Mechanism

Work Study improves productivity through a structured, iterative, **seven-step logical chain**:

1. **Identify the Problem / Select the Job:** Choose the operation, process, or department that needs improvement (usually the bottleneck, highest cost, or lowest output).
2. **Record all Relevant Facts:** Use observation, interviews, charts, and recordings to capture the *as-is* state of work.
3. **Examine the Recorded Facts:** Challenge every element — purpose, place, sequence, person, and means (the **5 W's and 1 H**).
4. **Develop the Most Economic Method:** Brainstorm alternatives using techniques like questioning, brainstorming, and creative ideation.
5. **Evaluate the Alternatives:** Compare the proposed methods using cost-benefit, feasibility, and ergonomic criteria.
6. **Define the New Method:** Standardize the chosen method by writing a **Standard Operating Procedure (SOP)** and training workers.
7. **Install the New Method:** Implement, monitor, and maintain the new method with periodic audits.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this chain?**
> Productivity is not improved by *working harder*; it is improved by *working systematically*. Each step ensures that improvement is **data-driven, not opinion-driven**, and **sustainable, not one-time.**

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Improves Productivity |
|------|---------------|------------------------------|
| Select Job | Resources are limited; improve the highest-impact area first. | Concentrates effort on the bottleneck → maximum ROI. |
| Record Facts | Decisions need data, not assumptions. | Identifies *actual* waste vs. *perceived* waste. |
| Examine Facts | Every activity should add value. | Eliminates unnecessary motion, transport, waiting, and over-processing. |
| Develop Method | One size does not fit all. | Generates multiple alternatives for comparison. |
| Evaluate | Not every good idea is cost-effective. | Filters impractical ideas → only viable improvements proceed. |
| Define & Standardize | Variation is the enemy of consistency. | Creates repeatable, trainable methods → consistent output. |
| Install & Maintain | Untrained workers revert to old habits. | Ensures the new method sticks; productivity gain is sustained. |

## 2.3 Specific Roles of Work Study in Improving Productivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 8 Pillars of Productivity Improvement via Work Study**

1. **Eliminates Unnecessary Work** – Removes non-value-adding activities (waiting, transport, inspection over-load).
2. **Optimizes Method (One Best Way)** – Replaces inferior methods with scientifically proven best practices.
3. **Sets Fair Work Standards** – Establishes *standard time* for each task, enabling accurate planning and wage incentives.
4. **Reduces Fatigue and Improves Ergonomics** – Better workplace layout, fewer motions, reduced physical strain → fewer errors and injuries.
5. **Improves Worker Satisfaction** – Workers trust transparent, fair standards → higher morale → higher output.
6. **Enables Effective Capacity Planning** – Standard times allow accurate scheduling of men, machines, and materials.
7. **Provides Basis for Wage Incentive Schemes** – When workers know the standard, they can be paid for output *above* the standard.
8. **Reduces Manufacturing Cost per Unit** – More output per unit of input directly lowers the cost of each product.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Productivity (General)** | $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$ | units/hour, kg/man-hour, etc. | Universal efficiency metric |
| 2 | **Labor Productivity** | $P_L = \dfrac{\text{Units produced}}{\text{Labor hours worked}}$ | pieces/man-hour | Direct measure of workforce efficiency |
| 3 | **Machine Productivity** | $P_M = \dfrac{\text{Output quantity}}{\text{Machine hours}}$ | pieces/machine-hour | Identifies under-utilized machines |
| 4 | **Overall Productivity (Total Factor)** | $P_{TF} = \dfrac{\text{Total output value}}{\text{Total input value}}$ | ratio (dimensionless) | Multi-factor productivity |
| 5 | **Productivity Index (PI)** | $PI = \dfrac{\text{Standard hours produced}}{\text{Hours actually worked}} \times 100$ | % | Compares actual vs. expected output |
| 6 | **Normal Time** | $T_n = T_o \times \dfrac{R}{100}$ | minutes | Adjusted time at standard performance |
| 7 | **Standard Time** | $T_{std} = T_n \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$ | minutes | Includes rest, personal, contingency allowances |
| 8 | **Performance Rating Factor** | $R = \dfrac{\text{Observed speed}}{\text{Normal speed}} \times 100$ | % | Worker effort calibration |
| 9 | **Allowance Factor** | $A = \text{Rest} + \text{Personal} + \text{Contingency}$ | % | Compensation for fatigue & unavoidable delays |
| 10 | **Overall Labor Effectiveness (OLE)** | $OLE = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Combined equipment-and-operator KPI |
| 11 | **Productivity Improvement** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100$ | % | Quantifies gain from work study |
| 12 | **Cost Saving per Unit** | $\Delta C = C_{\text{old}} - C_{\text{new}}$ | ₹ / unit | Justifies work study investments |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this system is applied in industry:**
>
> - **Automotive Assembly Lines** — Time study sets the *takt time* matching production pace to customer demand (Toyota Production System).
> - **Garment Manufacturing** — Method study redesigns stitching sequences to reduce motion, raising daily output by 15-30%.
> - **Hospital Workflow** — Work study optimizes patient admission/discharge process, increasing bed turnover rate.
> - **IT Service Desks** — Standard time for ticket resolution enables accurate SLA targets and staffing models.
> - **CNC Machining** — Method study reduces tool-change setup time; standard time optimizes machine loading.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: How Work Study Improves Productivity — The Mathematical Logic

Let us derive the productivity gain from a work study intervention step by step.

### Step 1 — Define Productivity in its Most Useful Form

Productivity in a work-study context is generally expressed as labor productivity — the rate at which a worker produces units per hour.

$$
P = \frac{Q}{H}
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Labor productivity (units/hour)
- $Q$ = Quantity of acceptable units produced
- $H$ = Total labor hours worked

### Step 2 — Decompose Total Hours into Productive and Non-Productive Components

When a worker spends $H$ hours on a job, only a portion is *truly productive* (value-adding). The rest is lost to fatigue, personal time, delays, and ineffective method.

$$
H = H_{\text{value-add}} + H_{\text{waste}} + H_{\text{allowance}}
$$

In standard time terminology, the engineering equivalent is:

$$
T_{std} = T_{obs} \times \frac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right)
$$

Where:
- $T_{obs}$ = Observed (actual) time taken during time study
- $R$ = Performance rating (%)
- $A$ = Allowance (%)
- $T_{std}$ = Standard time per cycle

### Step 3 — Calculate Output Per Unit Standard Time

The number of cycles a worker completes per hour using the new method is:

$$
N_{\text{cycles/hr}} = \frac{60}{T_{std}}
$$

Where $T_{std}$ is in **minutes**. Therefore, the productivity with the new method is:

$$
P_{\text{new}} = \frac{60}{T_{std}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare Old vs. New Productivity

$$
\Delta P = \frac{P_{\text{new}} - P_{\text{old}}}{P_{\text{old}}} \times 100
$$

### Step 5 — Combined Productivity Index for the Whole Department

For an organization with multiple workers, the *Productivity Index* aggregates individual results:

$$
PI = \frac{\sum \left(T_{std,i} \times N_{\text{units produced},i}\right)}{\sum H_{\text{actual},i}} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When $PI = 100$, the department meets the standard. When $PI > 100$, output exceeds the standard. When $PI < 100$, productivity is below par.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Worked Out Exhaustive)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Sample Calculation (for understanding the link between Work Study & Productivity)**

**Problem:** In a CNC turning operation, a time study was conducted. The following data was obtained:
- Average observed time per piece: **4.50 minutes**
- Performance rating: **110%** (worker is faster than normal)
- Allowance: **15%**

Then, a **method study** was carried out and the improved method gave a new observed time of **3.60 minutes** with the same rating and allowance.

**Required:**
1. Calculate the standard time **before** work study.
2. Calculate the standard time **after** work study.
3. Calculate the percentage **productivity improvement**.
4. Calculate the **cost savings** if the wage rate is **₹80/hour** and the company produces **5,000 pieces/month**.

---

### Solution — Step 1: Standard Time Before Work Study

$$
T_{std,\text{old}} = T_{obs} \times \frac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right)
$$

Substituting $T_{obs} = 4.50$ min, $R = 110$, $A = 15$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{std,\text{old}} &= 4.50 \times \frac{110}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{15}{100}\right) \\
&= 4.50 \times 1.10 \times 1.15 \\
&= 4.50 \times 1.265 \\
&= 5.6925 \;\text{min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 2: Standard Time After Work Study

With $T_{obs} = 3.60$ min, $R = 110$, $A = 15$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{std,\text{new}} &= 3.60 \times \frac{110}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{15}{100}\right) \\
&= 3.60 \times 1.10 \times 1.15 \\
&= 3.60 \times 1.265 \\
&= 4.554 \;\text{min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Step 3: Percentage Productivity Improvement

Productivity is inversely proportional to standard time per piece. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{T_{std,\text{old}} - T_{std,\text{new}}}{T_{std,\text{new}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{5.6925 - 4.554}{4.554} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1.1385}{4.554} \times 100 \\
&= 25.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The company now produces **25% more pieces** in the same available time — purely from work study.

### Solution — Step 4: Monthly Cost Savings

Time saved per piece:

$$
\Delta T = 5.6925 - 4.554 = 1.1385 \;\text{min/piece}
$$

Total time saved per month for 5,000 pieces:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total time saved} &= 1.1385 \times 5000 \\
&= 5692.5 \;\text{minutes} \\
&= \frac{5692.5}{60} \\
&= 94.875 \;\text{hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cost savings:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost saved} &= 94.875 \times 80 \\
&= \text{₹ } 7{,}590 \text{ per month}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Work Study Tools to Productivity Outcomes

| Work Study Tool | Productivity Lever | Typical Productivity Gain | Field of Application |
|----------------|--------------------|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Operation Process Chart | Eliminates unnecessary operations | 5–10% | Manufacturing planning |
| Flow Process Chart | Reduces transport & delays | 10–15% | Production line balancing |
| String Diagram / Flow Diagram | Optimizes workplace layout | 10–20% | Workshop, lab, kitchen |
| Two-Handed Process Chart | Improves simultaneous hand use | 15–25% | Assembly work, sewing |
| Motion Study (Therbligs) | Removes micro-motions | 5–30% | Repetitive manual tasks |
| Time Study (Stopwatch) | Sets accurate standard times | 5–15% | Wage incentive, planning |
| Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS) | Engineered standards (e.g., MTM, MOST) | 10–20% | High-volume assembly |
| Work Sampling | Identifies delay percentage | 5–10% | Office, indirect work |
| Activity Sampling | Capacity utilization | 5–15% | Machine monitoring |

## 3.4 The Productivity "Before-After" Comparison Model

The **Before-After** analytical model is the most common way work study is evaluated in industry:

$$
P_{\text{before}} = \frac{\text{Output before}}{\text{Input before}}, \quad P_{\text{after}} = \frac{\text{Output after}}{\text{Input after}}
$$

The **productivity ratio** after intervention is:

$$
\text{Productivity Ratio} = \frac{P_{\text{after}}}{P_{\text{before}}}
$$

If the ratio exceeds **1.0**, work study has produced a net positive effect. Ratios in well-executed work study projects typically fall in the range **1.10 to 1.40**, representing **10% to 40% productivity gains**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Work Study → Productivity Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT: Man Machine Material Method Environment] --> B[WORK STUDY SYSTEM]
    B --> B1[METHOD STUDY]
    B --> B2[WORK MEASUREMENT]
    B1 --> C1[Select Job to Record]
    C1 --> C2[Record Facts Using Charts]
    C2 --> C3[Examine with Critical Questioning]
    C3 --> C4[Develop Improved Method]
    C4 --> C5[Evaluate Alternatives]
    C5 --> C6[Define and Install New Method]
    C6 --> D[METHOD IMPROVEMENT]
    B2 --> E1[Choose Technique Time Study or PMTS]
    E1 --> E2[Record Performance and Time]
    E2 --> E3[Apply Performance Rating]
    E3 --> E4[Compute Normal Time]
    E4 --> E5[Add Allowances]
    E5 --> F[STANDARD TIME ESTABLISHED]
    D --> G[PRODUCTIVITY ENGINE]
    F --> G
    G --> H1[Higher Output per Hour]
    G --> H2[Lower Cost per Unit]
    G --> H3[Better Quality at Same Cost]
    G --> H4[Fair Wage Incentive Basis]
    G --> H5[Efficient Capacity Planning]
    H1 --> I[IMPROVED ORGANIZATIONAL PRODUCTIVITY]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    I --> J[FEEDBACK: Continuous Improvement Loop]
    J --> A
```

## 4.2 The Three-Stage Productivity Model via Work Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[STAGE 1: DIAGNOSE]
        X1[Observe Current Method] --> X2[Measure Current Time]
        X2 --> X3[Identify Waste and Non-Value-Add]
    end
    subgraph S2[STAGE 2: REDESIGN]
        X3 --> Y1[Apply Method Study 7 Step]
        Y1 --> Y2[Set New Standard Time]
        Y2 --> Y3[Validate Ergonomically]
    end
    subgraph S3[STAGE 3: DEPLOY]
        Y3 --> Z1[Train Operators]
        Z1 --> Z2[Implement SOP]
        Z2 --> Z3[Monitor and Audit]
        Z3 --> Z4[Sustained Productivity]
    end
    Z4 -.Continuous Feedback.-> X1
```

## 4.3 Work Study Techniques and Their Direct Productivity Lever

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[WORK STUDY] --> B[METHOD STUDY]
    A --> C[WORK MEASUREMENT]
    B --> B1[Process Chart]
    B --> B2[Flow Diagram]
    B --> B3[Two Handed Chart]
    B --> B4[Motion Study Therbligs]
    C --> C1[Stopwatch Time Study]
    C --> C2[PMTS MTM MOST]
    C --> C3[Work Sampling]
    C --> C4[Standard Data System]
    B1 --> P[PRODUCTIVITY GAIN: Reduced Waste]
    B2 --> P
    B3 --> P
    B4 --> P
    C1 --> Q[PRODUCTIVITY GAIN: Standardization]
    C2 --> Q
    C3 --> Q
    C4 --> Q
    P --> R[INCREASED OUTPUT AND REDUCED COST]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.4 Productivity Improvement Flow — From Data to Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Work Study Data] --> B[Calculate Standard Time]
    B --> C[Compute Productivity Index PI]
    C --> D{PI Greater Than 100?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Investigate Reasons for Outperformance]
    D -- No --> F[Identify Bottlenecks and Delays]
    F --> G[Re apply Method Study]
    G --> H[Establish New Standard]
    H --> I[Re train Workers]
    I --> J[Measure New PI]
    J --> D
    E --> K[Document Best Practices]
    K --> L[Benchmark Other Workstations]
    L --> M[Organization Wide Productivity Boost]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Work Study and list its two main components. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Work study is a systematic technique for investigating, analyzing, and improving work methods and establishing standard times for operations, as per **BS 3138:1979**. Its two main components are:
1. **Method Study** — to find the *one best way* of doing work.
2. **Work Measurement** — to determine the *standard time* for that method.

### Question 2: What is Productivity? State its formula. **[3 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Productivity is the ratio of output produced to the input consumed, indicating the efficiency of a system. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
P = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}
$$

In industrial engineering, labor productivity is commonly measured in *pieces per man-hour*, and machine productivity in *pieces per machine-hour*. Productivity improvement through work study is the key driver of lower cost per unit and higher profitability.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often write definitions but **forget the numerical calculation** — when a numerical is asked, even a small arithmetic slip costs the full 7 marks.
> - Many students confuse **Method Study** and **Work Measurement** — clearly state the *one best way* vs. *standard time* distinction.
> - Always carry **at least 3 significant figures** in intermediate calculations. Truncation errors at $T_{std}$ propagate to the final answer.
> - For productivity-related questions, do not forget to show the *before* and *after* comparison clearly — examiners award separate marks for both states.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1

#### (a) Explain in detail the **role of work study in improving productivity** in an industrial organization. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Work study is a powerful, scientific, and proven engineering technique that plays a pivotal role in enhancing organizational productivity. Its role can be explained through the following eight points:

1. **Eliminates Unnecessary Work:** Work study identifies and removes non-value-adding activities such as excessive transport, waiting, and redundant inspections. This directly increases the proportion of useful work in the working day.

2. **Establishes the One Best Method:** Method study, using tools like process charts and motion study, replaces inefficient methods with a scientifically designed **one best way** of doing each job.

3. **Sets Standard Times:** Work measurement (time study, PMTS) establishes fair standard times for each operation, eliminating arbitrary targets and enabling objective evaluation.

4. **Provides Basis for Wage Incentives:** With known standard times, incentive schemes like Halsey, Rowan, or Bedaux become fair and motivating — workers earn more for exceeding the standard, and the company gains in output.

5. **Improves Workplace Layout and Ergonomics:** Method study optimizes the placement of tools, materials, and machines. Reduced motion and better posture lower fatigue, accidents, and absenteeism.

6. **Enables Effective Production Planning:** Standard times are the basis of capacity calculation, line balancing, machine loading, and delivery scheduling — all of which improve throughput and reduce idle time.

7. **Reduces Manufacturing Cost:** Higher output and lower waste per unit reduce the **cost per piece**, improving the organization's competitive position and profitability.

8. **Improves Worker Satisfaction and Morale:** When workers trust the standards (because they are derived scientifically and not imposed), they cooperate better, leading to higher engagement and output.

**Conclusion:** Work study is therefore not merely a time-saving tool; it is a **management philosophy** that aligns human effort, materials, and machines toward maximum productive output. It is universally applicable — from manufacturing to services, from hospitals to hotels.

---

#### (b) In a manual assembly operation, a time study of 40 cycles gave an average observed time of **2.80 minutes/cycle** and a performance rating of **90%**. The total allowance is **20%**. After a method study, the new average time became **2.20 minutes/cycle** with the same rating. Calculate: **[7 Marks]**
1. Standard time before method study.
2. Standard time after method study.
3. Percentage productivity improvement.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $T_{obs,\text{old}} = 2.80$ min, $R = 90\%$, $A = 20\%$
- $T_{obs,\text{new}} = 2.20$ min, $R = 90\%$, $A = 20\%$

**Step 1 — Standard Time Before Method Study:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{std,\text{old}} &= T_{obs} \times \frac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right) \\
&= 2.80 \times \frac{90}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{20}{100}\right) \\
&= 2.80 \times 0.90 \times 1.20 \\
&= 3.024 \;\text{min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Standard Time After Method Study:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{std,\text{new}} &= 2.20 \times 0.90 \times 1.20 \\
&= 2.376 \;\text{min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Productivity Improvement:**

Since productivity $\propto 1/T_{std}$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= \frac{T_{std,\text{old}} - T_{std,\text{new}}}{T_{std,\text{new}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3.024 - 2.376}{2.376} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.648}{2.376} \times 100 \\
&= 27.27\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The work study intervention improves productivity by approximately **27.27%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Set

#### (a) Discuss the **relationship between method study and productivity** with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Method study and productivity are intricately linked — method study is the *engine*, and productivity gain is the *output*. The relationship can be understood through the following framework:

**Definition Recap:** Method study is the systematic recording, examination, and critical analysis of the existing method of doing work to develop a more efficient method.

**How Method Study Directly Improves Productivity:**

1. **Identifies Hidden Waste:** The 7-step method study (select, record, examine, develop, evaluate, define, install) exposes waste that workers and supervisors may have normalized — such as walking long distances, double-handling, or doing two jobs in the wrong sequence.

2. **Reduces Cycle Time:** By removing non-value-adding activities, the *total cycle time* of an operation falls. Lower cycle time means more pieces per shift, i.e., higher productivity.

3. **Standardizes Operations:** Once a method is finalized, it is documented as an SOP. This eliminates variation between workers and shifts — leading to consistent quality and predictable output.

4. **Lowers Cost:** Less time, less motion, and less rework → lower labor cost per unit → higher margin.

5. **Improves Worker Safety & Morale:** Better layout and ergonomics → fewer injuries → less absenteeism → more productive hours per worker per year.

**Suitable Industrial Example:**

Consider a packaging line where workers walk **6 meters** to fetch a box before each pack. A method study uses a **string diagram** to visualize this motion and suggests placing the box stack beside the worker. Result:
- Walking distance drops from **6 m to 0.5 m**.
- Time saved per pack = **8 seconds**.
- Over a shift of **8 hours × 200 packs** = **1,600 seconds = 26.67 minutes saved**.
- This is a **5.5% productivity gain** purely from layout change — no new equipment needed.

**Conclusion:** Method study is the *low-cost, high-impact* component of work study for improving productivity.

---

#### (b) In a manufacturing unit, the standard time for an operation is **6.0 minutes** with a performance rating of **100%** and an allowance of **10%**. After a method improvement, the new observed time is **4.8 minutes** at a rating of **105%**, with allowance reduced to **8%** due to better method. The operator works **8 hours/day** and wage rate is **₹100/hour**. The monthly production target is **20,000 pieces**. Calculate: **[7 Marks]**
1. New standard time after improvement.
2. The number of pieces that can be produced per day after improvement.
3. Whether the monthly target can be met.
4. The percentage reduction in standard time.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Old: $T_{std,\text{old}} = 6.0$ min, $R_{old} = 100\%$, $A_{old} = 10\%$
- New: $T_{obs,\text{new}} = 4.8$ min, $R_{new} = 105\%$, $A_{new} = 8\%$
- Shift: **8 hours/day**; Wage: **₹100/hour**; Target: **20,000 pieces/month** (assume 25 working days → 800 pieces/day).

**Step 1 — New Standard Time After Improvement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{std,\text{new}} &= T_{obs} \times \frac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{A}{100}\right) \\
&= 4.8 \times \frac{105}{100} \times \left(1 + \frac{8}{100}\right) \\
&= 4.8 \times 1.05 \times 1.08 \\
&= 5.4432 \;\text{min/piece}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Pieces Produced Per Day (8 hours = 480 minutes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pieces/day} &= \frac{480}{5.4432} \\
&= 88.18 \;\text{pieces (rounded to 88)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — this seems too low. Let us reconsider: the operator can take multiple pieces; the standard time **per piece** is **5.44 minutes**, so in 480 minutes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{480}{5.4432} = 88.18 \approx 88 \text{ pieces/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Monthly Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Monthly output} &= 88 \times 25 \\
&= 2{,}200 \text{ pieces/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

The target of **20,000 pieces/month is NOT met** by a single operator. This means **multiple operators/machines** are required: $20{,}000 / 2{,}200 \approx 9.09 \rightarrow 10$ operators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on this calculation:** If the problem intends the operator to be one of many, then 10 such operators can produce ~22,000 pieces/month — comfortably meeting the 20,000 target. This shows how work study feeds directly into **capacity planning**.

**Step 4 — Percentage Reduction in Standard Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Reduction} &= \frac{T_{std,\text{old}} - T_{std,\text{new}}}{T_{std,\text{old}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6.0 - 5.4432}{6.0} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{0.5568}{6.0} \times 100 \\
&= 9.28\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Productivity Increase:**

$$
\text{Productivity increase} = \frac{6.0}{5.4432} = 1.102 \;\text{(or } 10.2\% \text{ improvement)}
$$

**Conclusion:** A **9.28% reduction in standard time** translates to a **10.2% productivity gain**, and the company now needs fewer operators to meet the same monthly target — saving labor cost and improving competitiveness.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU 2024)**

- ✅ **Work Study Definition (BS 3138)** — *Method Study + Work Measurement*; write the full BIS/BS number for full marks.
- ✅ **Two Branches** — *Method Study* (how) and *Work Measurement* (how long).
- ✅ **7 Steps of Method Study** — Select, Record, Examine, Develop, Evaluate, Define, Install.
- ✅ **Productivity Formula** — $P = \dfrac{\text{Output}}{\text{Input}}$.
- ✅ **Standard Time Formula** — $T_{std} = T_o \times \dfrac{R}{100} \times \left(1 + \dfrac{A}{100}\right)$.
- ✅ **Performance Rating** — Always expressed as a percentage; basis = "**Normal**" pace.
- ✅ **Allowances** — Rest + Personal + Contingency + Special (if any).
- ✅ **Productivity Index** — $PI = \dfrac{\Sigma \text{Standard hours produced}}{\Sigma \text{Hours actually worked}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Productivity is inversely proportional to standard time** — so a drop in $T_{std}$ means a rise in $P$.
- ✅ **8 Roles of Work Study in Productivity** — waste removal, method standardization, fair standard time, ergonomic gains, wage incentives, capacity planning, cost reduction, and worker satisfaction.
- ✅ **Typical productivity gains from work study** — 10% to 40% (always quote a range to show depth).
- ✅ **Heinrich's Law / Industrial Engineering Axiom** — *"Without a standard, there is no logical basis for improvement."*
- ✅ **Mermaid Block Pitfall** — Node IDs must be alphanumeric (e.g., `stepA`), never `end` or `subgraph`.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Pattern** — Part A (3 marks: definition/concept); Part B (14 marks: 7 + 7 — theory + numerical).
- ✅ **Common Valuation Pitfall** — Forgetting to show *Before vs. After* comparison in numericals; examiners mark this strictly.
- ✅ **Standard values to remember** — Typical allowance = 10–25%; Normal rating = 100%; 1 hour = 60 minutes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Contributions of Method Study and Work Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Specific Contributions of Method Study and Work Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Method Study** is a systematic, critical, and scientific examination of the *modes, movements, materials, and manpower* involved in performing a task or operation, with the objective of devising a *better, easier, and more economical* method that increases productivity, reduces fatigue, and improves workplace safety. Method Study primarily answers the question **"HOW"** a job should be performed.

**Work Measurement** is the application of techniques designed to establish and validate the *time required for a qualified worker* to perform a specified task at a *defined level of performance*. Work Measurement primarily answers the question **"HOW LONG"** a job should take.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Significance):** Method Study and Work Measurement are the two **fundamental pillars of Work Study**. While Method Study reduces *unnecessary work content*, Work Measurement establishes the *standard time* for the residual necessary work. Together, they form the scientific foundation for productivity engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you run a small restaurant in Kerala making Appam & Stew. Customers complain that food arrives too late.

- **Method Study** is like rearranging your kitchen. You notice the rice container is far from the stove, the stew vessels are stacked behind the gas, and the chef walks 8 steps per order. By moving the rice closer, stacking vessels in rotation order, and laying out items in the *sequence of use*, you cut the cooking time from 15 minutes to 9 minutes — **without working harder**, only **working smarter**. This is the *HOW* of doing the job.

- **Work Measurement** is like timing 50 orders with a stopwatch. You notice a *qualified* chef consistently takes 9 minutes for a standard order when applying a *normal effort* (not hurrying, not slacking). You add 15% allowance for tea breaks, prayer, and rest, giving a **Standard Time of 10.35 minutes per order**. Now you can confidently tell a customer *"Your food will be ready in 10 minutes"*, and schedule staff correctly. This is the *HOW LONG* of doing the job.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Anchor:** Method Study = **HOW** (Eliminate waste). Work Measurement = **HOW LONG** (Establish standard). One optimises *content*, the other quantifies *duration*.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

The key quantitative metric in this topic is **Standard Time (ST)**, measured in:

- **Seconds (s)**
- **Minutes (min)**
- **Standard Minute Value (SMV)** — a dimensionless number representing the minutes of standard work content per unit.
- **Man-hours / Day** for aggregate planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The phrase *"specific contributions"* in your syllabus module refers to the *granular, individual benefits* each technique delivers to a manufacturing or service organisation. Examiners frequently test this as a 3-mark short answer or a 7-mark descriptive question.

---

## 1.4 Distinction: Method Study vs. Work Measurement

| Basis | Method Study | Work Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Question** | HOW should the work be done? | HOW LONG should it take? |
| **Nature** | Qualitative (process improvement) | Quantitative (time standardisation) |
| **Primary Tool** | Process Charts, Flow Diagrams, String Diagrams | Stopwatch Time Study, Work Sampling, PMTS |
| **Output** | Improved Method, Reduced Steps | Standard Time, Production Standards |
| **Order of Application** | First (must come BEFORE timing) | Second (requires a stable method) |
| **Effect on Cost** | Indirect reduction (less waste) | Direct reduction (better estimation) |

---

## 1.5 Relationship: The Work Study Cycle

Method Study and Work Measurement are **sequential and complementary**:

1. **Method Study** → Find the *one best way* to do the job.
2. **Work Measurement** → Time the *one best way* to set standards.
3. The cycle is closed by **Incentive Schemes** and **Production Planning**, which use the standard time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this order matters:** You cannot accurately time a poor method. If you measure a wasteful process, you end up *standardising waste* — a common KTU 2024 viva trap. Always improve the method FIRST, then time the improved method.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Specific Contributions of Method Study (The "HOW" Benefits)

Method Study delivers a structured bouquet of contributions that can be categorised as **Direct** (immediate) and **Indirect** (long-term) benefits.

### 2.1.1 Direct Contributions

- **Improvement of Processes and Procedures:** Re-engineers the sequence of operations to eliminate non-value-adding steps.
- **Improved Layout and Workspace Design:** Better plant layout, office layout, and workstation ergonomics — reduces material handling by up to 30–40% in many KTU-case studies.
- **Reduction in Unnecessary Movements:** Implements principles of *motion economy* (Therbligs).
- **Improved Safety and Working Conditions:** Identifies hazardous steps and redesigns them out of existence.
- **Reduction in Fatigue:** Combines improved posture, better tool placement, and shorter work cycles.
- **Elimination of Waste:** Targets the **8 wastes (DOWNTIME)** — Defects, Overproduction, Waiting, Non-utilised talent, Transportation, Inventory, Motion, Extra-processing.

### 2.1.2 Indirect Contributions

- **Improved Worker Morale:** Workers see management cares about their effort.
- **Standardisation of Methods:** A documented "One Best Way" enables training.
- **Better Utilisation of Men, Machines, and Materials:** Higher *Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)*.
- **Foundation for Ergonomics and Human Factors:** Sets base data for further refinements.
- **Reduced Production Cost (Indirect):** Through fewer rejects and less rework.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** When asked *"List the contributions of Method Study"*, present them in a *bullet hierarchy* — Direct vs. Indirect. Examiners reward structured answers.

---

## 2.2 Specific Contributions of Work Measurement (The "HOW LONG" Benefits)

Work Measurement provides the *quantitative backbone* of industrial engineering practice.

### 2.2.1 Core Contributions

- **Setting Standard Times** for every operation, forming the basis of all planning.
- **Production Planning and Control (PPC):** Daily/weekly production targets are derived directly from standard times.
- **Scheduling and Line Balancing:** Determines cycle times and takt time for assembly lines.
- **Manpower Planning:** Calculates *how many workers* are needed for a given output.
- **Machine Capacity Planning:** Determines *how many machines* are needed.
- **Cost Estimation and Cost Control:** Labour cost per unit = Standard Time × Wage Rate.
- **Incentive Wage Schemes:** Forms the basis of Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, and Emerson plans.
- **Performance Evaluation and Appraisal:** Actual Time vs. Standard Time gives efficiency %.
- **Budgeting and Quoting:** Tendering requires accurate time-based cost.
- **Determining Delivery Dates:** Realistic lead times depend on standard times.

### 2.2.2 Indirect Contributions

- **Worker–Management Trust:** Fair standards reduce disputes.
- **Basis for Process Improvement (Kaizen):** Variances from standard flag improvement opportunities.
- **Comparison of Alternative Methods:** When two methods exist, the one with lower standard time wins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic — "PLACEMATS":** Work Measurement contributions: **P**lanning, **L**ine balancing, **A**ccurate costing, **C**apacity, **E**stimates, **M**anpower, **A**praisal, **T**endering, **S**cheduling.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **tested every KTU cycle**. Master each one. Note: per protocol, vertical bars are rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Basic Time** | $BT = OT \times \frac{R}{100}$ | OT = Observed Time, R = Rating % |
| 2 | **Normal Time** | $NT = BT = OT \times R$ | Where R is the rating factor (e.g., 1.10 for fast worker) |
| 3 | **Standard Time** | $ST = NT + Allowances$ | ST = NT × (1 + Allowance Fraction) |
| 4 | **Allowance Form** | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | A = total allowance as a fraction |
| 5 | **Standard Minute Value (SMV)** | $SMV = ST \text{ (in minutes)}$ | Used in garment industry, no unit |
| 6 | **Efficiency %** | $Efficiency = \frac{ST}{AT} \times 100$ | AT = Actual Time taken |
| 7 | **Performance Rating** | $R = \frac{\text{Observed Speed}}{\text{Normal Speed}} \times 100$ | In percentage, or as a decimal |
| 8 | **Relaxation Allowance** | $RA$ = rest % + personal % + contingency % | Sum of sub-allowances |
| 9 | **Number of Machines (Workload)** | $N = \frac{T \times P}{C \times S}$ | T = Std Time, P = Output, C = Cycle, S = Shift hrs |
| 10 | **Cycle Time (Takt Time)** | $T_c = \frac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ | Used in line balancing |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing (Auto, Garment, FMCG):** Standard times drive production lines, target costing, and Six Sigma.
- **Healthcare:** Method Study redesigns patient flow in OPDs; Work Measurement sets consultation time standards.
- **IT & Software:** Cycle time, lead time, and throughput metrics are direct descendants of Work Measurement.
- **Logistics & Warehousing:** Layout design (Method Study) and pick-rate standards (Work Measurement).
- **KTU Internship Context:** Most Kerala engineering students encounter these techniques in their **6-month industry internship** in plants like HMT, Keltron, BPCL, MRF, or Apollo Tyres.

> [!TIP]
> **Connection to Other Modules:** Module 1 (Productivity) and Module 3 (Inventory & Quality) both depend on *standard times* set by Work Measurement. This topic is therefore a **gateway concept** for the rest of the syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How Each Contribution is Achieved

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix demonstrates the **logical flow** from observation → method improvement → time standardisation → organisational benefit. It is the most examination-friendly way to present this topic.

| Stage | Activity | Method Study Tool | Work Measurement Tool | Resulting Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Observe the existing process | Process chart, Two-handed chart | Stopwatch time study | Baseline established |
| 2 | Question every step (5 Ws + 1 H) | Critical examination | Rating the operator | Waste identified |
| 3 | Develop a better method | Flow process chart, String diagram | — | Improved method |
| 4 | Implement the improved method | Layout, jig, fixture design | — | Reduced work content |
| 5 | Time the improved method | — | Time study / PMTS | Standard Time |
| 6 | Apply Standard Time organisation-wide | — | Database, ERP | Productivity, cost, planning |

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Standard Time (Step-by-Step)

This is the most important derivation in this module. Follow each line carefully.

### Step 1: Define the Variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
OT &= \text{Observed Time (raw stopwatch reading)} \\
R &= \text{Performance Rating (a dimensionless factor)} \\
NT &= \text{Normal Time (what a qualified worker does at normal pace)} \\
A &= \text{Total Allowance as a fraction (e.g., 0.15 for 15\%)} \\
ST &= \text{Standard Time (the final, organisation-wide standard)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Convert Observed Time to Normal Time

The observer adjusts the raw time to account for the operator's actual speed relative to the *concept of "normal"*. A worker faster than normal has R > 1.00; a slower worker has R < 1.00.

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= OT \times R \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A worker takes $OT = 1.20$ min on a cycle, and the observer rates them at $R = 110$ (i.e., 10% faster than normal).

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= 1.20 \times 1.10 = 1.32 \text{ minutes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Meaning:** A *qualified* worker moving at a *normal* pace would need 1.32 min — slower than the rated worker, because the rating reveals the rated worker was hurrying.

---

### Step 3: Add Allowances to Normal Time

A worker cannot sustain continuous work 100% of the shift. We add *allowances* for rest, personal needs, and contingencies.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT + (Allowances) \\
   &= NT + (NT \times A) \\
   &= NT \times (1 + A) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Continuing the Example:** Let $A = 0.20$ (20% allowance).

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= 1.32 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
   &= 1.32 \times 1.20 \\
   &= 1.584 \text{ minutes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Meaning:** For every piece, a worker should be allowed **1.584 min**, so that across the shift (after factoring rest, tea, prayer, fatigue), the average pace is "normal" — not rushed.

---

### Step 4: The Full Standard Time Identity (Compact Form)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{ST = OT \times R \times (1 + A)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is THE KTU formula.** Memorise it. Substituting $NT = OT \times R$ inside the allowance formula is the most common derivation in KTU ESE answers.

---

### Step 3: Detailed Numerical Problem (KTU-style)

**Problem:** In a time study of a drilling operation, the observed times (in minutes) for 10 cycles are: **0.50, 0.52, 0.48, 0.55, 0.51, 0.49, 0.53, 0.50, 0.52, 0.50**. The performance rating of the operator is **120%**. Total allowance is **15%**. Compute the Standard Time per piece.

#### Solution:

**Step A — Compute the Average Observed Time (Mean):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum OT &= 0.50+0.52+0.48+0.55+0.51+0.49+0.53+0.50+0.52+0.50 \\
        &= 5.10 \text{ minutes} \\
\overline{OT} &= \frac{\sum OT}{n} = \frac{5.10}{10} = 0.510 \text{ min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step B — Apply the Rating (R = 1.20):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= \overline{OT} \times R \\
   &= 0.510 \times 1.20 \\
   &= 0.612 \text{ min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Add Allowances (A = 0.15):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT \times (1 + A) \\
   &= 0.612 \times 1.15 \\
   &= 0.7038 \text{ min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{ST \approx 0.704 \text{ minutes per piece}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trick:** Some students forget to convert 120% → 1.20, treating it as 120 minutes. Always write the rating as a *decimal* before multiplying.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Efficiency and Standard Hours

### Step 1: Define Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
Efficiency \,(\%) &= \frac{Standard \ Time \ Produced}{Actual \ Time \ Spent} \times 100 \\
                  &= \frac{ST \times N}{AT} \times 100 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $N$ = number of pieces produced in the period.

**Numerical Example:** In an 8-hour shift ($AT = 480$ min), a worker produces $N = 600$ pieces. The standard time is $ST = 0.704$ min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Efficiency &= \frac{0.704 \times 600}{480} \times 100 \\
           &= \frac{422.4}{480} \times 100 \\
           &= 88.0\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The worker is operating at **88% efficiency** — slightly below standard. This is realistic and healthy (most KTU problems expect results in the 80–110% range).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: What if Method Study is NOT done first?

| Scenario | Without Method Study | With Method Study |
|---|---|---|
| Work content | High (many wasteful steps) | Reduced (lean steps) |
| Observed Time | Larger | Smaller |
| Standard Time | Inflated | Realistic, lean |
| Cost per unit | Higher | Lower |
| Worker fatigue | High | Low |
| Result | You "standardise waste" | You "standardise value" |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often say "Method Study and Work Measurement are independent and can be done in any order." This is **WRONG**. The *International Labour Organization (ILO)* and *BS 3138:1979* both mandate that Method Study precedes Work Measurement. The standard time of a wasteful process perpetuates waste.

---

## 3.5 Quick Reference: Allowances Breakdown (Indian Industry Standards)

| Allowance Type | Typical Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Rest Allowance** | 4% – 10% | Recover from physical/mental fatigue |
| **Personal Allowance** | 5% – 7% | Toilet, drinking water, prayer |
| **Contingency Allowance** | 3% – 5% | Minor delays, tool adjustment |
| **Special Allowance** | 1% – 5% | Awkward posture, heat, dust |
| **Total (Typical)** | **10% – 25%** | Varies by job nature |

> [!TIP]
> For **light work** (assembly, inspection), use **10–15%** total. For **heavy work** (forging, foundry), use **20–30%**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Method Study & Work Measurement Ecosystem

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *system-level interactions* between Method Study, Work Measurement, and the organisational functions they support. It replaces a physical diagram with a structured flow architecture.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Observed Process] --> B1[Method Study Phase]
    B1 --> B1a[Process Chart Analysis]
    B1 --> B1b[Critical Examination]
    B1 --> B1c[Flow Diagram Design]
    B1c --> B1d[Improved Method Selected]
    B1d --> C1[Work Measurement Phase]
    C1 --> C1a[Stopwatch Time Study]
    C1 --> C1b[Performance Rating]
    C1 --> C1c[Allowance Calculation]
    C1c --> D1[Standard Time Established]
    D1 --> E1[Productivity Uplift]
    D1 --> E2[Production Planning and Control]
    D1 --> E3[Cost Estimation and Costing]
    D1 --> E4[Incentive Wage Scheme Design]
    D1 --> E5[Capacity and Manpower Planning]
    D1 --> E6[Line Balancing and Scheduling]
    E1 --> F1[Organisational Profit and Growth]
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    E6 --> F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Method Study (B1) refines the process, feeding into Work Measurement (C1), which produces Standard Time (D1). Standard Time is then *consumed* by six downstream organisational functions (E1–E6), all converging on organisational growth (F1). This single architecture can be the answer to a 7-mark KTU question.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: The 8-Step Method Study Procedure

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Step 1 Select Job to Study] --> S2[Step 2 Record the Facts Using Charts]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Examine the Facts Critically]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Develop the Improved Method]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Define the New Method]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Install the New Method]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Maintain the New Method]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8 Repeat on the Next Job]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** The 8-step procedure is *Gilbreth's classic framework*. Each step delivers a *contribution*. Step 3 (critical examination) is the intellectual heart of Method Study — this is where the **specific contributions** of Method Study are unlocked.

---

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Work Measurement Subsystems

```mermaid
graph TD
    W1[Work Measurement] --> W2[Direct Methods]
    W1 --> W3[Indirect Methods]
    W2 --> W2a[Stopwatch Time Study]
    W2 --> W2b[Predetermined Motion Time Systems PMTS]
    W3 --> W3a[Work Sampling]
    W3 --> W3b[Standard Data System]
    W3 --> W3c[Estimating from Formulae]
    W2a --> OUT1[Standard Time]
    W2b --> OUT1
    W3a --> OUT1
    W3b --> OUT1
    W3c --> OUT1
    OUT1 --> APP1[Planning Application]
    OUT1 --> APP2[Costing Application]
    OUT1 --> APP3[Incentive Application]
    OUT1 --> APP4[Capacity Application]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Use:** In KTU viva, if asked *"What are the methods of Work Measurement?"* — point to this diagram. It shows you understand the **two categories** (Direct vs. Indirect) and the **four major techniques**.

---

## 4.4 Cause-Effect Diagram: Specific Contributions Triggered

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT[Method Study and Work Measurement]
    ROOT --> C1[Process Improvement]
    ROOT --> C2[Time Standardisation]
    ROOT --> C3[Cost Reduction]
    ROOT --> C4[Worker Welfare]
    ROOT --> C5[Planning Capability]
    C1 --> C1a[Reduced Steps]
    C1 --> C1b[Better Layout]
    C2 --> C2a[Standard Time]
    C2 --> C2b[Performance Benchmark]
    C3 --> C3a[Lower Labour Cost]
    C3 --> C3b[Accurate Estimation]
    C4 --> C4a[Reduced Fatigue]
    C4 --> C4b[Improved Safety]
    C5 --> C5a[Production Targets]
    C5 --> C5b[Manpower and Machine Planning]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, Part A has 8 questions of 3 marks each, and the student answers **5**. The two questions below are *representative* of the most-likely 3-mark patterns on this topic.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**"List any six specific contributions of Method Study in an industrial organisation."**

**Model Answer (6 points, ~30 words each):**

1. **Process Improvement:** Eliminates unnecessary operations, delays, and transport — reduces work content.
2. **Improved Layout:** Better placement of men, machines, and materials — lowers material handling cost.
3. **Standardisation of Methods:** Creates a single "one best way" for training and reference.
4. **Reduced Fatigue and Improved Safety:** Ergonomic redesigns of tools, heights, and motions.
5. **Improved Worker Morale:** Workers perceive that management cares about reducing effort.
6. **Foundation for Ergonomics:** Provides data for human-factors engineering refinements.

> [!TIP]
> **Strategy:** For 3-mark Part A, list 6 bullet points, each with a short *active* verb and a *consequence* (e.g., "Standardisation → training efficiency").

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**"How does Work Measurement contribute to production planning in a manufacturing industry?"**

**Model Answer (Structured):**

Work Measurement contributes to production planning by providing **standard times** for each operation. These standards are used to:

- **Set daily/weekly production targets** based on available man-hours.
- **Determine the number of machines and operators** required to meet a sales order.
- **Schedule operations** in correct sequence using the cycle-time data.
- **Load the plant** with the correct mix of jobs.
- **Determine delivery commitments** to customers with confidence.
- **Balance assembly lines** by distributing work according to standard times per station.

Thus, without Work Measurement, production planning is reduced to *guesswork*. With it, planning becomes **scientific, fair, and reliable**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE Module-Internal-Choice rule: each Part B question is for 14 marks with **sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**, and you answer **ONE** of the two alternative full questions. We provide two completely independent choices below.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — Method Study Focused

#### [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **specific contributions of Method Study** to industrial productivity. Differentiate between the *direct* and *indirect* benefits.

**(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **8-step procedure of Method Study** as given by Gilbreth.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Definition Context (1 mark):** Method Study is the systematic recording, examination, and critical evaluation of existing and proposed ways of doing work, to develop and apply *easier and more effective* methods.

**2. Direct Contributions (4 marks):**

| Direct Contribution | Engineering Outcome |
|---|---|
| Improvement of processes | Eliminates non-value-adding steps; reduces cycle time. |
| Reduction in unnecessary movement | Application of motion economy; less effort. |
| Improved layout | Plant, office, and warehouse layout become leaner. |
| Improved safety | Hazardous steps are redesigned out. |
| Reduced fatigue | Posture, height, and tool design improved. |
| Elimination of waste | Targets the 8 wastes (DOWNTIME). |

**3. Indirect Contributions (2 marks):**

- **Improved worker morale** (workers trust management).
- **Standardisation** enables training and replication.
- **Foundation** for further work-study refinements (ergonomics, time study).
- **Cost reduction** flows from less waste, fewer rejects, less rework.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step-by-step 8-Step Procedure (with flowchart logic, 7 marks):**

| Step | Name | Activity |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Select** | Choose the job to be studied (problem or scope). |
| 2 | **Record** | Capture facts using process charts, flow diagrams, two-handed charts. |
| 3 | **Examine** | Critical examination using 5 Ws + 1 H (What, Where, When, Who, Why, How). |
| 4 | **Develop** | Generate a new improved method. |
| 5 | **Define** | Document the new method clearly. |
| 6 | **Install** | Implement the new method on the shop floor. |
| 7 | **Maintain** | Audit, supervise, and prevent regression. |
| 8 | **Repeat** | Apply the procedure to the next job. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "**S**anta **R**eally **E**njoys **D**iwali **D**eliciously **I**n **M**y **R**ecipe" = Select, Record, Examine, Develop, Define, Install, Maintain, Repeat.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Work Measurement Focused

#### [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **specific contributions of Work Measurement** to an industrial enterprise. State the relationship between standard time and each contribution.

**(b) [7 Marks]** In a time study of an assembly operation, **10 cycles** are observed with the following times (in minutes):

$$
0.60, \; 0.62, \; 0.58, \; 0.61, \; 0.59, \; 0.63, \; 0.60, \; 0.61, \; 0.58, \; 0.58
$$

The performance rating of the operator is **115%**, and the total allowance is **20%**. Calculate the **Standard Time per piece** and the **efficiency** if the worker produces **400 pieces in an 8-hour shift**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Work Measurement is the application of techniques to determine the time required, by a qualified worker, to perform a task at a defined level of performance.

**2. Specific Contributions (5 marks):**

- **Production Planning:** Standard Time is multiplied by the demand to compute man-hours, which dictates the production plan.
- **Scheduling and Line Balancing:** Takt time and cycle time come from ST. Workstations are balanced to avoid bottlenecks.
- **Costing:** Labour cost per unit = ST × wage rate. Used in tenders and cost sheets.
- **Incentive Schemes:** Halsey, Rowan, Emerson, and Bedaux plans use ST as the basis of bonus.
- **Manpower & Capacity Planning:** Number of workers/machines = (ST × Output) / (Cycle × Shift hours).
- **Performance Appraisal:** Efficiency = (Standard Time Produced / Actual Time) × 100.
- **Delivery Date Estimation:** ST × order quantity gives accurate lead time.

**3. Relationship Statement (1 mark):** Each contribution derives from **one formula**: *Standard Time = Normal Time × (1 + Allowance)*. This single output cascades to all 7 benefits above.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Compute the Sum of Observed Times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum OT &= 0.60+0.62+0.58+0.61+0.59+0.63+0.60+0.61+0.58+0.58 \\
        &= 6.00 \text{ minutes} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Average Observed Time (Mean OT):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{OT} &= \frac{6.00}{10} = 0.600 \text{ min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Performance Rating (R = 1.15):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= 0.600 \times 1.15 = 0.690 \text{ min} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Add Allowances (A = 0.20):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= 0.690 \times (1 + 0.20) \\
   &= 0.690 \times 1.20 \\
   &= 0.828 \text{ min per piece} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Efficiency in the 8-Hour Shift:**

Actual time available: $AT = 8 \text{ hr} = 480 \text{ min}$. Pieces produced: $N = 400$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Efficiency &= \frac{ST \times N}{AT} \times 100 \\
           &= \frac{0.828 \times 400}{480} \times 100 \\
           &= \frac{331.2}{480} \times 100 \\
           &= 69.0\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{ST = 0.828 \text{ min/piece} \quad ; \quad Efficiency = 69\%}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Deductions):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert 115% → 1.15.** Many students multiply by 115. Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Averaging without summing properly.** Some skip Step 1. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Applying allowance to OT instead of NT.** Allowance is on Normal Time only. Loss: 1 mark.
> 4. **No units in final answer.** Always write "min/piece" or "minutes". Loss: 0.5 mark.
> 5. **Efficiency denominator mistake.** It is *Actual Time Spent* (480 min), not the *number of pieces*. Loss: 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. Use it the night before the exam.**

### ✅ Method Study — Key Takeaways

- ⭐ Method Study answers **"HOW"** the work should be done.
- ⭐ The 8-step procedure: **Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Define → Install → Maintain → Repeat**.
- ⭐ Tools: Process Charts, Flow Process Charts, Two-handed Charts, String Diagrams, Cycle Graphs, Multiple Activity Charts.
- ⭐ Direct contributions: process improvement, layout, safety, fatigue reduction.
- ⭐ Indirect contributions: morale, standardisation, ergonomics foundation.
- ⭐ **Always precede Work Measurement** — you cannot time waste scientifically.

### ✅ Work Measurement — Key Takeaways

- ⭐ Work Measurement answers **"HOW LONG"** the work should take.
- ⭐ The master formula: $ST = OT \times R \times (1 + A)$.
- ⭐ Normal Time = OT × R; Allowance is added to Normal Time, never to Observed Time.
- ⭐ Allowances: rest (4–10%), personal (5–7%), contingency (3–5%), special (1–5%) → Total 10–25%.
- ⭐ Tools: Stopwatch Time Study, PMTS (MTM, MOST), Work Sampling, Standard Data Systems, Analysing Estimating.
- ⭐ Contributions: planning, scheduling, costing, incentives, capacity, manpower, appraisal, delivery dates.
- ⭐ Efficiency formula: $Efficiency = \frac{ST \times N}{AT} \times 100$.

### ✅ Numerical Pattern Mastery

- ⭐ **Always** compute mean OT first (sum/n).
- ⭐ **Always** convert rating % to decimal (e.g., 115% → 1.15).
- ⭐ **Always** add allowance to Normal Time, not Observed Time.
- ⭐ **Always** state the final unit (min, sec, or hours).

### ✅ Conceptual Distinctions to Remember

- ⭐ **Method Study = Qualitative (process-focused).**
- ⭐ **Work Measurement = Quantitative (time-focused).**
- ⭐ Method Study is the **necessary precondition** for valid Work Measurement.
- ⭐ Work Measurement gives **standards**; Method Study gives **methods**; both serve **productivity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Final Note:** "Specific Contributions" questions are favourites of KTU examiners because they test *both* conceptual depth and applied calculation. Master the 5-W-1-H of Method Study, the 8 steps, the ST formula, and the contributions list — and you can score full marks regardless of which way the question is framed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Study: Procedure of Method Study - Selection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Study: Procedure of Method Study - Selection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method Study** is defined as the systematic, critical, and detailed examination of the existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the objective of developing easier, more efficient, and economic methods that reduce human effort, fatigue, and waste of resources.

The **procedure of method study** consists of a well-defined sequence of **six basic steps** as codified by the International Labour Organization (ILO) and followed universally in KTU-aligned industrial engineering practice:

1. **Select** the work to be studied
2. **Record** all the relevant facts of the present method
3. **Examine** the recorded facts critically
4. **Develop** the most practical, economic, and effective method
5. **Install** the new method as standard practice
6. **Maintain** the new method by periodic review

The **first step — Selection** — is the foundation of the entire study. It is the process of **identifying, screening, and choosing** that particular job, operation, process, or activity from a large number of candidates in the organization, which offers the **maximum potential for productivity improvement**, cost reduction, ergonomic enhancement, or quality upliftment upon being subjected to method study.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Doctor's Triage" Approach**
> Imagine a doctor in a hospital emergency room with 100 patients. The doctor cannot treat everyone in detail at once. She uses a process called **triage** — she quickly checks vital signs, severity of pain, probability of recovery, and cost of treatment, and then selects the most critical patients (or those where intervention will yield the maximum benefit) for detailed investigation.
>
> **Method Study Selection is exactly this.** A manufacturing plant has hundreds of operations, machines, and workflows. The industrial engineer (acting as the "doctor") cannot study everything. He must first *select* that operation which — when improved — will yield the **biggest payback (in money, time, safety, or quality)** for the organization.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Selection (KTU High-Yield Terms)

The following are the **standard selection criteria metrics** that a KTU student must memorize:

- **Bottleneck operations** (operations that limit overall output)
- **Operations with high labor content** (man-hours consumed)
- **Operations with high material cost**
- **Operations with quality/rejection problems**
- **Operations causing worker fatigue, accidents, or health hazards**
- **Operations with excessive waiting, transportation, or storage**
- **New products/processes being introduced**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Word to Remember:** Selection is about **choosing the RIGHT problem**, not solving it. A wrongly selected job wastes months of analyst effort, while a correctly selected job can save the company crores of rupees annually.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Logical Breakdown of the Selection Step

The selection step is **not a single decision** — it is a **mini-procedure** by itself. A KTU board examiner expects the student to know that selection involves the following sub-stages:

#### Sub-stage A: List All Candidate Jobs
- The analyst first prepares a comprehensive list of **all operations, products, processes, or procedures** in the plant/department under consideration.
- This list may be huge (sometimes hundreds of items).

#### Sub-stage B: Gather Preliminary Data
For each candidate, collect data on:
- **Output / volume** of production
- **Man-hours** consumed per week/month
- **Material cost** involved
- **Number of rejections / rework** instances
- **Number of accidents / safety incidents**
- **Complaints received** from workers or customers

#### Sub-stage C: Apply Selection Criteria (Screen the List)
The analyst applies a set of **pre-defined selection criteria** to filter the long list down to a few high-potential candidates.

#### Sub-stage D: Quantitative Evaluation
A **scoring model** (sometimes called the *"Selection Rating Sheet"* or *"Kelvin Top Set"* approach) is used. Each candidate is rated on multiple criteria, scores are summed, and the highest-scoring candidate is chosen.

#### Sub-stage E: Obtain Management Approval
The selected job is discussed with **supervisors, department heads, and top management** to obtain approval, resources, and clearance to begin the detailed study.

### 2.2 The Eight Standard Selection Criteria (KTU Mandatory List)

A KTU 2024 examiner frequently asks the student to **list the criteria for selection**. The universally accepted list is:

| # | Criterion | What it Means |
|---|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Economic considerations** | Operations that consume high cost (labor + material) |
| 2 | **Technical considerations** | Operations causing quality problems, bottlenecks, or excessive waste |
| 3 | **Human considerations** | Operations causing fatigue, accidents, monotony, or dissatisfaction |
| 4 | **Operations with high repetition** | Jobs done frequently where small improvements give large cumulative benefit |
| 5 | **Operations forming a bottleneck** | Operations limiting the throughput of the entire system |
| 6 | **Operations with excessive material handling** | Where transportation/waiting is high |
| 7 | **New products / processes** | Where methods are not yet standardized |
| 8 | **Customer complaints** | Areas where the customer has reported dissatisfaction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember the categories:** **"ETHICS"**
> - **E**conomic
> - **T**echnical
> - **H**uman
> - **I**nput-output (bottleneck / repetition)
> - **C**ustomer complaint
> - **S**afety / New setup

### 2.3 The Selection Rating Sheet (Quantitative Model)

The standard quantitative approach used in industry is the **Selection Rating Sheet**. Each candidate job is rated on multiple parameters, and the job with the **highest total score** is selected for detailed study.

Let the parameters and weights be denoted as follows:

- Let $n$ = total number of selection criteria
- Let $w_i$ = **weight** assigned to criterion $i$ (importance factor)
- Let $s_i$ = **score** awarded to candidate job on criterion $i$
- The **Total Selection Score (TSS)** for a candidate is:

$$
\text{TSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i
$$

- The candidate with the **maximum TSS** is selected.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Total Selection Score (TSS) | $\text{TSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$ | Dimensionless index (higher = better) |
| Normalized Weight | $w_i^{norm} = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | Sum of all $w_i^{norm} = 1$ |
| Weighted Average Score | $\bar{S}_w = \dfrac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | Used to compare jobs fairly |
| Bottleneck Identification | Throughput = $\min(\text{Capacity}_1, \text{Capacity}_2, \ldots, \text{Capacity}_k)$ | The smallest capacity = bottleneck |
| Cost Reduction Potential | $\Delta C = (C_{old} - C_{new}) \times Q$ | Where $Q$ = annual quantity |
| Payback Indicator | $\text{Savings Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Saving}}{\text{Investment}}$ | Higher = better candidate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NEVER use the symbol** $\vert x \vert$ (with vertical bars) inside any markdown table row. The pipe character breaks the table parser. Always use the LaTeX form $\lvert x \rvert$ or simply write *absolute value of x* in words.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing Plants:** Used to choose which assembly line or machining cell to optimize.
- **Hospitals:** Used to streamline patient flow in the emergency department.
- **Banks:** Used to redesign the loan approval process.
- **Software Companies:** Used to optimize the onboarding workflow of new employees.

Selection, when done correctly, ensures that the analyst's **time, money, and effort** are invested only in those areas where the **return on investment (ROI) of the study** will be the maximum.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Selection Score Equation

**Starting Point:**
We have $m$ candidate jobs (say $J_1, J_2, \ldots, J_m$) and $n$ selection criteria (say $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$).

**Step 1 — Define the score matrix:**
Let $S$ be an $m \times n$ matrix where each element $S_{ij}$ represents the score awarded to job $J_i$ on criterion $C_j$.

$$
S = \begin{bmatrix} s_{11} & s_{12} & \cdots & s_{1n} \\ s_{21} & s_{22} & \cdots & s_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ s_{m1} & s_{m2} & \cdots & s_{mn} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the weight vector:**
Let $\mathbf{w}$ be a $1 \times n$ row vector of criterion weights:

$$
\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 & w_2 & \cdots & w_n \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Total Selection Score (TSS) for each job:**
The TSS for job $J_i$ is the dot product of the $i$-th row of $S$ with the weight vector $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\text{TSS}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
$$

**Step 4 — Written in matrix form for elegance:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \text{TSS}_1 \\ \text{TSS}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \text{TSS}_m \end{bmatrix} = S \cdot \mathbf{w}^{T}
$$

**Step 5 — Normalize if needed:**
If the analyst wants the TSS to be a number between 0 and 1 (for easy comparison), divide by the sum of weights:

$$
\text{TSS}_i^{norm} = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j}
$$

**Step 6 — Selection Rule:**
The job selected for method study is the one with the maximum TSS:

$$
J_{selected} = \arg\max_{i} \text{TSS}_i
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem Statement (Model):**
A manufacturing company has 3 candidate jobs ($J_1, J_2, J_3$) for method study. The analyst uses 4 selection criteria with the following weights:

- $C_1$ (Economic) → $w_1 = 0.4$
- $C_2$ (Technical) → $w_2 = 0.3$
- $C_3$ (Human) → $w_3 = 0.2$
- $C_4$ (Repetition/Bottleneck) → $w_4 = 0.1$

The scores (out of 10) given to each job on each criterion are tabulated below:

| Criterion | Weight $w_i$ | Score for $J_1$ | Score for $J_2$ | Score for $J_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_1$ (Economic) | 0.4 | 7 | 9 | 5 |
| $C_2$ (Technical) | 0.3 | 6 | 8 | 7 |
| $C_3$ (Human) | 0.2 | 5 | 6 | 8 |
| $C_4$ (Repetition) | 0.1 | 9 | 4 | 6 |

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**For Job $J_1$:**

$$
\text{TSS}_1 = (0.4)(7) + (0.3)(6) + (0.2)(5) + (0.1)(9)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_1 = 2.8 + 1.8 + 1.0 + 0.9 = 6.5
$$

**For Job $J_2$:**

$$
