\text{TSS}_2 = (0.4)(9) + (0.3)(8) + (0.2)(6) + (0.1)(4)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_2 = 3.6 + 2.4 + 1.2 + 0.4 = 7.6
$$

**For Job $J_3$:**

$$
\text{TSS}_3 = (0.4)(5) + (0.3)(7) + (0.2)(8) + (0.1)(6)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_3 = 2.0 + 2.1 + 1.6 + 0.6 = 6.3
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
J_{selected} = \arg\max(6.5,\ 7.6,\ 6.3) = J_2
$$

**$\therefore$ Job $J_2$ is selected for method study** (it has the highest weighted score of **7.6**).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How to Use Selection Criteria in Practice

| Scenario in the Plant | Which Criterion Applies | Why it Should be Selected |
|---|---|---|
| A CNC machine is producing 60% rejection | Technical | Quality cost is high, customer complaints rising |
| Workers in painting section report severe headaches | Human | Health hazard; legal liability |
| Loading/unloading takes 2 hours per shift | Economic + Repetition | High time cost, done every shift |
| New product introduced last month | New Process | No standard method exists yet |
| A worker slipped on oil near a machine | Safety/Human | Accident risk; lost-time injury |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 University Exam, a 14-mark question may ask the student to *"Select a suitable job for method study in a given scenario and justify the selection using appropriate criteria."* The above matrix is the answer-template the examiner expects.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Six Basic Steps of Method Study (with Selection Highlighted)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: SELECT the Work to be Studied"]:::sel
    B["Step 2: RECORD the Present Method"]
    C["Step 3: EXAMINE the Facts Critically"]
    D["Step 4: DEVELOP the Improved Method"]
    E["Step 5: INSTALL the New Method as Standard"]
    F["Step 6: MAINTAIN the New Method"]

    A ==> B
    B ==> C
    C ==> D
    D ==> E
    E ==> F
    F -. feedback loop to A .-> A

    classDef sel fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **gold-highlighted block** is the *Selection* step — the topic of this note module. Note the feedback loop: maintenance can reveal new problems, triggering a fresh selection cycle.

### 4.2 Detailed Sub-Procedure of the Selection Step

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SA["List all candidate jobs in the plant"]
    SB["Gather preliminary data on each job"]
    SC["Apply the 8 selection criteria"]
    SD["Use the Selection Rating Sheet to score"]
    SE["Rank jobs by Total Selection Score"]
    SF["Obtain management approval"]
    SG["Begin the study with the selected job"]

    SA --> SB
    SB --> SC
    SC --> SD
    SD --> SE
    SE --> SF
    SF --> SG

    SBsub1["Output per week"]:::data -.-> SB
    SBsub2["Man-hours consumed"]:::data -.-> SB
    SBsub3["Rejection rate"]:::data -.-> SB
    SBsub4["Accident reports"]:::data -.-> SB

    classDef data fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Selection Decision

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP["Input Layer: Plant Data"]:::in
    CRIT["Criterion Layer: Economic / Technical / Human / Repetition / Safety"]:::crit
    WEIGHT["Weight Assignment Layer"]:::wt
    SCORE["Scoring Layer: Score each job 0 to 10"]:::sc
    CALC["Computation Layer: TSS = Sum of w_i x s_i"]:::calc
    OUT["Output Layer: Ranked List of Jobs"]:::out
    PICK["Selection Layer: Pick top-ranked job"]:::pick

    INP --> CRIT --> WEIGHT --> SCORE --> CALC --> OUT --> PICK

    classDef in fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef crit fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef wt fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef sc fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000
    classDef pick fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Decision Logic of the Selection Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{"Does the job consume high cost?"}
    Q2{"Does it have quality problems?"}
    Q3{"Does it cause worker fatigue / hazard?"}
    Q4{"Is it a bottleneck or repeated frequently?"}
    SEL["SELECT this job for method study"]
    HOLD["Hold for next review cycle"]

    Q1 -- Yes --> SEL
    Q1 -- No --> Q2
    Q2 -- Yes --> SEL
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> SEL
    Q3 -- No --> Q4
    Q4 -- Yes --> SEL
    Q4 -- No --> HOLD
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define method study. List the six basic steps of method study in their correct sequence.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Method study is the systematic, critical, and detailed examination of the existing and proposed ways of doing work, with the objective of developing easier, more efficient, and economic methods.

**Six Basic Steps in Sequence (2 Marks):**

1. Select
2. Record
3. Examine
4. Develop
5. Install
6. Maintain

Mnemonic: **"SREDIM"** — *Select, Record, Examine, Develop, Install, Maintain.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State any four criteria used for selecting a job for method study.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Any four of the following (½ mark each):

1. Economic considerations — high labor or material cost.
2. Technical considerations — quality problems or excessive waste.
3. Human considerations — fatigue, accidents, or monotony.
4. Operations forming a bottleneck in production.
5. Operations with high repetition frequency.
6. New products or processes without a standard method.
7. Operations with high customer complaints.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure of selection of a job for method study. List the various selection criteria with examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Three jobs $J_A, J_B, J_C$ are being considered for method study. Using the Selection Rating Sheet data given below, determine which job should be selected. Take weights as: Economic = 0.4, Technical = 0.3, Human = 0.2, Repetition = 0.1. **(7 Marks)**

| Criterion | Score $J_A$ | Score $J_B$ | Score $J_C$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Economic (w=0.4) | 8 | 6 | 7 |
| Technical (w=0.3) | 7 | 9 | 6 |
| Human (w=0.2) | 5 | 7 | 9 |
| Repetition (w=0.1) | 6 | 8 | 5 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure of Selection (7 Marks):**

The selection of a job for method study involves the following sub-stages:

1. **List all jobs/processes** in the department that could potentially be studied.
2. **Collect preliminary data** such as output, man-hours, rejection rate, accident records, and customer complaints.
3. **Apply the selection criteria** — Economic, Technical, Human, Repetition, Bottleneck, Safety, Customer complaint, New process.
4. **Use a Selection Rating Sheet** to score each candidate job against the criteria.
5. **Assign weights** to each criterion based on its relative importance.
6. **Compute the Total Selection Score (TSS)** for each job using the formula:

$$
\text{TSS}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
$$

7. **Rank the jobs** in descending order of TSS.
8. **Obtain management approval** for the top-ranked job.
9. **Begin the detailed method study** on the selected job.

**Examples of selection criteria:**

- *Economic:* A packaging operation consuming 12 man-hours/day.
- *Technical:* A welding operation with 20% rejection.
- *Human:* A painting booth with high VOC exposure.
- *Repetition:* A daily loading/unloading task.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**For Job $J_A$:**

$$
\text{TSS}_A = (0.4)(8) + (0.3)(7) + (0.2)(5) + (0.1)(6)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_A = 3.2 + 2.1 + 1.0 + 0.6 = 6.9
$$

**For Job $J_B$:**

$$
\text{TSS}_B = (0.4)(6) + (0.3)(9) + (0.2)(7) + (0.1)(8)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_B = 2.4 + 2.7 + 1.4 + 0.8 = 7.3
$$

**For Job $J_C$:**

$$
\text{TSS}_C = (0.4)(7) + (0.3)(6) + (0.2)(9) + (0.1)(5)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_C = 2.8 + 1.8 + 1.8 + 0.5 = 6.9
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\arg\max(6.9,\ 7.3,\ 6.9) = J_B
$$

**$\therefore$ Job $J_B$ should be selected** for method study, with the highest Total Selection Score of **7.3**.

---

**Q3B. [Alternative Choice — KTU Model Question]**
**(a)** What is method study? Why is the "Selection" step considered the most critical step in the procedure? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing unit has identified five operations as candidates for method study. Apply the selection rating technique using the criteria and weights provided, and recommend the most suitable job. **(7 Marks)**

| Criterion | Weight | Job P | Job Q | Job R | Job S | Job T |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cost of operation | 0.35 | 9 | 7 | 6 | 8 | 5 |
| Quality of output | 0.25 | 6 | 8 | 9 | 7 | 7 |
| Worker safety | 0.20 | 5 | 9 | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Repetitiveness | 0.20 | 8 | 6 | 7 | 9 | 6 |

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Method Study & Importance of Selection (7 Marks):**

Method study is the systematic recording, examination, and critical analysis of existing and proposed methods of work to develop improved methods that are more economic, easier, and safer.

The **Selection step is the most critical** because:

- It determines **where the analyst's limited time and resources will be spent**.
- A wrong choice wastes months of effort on a low-impact job.
- A correct choice can yield **savings of crores of rupees** for the organization.
- The quality of all subsequent steps (Record, Examine, Develop, Install, Maintain) depends on the quality of the selection.
- It aligns the method study effort with the **strategic priorities** of the organization.

Selection is essentially a **decision-making problem** that uses both qualitative judgment and quantitative scoring.

**(b) Selection Rating Computation (7 Marks):**

**For Job P:**

$$
\text{TSS}_P = (0.35)(9) + (0.25)(6) + (0.20)(5) + (0.20)(8)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_P = 3.15 + 1.50 + 1.00 + 1.60 = 7.25
$$

**For Job Q:**

$$
\text{TSS}_Q = (0.35)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.20)(6)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_Q = 2.45 + 2.00 + 1.80 + 1.20 = 7.45
$$

**For Job R:**

$$
\text{TSS}_R = (0.35)(6) + (0.25)(9) + (0.20)(7) + (0.20)(7)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_R = 2.10 + 2.25 + 1.40 + 1.40 = 7.15
$$

**For Job S:**

$$
\text{TSS}_S = (0.35)(8) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(6) + (0.20)(9)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_S = 2.80 + 1.75 + 1.20 + 1.80 = 7.55
$$

**For Job T:**

$$
\text{TSS}_T = (0.35)(5) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(8) + (0.20)(6)
$$

$$
\text{TSS}_T = 1.75 + 1.75 + 1.60 + 1.20 = 6.30
$$

**Conclusion:**

$$
\arg\max(7.25,\ 7.45,\ 7.15,\ 7.55,\ 6.30) = S
$$

**$\therefore$ Job S should be selected** for method study, with the highest Total Selection Score of **7.55**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **skip the "list all jobs" step** and jump directly to one job — losing 1 mark.
> - In numerical questions, students frequently **forget to multiply by the weight** — leading to wrong TSS values.
> - Many students write the steps of method study but **do not number them in the correct order (SREDIM)** — losing easy marks.
> - The **mnemonic and at least 2 examples** of selection criteria must be mentioned for full marks.
> - Always **state the conclusion explicitly** with the value of TSS and the selected job's name.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Method Study: Selection**

- Method study is a **systematic, critical examination** of work methods.
- The procedure has **6 steps** in the order **SREDIM** (Select, Record, Examine, Develop, Install, Maintain).
- **Selection is the FIRST and most critical step** — wrong selection wastes resources.
- Selection is a **mini-procedure**: list jobs → collect data → apply criteria → score → rank → approve.
- There are **3 main categories of selection criteria**: Economic, Technical, Human (plus auxiliary criteria like Repetition, Bottleneck, Safety, New Process, Customer complaints).
- The **Selection Rating Sheet** uses weights and scores to compute the **Total Selection Score (TSS)**.
- The formula is:

$$
\text{TSS}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j \cdot s_{ij}
$$

- The job with the **maximum TSS** is selected.
- **Management approval** is essential before proceeding to the Record step.
- Selection ensures the analyst's effort is focused on the **highest-payoff** opportunity.
- Real-world analogy: Selection = **Doctor's Triage** — choose the patient who will benefit most from treatment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recording and Representation of Work and Activities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recording and Representation of Work and Activities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2: RECORDING AND REPRESENTATION OF WORK AND ACTIVITIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recording of Work and Activities** is the **second stage** of the **Method Study procedure** (after "Select" and before "Examine"). It involves the **systematic documentation** of the existing method of work by observing, noting, and representing all activities, movements, inspections, delays, and storages performed by men, machines, or materials in their proper sequence, using standardized symbols, charts, diagrams, and motion pictures.

In the **International Labour Organization (ILO) 8-step Method Study framework**, the recording stage ensures that the *current method* is captured with **sufficient accuracy and detail** so that critical examination can be performed later.

### 1.2 Purpose of Recording

1. To obtain a **factual, permanent record** of the present method.
2. To enable the analyst to **critically examine** the work at a later time.
3. To provide a **basis for comparison** with the proposed (improved) method.
4. To assist in **communicating** the method to supervisors, workers, and management.
5. To serve as an **aid to training** new operators.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "A Crime Scene Investigator"** 🔍
> Imagine a detective arriving at a crime scene. Before he can analyze *who* did it or *why*, he must first **meticulously record** the scene — photographs, sketches, measurements, footprints. He cannot rewind the event, so the recording becomes the *only* truth.
>
> Similarly, an Industrial Engineer cannot stop the production line. Work happens continuously. The **charts and diagrams** are the engineer's *photographs* of work. Just as a sketch of a footprint tells a detective the shoe size, an **Operation symbol (O)** on a flow chart tells the IE that *value is being added*. A **Delay symbol (D)** tells them that *time is being wasted*. Without these records, any improvement would be mere guesswork.

### 1.4 Classification of Recording Techniques

The recording techniques are broadly classified into **three major categories**:

| S.No. | Category | Examples |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Charts / Diagrams (Graphical Recording)** | Outline Process Chart, Flow Process Chart (Man/Material/Equipment), Two-Handed Process Chart, Multiple Activity Chart, String Diagram, Travel Chart, Cyclegraph, Chronocyclegraph, SIMO Chart, Man-Machine Chart |
| 2 | **Photographic / Motion Study Recording** | Still Photographs, Motion Pictures (Cine-Films), Video Recordings, Micro-motion Study films |
| 3 | **Diagrammatic Models (3-D)** | Models / Mock-ups of workplace layout, Scale models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 syllabus** places special emphasis on **Process Charts, Two-Handed Process Charts, Multiple Activity Charts, String Diagrams, Cyclegraphs, and Travel Charts** under the recording module.

### 1.5 The Five Standard ANSI Process Chart Symbols

Every chart in work study uses the **5 standard symbols** defined by the **American National Standards Institute (ANSI)**:

| Symbol | Name | Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| `○` | **Operation (O)** | A purposeful change in a workpiece, or a productive activity. | **Value Addition** ✅ |
| `□` | **Inspection (I)** | Checking quality, quantity, or attributes of an item. | Quality verification |
| `→` | **Transport (T)** | Movement of worker, material, or equipment from one place to another. | Wasted effort (no value addition) |
| `D` | **Delay (D)** | Temporary holding of material or worker due to interruption. | Wasted time ⏳ |
| `▽` | **Storage (S)** | Permanent holding of material (cannot be removed without authorization). | Inventory cost |

> [!NOTE]
> A **6th symbol** (a circle inside a square, ⊡) is sometimes used to represent a **Combined Operation and Inspection**. A **7th symbol** (a half-shaded circle) is used in some texts to represent a **combined Op + Transport** activity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theory of Every Recording Technique

### 2.1.1 Outline Process Chart (OPC)

> **Definition:** A chart that gives a **broad, summarized picture** of an entire process using only the **main operations and inspections**, ignoring transport, delay, and storage.

**Key Features:**
- Provides an **overall bird's-eye view** of the whole process.
- Uses **only Operation and Inspection symbols**.
- Ideal for **preliminary study** and at the start of method investigation.
- **Does not show** every detail — only major events.

**When to Use:**
- At the beginning of any method study.
- For very long, multi-stage processes.
- For management presentations where simplicity is key.

> [!NOTE]
> The Outline Process Chart is often called a **"Top Process Chart"** because it sits at the top of detailed sub-charts.

---

### 2.1.2 Flow Process Chart (FPC) — *The Workhorse of Method Study*

> **Definition:** A graphic representation of all events occurring during a process, recorded in the **exact sequence** in which they occur, using **all five process chart symbols**.

**Two Classifications of Flow Process Chart:**

**A) Based on the Subject Being Recorded:**
- **Man/Material Process Chart** — tracks the activities of a *worker*.
- **Material Process Chart** — tracks the *material* as it moves through the process.
- **Equipment/Machine Process Chart** — tracks the *machine* activities.

**B) Based on the Type of Process:**
- **Product Type (Maker)** — the chart is built around **one specific product/item** being made (best for batch production).
- **Process Type (Operator)** — the chart is built around **one worker's activities** (best when one operator handles many products).

**Construction Steps (to be shown later in Section 3):**
1. Define the boundaries: where does the study **start** and **end**?
2. Observe the process carefully.
3. Record each event using the appropriate symbol.
4. Note the time taken (if available) and distance moved.
5. Summarize the totals at the bottom.

**Information to be Recorded in the Chart:**
- Sequence of events
- Symbol used
- Brief description of event
- Time taken (minutes/seconds)
- Distance moved (meters/feet)
- Place/location
- Worker/Operator name

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners **frequently ask** the difference between **Product Type and Process Type** flow charts. Always remember: **Product chart follows the item; Process chart follows the operator**.

---

### 2.1.3 Two-Handed Process Chart (Right-Hand / Left-Hand Chart)

> **Definition:** A chart that records the **simultaneous activities of both hands** (or both limbs) of an operator performing a repetitive task on a single workstation.

**Key Characteristics:**
- **Two columns** — one for the left hand and one for the right hand.
- Activities of both hands are recorded in their **true time relationship** (simultaneous actions appear on the same horizontal line).
- Used for **short-cycle, repetitive operations** (assembly, packaging, etc.).
- Helps identify: **idle time of each hand**, **unbalanced hand motions**, and **excessive reach/move** events.

> [!NOTE]
> The **two-handed process chart is the bridge** between the macro-level flow process chart and the micro-level **micro-motion study (Therbligs)**.

---

### 2.1.4 Multiple Activity Chart (MAC)

> **Definition:** A chart that records the **simultaneous activities of two or more subjects** (workers, machines, or a man-machine combination) on a **common time scale** to analyze the **synchronization and utilization** of each.

**Subject Combinations:**
- **Worker–Worker Chart** (multi-man chart)
- **Man–Machine Chart** (single operator with multiple machines)
- **Multi-Machine Chart** (multiple machines attended by one operator)

**Key Metrics Computed from a MAC:**
- **% Utilization** of each worker / machine
- **Idle time** of each subject
- **Bottleneck operator/machine** identification
- **Optimal number** of machines per operator (for man-machine charts)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Multiple Activity Chart is the only chart** that can answer the question: *"What should be the ideal number of machines assigned to one operator?"* This is a **favourite KTU question**.

---

### 2.1.5 String Diagram

> **Definition:** A **plan-view (top-down, scale drawing)** of the work area on which a **thread or string** is stretched to show the **path of movement** of a worker, material, or vehicle.

**Key Points:**
- Workplace layout is drawn to scale (1:50 or 1:100 typically).
- **Pins or nails** are fixed at locations of activities.
- **Thread or string** connects the pins in the order of movement.
- The **length of the string is then measured** and converted to actual distance.
- Colored strings or different symbols can be used to distinguish workers.

**Uses:**
- Identifies **back-tracking, crisscrossing, and congestion** patterns.
- Useful for **redesigning workplace layout** to minimize travel.
- Particularly valuable when **several workers** share the same area.

**Procedure (detailed steps in Section 3):**
1. Obtain a scale plan of the work area.
2. Identify all activity points visited.
3. Mark these with numbered pins.
4. Connect pins in sequence with a continuous string.
5. Measure the total string length.
6. Convert to actual distance using the scale.

---

### 2.1.6 Travel Chart

> **Definition:** A **matrix (grid) form** of recording movements between locations, where the **rows represent origins** and the **columns represent destinations**, and the **cell entries represent the frequency** (or distance) of movement between those points.

**Layout of a Travel Chart:**

| | Machine 1 | Machine 2 | Inspection | Storage | Total Out |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Machine 1** | — | 5 | 2 | 1 | 8 |
| **Machine 2** | 3 | — | 6 | 2 | 11 |
| **Inspection** | 1 | 4 | — | 3 | 8 |
| **Storage** | 0 | 1 | 2 | — | 3 |
| **Total In** | 4 | 10 | 10 | 6 | **30** |

**Uses:**
- **Plant layout design** — departments with the highest inter-movement must be placed **adjacent**.
- Comparison of two existing layouts.
- The cell with the **highest value** indicates the **most critical pair** to be located together.

> [!NOTE]
> The **diagonal of the travel chart is left blank** (or filled with `—`) because movement from a point to itself is meaningless.

---

### 2.1.7 Cyclegraph and Chronocyclegraph

> **Definition:**
> - **Cyclegraph:** A photographic technique where a **small electric bulb** is attached to a body part (typically the hand or finger), and a **long-exposure photograph** is taken during motion. The light traces the **path** of movement.
> - **Chronocyclegraph:** A **refinement** of the cyclegraph where the light source is **intermittently flickered** at a **constant frequency** (e.g., 10 flickers/second). This produces a **dotted line** where the **spacing between dots indicates speed** — **closer dots = slower motion**, **wider dots = faster motion**.

**Key Features:**
- Provides a **permanent, objective record** of motion paths.
- Reveals **microscopic irregularities** in motion that the human eye cannot detect.
- Particularly useful for **highly repetitive, short-cycle jobs** (e.g., watchmaking, micro-assembly).
- **Three-dimensional chronocyclegraphs** use two bulbs at right angles to capture motion in 3D space.

**Interpreting Dot Density:**
- Dots **close together** → Hand is **moving slowly** (dwell/hesitation).
- Dots **far apart** → Hand is **moving quickly** (free swing/transport empty).
- **Continuous solid line** → Stationary or very fast motion (bunches of dots blur).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **master reference** for any numerical problem on this topic. Memorize the structure and key results.

| S.No. | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | String Diagram Distance | $D_{actual} = L_{string} \times ScaleFactor$ | meters | $L_{string}$ measured in cm, scale = $1 : N$ |
| 2 | Man-Machine Utilization | $\%U_{man} = \dfrac{T_{working}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100$ | % | $T_{cycle}$ = total cycle time |
| 3 | Man-Machine Utilization | $\%U_{machine} = \dfrac{T_{processing}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100$ | % | $T_{processing}$ = actual machining time |
| 4 | Optimum No. of Machines (for an operator) | $N_{opt} = \dfrac{T_{cycle \,(man+1\,machine)}}{T_{cycle \,(machine)}}$ | integer | Round **up** for safety, **down** for cost |
| 5 | Cycle Time of an Operator on $n$ machines | $T_{cycle} = T_{setup} + n \cdot T_{process}$ | min | $T_{setup}$ = per-load setup time |
| 6 | Time Saved (Before–After Study) | $T_{saved} = T_{before} - T_{after}$ | min | Positive value confirms improvement |
| 7 | % Time Saving | $\%S = \dfrac{T_{before} - T_{after}}{T_{before}} \times 100$ | % | Compare with same product/job |
| 8 | % Reduction in Travel | $\%R = \dfrac{D_{old} - D_{new}}{D_{old}} \times 100$ | % | From string diagram studies |
| 9 | Idle Time of Hand | $T_{idle} = T_{cycle} - T_{useful \,activity}$ | min | Per hand, in two-handed chart |
| 10 | Symmetry Index (for two-handed) | $SI = \dfrac{T_{L,useful}}{T_{R,useful}}$ | ratio | Closer to 1 = better balanced |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical bars** are used in the table — the *unit notation* uses **brackets or fraction format** so that markdown table structure remains intact.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Chart / Diagram | Real-World Application |
|:---|:---|
| **Outline Process Chart** | ISO quality manuals, Management review presentations |
| **Flow Process Chart** | Six Sigma DMAIC projects, Lean Manufacturing value-stream mapping |
| **Two-Handed Process Chart** | Ergonomic redesign of assembly lines (automobile, electronics) |
| **Multiple Activity Chart** | CNC cell optimization, operator-to-machine ratio decisions |
| **String Diagram** | Hospital ward layout, warehouse picking routes, supermarket shelf design |
| **Travel Chart** | Factory layout (CRAFT, CORELAP algorithms), office seating plans |
| **Cyclegraph / Chronocyclegraph** | Sports biomechanics, surgical training, micro-soldering analysis |

# 3. Step-by-Step Construction Procedures & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is **not formula-heavy** in the classical sense, but the *construction procedures* and *decision rules* for each chart are themselves **analytical derivations**. We present them as structured step-by-step processes.

## 3.1 Detailed Construction Procedure — Flow Process Chart (Material Type, Product Type)

### Step 1: Define Boundaries
Clearly state:
- **Starting point** of the study (e.g., "Raw material received at stores").
- **Ending point** of the study (e.g., "Finished goods packed and ready for dispatch").

### Step 2: Observe and Time the Process
- Walk through the process **2-3 times** for a general understanding.
- During the actual recording, observe a **typical cycle**.
- Note each event **as it happens** (in real time).

### Step 3: Classify Each Event
For every event, ask:
- Is something being **done** to the material? → **Operation (O)**
- Is the material being **checked**? → **Inspection (I)**
- Is the material **moving** from one place to another? → **Transport (T)**
- Is the material **waiting** temporarily? → **Delay (D)**
- Is the material being **stored** permanently? → **Storage (S)**

### Step 4: Record in Sequence
Use the **standard 5-column format** of the FPC:

| Column 1 | Column 2 | Column 3 | Column 4 | Column 5 |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Symbol | Description of Event | Time (min) | Distance (m) | Place / Location |

### Step 5: Calculate Totals
- **Total operations**, **total inspections**, **total transports**, **total delays**, **total storages**.
- **Total time** = sum of all individual times.
- **Total distance** = sum of all transport distances.

### Step 6: Compute the **% Value-Added Time**

$$
\%V_{added} \;=\; \frac{T_{operations} \,+\, T_{inspections}}{T_{total}} \times 100
$$

> **Interpretation:** Higher %V means the process is more efficient; lower %V means **waste** (delay, transport, storage) is high.

### Step 3.1 — Worked Example: Flow Process Chart for "Filing an Application"

Let us illustrate with a **familiar administrative process** — *A student submitting an internal marks complaint to the college office*.

| S.No. | Symbol | Description | Time (min) | Distance (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $\bigcirc$ | Student writes complaint letter | 10 | — |
| 2 | $\rightarrow$ | Student walks to office | 3 | 80 |
| 3 | $\square$ | Clerk checks the letter format | 2 | — |
| 4 | $\bigcirc$ | Clerk registers complaint in log | 2 | — |
| 5 | $\rightarrow$ | Clerk walks to HOD cabin | 1 | 30 |
| 6 | $\square$ | HOD reviews the complaint | 5 | — |
| 7 | $D$ | Student waits for response | 25 | — |
| 8 | $\rightarrow$ | HOD walks back to office | 1 | 30 |
| 9 | $\bigcirc$ | Clerk prepares reply letter | 3 | — |
| 10 | $\rightarrow$ | Student walks back to classroom | 3 | 80 |
| | | **TOTALS** | **55** | **220** |

**Summary Counts:**
- Operations: **4** (S.No. 1, 4, 6, 9)
- Inspections: **2** (S.No. 3, 6)
- Transports: **4** (S.No. 2, 5, 8, 10)
- Delays: **1** (S.No. 7)
- Storages: **0**

**Calculations:**

$$
\text{Total Time} \;=\; 55 \; \text{min}
$$

$$
\text{Total Distance} \;=\; 220 \; \text{m}
$$

$$
\text{Value-Added Time} \;=\; 10 + 2 + 2 + 5 + 3 \;=\; 22 \; \text{min}
$$

$$
\%V_{added} \;=\; \frac{22}{55} \times 100 \;=\; 40.0\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **%V of only 40%** clearly shows that **60% of the time is non-value-added** (wasted in walking and waiting). This single metric justifies the need for method improvement.

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction — String Diagram

### Step 1 — Obtain Scale Plan
- Get a **floor plan** of the work area.
- Ensure it is drawn to a **known scale** (e.g., $1 \text{ cm} = 1 \text{ m}$, or $1:50$).

### Step 2 — Mark Activity Points
- Number the locations where activities occur: $1, 2, 3, \ldots, n$.
- Use a small **circle with the number** at each location.

### Step 3 — Connect with String
- Fix a **pin or nail** at point $1$.
- Tie the thread and stretch it to point $2$, then $3$, and so on.
- Return to point $1$ to close the loop (if the worker returns).

### Step 4 — Measure String Length
- Carefully remove the string.
- **Measure its length** on a ruler ($L_{string}$ in cm).

### Step 5 — Convert to Actual Distance
Using the scale factor:

$$
D_{actual} \;=\; L_{string} \times \frac{1}{Scale\,Ratio}
$$

> **Example:** If the plan scale is $1:50$ and the measured string is $120 \,\text{cm}$, then:
>
> $$
> D_{actual} \;=\; 120 \times 50 \;=\; 6000 \;\text{cm} \;=\; 60 \;\text{m}
> $$

### Step 6 — Identify Layout Issues
- Look for **back-tracking** (movement reversing direction).
- Look for **crossings** (string passes over itself).
- Look for **excessive long stretches** indicating remote placement.

### Step 7 — Propose Improved Layout
- Rearrange departments/machines and **re-draw** the string diagram.
- Calculate the **new distance** and the **% reduction** in travel.

$$
\%R \;=\; \frac{D_{old} - D_{new}}{D_{old}} \times 100
$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Construction — Two-Handed Process Chart

### Step 1 — Observe the Operator
- Watch the operator perform **several cycles** of the same task.
- Identify the **starting position** (e.g., both hands idle, part on the table).

### Step 2 — Draw the Chart
- Two parallel vertical columns labelled **LEFT HAND** and **RIGHT HAND**.
- Use the 5 process chart symbols (Operation, Transport, Inspection, Hold, Wait).
- **Simultaneous motions are placed on the same horizontal line.**

### Step 3 — Time Each Activity
- Use a **stopwatch** to record time (in minutes or seconds) of each micro-activity.
- Write the time at the right of each symbol.

### Step 4 — Analyze
- Find the **idle periods** (Hold or Wait) for each hand.
- The total time for the right hand and left hand **should be equal** for balance.
- Compute the **Symmetry Index**:

$$
SI \;=\; \frac{T_{L,useful}}{T_{R,useful}}
$$

> **Ideal:** $SI = 1$ (perfectly balanced hands).
>
> **Acceptable range:** $0.85 \le SI \le 1.15$.

### Worked Mini-Example

Imagine an operator picking a bolt with the **right hand** and holding a wrench in the **left hand** (tightening a nut).

| Time (s) | LEFT HAND | RIGHT HAND |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 0–1 | (idle) | O: Pick up bolt |
| 1–2 | D: Hold wrench | →: Move bolt to hole |
| 2–3 | D: Hold wrench | O: Insert bolt |
| 3–5 | O: Tighten nut (2 s) | D: Hold bolt in place |
| 5–6 | →: Place wrench down | →: Release bolt |
| | **Total = 6 s** | **Total = 6 s** |

In this case, $T_L = 6$ s, $T_R = 6$ s, $SI = 1.0$ — **perfectly balanced**.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Construction — Multiple Activity Chart (Man–Machine Type)

### Step 1 — Identify Subjects
- One operator (O) and one or more machines (M1, M2, …).

### Step 2 — Establish Time Scale
- Common horizontal axis: **time in seconds or minutes**.

### Step 3 — Record Activities
For each subject, two states:
- **Working** (active) — drawn as a horizontal bar.
- **Idle** (waiting) — left blank.

### Step 4 — Calculate % Utilization

$$
\%U_{man} \;=\; \frac{\sum T_{working,man}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%U_{machine} \;=\; \frac{\sum T_{working,machine}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100
$$

### Step 5 — Determine Optimum Number of Machines

For an operator attending $n$ identical machines, where:
- $T_m$ = machine processing time per cycle.
- $T_o$ = operator's loading/unloading time per machine per cycle.

**Condition for balanced operation:**

$$
n \cdot T_m \;\ge\; (n - 1) \cdot T_o
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The above condition ensures that the **machine never waits** for the operator. If the operator is too slow, machines will be **idle**, and vice versa.

**Solving for $n$:**

$$
n_{opt} \;=\; \frac{T_o}{T_o - T_m}
$$

> **Rule:** $T_o > T_m$ (operator slower than machine), otherwise $n$ is **infinite** (machine is the bottleneck).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of All Recording Techniques

| Parameter | OPC | FPC | 2-Handed | MAC | String Diagram | Travel Chart | Cyclegraph |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Symbols Used** | 2 (O,I) | 5 (All) | 5 (All) | 2 states (W/I) | None (physical) | None (numeric) | None (photographic) |
| **Time Recorded** | No | Yes | Yes (micro) | Yes | Indirect (distance) | Frequency only | Indirect (dot density) |
| **Distance Recorded** | No | Yes | No | No | Yes (primary) | No | No |
| **Number of Subjects** | Many (sequential) | One (per chart) | Two (hands) | 2 or more | One (per string) | Many (matrix) | One (per bulb) |
| **Best For** | Overview | Whole process | Micro-task | Synchronization | Path analysis | Layout planning | Motion pattern |
| **Output Type** | Summary | Detailed | Detailed | Synchronized | Visual path | Frequency matrix | Photograph |
| **Skill Required** | Low | Medium | High | High | Medium | Medium | Very High |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are framed in **exact KTU 2024 Scheme format**: Part A = 3 marks, Part B = 14 marks with **internal choice** (Option A or Option B).

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between an Outline Process Chart and a Flow Process Chart.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Outline Process Chart | Flow Process Chart |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Records only **main Operations and Inspections**. | Records **all 5 events** — Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, Storage. |
| 2 | Provides a **summary / bird's-eye view** of the process. | Provides a **detailed, comprehensive view** of the process. |
| 3 | **Time and distance are not recorded**. | **Time and distance are recorded** for every event. |
| 4 | Used as a **preliminary** study tool. | Used for **in-depth analysis** of the existing method. |
| 5 | Less symbols, **simpler** to draw. | Uses all 5 standard symbols, **more complex**. |

*(1 mark for each valid point, max 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a Cyclegraph? How is it different from a Chronocyclegraph?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Cyclegraph** is a photographic recording technique in which a **small electric bulb** is attached to a moving body part (typically the hand), and a **long-exposure photograph** is taken. The bulb traces the **path of motion** as a continuous light trail on the photograph. **(1.5 marks)**
- A **Chronocyclegraph** is a **refinement** of the cyclegraph in which the light source is made to **flicker intermittently at a known frequency** (e.g., 10 flashes/second). This produces a **dotted trail**, where the **spacing of dots indicates the speed** of the hand — **closer dots = slower motion**, **wider dots = faster motion**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **two types of Flow Process Charts** (Product Type and Process Type) with a **suitable example** for each. Discuss when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **String Diagram**? Describe in detail the **step-by-step procedure** for constructing a string diagram. Mention any **two advantages** of using a string diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (a) — Flow Process Chart Types (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A Flow Process Chart (FPC) is a graphic record of all events in a process, using the 5 ANSI symbols (O, I, T, D, S), recorded in the exact sequence in which they occur.

**Type 1: Product-Type (Maker) Flow Process Chart (3 marks)**
- The chart is built around **one specific product or item**.
- Every event in the life of **that single product** is recorded — from raw material receipt to final dispatch.
- **Example:** Following *one specific engine piston* through a manufacturing process — forging → machining → grinding → inspection → storage → dispatch.
- The chart is **material-centric**.
- **Best for:** Job-shop or batch production, when a single unit is being tracked.

**Type 2: Process-Type (Operator) Flow Process Chart (3 marks)**
- The chart is built around **one worker or operator's activities** during a fixed time period (e.g., a shift).
- It records everything the operator does, regardless of which product is being made.
- **Example:** Recording what a single assembly-line worker does between 9 AM and 10 AM — picks part A, fits to part B, waits, picks part C, etc.
- The chart is **operator-centric**.
- **Best for:** Mass production, when a single operator handles many products.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A (b) — String Diagram (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A String Diagram is a **scale plan-view of a work area** in which the **path of movement** of a worker, material, or vehicle is traced using a **thread or string** stretched between **pins** marking the activity points.

**Step-by-Step Construction (5 marks):**
1. **Obtain a scale plan** of the work area, drawn to a known ratio (e.g., 1:50).
2. **Identify all activity points** — locations where the worker stops to perform an action.
3. **Mark these points with numbered pins** or stickers (1, 2, 3, …, n).
4. **Tie a thread at point 1** and stretch it to point 2, then 3, …, following the exact sequence of movement.
5. **Return to point 1** (if the worker ends there) to close the loop.
6. **Remove the thread carefully** and measure its total length $L_{string}$ (in cm).
7. **Convert to actual distance** using $D_{actual} = L_{string} \times \text{Scale Factor}$.

**Two Advantages (1 mark):**
1. Provides a **visual, intuitive** picture of movement patterns — easy to communicate.
2. Identifies **back-tracking, crisscrossing, and excessive travel** — guides layout redesign.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Explain the **Two-Handed Process Chart** and the **Multiple Activity Chart** with **suitable diagrams and examples**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is a **Travel Chart**? Explain its **construction** and discuss its **role in plant layout design** with an example. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (a) — Two-Handed & Multiple Activity Charts (7 marks)**

**Two-Handed Process Chart (3.5 marks):**
- It is a chart that **simultaneously records the activities of both hands** of an operator.
- The chart has **two parallel columns** — left hand and right hand.
- **Simultaneous motions are placed on the same horizontal line**.
- It uses the **5 process chart symbols** (O, I, T, D, H — Hold).
- **Example:** A worker picking up a screw with the right hand while holding a screwdriver in the left hand. The right hand's "pick up screw" (O) occurs **at the same time** as the left hand's "grasp screwdriver" (O).
- **Used to:** Identify idle time of each hand, detect unbalanced motions, improve the workplace for both hands.

**Multiple Activity Chart (3.5 marks):**
- It records the **simultaneous activities of two or more subjects** (workers, machines, or man–machine combination) on a **common time scale**.
- Two states are shown for each subject — **Working (W)** as a horizontal bar, and **Idle (I)** as a blank.
- **Example:** One operator tending two lathes. While Lathe-1 is cutting metal (W), the operator attends to Lathe-2 (loading, W), then Lathe-1 finishes, operator unloads Lathe-1 (W), and so on.
- **Used to:** Find the **% utilization** of each subject, identify the **bottleneck**, and determine the **optimum number of machines** per operator.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B (b) — Travel Chart (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A Travel Chart is a **matrix (grid) record** of movements between locations, where **rows are origins, columns are destinations**, and **cells contain the frequency** (or distance) of movement between each pair.

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **List all locations** (machines, departments, stores) as both row and column labels.
2. **Observe movements** over a representative period (e.g., one full shift).
3. **For every movement**, locate the cell at the intersection of origin (row) and destination (column) and **increment the count** by 1 (or add the distance).
4. **Sum each row** = total outgoing movements; **sum each column** = total incoming movements.
5. **Grand total** = total movements in the system.

**Role in Plant Layout Design (3 marks):**
- The travel chart reveals **which pairs of departments interact most frequently**.
- In a new layout, these **high-frequency pairs should be placed adjacent** to each other — minimizing total travel distance.
- It is the **data source** for systematic layout-planning algorithms like **CRAFT (Computerized Relative Allocation of Facilities Technique)**.
- **Example:** If the cell "Machine 1 → Inspection" shows the value **50** (the highest), then in the new layout, **Machine 1 and the Inspection station should be located side by side**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in Recording and Representation questions — and where marks are LOST:**

| Mistake | Mark Loss | Correct Approach |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| Confusing **Outline Process Chart** with **Flow Process Chart** symbols (using only 2 in FPC). | -1 to -2 marks | FPC **must use all 5** symbols. |
| Writing **Travel Chart values on the diagonal** (movement of a point to itself). | -1 mark | Diagonal is always **blank/dash**. |
| Saying **Chronocyclegraph** uses a *continuous* light (this is wrong — it is *intermittent*). | -2 marks | Cyclegraph = continuous trail; Chronocyclegraph = dotted trail. |
| Forgetting to **state the scale** when solving a string diagram problem. | -1 mark | Always state: *"Plan scale = 1:50"* before computing distance. |
| Computing **% Utilization** without using the **correct cycle time** (using one subject's working time as cycle). | -2 marks | Cycle time is the **total elapsed time** from start to end of the cycle, not the working time of one subject. |
| Mixing up **Product Type** and **Process Type** flow charts in the definition. | -1.5 marks | Product = follows the **item**; Process = follows the **operator**. |
| In **Two-Handed Chart**, drawing both hands' activities **sequentially** (one below the other), forgetting simultaneity. | -2 marks | Use a **common time scale**; simultaneous actions on the **same horizontal line**. |
| Forgetting to **measure time** in flow process chart problems. | -1 mark | FPC is **not** just symbols — it includes **time and distance columns**. |

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must-Know Bullet Points:**

- **Recording** is the **2nd step** of Method Study (after Select, before Examine).
- **5 ANSI Symbols:** Operation (○), Inspection (□), Transport (→), Delay (D), Storage (▽).
- **Outline Process Chart** = bird's-eye view, **2 symbols only** (O and I).
- **Flow Process Chart** = detailed, **all 5 symbols**, records **time and distance**.
- **Product Type FPC** follows the **item**; **Process Type FPC** follows the **operator**.
- **Two-Handed Process Chart** = simultaneous left and right hand activities on a common time scale.
- **Multiple Activity Chart** = multiple subjects (workers/machines) on a common time scale; uses bars (working) and blanks (idle).
- **String Diagram** = scale floor plan + string for path; **distance** = string length × scale factor.
- **Travel Chart** = matrix of movements; **diagonal is blank**; high-frequency pairs must be adjacent in layout.
- **Cyclegraph** = long-exposure photograph with a **continuous light**; shows motion **path**.
- **Chronocyclegraph** = **flickering light** at known frequency; shows motion **path + speed** (dot density).
- **% Value-Added Time** = (Operation + Inspection time) / Total Time × 100.
- **% Utilization** of a subject = (Working time) / (Cycle time) × 100.
- **Optimum machines for an operator:** $n_{opt} = T_o / (T_o - T_m)$ (only valid if $T_o > T_m$).
- **Symmetry Index** for two-handed chart = $T_{L,useful} / T_{R,useful}$; ideal = 1.0.
- **String diagram steps:** Scale plan → mark activity points → connect with string → measure → convert to actual distance → identify issues → propose new layout.
- **Travel chart** is the **primary data source** for **CRAFT and CORELAP** layout algorithms.
- **Three categories of recording:** Charts/Diagrams, Photographic (Cine/Video), 3-D Scale Models.
- **Photographic methods** are best for **highly repetitive, short-cycle** jobs; **charts** are best for **longer processes**.
- **SIMO Chart** = Simultaneous Motion Chart — extends two-handed chart to **other body parts** (eyes, legs, trunk).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Critical Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Critical Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Analysis (in Industrial Engineering context):** A systematic quantitative technique used in **Project Management** to plan, schedule, coordinate, and control the sequence of activities in a project. It is the mathematical backbone of the **Critical Path Method (CPM)** and **Project Evaluation and Review Technique (PERT)**. Critical Analysis identifies the **longest path of dependent activities** (the *Critical Path*) that determines the minimum project completion time — the project's **makespan**. In formal terms, **Critical Analysis** is the discipline of: - Modeling a project as a **Network of Activities** (nodes/arrows). - Computing the **Earliest Start Time (EST)**, **Earliest Finish Time (EFT)**, **Latest Start Time (LST)**, **Latest Finish Time (LFT)** for every activity. - Calculating the **Total Float (TF)**, **Free Float (FF)**, and **Independent Float (IF)**. - Identifying the **Critical Path(s)** — those activities where TF = 0, meaning any delay directly delays the entire project. The standard metrics used are: - **Critical Path (CP)** — the longest-duration path through the network. - **Project Duration (T_p)** — sum of durations of all activities on the critical path. - **Float/Slack** — the amount of time a non-critical activity can be delayed without affecting project completion. - **Crash Cost (C_c)** and **Crash Time (T_c)** — used during *crashing analysis* to compress project duration at minimum cost. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy — "Planning a Wedding":** > Imagine you are planning a wedding. You must book the hall, order the cake, send invitations, arrange the photographer, and finalize the dress. Some tasks must happen *in order* (you cannot send invitations before fixing the wedding date). Some tasks can happen *in parallel* (the cake can be ordered while the photographer is being arranged). > > The **Critical Path** is the chain of tasks where *no waiting is allowed*. If any task on this chain slips by even one day, the entire wedding gets postponed. Other tasks have **float** — they can wait a few days without affecting the final day. > > **Critical Analysis** is the science of finding that all-important chain and the safe "waiting room" for every other task. Another analogy: building a house. You cannot plaster walls before laying bricks, but you can paint the doors while the electrician wires the rooms. The plastering-brick sequence is on the **critical path**. --- ## 1.3 Why Critical Analysis Matters in Industrial Engineering | Domain | Application of Critical Analysis | |---|---| | **Construction Industry** | Schedule high-rise buildings, highways, bridges. | | **Manufacturing** | Plan new product launches, plant shutdowns for maintenance. | | **Shipbuilding / Aerospace** | Coordinate thousands of components and subsystems. | | **R&D Projects** | Manage new drug development, software releases. | | **Defense / Government** | Schedule procurement, commissioning of large systems. | **Key Standard Metric:** *Activity Slack / Float* measured in **days, weeks, or hours** depending on project scale. The **Project Duration (T_p)** is measured in the same units as activity durations. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Building Blocks of a Project Network Every project can be broken down into: - **Activity (A_i):** A task that consumes time and resources. - **Event / Node:** A milestone marking the start or completion of an activity. - **Dummy Activity:** A logical connector with **zero duration** and **zero cost**, used to express dependencies when two arrows would otherwise share the same start and end nodes (in AOA representation). Two representations exist: - **AOA (Activity on Arrow / Arrow Diagram):** Arrows = activities, Nodes = events. - **AON (Activity on Node / Precedence Diagram):** Nodes = activities, Arrows = logical precedence. > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **AOA method** traditionally, though AON is also accepted. The convention below uses AOA. --- ## 2.2 Network Construction Rules (AOA) 1. Each **activity** is represented by a single unique arrow. 2. **Nodes** are numbered so that the arrow always points from a *lower* number to a *higher* number ($i \rightarrow j$, where $i < j$). This is called the **Fulkerson's Rule**. 3. **No two activities** can have the same start and end nodes — use **dummy activities** to resolve this. 4. **Network should not have loops.** 5. There should be only **one START node** and **one END node** (use dummies if required). --- ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logical Procedure for Critical Analysis 1. **List all activities**, their **predecessors**, and **durations**. 2. **Draw the network** using AOA conventions. 3. **Number the nodes** following Fulkerson's Rule. 4. **Forward Pass** — compute EST and EFT for every node and activity. 5. **Backward Pass** — compute LFT and LST for every node and activity. 6. **Compute Floats** — Total, Free, Independent. 7. **Identify the Critical Path** — activities with **Total Float = 0**. 8. **Compute Project Duration (T_p)** — total time of the critical path. 9. *(Optional)* **Crashing analysis** to compress T_p at minimum additional cost. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Earliest Finish Time of Activity $(i,j)$ | $EFT_{ij} = EST_i + t_{ij}$ | $t_{ij}$ = duration of activity $(i,j)$ | | 2 | Earliest Start Time of Node $j$ | $EST_j = \max(EST_i + t_{ij})$ over all paths reaching $j$ | Longest path into node $j$ | | 3 | Latest Finish Time of Node $i$ | $LFT_i = \min(LFT_j - t_{ij})$ over all outgoing paths | Shortest reverse path from $i$ to end | | 4 | Latest Start Time of Activity $(i,j)$ | $LST_{ij} = LFT_j - t_{ij}$ | Latest the activity can begin | | 5 | Project Duration | $T_p = EST_{\text{end}} = LFT_{\text{start}}$ | Length of critical path | | 6 | Total Float (TF) | $TF_{ij} = LST_{ij} - EST_{ij}$ | Maximum delay without delaying project | | 7 | Total Float (alt form) | $TF_{ij} = LFT_j - EST_i - t_{ij}$ | Same as above | | 8 | Free Float (FF) | $FF_{ij} = EST_j - EFT_{ij}$ | Delay without delaying any successor | | 9 | Independent Float (IF) | $IF_{ij} = \max(0,\, EST_j - LFT_i - t_{ij})$ | Delay without delaying either side | | 10 | Crash Cost Slope | $S = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$ | $C_c$ = crash cost, $C_n$ = normal cost, $T_n$ = normal time, $T_c$ = crash time | | 11 | Optimal Crashing | Crash activity with **lowest positive cost slope** on critical path | Minimize $\sum S \cdot \Delta T$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Path Identification Rule:** An activity is **critical if and only if its Total Float = 0**. The critical path is the chain of such activities connecting START to END nodes. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Construction of flyovers and metro lines** in India: Every KTU civil graduate will encounter CPM charts on-site. - **Shipbuilding in Cochin Shipyard:** Thousands of sub-assemblies coordinated using PERT/CPM. - **Plant maintenance shutdowns:** Critical Analysis tells the maintenance engineer *exactly* which equipment to fix first to minimize downtime. - **Software product launches:** Used in agile-scrum hybrids for sprint planning. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Numerical Example — Worked-Out from Scratch > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem Statement:** A small project consists of 7 activities A, B, C, D, E, F, G with the following logical relationships and durations (in days): | Activity | Duration (days) | Predecessors | |---|---|---| | A | 4 | — | | B | 3 | A | | C | 2 | A | | D | 5 | B | | E | 6 | C | | F | 4 | D, E | | G | 3 | F | ### Step 1: Construct the AOA Network ``` START ──A──> (1) ──B──> (2) ──D──> (4) ──F──> (5) ──G──> END │ │ ↑ C │ │ ↓ │ │ (3) ──E─────────┘ (dummy 4→3 not needed; reversed) ``` To avoid two activities starting and ending at the same event (B and C both start at node 1), we insert a **dummy** from node 2 → node 3. This ensures D depends on B but not on C, while F depends on both D and E. ### Step 2: Number the Nodes (Fulkerson's Rule: $i < j$) | Node Number | Event Description | |---|---| | 1 | Start (after A) | | 2 | After B | | 3 | After C | | 4 | After D, after E (with dummy 2→3) | | 5 | After F | | 6 | After G → END | ### Step 3: Forward Pass — Compute $EST_i$ for Every Node The rule is: $$EST_j = \max_{i \rightarrow j} (EST_i + t_{ij})$$ **Node 1 (after A):** $$EST_1 = 0 + 4 = 4$$ **Node 2 (after B):** $$EST_2 = EST_1 + t_B = 4 + 3 = 7$$ **Node 3 (after C):** $$EST_3 = EST_1 + t_C = 4 + 2 = 6$$ **Node 4 (after D, E, and dummy):** - Via D: $EST_2 + t_D = 7 + 5 = 12$ - Via E: $EST_3 + t_E = 6 + 6 = 12$ - Via dummy (2→3 is in reverse, so dummy goes 3→4? Let's fix: dummy is from node 3 to node 4 to ensure F waits for E. Wait — actually dummy should connect B's tail to C's head so that D depends only on B. Let me redo.) **Corrected network with dummy:** Dummy activity goes from **node 3 → node 2** (so D depends on B but E depends only on C, while F depends on both D and E at node 4). $$EST_4 = \max(EST_2 + t_D,\; EST_3 + t_E) = \max(7+5,\; 6+6) = \max(12, 12) = 12$$ **Node 5 (after F):** $$EST_5 = EST_4 + t_F = 12 + 4 = 16$$ **Node 6 / END (after G):** $$EST_6 = EST_5 + t_G = 16 + 3 = 19$$ So the **Project Duration = 19 days**. ### Step 4: Backward Pass — Compute $LFT_i$ for Every Node The rule is: $$LFT_i = \min_{i \rightarrow j} (LFT_j - t_{ij})$$ **Node 6 (END):** $$LFT_6 = EST_6 = 19$$ **Node 5 (before G):** $$LFT_5 = LFT_6 - t_G = 19 - 3 = 16$$ **Node 4 (before F):** $$LFT_4 = LFT_5 - t_F = 16 - 4 = 12$$ **Node 3 (before E):** $$LFT_3 = LFT_4 - t_E = 12 - 6 = 6$$ **Node 2 (before D, also has dummy out):** $$LFT_2 = \min(LFT_4 - t_D) = \min(12 - 5) = 7$$ **Node 1 (before B, C):** $$LFT_1 = \min(LFT_2 - t_B,\; LFT_3 - t_C) = \min(7 - 3,\; 6 - 2) = \min(4, 4) = 4$$ ### Step 5: Compute EST, EFT, LST, LFT and Floats for Each Activity | Activity | Duration | EST | EFT | LST | LFT | Total Float | Free Float | Independent Float | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | A (1,1') | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | B (1',2) | 3 | 4 | 7 | 4 | 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | C (1',3) | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4 | 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | D (2,4) | 5 | 7 | 12 | 7 | 12 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | E (3,4) | 6 | 6 | 12 | 6 | 12 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | F (4,5) | 4 | 12 | 16 | 12 | 16 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | G (5,6) | 3 | 16 | 19 | 16 | 19 | 0 | 0 | 0 | ### Step 6: Identify Critical Path Since **every activity has Total Float = 0**, every activity is critical. The critical paths are: $$A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow F \rightarrow G \quad (4+3+5+4+3 = 19)$$ $$A \rightarrow C \rightarrow E \rightarrow F \rightarrow G \quad (4+2+6+4+3 = 19)$$ Both paths give 19 days, confirming the project duration. --- ## 3.2 Crashing Analysis — Step-by-Step Derivation Suppose the manager wants to **reduce the project from 19 days to 14 days**. | Activity | Normal Time (T_n) | Crash Time (T_c) | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Cost (₹) | Cost Slope (₹/day) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | A | 4 | 3 | 1000 | 1500 | 500 | | B | 3 | 2 | 800 | 1200 | 400 | | C | 2 | 1 | 600 | 900 | 300 | | D | 5 | 3 | 1500 | 2500 | 500 | | E | 6 | 4 | 1800 | 2600 | 400 | | F | 4 | 2 | 1200 | 2000 | 400 | | G | 3 | 2 | 900 | 1300 | 400 | **Cost Slope Formula:** $$S = \dfrac{C_c - C_n}{T_n - T_c}$$ For activity E: $$S_E = \dfrac{2600 - 1800}{6 - 4} = \dfrac{800}{2} = 400 \text{ ₹/day}$$ ### Step-by-Step Crashing Logic 1. **Current duration = 19 days.** Both paths A→B→D→F→G and A→C→E→F→G are critical. 2. **To crash 1 day**, must reduce *both* critical paths simultaneously (or a common activity). 3. **Common activities:** A, F, G. Cheapest to crash among them = G (₹400/day) or F (₹400/day) or A (₹500/day). 4. **Crash G by 1 day (19 → 18):** Cost = ₹400. 5. Now we need to reduce both paths. Pick cheapest cost-slope activity on each path: - Path 1 (A-B-D-F-G): cheapest among B(400), D(500), F(400), A(500), G(already crashed). - Path 2 (A-C-E-F-G): cheapest among C(300), E(400), F(400), A(500), G(already crashed). 6. Crash C by 1 day (Path 2 saves 1 day, Path 2 becomes 17). Cost = ₹300. 7. Now Path 1 is still 17 days (was 17 after G crash). Path 2 is now 17. Project = 17 days. Total cost of crashing so far = ₹700. 8. Continue iteratively until target reached (14 days). > [!NOTE] > This is the **Minimum Cost Project Schedule** approach — the heart of CPM crashing analysis that KTU examiners love testing. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 AOA Network Diagram of the Worked Example ```mermaid graph LR startNode((START / 0)) nodeA((1)) nodeB((2)) nodeC((3)) nodeD((4)) nodeE((5)) endNode((END / 6)) startNode -- "A
t=4" --> nodeA nodeA -- "B
t=3" --> nodeB nodeA -- "C
t=2" --> nodeC nodeC -. "DUMMY
t=0" .-> nodeB nodeB -- "D
t=5" --> nodeD nodeC -- "E
t=6" --> nodeD nodeD -- "F
t=4" --> nodeE nodeE -- "G
t=3" --> endNode style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > In this example, all activity nodes are highlighted (gold) because **all activities are critical**. A dummy activity (dashed arrow) connects node 3 to node 2 to enforce logical precedence without double-counting. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Critical Analysis Process ```mermaid flowchart TD inputBlock["Project Scope
and Activity List"] predBlock["Predecessor
Matrix"] durationBlock["Activity
Durations"] networkBlock["Construct AOA
Network"] numberingBlock["Fulkerson's Node
Numbering"] forwardBlock["FORWARD PASS
EST, EFT"] backwardBlock["BACKWARD PASS
LST, LFT"] floatBlock["Compute Floats
TF, FF, IF"] critBlock["Identify
Critical Path"] durationOut["Project Duration Tp"] crashBlock["Crashing
Analysis"] optimalBlock["Optimal Cost-Time
Schedule"] inputBlock --> networkBlock predBlock --> networkBlock durationBlock --> networkBlock networkBlock --> numberingBlock numberingBlock --> forwardBlock numberingBlock --> backwardBlock forwardBlock --> floatBlock backwardBlock --> floatBlock floatBlock --> critBlock critBlock --> durationOut durationOut --> crashBlock crashBlock --> optimalBlock style critBlock fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF,stroke-width:2px style optimalBlock fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** The red block ("Identify Critical Path") is the *epicenter* of Critical Analysis. The green block ("Optimal Cost-Time Schedule") is the **final deliverable** that management uses to make real-world decisions on resource allocation. --- ## 4.3 Critical Path Identification Logic Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR actBlock["Activity i,j"] estStart["EST_i"] eftOut["EFT = EST + t"] lstCalc["LST = LFT - t"] tfCalc["TF = LST - EST"] checkTF{"TF = 0 ?"} critYes["CRITICAL
Highlight Path"] critNo["Non-Critical
Has Float = TF days"] actBlock --> estStart estStart --> eftOut actBlock --> lstCalc eftOut --> tfCalc lstCalc --> tfCalc tfCalc --> checkTF checkTF -- YES --> critYes checkTF -- NO --> critNo ``` > [!NOTE] > Every activity flows through the same arithmetic. The decision diamond (TF = 0?) is what *separates* critical from non-critical activities. This binary logic is the very soul of Critical Analysis. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** ### **Q1. Define Critical Path. What is its significance in project management? (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The Critical Path is the **longest sequence of activities** in a project network that determines the **minimum time required to complete the project**. Activities on the critical path have **Total Float = 0**, meaning any delay in them directly delays the entire project. **Significance:** 1. Determines the **minimum project completion time** (makespan). 2. Helps the manager **focus monitoring efforts** on critical activities. 3. Forms the basis for **crashing decisions** — to compress schedule, only critical activities can be shortened. 4. Provides the **baseline schedule** against which actual progress is compared. --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between Total Float and Free Float. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Total Float (TF) | Free Float (FF) | |---|---|---| | Definition | Maximum delay without delaying **project completion** | Maximum delay without delaying **any successor activity** | | Formula | $TF_{ij} = LST_{ij} - EST_{ij}$ | $FF_{ij} = EST_j - EFT_{ij}$ | | Scope | Project-level | Activity-level (successors) | | Larger or Smaller | Always $\geq$ Free Float | Always $\leq$ Total Float | If TF > 0, the activity has spare capacity. If FF > 0 but TF = 0, the activity is *partially* flexible. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question Paper Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **A construction project consists of the following activities. Draw the network, compute EST, LST, EFT, LFT, find the critical path and project duration.** (14 Marks) | Activity | Predecessors | Duration (days) | |---|---|---| | A | — | 5 | | B | A | 6 | | C | A | 7 | | D | B | 4 | | E | C | 5 | | F | D | 6 | | G | E | 4 | | H | F, G | 3 | #### **Part (a): Network Construction and Forward Pass (7 Marks)** **Solution:** **Step 1 — AOA Network (with dummy to resolve B & C parallel start):** Nodes numbered using Fulkerson's rule (start = 1). **Step 2 — Forward Pass Calculations:** $$EST_1 = 0$$ $$EST_2 = EST_1 + t_A = 0 + 5 = 5$$ $$EST_3 = EST_2 + t_B = 5 + 6 = 11$$ $$EST_4 = EST_2 + t_C = 5 + 7 = 12 \quad \text{(via dummy 3→4?)}$$ To enforce F→(waits for D) and G→(waits for E), use dummy from node 3 to node 4 so that H waits for both F and G. $$EST_5 = EST_3 + t_D = 11 + 4 = 15$$ $$EST_6 = EST_4 + t_E = 12 + 5 = 17$$ $$EST_7 = EST_5 + t_F = 15 + 6 = 21$$ $$EST_8 = EST_6 + t_G = 17 + 4 = 21$$ $$EST_9 = \max(EST_7 + t_H,\; EST_8 + t_H) = \max(21+3,\; 21+3) = 24$$ $$\boxed{T_p = 24 \text{ days}}$$ #### **Part (b): Backward Pass and Critical Path (7 Marks)** **Backward Pass:** $$LFT_9 = 24$$ $$LFT_8 = LFT_9 - t_H = 24 - 3 = 21$$ $$LFT_7 = LFT_9 - t_H = 24 - 3 = 21$$ $$LFT_6 = LFT_8 - t_G = 21 - 4 = 17$$ $$LFT_5 = LFT_7 - t_F = 21 - 6 = 15$$ $$LFT_4 = LFT_6 - t_E = 17 - 5 = 12$$ $$LFT_3 = LFT_5 - t_D = 15 - 4 = 11$$ $$LFT_2 = \min(LFT_3 - t_B,\; LFT_4 - t_C) = \min(11 - 6,\; 12 - 7) = \min(5, 5) = 5$$ **Critical Activities (TF = 0):** A, B, D, F, H **and** A, C, E, G, H **Critical Paths:** $$\text{Path 1: } A \rightarrow B \rightarrow D \rightarrow F \rightarrow H = 5 + 6 + 4 + 6 + 3 = 24 \text{ days}$$ $$\text{Path 2: } A \rightarrow C \rightarrow E \rightarrow G \rightarrow H = 5 + 7 + 5 + 4 + 3 = 24 \text{ days}$$ Both are critical and equal to the project duration. **Project Duration = 24 days.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **The following data pertains to a project. The management wants to crash the project duration. Compute the optimal cost-time schedule using the cost slope method.** (14 Marks) | Activity | Normal Time | Crash Time | Normal Cost (₹) | Crash Cost (₹) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1-2 | 6 | 4 | 800 | 1400 | | 2-3 | 4 | 3 | 600 | 900 | | 2-4 | 8 | 6 | 1200 | 1800 | | 3-4 | 5 | 3 | 700 | 1100 | | 4-5 | 7 | 5 | 1000 | 1600 | #### **Part (a): Construct the Network and Identify the Critical Path at Normal Duration (7 Marks)** **Solution:** $$EST_1 = 0$$ $$EST_2 = 0 + 6 = 6$$ $$EST_3 = EST_2 + t_{2,3} = 6 + 4 = 10$$ $$EST_4 = \max(EST_2 + t_{2,4},\; EST_3 + t_{3,4}) = \max(6+8,\; 10+5) = \max(14, 15) = 15$$ $$EST_5 = EST_4 + t_{4,5} = 15 + 7 = 22$$ $$T_p = 22 \text{ days at normal duration.}$$ **Critical Path at normal duration:** $1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 5$ (via path 2-3-4 since $10+5=15 > 6+8=14$). #### **Part (b): Compute Cost Slopes and Crash the Project Iteratively (7 Marks)** **Cost Slope Table:** $$S_{1,2} = \dfrac{1400 - 800}{6 - 4} = 300 \text{ ₹/day}$$ $$S_{2,3} = \dfrac{900 - 600}{4 - 3} = 300 \text{ ₹/day}$$ $$S_{2,4} = \dfrac{1800 - 1200}{8 - 6} = 300 \text{ ₹/day}$$ $$S_{3,4} = \dfrac{1100 - 700}{5 - 3} = 200 \text{ ₹/day}$$ $$S_{4,5} = \dfrac{1600 - 1000}{7 - 5} = 300 \text{ ₹/day}$$ **Crashing Iteration 1 — Crash $(3,4)$ by 1 day (cheapest on critical path, ₹200):** - $T_{3,4}$ goes from 5 → 4. - New $EST_4 = \max(6+8, 10+4) = \max(14, 14) = 14$. - Both paths are now critical (Path 1: 1-2-4-5 = 6+8+7 = 21; Path 2: 1-2-3-4-5 = 6+4+4+7 = 21). - $T_p = 21$ days. Extra cost = ₹200. **Crashing Iteration 2 — Now both paths critical. Crash (3,4) by 1 more day AND a path-1 activity by 1 day:** - (3,4) already at 4, can crash 1 more day → 3. Cost ₹200. - Path 1 has (2,4) and (4,5); both at ₹300. Pick (2,4): 8 → 7. Cost ₹300. - New durations: $T_{3,4}=3, T_{2,4}=7$. Path 1: 6+7+7 = 20. Path 2: 6+4+3+7 = 20. - $T_p = 20$ days. Extra cost = ₹500. **Cumulative = ₹700.** **Continue similarly** until crash limits are reached or desired project duration is achieved. **Final Optimal Schedule:** Target duration and incremental crashing cost are computed iteratively — the KTU answer should tabulate each iteration showing the activity crashed, the cost incurred, the new project duration, and the cumulative cost. The project is **fully crashed** when no further compression is possible without violating crash-time limits. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting that once you crash one critical path, you must crash the *other* parallel critical path too — otherwise the project doesn't compress. Students lose 3–4 marks here. > 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Using the wrong sign in the cost-slope formula. The correct formula is $S = (C_c - C_n) / (T_n - T_c)$, *not* the inverse. > 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing **Free Float** with **Total Float** in 14-mark questions — they are NOT the same. Always write both formulas explicitly. > 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Drawing a network without a dummy when two activities share the same start and end events. This causes logical errors that examiners immediately detect. > 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to check that a chosen activity for crashing is still within its **crash limit** ($T_{current} \geq T_{crash}$). Examiners deduct 2 marks for crashing beyond the limit. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Critical Path = Longest Path** through the project network. - **Total Float = 0** ⟹ Activity is on the critical path. - **Free Float** is *less than or equal to* Total Float — always. - **Independent Float** can be zero or positive, never negative. - **Forward Pass** uses **MAX** (we want the longest time to reach a node). - **Backward Pass** uses **MIN** (we want the latest start that still finishes the project on time). - **Dummy Activity** has **zero duration, zero cost**, used only for logical dependency in AOA diagrams. - **Fulkerson's Rule:** $i < j$ for every activity arrow $(i, j)$ — keeps numbering consistent. - **Cost Slope** units: **₹ (or $) per unit time (day/week)**. - **Crashing Rule:** Always pick the activity with the **lowest positive cost slope on the critical path**. - **When multiple critical paths exist**, you must crash an activity on *each* path (or a common one) to reduce $T_p$. - **Project Duration** = Sum of durations along *any* critical path = $EST$ of end node = $LFT$ of start node. - **EST** of the start node is always **0**. - **LFT** of the end node always equals **$T_p$**. - **PERT** assumes probabilistic activity times; **CPM** assumes deterministic times — for KTU, focus on CPM with single-time estimates unless the question explicitly mentions three-time estimates. - **Network must not have cycles/loops** — this is a fatal error. - **Common formulas to memorize (verbatim):** * $EFT_{ij} = EST_i + t_{ij}$ * $LST_{ij} = LFT_j - t_{ij}$ * $TF_{ij} = LST_{ij} - EST_{ij} = LFT_j - EFT_{ij}$ * $FF_{ij} = EST_j - EFT_{ij}$ * $S = (C_c - C_n) / (T_n - T_c)$

topics.title: Developing Best Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Developing Best Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's Industrial Engineering syllabus (Work Study domain), **Method Study** is a systematic technique for the critical examination of the methods used in performing a job, with the objective of developing the most efficient and economical method. The technique follows a standardized six-step procedure prescribed by the International Labour Organization (ILO).

**"Developing the Best Method"** refers specifically to **Step 4 of the Method Study Procedure**. After the existing method has been recorded (Step 2) and critically examined (Step 3), the Industrial Engineer applies creative and analytical techniques to generate, evaluate, and select a superior alternative method of performing the work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 2 - Method Study):**
> The official six-step procedure is:
> **Select → Record → Examine → Develop → Define → Install & Maintain**
> "Develop" is the creative-synthesis stage where innovation is converted into a practical improvement plan.

## 1.2 The Six-Step Method Study Procedure (Recap)

| Step | Name | Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Select** | Identify the job to be studied based on economic importance, frequency, and scope for improvement. |
| 2 | **Record** | Document the current method using charts (Outline Process Chart, Flow Process Chart, Two-Handed Process Chart, Multiple Activity Chart, Flow Diagram, String Diagram, Cyclegraph, Chronocyclegraph). |
| 3 | **Examine** | Critically examine the recorded facts using the *Questioning Technique* and *Challenge Technique*. |
| 4 | **Develop** ⭐ | Generate improved alternatives and select the best practical method. **(Current Topic)** |
| 5 | **Define** | Present the proposed method clearly so it can be easily understood. |
| 6 | **Install & Maintain** | Implement the new method and sustain improvements through standardization and training. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Kitchen Renovation" Analogy:**
> Imagine your mother has been making tea in a cluttered kitchen for 20 years using a fixed routine. You secretly record (by video) every step she takes. After watching, you critically *examine* the recording and notice she walks back and forth to the sugar jar 4 times, opens 3 drawers unnecessarily, and uses two vessels where one would do. Now, in the **"Develop"** step, you sit with a blank sheet and brainstorm: *"What if I move the sugar near the stove? What if I use a single vessel? What if I combine boiling and pouring?"* — You then pick the most **practical**, **safe**, and **economical** combination. That entire brainstorming-and-selection process is "Developing the Best Method" in Industrial Engineering terms.

The heart of Step 4 is the application of a creative questioning framework that forces the analyst to challenge every existing detail and synthesize a new workflow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Philosophy: The ECRS Principle

The most universally applied framework for developing a new method is the **ECRS Principle** (sometimes called the *4-Stage Improvement Cycle*). It provides four logical operations that can be applied to any activity, process, or motion.

| Letter | Operation | Objective | KTU-Exam Keyword |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **E** | **Eliminate** | Remove the activity completely if it adds no value. | *"Can we do without it?"* |
| **C** | **Combine** | Merge two or more activities into one (e.g., two-handed vs one-handed work). | *"Can it be merged with another?"* |
| **R** | **Rearrange (Re-sequence)** | Change the order of activities to improve flow. | *"Can the order be changed?"* |
| **S** | **Simplify** | Make the remaining work easier to perform (better tools, ergonomics). | *"Can it be made easier?"* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The ECRS sequence is **NOT arbitrary**. Elimination must always be attempted **first** because combining, rearranging, or simplifying an unnecessary activity is wasteful. KTU questions frequently ask: *"Why is Eliminate placed first in the ECRS sequence?"*

## 2.2 Techniques Used for Developing the Best Method

The Industrial Engineer does not rely on a single tool. Five major techniques are used in KTU-aligned Industrial Engineering problems:

### Technique 1 — The Creative Questioning Technique (Challenge Technique)
A structured set of questions applied to every recorded activity. The two-question word for each activity is **"What?"** and **"Why?"** — supplemented by *How, When, Where,* and *Who*.

The standard 5-W and 1-H challenge model used in KTU answer scripts:

$$\text{Why Challenge} = \{\text{What is done?}, \text{Why is it done?}, \text{Where is it done?}, \text{When is it done?}, \text{Who does it?}, \text{How is it done?}, \text{How much does it cost?}\}$$

### Technique 2 — Brainstorming
A group creativity session where a team of supervisors, operators, and engineers generate as many improvement ideas as possible. The four cardinal rules of brainstorming (Alex Osborn) are:

1. **No criticism** of ideas.
2. **Freewheeling** — wild ideas are welcome.
3. **Quantity** — the more ideas, the better.
4. **Combination and improvement** of ideas is encouraged.

### Technique 3 — Check Lists
Pre-prepared lists of standard improvement prompts (e.g., *workplace layout, material handling, tools used, working conditions*) used by the analyst to trigger ideas systematically.

### Technique 4 — Application of Principles of Motion Economy
Gilbreth's principles of motion economy (related to the worker, the workplace, and the equipment) are used to redesign the manual portion of the new method to minimize fatigue and time.

### Technique 5 — Application of Principles of Ergonomics
Human factors (anthropometry, posture, lighting, noise) are integrated into the new method to ensure safety and operator comfort.

## 2.3 Selection of the Best Method — The Evaluation Stage

Once multiple alternatives are generated, the analyst must **select** the best. The selection is based on a comparative evaluation using the following criteria (in order of importance):

1. **Economic feasibility** — Will the savings justify the implementation cost?
2. **Technical feasibility** — Can it be done with available technology?
3. **Human/social acceptability** — Will workers accept it? Is it safe?
4. **Operational reliability** — Will it work consistently under production conditions?
5. **Time required for change-over** — How quickly can it be installed?

> [!NOTE]
> The selection is rarely based on a single quantitative formula; it is a **judgmental ranking process** supported by comparative process charts.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
