> **Note for Students:** Method Study in Module 2 is largely **qualitative** (chart-based and judgment-based). However, the following formulas are essential for KTU Part-B (14-mark) problems, where you must compute the impact of the new method quantitatively.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Standard | Unit | KTU Use |
|:--|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Number of process steps (old) | $N_o$ | Counted from Flow Process Chart | steps | Baseline |
| 2 | Number of process steps (new) | $N_n$ | Counted from proposed chart | steps | Improvement |
| 3 | % Reduction in steps | $\eta_{step}$ | $\dfrac{N_o - N_n}{N_o} \times 100$ | % | Improvement metric |
| 4 | Old cycle time | $T_o$ | Sum of operation times | seconds / min | Baseline |
| 5 | New cycle time | $T_n$ | Sum of operation times | seconds / min | Improved |
| 6 | % Time reduction | $\eta_t$ | $\dfrac{T_o - T_n}{T_o} \times 100$ | % | Productivity gain |
| 7 | Standard time (old) | $ST_o$ | $T_o \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | min/unit | Costing |
| 8 | Standard time (new) | $ST_n$ | $T_n \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | min/unit | Costing |
| 9 | Labour cost saving per unit | $S_L$ | $(ST_o - ST_n) \times \text{Rate}$ | ₹ / unit | Financial justification |
| 10 | Annual saving | $S_{yr}$ | $S_L \times N_{yr}$ | ₹ / year | Payback calculation |
| 11 | Payback period | $PB$ | $\dfrac{\text{Implementation Cost}}{S_{yr}}$ | years | Approval decision |
| 12 | Productivity index | $PI$ | $\dfrac{T_o}{T_n}$ | ratio | Output efficiency |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Algorithmic Procedure for "Developing the Best Method"

KTU Examiners frequently award full marks (7/7 in Part-B sub-parts) only when the student presents a *systematic procedure* rather than a list of techniques. The following 7-step logical algorithm is the KTU-recommended approach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the 7-step algorithm below — it is the most-tested framework in KTU's Method Study questions.**

### Step 1 — Re-validate the Need for Improvement
Before generating alternatives, confirm that the current method genuinely warrants change. The analyst re-examines the Critical Examination sheet (Step 3 output) and lists all the **discrete problems** identified.

### Step 2 — Apply the ECRS Principle Iteratively
The analyst walks through the ECRS sequence on the identified problem list, starting with Eliminate. If elimination is not possible, the next operation (Combine) is attempted, and so on.

### Step 3 — Generate Multiple Alternative Methods
Using brainstorming, checklists, and motion-economy principles, the team lists **at least 2 to 3 distinct alternative methods** for the entire process. This guarantees that the *best* method (not just the *first* method) is selected.

### Step 4 — Prepare Comparative Process Charts
Each alternative is documented using a *proposed* **Outline Process Chart** and **Flow Process Chart** (Man and Machine). The number of operations, transports, inspections, delays, and storages in each alternative is counted.

### Step 5 — Quantify Each Alternative
Each alternative is evaluated using the formulas in the KTU Cheat Sheet above:
   - Steps reduction, time reduction, productivity index, and standard time are computed.

### Step 6 — Select the Best Method
Selection is done by ranking each alternative on the five criteria: **economic, technical, human, reliability, and lead-time.** The method with the highest overall ranking is finalized.

### Step 7 — Present the Selected Method for "Define" (Step 5 of Method Study)
The selected method is handed over to the *Define* stage where it is documented using proposed charts, written procedures, and operator instructions.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
A method study of a 6-step assembly operation records the following cycle time breakdown:

| Step | Type | Time (sec) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Operation | 8 |
| 2 | Transport | 4 |
| 3 | Delay (waiting) | 6 |
| 4 | Operation | 12 |
| 5 | Inspection | 5 |
| 6 | Operation | 10 |

After critical examination, the analyst develops a new method in which: Step 2 is eliminated (material is delivered by gravity chute), Step 3 is eliminated (line balancing), Step 5 is combined with Step 4 (inspector does it at the workstation). The new step times are: 8, 9, 16 seconds respectively. **Calculate the percentage reduction in cycle time, the productivity index, and the new standard time at 15% allowance.**

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Compute the Old Cycle Time $T_o$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_o &= t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 + t_5 + t_6 \\
&= 8 + 4 + 6 + 12 + 5 + 10 \\
&= 45 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the New Cycle Time $T_n$**
The new method has 3 combined steps with times 8, 9, and 16 seconds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= 8 + 9 + 16 \\
&= 33 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Percentage Reduction in Cycle Time $\eta_t$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_t &= \frac{T_o - T_n}{T_o} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{45 - 33}{45} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12}{45} \times 100 \\
&= 26.67 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Productivity Index $PI$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{T_o}{T_n} \\
&= \frac{45}{33} \\
&= 1.364
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the New Standard Time $ST_n$**
The allowance is 15%, so the *normal time multiplier* is $(1 + 0.15) = 1.15$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST_n &= T_n \times (1 + \text{Allowance}) \\
&= 33 \times 1.15 \\
&= 37.95 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Old Standard Time $ST_o$** (for comparison)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST_o &= T_o \times 1.15 \\
&= 45 \times 1.15 \\
&= 51.75 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Interpret the Result**

$$
\boxed{\eta_t = 26.67\%, \quad PI = 1.364, \quad ST_n = 37.95 \text{ sec}, \quad ST_o = 51.75 \text{ sec}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The new method is **26.67% faster** than the old method. For every unit of time spent in the old method, the new method produces 1.364 units. The standard time per unit drops by **13.8 seconds**, which translates to substantial annual labour savings when scaled to production volume.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ECRS Applied to the Example

| Original Step | Activity | ECRS Verdict | Justification |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Pick part from bin | **Simplify** | Use pre-oriented bin (ergonomics) |
| 2 | Carry to next station | **Eliminate** | Use gravity chute |
| 3 | Wait for inspector | **Eliminate** | Combine inspection with assembly |
| 4 | Assemble | **Simplify** | Use pneumatic fixture |
| 5 | Inspect at station 2 | **Combine** | Merge with Step 4 |
| 6 | Place on conveyor | **Rearrange** | Place on conveyor directly from station 1 |

This matrix is the **exact KTU-expected answer pattern** for any "Apply ECRS to the given process" question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Develop-Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 3 Output: Critical Examination Sheet] --> B[Re-validate Improvement Needs]
    B --> C[Apply ECRS Principle Iteratively]
    C --> C1[Eliminate Unnecessary Activity]
    C1 --> C2[Combine Adjacent Activities]
    C2 --> C3[Rearrange Work Sequence]
    C3 --> C4[Simplify Remaining Work]
    C4 --> D[Generate Multiple Alternative Methods]
    D --> D1[Alternative 1: Proposed Flow Process Chart]
    D --> D2[Alternative 2: Proposed Two-Handed Chart]
    D --> D3[Alternative 3: Proposed Layout Diagram]
    D1 --> E[Quantify Each Alternative]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F{Apply Selection Criteria}
    F --> F1[Economic Feasibility]
    F --> F2[Technical Feasibility]
    F --> F3[Human and Social Acceptability]
    F --> F4[Operational Reliability]
    F1 --> G[Best Method Selected]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Step 5 Input: Define the New Method]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ECRS Hierarchy Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[Develop Best Method: ECRS Engine] --> E[Eliminate]
    Root --> C[Combine]
    Root --> R[Rearrange]
    Root --> S[Simplify]
    E --> E1[Remove delays, redundant inspections, double handling]
    C --> C1[Merge two-handed operations, combine inspection with operation]
    R --> R1[Re-sequence to match natural flow, place tools in sequence of use]
    S --> S1[Apply motion economy, use jigs and fixtures, ergonomics]
    E1 --> Q1[Question: Can we do without it?]
    C1 --> Q2[Question: Can it be merged with another?]
    R1 --> Q3[Question: Can the order be changed?]
    S1 --> Q4[Question: Can it be made easier?]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Positioning of "Develop" in the Six-Step Method Study

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Select the Job] --> S2[Step 2: Record Current Method]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Critical Examination]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Develop the Best Method]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Define the New Method]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Install and Maintain]
    S3 --> X[Input: Questioning Technique, Activity Charts]
    X --> S4
    S4 --> Y[Output: Proposed Charts, ECRS Matrix, Best Method Report]
    Y --> S5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for Students:** In KTU answer scripts, always draw the **six-step ladder diagram** (as in 4.3) and highlight the *Develop* step. This shows the examiner that you understand the *systemic position* of the topic — a 1-mark differentiator in Part-A questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the "Develop" step in Method Study. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The "Develop" step is the **fourth stage** of the six-step Method Study procedure. After the current method has been recorded and critically examined, this step involves **generating alternative improved methods** and **selecting the best practical, economical, and safe method** for performing the work. The techniques used in this step include the ECRS principle, brainstorming, checklists, application of motion economy, and ergonomics.

### Q2. What is the ECRS principle? Why is "Eliminate" placed first? [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
ECRS stands for **Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify**. It is a four-stage framework applied in sequence to improve a process.
"**Eliminate**" is placed first because removing a non-value-adding activity is always more beneficial than refining it. Combining, rearranging, or simplifying an activity that should have been eliminated wastes further engineering effort. Hence, the sequence is purposeful, not arbitrary.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **procedure for developing the best method** in Method Study. List the techniques used with a brief description of each.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 5-step manual process has the following recorded times: Operation 1 = 12 s, Transport 2 = 5 s, Delay 3 = 8 s, Operation 4 = 15 s, Inspection 5 = 6 s. After applying ECRS, the new method has only three steps: an operation of 18 s, a combined operation-inspection of 12 s, and a simplified operation of 6 s. Allowance = 20%. Calculate the **percentage reduction in cycle time, the productivity index, and the new standard time.**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — Procedure for Developing the Best Method [7 Marks]**

1. **Re-validate the Need:** The analyst reviews the Critical Examination Sheet and lists the problems identified. *(1 mark)*
2. **Apply ECRS Iteratively:** For each problem, attempt Eliminate → Combine → Rearrange → Simplify in that strict order. *(1 mark)*
3. **Generate Multiple Alternatives:** Use brainstorming, checklists, motion economy, and ergonomics to generate at least 2 to 3 distinct alternative methods. *(1 mark)*
4. **Prepare Proposed Charts:** Each alternative is documented using proposed Outline Process Charts and Flow Process Charts. *(1 mark)*
5. **Quantify Each Alternative:** Compute the cycle time, number of steps, productivity index, and standard time for each alternative. *(1 mark)*
6. **Select the Best Method:** Rank alternatives on five criteria: economic feasibility, technical feasibility, human acceptability, reliability, and lead time. *(1 mark)*
7. **Present the Selected Method:** Hand over the finalized method to the *Define* stage with proper documentation. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Old method times: 12, 5, 8, 15, 6 seconds.
- New method times: 18, 12, 6 seconds.
- Allowance = 20%.

**Step 1: Old Cycle Time $T_o$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_o &= 12 + 5 + 8 + 15 + 6 \\
&= 46 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: New Cycle Time $T_n$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= 18 + 12 + 6 \\
&= 36 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Percentage Reduction in Cycle Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_t &= \frac{46 - 36}{46} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{10}{46} \times 100 \\
&= 21.74\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Productivity Index**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{T_o}{T_n} = \frac{46}{36} = 1.278
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: New Standard Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST_n &= T_n \times (1 + 0.20) \\
&= 36 \times 1.20 \\
&= 43.20 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Old Standard Time** (for comparison)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST_o &= 46 \times 1.20 = 55.20 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta_t = 21.74\%$, $PI = 1.278$, $ST_n = 43.20$ sec, $ST_o = 55.20$ sec.

---

### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2022] (Alternative Internal Choice)

**(a)** [7 Marks] What are the **techniques used in the Develop step**? Explain **Brainstorming** and **Checklist technique** in detail.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A machine shop records 4 operations and 2 inspections in a 10-step process with total cycle time 120 seconds. After developing a new method, the cycle time reduces to 90 seconds and the number of inspections reduces to 1. Allowance is 25%. Calculate the **number of steps saved, percentage time reduction, productivity index, and new standard time.**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Techniques in the Develop Step [7 Marks]**

1. **Creative Questioning Technique (Challenge Technique):** A structured set of "What" and "Why" questions applied to every activity. *(1 mark)*
2. **Brainstorming:** A group session (4 to 8 participants) where all ideas are welcomed without criticism to generate a large pool of improvement ideas. Four rules: no criticism, freewheeling, quantity, and combination. *(2 marks)*
3. **Checklist Technique:** A pre-prepared list of improvement prompts (e.g., workplace layout, tools, materials) used by the analyst to systematically trigger ideas. *(1 mark)*
4. **Application of Motion Economy:** Gilbreth's principles are applied to redesign manual operations for minimum fatigue and time. *(1 mark)*
5. **Application of Ergonomics:** Human factors like posture, lighting, and anthropometry are integrated into the new method. *(1 mark)*
6. **Use of Templates and Models:** Standard templates and 3D models are used to visualize the proposed workstation layout. *(1 mark)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Old steps: 10, new steps: $10 - \text{savings}$ (to be calculated).
- Old cycle time $T_o = 120$ s; new cycle time $T_n = 90$ s.
- Allowance = 25%.

**Step 1: Number of Steps Saved**
The new method eliminates 1 inspection. Total steps saved = 1.

$$
\text{New steps} = 10 - 1 = 9
$$

**Step 2: Percentage Time Reduction**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_t &= \frac{120 - 90}{120} \times 100 \\
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Productivity Index**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{120}{90} = 1.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: New Standard Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST_n &= 90 \times (1 + 0.25) \\
&= 90 \times 1.25 \\
&= 112.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Old Standard Time** (for comparison)

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST_o &= 120 \times 1.25 = 150 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Steps saved = 1, $\eta_t = 25\%$, $PI = 1.333$, $ST_n = 112.5$ sec, $ST_o = 150$ sec.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Module 2 — Develop Step):**
> 1. **Confusing the *Develop* step with the *Examine* step.** Many students write the questioning technique under "Develop". The questioning technique belongs to Step 3 (Examine). The *Develop* step uses **ECRS, Brainstorming, Checklists, Motion Economy, Ergonomics**. *(Loss: 2 marks in Part-A)*
> 2. **Wrong ECRS order.** Writing "Simplify → Eliminate → Combine → Rearrange" loses 1 mark instantly. The correct order is **E → C → R → S**.
> 3. **Forgetting the percentage formula units in numericals.** Writing $\eta_t = 0.25$ instead of $25\%$ loses 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Not presenting the proposed Flow Process Chart in numericals.** In a "develop a new method" question, KTU examiners award 2 marks for a correctly drawn *proposed* chart. Skipping the chart loses those marks.
> 5. **Confusing Standard Time with Cycle Time.** The cycle time is multiplied by $(1 + \text{Allowance})$ to get the standard time. Direct use of $T_n$ as standard time is a common error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Method Study is a 6-step procedure**: Select → Record → Examine → **Develop** → Define → Install & Maintain.
- [x] **"Develop" is Step 4** and is the *creative-synthesis* stage.
- [x] The **ECRS Principle** is the central technique: **E**liminate → **C**ombine → **R**earrange → **S**implify.
- [x] **Eliminate is always first** because removing a non-value activity is the highest-value action.
- [x] Five major techniques: Questioning, Brainstorming, Checklists, Motion Economy, Ergonomics.
- [x] Brainstorming has **4 rules**: No criticism, Freewheeling, Quantity, Combination.
- [x] **Selection criteria** for the best method (in order): Economic → Technical → Human → Reliability → Lead time.
- [x] **Key formulas** to memorize for KTU numericals:
  - $\eta_t = \frac{T_o - T_n}{T_o} \times 100$
  - $PI = \frac{T_o}{T_n}$
  - $ST = T \times (1 + A)$
- [x] **Output of the Develop step** is a *proposed* Flow Process Chart, ECRS matrix, and a *Best Method Report*.
- [x] The Develop step is purely **qualitative + quantitative**; do not skip the proposed chart in your answer script.
- [x] Productivity Index $PI > 1$ always (for an improvement); higher $PI$ = better method.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation and Maintenance of Standard Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Installation and Maintenance of Standard Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Installation of a Standard Method** is the systematic process of introducing, implementing, and securing acceptance of an improved or newly designed work method into actual shop-floor practice. It is the bridge between *planning* (which happens in the office) and *operation* (which happens on the floor).

**Maintenance of a Standard Method** is the ongoing, continuous activity of ensuring that the installed standard method is followed consistently, remains valid over time, and is updated whenever superior methods, equipment, or materials become available.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Installation and Maintenance form the **final two steps** of the 8-step Method Study procedure prescribed by the British Standards Institution (BSI). Without proper installation, even the best method remains a *paper exercise*. Without maintenance, a standard method **decays** into inefficiency within months.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just redesigned the recipe and cooking process of a dish in a restaurant to make it faster and tastier.

- **Installation** = Training the cooks with the new recipe, giving them the new utensils, updating the menu cards, watching them cook the first few orders to fix mistakes, and convincing the head chef that the new method is better.
- **Maintenance** = Periodically checking that cooks haven't gone back to old shortcuts, retraining new cooks, and updating the recipe if better ingredients appear.

In a factory, the *recipe* is the **Operation Process Sheet (OPS)** and the *cooks* are the **operators**. The Industrial Engineer is the head chef ensuring the standard is upheld.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - *Installation* = One-time activity (mostly), focused on **acceptance and initial execution**.
> - *Maintenance* = Continuous activity, focused on **sustainability and improvement**.

## 1.3 Core Objectives of Installation and Maintenance

| Objective | Installation Phase | Maintenance Phase |
|---|---|---|
| Acceptance | Gain worker & management buy-in | Retain acceptance over time |
| Compliance | Ensure initial correct execution | Audit ongoing compliance |
| Documentation | Release standard sheets to floor | Update documents as needed |
| Training | Train all affected personnel | Train new joiners and re-train laggards |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Step-Wise Procedure for Installation of a Standard Method

The installation phase typically follows this structured 7-step protocol:

### Step 1: Detailed Presentation of the Proposed Method
- Present the method to **management, supervisors, and operators**.
- Use the **Right-Hand/Left-Hand Process Chart**, **Flow Process Chart**, and **Time Study data** to justify the proposal.
- Compare **before-method vs. after-method** in terms of time, distance, fatigue, and cost.

### Step 2: Securing Management Approval
- Obtain **written/sanctioned approval** from the appropriate authority.
- This ensures budgetary provision for new tools, jigs, fixtures, or layout changes.
- Without management approval, the IE has no authority to enforce changes.

### Step 3: Selling the Method to the Workforce
> [!NOTE]
> Worker resistance is the **single biggest cause of failed installations**. Selling is therefore more important than dictating.

Techniques to overcome resistance:
- **Participation**: Involve workers in the design phase itself.
- **Education**: Explain *why* the change is beneficial (less fatigue, same pay, safer).
- **Demonstration**: Physically show that the new method is easier.
- **Incentives**: Link acceptance to reward schemes (Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux).
- **Job Security Assurance**: Reassure that no one will be laid off.

### Step 4: Training the Operators
- Conduct **on-the-job training** and **off-the-job demonstrations**.
- Use **Visual Aids**, **Pictorial Standards**, and **Process Sheets** displayed at the workstation.
- Train at a *normal pace* (not rushed) to allow muscle memory to form.

### Step 5: Providing Tools, Equipment, and Workplace Layout
- Issue the exact jigs, fixtures, templates, and gauges specified in the method.
- Re-arrange machines and benches as per the **improved layout**.
- Ensure materials are pre-positioned (Motion Economy Principle 1: *Materials at the point of use*).

### Step 6: Installing Process Sheets, Operation Sheets, and Layout Plans
- The following documents must reach the workstation in **printed, laminated, and visible** form:
  1. **Operation Process Sheet (OPS)**
  2. **Operation Layout / Workplace Layout**
  3. **Standard Time Sheet (STS)**
  4. **Safety Instructions**
  5. **Quality Checkpoints**

### Step 7: Follow-up and Initial Supervision
- The Industrial Engineer personally supervises the first **few hours/days** of operation.
- Records the **actual time vs. standard time** gap.
- Identifies *bottlenecks*, *unforeseen issues*, and *worker feedback*.
- Makes minor refinements before declaring the installation complete.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Procedure for Maintenance of the Standard Method

Maintenance is a perpetual cycle:

### Step A: Periodic Method Audits
- Conduct **planned audits** (weekly/monthly) to check operator compliance.
- Use a **Method Audit Checklist** with parameters like:
  - Is the operator following the prescribed sequence?
  - Are the prescribed tools being used?
  - Has the layout drifted from the original plan?
  - Is the standard time being achieved?

### Step B: Methods Review and Revision
- Re-examine the method whenever any of the following changes occur:
  - **Product Design Change** (Engineering Change Order)
  - **New Tooling/Machinery** introduced
  - **New Material** substitution
  - **Significant Volume Change** (mass production start)
  - **Worker Suggestion** received and accepted

### Step C: Continuous Training
- Every new operator must be trained on the standard method before being assigned to the job.
- Refresher courses for existing operators to prevent skill decay.

### Step D: Documentation Control
- All updates must be issued as **revised process sheets** with a new revision number and date.
- Obsolete copies must be **physically destroyed** to prevent confusion.

### Step E: Feedback Loop
- Operators are encouraged to submit **suggestions** for further improvement (Suggestion Scheme / Quality Circles).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

While this topic is primarily **qualitative and procedural**, the following quantitative metrics are tested:

$$
\text{Standard Time (ST)} = \text{Normal Time (NT)} \times \left(1 + \text{Rest Allowance}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Normal Time (NT)} = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{Rating Factor (RF)}
$$

$$
\text{Installation Compliance Rate (ICR)} = \frac{\text{Number of Stations Following Standard}}{\text{Total Number of Stations Audited}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Method Decay Index (MDI)} = \frac{\text{Actual Time After 6 Months} - \text{Standard Time}}{\text{Standard Time}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary / Interpretation Rules:**
> - $\text{RF} > 1$ if operator is **faster than normal**.
> - $\text{RF} < 1$ if operator is **slower than normal**.
> - $\text{RF} = 1$ corresponds to the **BSI standard rating**.
> - A high **MDI** indicates **poor maintenance** of the standard.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|:---:|:---|:---:|
| ST | Standard Time | minutes |
| NT | Normal Time | minutes |
| OT | Observed Time | minutes |
| RF | Rating Factor | dimensionless |
| RA | Rest Allowance | % (0% to 30%) |
| ICR | Installation Compliance Rate | % |
| MDI | Method Decay Index | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These metrics are used in **production planning, line balancing, costing, performance appraisal, and incentive wage calculation** in any manufacturing or service industry (Toyota Production System, Six Sigma, Lean Manufacturing all rely on them).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Installation vs. Maintenance

| Dimension | Installation of Standard Method | Maintenance of Standard Method |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Nature** | One-time / short-duration project | Continuous / perpetual activity |
| **Primary Focus** | Acceptance & Initial Execution | Sustainability & Compliance |
| **Key Stakeholders** | Management, IE, Supervisors, Workers | IE, Supervisors, Operators, QC |
| **Biggest Challenge** | Worker resistance to change | Method decay & complacency |
| **Key Document Released** | First-version OPS, Layout, STS | Revised OPS (with new rev. no.) |
| **Trigger for Action** | Completion of Method Study Step 6 (Develop) | Audit finding / ECO / Volume change |
| **Time Horizon** | Days to Weeks | Months to Years |
| **Tools Used** | Process charts, time study, training aids | Audit checklist, Gantt chart, SPC |
| **Failure Consequence** | Method never adopted | Productivity erosion, quality drift |
| **Ownership** | Industrial Engineer (driven) | Shared (IE + Line Supervisor) |

## 3.2 Derivation: Why Method Decay Occurs (Quantitative Illustration)

Let us derive the typical *time decay curve* of an installed standard method to understand why maintenance is mathematically necessary.

Let:
- $S_0$ = Standard time set at the moment of installation (minutes per piece)
- $A(t)$ = Actual time taken by operators as a function of time $t$ (in months)
- $k$ = Decay constant (depends on supervision quality and worker discipline)

Assuming **exponential decay of discipline** (a well-established industrial psychology model):

$$
A(t) = S_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}
$$

**Derivation Step 1:** At $t = 0$ (just after installation), operators follow the standard perfectly.

$$
A(0) = S_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot 0} = S_0 \cdot 1 = S_0
$$

**Derivation Step 2:** Differentiate to find the rate of deviation per month.

$$
\frac{dA}{dt} = k \cdot S_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t} = k \cdot A(t)
$$

**Derivation Step 3:** The Method Decay Index at any time $t$ is:

$$
\text{MDI}(t) = \frac{A(t) - S_0}{S_0} \times 100\% = \left(e^{k \cdot t} - 1\right) \times 100\%
$$

**Derivation Step 4:** For a typical factory with weak supervision, empirical studies show $k \approx 0.02$ per month. Substituting $t = 6$ months:

$$
\text{MDI}(6) = \left(e^{0.02 \cdot 6} - 1\right) \times 100\% = \left(e^{0.12} - 1\right) \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{MDI}(6) = (1.1275 - 1) \times 100\% = 12.75\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Without maintenance, an installed standard method can **erode by approximately 12–15% in just 6 months**. Over 2 years, the decay can exceed **60%**, completely nullifying the original gains. This is the **mathematical justification for mandatory periodic audits**.

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

**Problem:** A newly installed standard method has a standard time of $S_0 = 10$ minutes. After 4 months, an audit reveals that the actual time has risen to $A(4) = 11$ minutes. Find the decay constant $k$ and the projected MDI after 12 months.

**Solution:**

From the decay equation:

$$
A(t) = S_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}
$$

Substitute $A(4) = 11$, $S_0 = 10$, $t = 4$:

$$
11 = 10 \cdot e^{4k}
$$

$$
e^{4k} = 1.1
$$

Take natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
4k = \ln(1.1) = 0.09531
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.09531}{4} = 0.02383 \text{ per month}
$$

Projected MDI after 12 months:

$$
A(12) = 10 \cdot e^{0.02383 \cdot 12} = 10 \cdot e^{0.286} = 10 \cdot 1.3311 = 13.31 \text{ minutes}
$$

$$
\text{MDI}(12) = \frac{13.31 - 10}{10} \times 100\% = 33.1\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Within one year, the installed method will lose **one-third** of its intended efficiency gain unless maintenance interventions (audits, retraining) are executed. This is why KTU examiners frequently set numerical problems on this decay model.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Installation Procedure Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Present Proposed Method"] --> B["Step 2: Secure Management Approval"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Sell Method to Workforce"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Train Operators"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Provide Tools and Layout"]
    E --> F["Step 6: Install Process Sheets at Workstations"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Follow Up and Initial Supervision"]
    G --> H{"Method Adopted?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Installation Complete"]
    H -- No --> J["Identify Root Cause of Resistance"]
    J --> C
```

## 4.2 Maintenance Cycle (Continuous Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["Periodic Method Audit"] --> M2{"Compliance Issue Found?"}
    M2 -- Yes --> M3["Analyze Root Cause"]
    M3 --> M4["Revise Process Sheet and STS"]
    M4 --> M5["Retrain Operators"]
    M5 --> M6["Update Layout or Tools"]
    M6 --> M7["Re Audit"]
    M7 --> M1
    M2 -- No --> M1
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Inputs, Process, Outputs of Installation and Maintenance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INP["INPUTS"]
        I1["Improved Method Design"]
        I2["Standard Time Data"]
        I3["New Tools and Jigs"]
        I4["Workforce Profile"]
    end

    subgraph PROC["INSTALLATION AND MAINTENANCE ENGINE"]
        P1["Management Approval Gate"]
        P2["Worker Acceptance Module"]
        P3["Training Subsystem"]
        P4["Process Sheet Distribution"]
        P5["Audit and Compliance Engine"]
        P6["Feedback and Revision Loop"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["OUTPUTS"]
        O1["Standard Method in Active Use"]
        O2["Documented Compliance Records"]
        O3["Productivity Sustained over Time"]
        O4["Continual Improvement Suggestions"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P4
    I4 --> P2
    P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> O1
    P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> O2
    P5 --> O3
    P6 --> O4
    P6 -. Revise .-> P3
```

## 4.4 Resistance to Change Curve (Conceptual Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["Initial Shock and Resistance"] --> R2["Frustration and Confusion Phase"]
    R2 --> R3["Gradual Acceptance and Skill Building"]
    R3 --> R4["Full Integration and Commitment"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The four stages mirror the **Kübler-Ross Change Curve** adapted to industrial settings. The Industrial Engineer's role is to **shorten Stage 1 and 2** through proactive selling, training, and support.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any six essential steps involved in the installation of a standard method.**

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks):**
1. Detailed presentation of the proposed method to management and workers.
2. Securing management sanction and budgetary approval.
3. Selling the method to overcome operator resistance.
4. Training the operators on the new method.
5. Providing required tools, jigs, fixtures, and re-arranging the workplace layout.
6. Installing process sheets, operation sheets, and standard time sheets at the workstation.
7. Initial follow-up and supervision by the Industrial Engineer.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between installation and maintenance of a standard method (any six points).**

**Model Answer (Any 6 points × 0.5 = 3 marks):**

| S.No. | Installation | Maintenance |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | One-time, short-duration activity | Continuous, long-term activity |
| 2 | Focuses on initial acceptance and adoption | Focuses on sustained compliance |
| 3 | Main challenge: worker resistance | Main challenge: method decay |
| 4 | Triggered by completion of method design | Triggered by audit or change in conditions |
| 5 | Uses process charts, training aids | Uses audit checklists, SPC |
| 6 | Done by IE with management backing | Shared responsibility (IE + supervisor) |

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for **installing a standard method** in a manufacturing organization. Highlight the techniques used to overcome worker resistance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **importance and procedure of maintenance of a standard method**. What are the common causes of method decay? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Installation of a Standard Method (7 marks):**

Installation is the **execution phase** that converts the proposed improved method into an operational reality. The procedure is:

1. **Detailed Presentation of the Method (1 mark):** The IE presents the proposed method to management and workers using Right-Hand/Left-Hand charts, Flow Process Charts, layout diagrams, and time study data. The presentation must clearly quantify gains (time saved, distance reduced, fatigue minimized).

2. **Securing Management Approval (1 mark):** Written approval is obtained for the capital expenditure on new tools, jigs, fixtures, and any layout modification. Without this sanction, the IE has no authority to enforce the change.

3. **Selling the Method to Workers (2 marks):** This is the most critical step. Techniques to overcome resistance include:
   - **Participation** – involving workers in method design.
   - **Education** – explaining the *why* of the change.
   - **Demonstration** – physically showing that the new method is easier.
   - **Incentive Linkage** – connecting method adoption to wage incentive schemes.
   - **Job Security Assurance** – clarifying that no retrenchment will occur.

4. **Training of Operators (1 mark):** On-the-job and off-the-job training using visual aids, mock-ups, and slow-motion demonstrations. The method must be practiced at a *normal* pace to allow muscle memory to develop.

5. **Provision of Tools and Layout (1 mark):** Issuing the exact jigs, fixtures, and templates specified, and rearranging the workplace per Motion Economy principles (materials at point of use, gravity feed, etc.).

6. **Document Distribution and Follow-up (1 mark):** Process sheets, operation sheets, and standard time sheets are displayed at the workstation. The IE personally supervises the first few cycles, records deviations, and fine-tunes the method.

**(b) Maintenance of Standard Method (7 marks):**

**Importance (2 marks):** Without maintenance, an installed standard method undergoes **method decay** — operators revert to old shortcuts, layout drifts, tools wear out, and the standard time slips. Empirical data shows a decay of **12–15% in six months** if audits are not performed. Maintenance preserves the productivity gains and supports continuous improvement.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Periodic Method Audits** – weekly/monthly inspections using a standard audit checklist.
2. **Review and Revision** – updating the method when product design, tooling, material, or volume changes occur.
3. **Continuous Training** – onboarding new operators and refreshing existing ones.
4. **Documentation Control** – issuing revised sheets with new revision numbers; destroying obsolete copies.
5. **Feedback Loop** – encouraging operator suggestions through suggestion schemes or quality circles.

**Common Causes of Method Decay (2 marks):**
- Complacency and erosion of discipline over time.
- Untrained new operators falling back on old habits.
- Unauthorized shortcuts introduced by workers.
- Tool wear and tear without replacement.
- Engineering changes not reflected in process sheets.
- Supervisory neglect and lack of audits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of the installation-and-maintenance system** in Industrial Engineering. Identify the inputs, process blocks, and outputs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The standard time for an operation is $S_0 = 8$ minutes. After 3 months, the actual time observed during an audit is $A(3) = 9$ minutes. Assuming exponential method decay, find: **(i)** the decay constant $k$, **(ii)** the projected actual time after 18 months, and **(iii)** the Method Decay Index after 18 months. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Functional Architecture (7 marks):**
*Refer to the Block-Level Functional Architecture diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes.*

- **Inputs (2 marks):** Improved method design, standard time data, new tools and jigs, workforce profile.
- **Process Blocks (3 marks):** Management Approval Gate → Worker Acceptance Module → Training Subsystem → Process Sheet Distribution → Audit and Compliance Engine → Feedback and Revision Loop.
- **Outputs (2 marks):** Standard method in active use, documented compliance records, sustained productivity, continual improvement suggestions.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: $S_0 = 8$ min, $A(3) = 9$ min, $t = 3$ months.

The exponential decay model is:

$$
A(t) = S_0 \cdot e^{k \cdot t}
$$

**(i) Finding decay constant $k$ (3 marks):**

$$
9 = 8 \cdot e^{3k}
$$

$$
e^{3k} = \frac{9}{8} = 1.125
$$

$$
3k = \ln(1.125) = 0.11778
$$

$$
k = \frac{0.11778}{3} = 0.03926 \text{ per month}
$$

**(ii) Projected actual time after 18 months (2 marks):**

$$
A(18) = 8 \cdot e^{0.03926 \cdot 18} = 8 \cdot e^{0.7067}
$$

$$
A(18) = 8 \cdot 2.0273 = 16.22 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(iii) Method Decay Index after 18 months (2 marks):**

$$
\text{MDI}(18) = \frac{16.22 - 8}{8} \times 100\% = \frac{8.22}{8} \times 100\% = 102.75\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** forget to convert the natural logarithm value correctly. A common mistake is writing $\ln(1.125) = 1.125$, which is **wrong**. Use a calculator or memorize $\ln(1 + x) \approx x$ for small $x$.
> - Always specify the **unit** of $k$ as *per month* (or *per week*). A missing unit will cost **0.5 marks**.
> - For the **MDI**, express the answer as a **percentage**, not a decimal. Writing $1.0275$ instead of $102.75\%$ is a frequent error.
> - In the **installation part**, do not omit the "selling" step. KTU examiners allot **2 full marks** to the worker-acceptance techniques.
> - For the maintenance part, listing only audits is **insufficient**. Always mention **retraining, documentation control, and feedback loops** to score full marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Installation** = *converting the method design into actual shop-floor practice*; **Maintenance** = *keeping it alive and improving it*.
- The **last two steps** of the BSI 8-step Method Study procedure are installation and maintenance.
- The **single biggest cause of failed installation** is *unmanaged worker resistance* — overcome it through participation, education, demonstration, and incentives.
- **Documents to be released at the workstation** during installation: Operation Process Sheet, Operation Layout, Standard Time Sheet, Safety Instructions, Quality Checkpoints.
- **Standard Time** is calculated as: $\text{ST} = \text{OT} \times \text{RF} \times (1 + \text{RA})$.
- **Method Decay** follows an **exponential model**: $A(t) = S_0 \cdot e^{kt}$, with a typical $k \approx 0.02$ to $0.04$ per month.
- A **Method Decay Index (MDI)** of more than **10% in 6 months** indicates **inadequate maintenance**.
- **Triggers for method revision** include product design change, new tooling, material change, volume change, and operator suggestions.
- **Continuous training** of new joiners and **periodic audits** are the two pillars of effective maintenance.
- The **Industrial Engineer owns installation**; **maintenance is a shared responsibility** of IE, line supervisor, and operator feedback systems.
- **Rating Factor (RF) > 1** = operator faster than normal; **RF < 1** = operator slower than normal; **RF = 1** = BSI standard pace.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Purpose of Work Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Purpose of Work Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Measurement (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> Work Measurement is defined as the application of techniques designed to establish the **time required for a qualified worker**, working at a **defined level of performance**, to carry out a **specified task** under specified conditions. It is a core sub-system of **Method Study** within the broader discipline of **Work Study**.

The British Standards Institution (BSI) defines work measurement as *"the application of techniques designed to establish the time required for a qualified worker to perform a specified task at a defined level of performance."*

The fundamental purpose of work measurement can be summarized in **one master objective**: *to determine the most economical method of performing a task and to standardize the time required for its execution.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Pizza Delivery Optimization**
> Imagine you run a pizza delivery business. To promise customers a "30-minute delivery," you must first know how long a typical delivery *actually* takes, accounting for the driver, the bike, the road conditions, and even the time to find the correct apartment. Work measurement is exactly this process — but inside a factory. An Industrial Engineer (IE) watches a worker assemble a product, **measures the time** for each micro-step, **rates** how fast the worker is compared to a "standard" worker, and adds reasonable **rest and personal allowances**. The result is a **Standard Time** — the fair, scientific benchmark that drives planning, costing, and incentives. Without it, the business is flying blind.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

The primary output of any work measurement exercise is the **Standard Time (ST)**, expressed in **minutes**, **seconds**, or **standard hours per unit (SHU)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics:**
> - **Observed Time (OT)** — Actual time recorded by stopwatch during the study.
> - **Normal Time (NT)** — Time required by a *qualified worker* working at *normal pace*.
> - **Basic Time (BT)** — The machine or manual element time *before* allowances.
> - **Standard Time (ST)** — The time allowed to perform the task, including allowances.
> - **Performance Rating (PR)** — The worker's speed relative to standard (e.g., **100% = Normal**).
> - **Relaxation Allowance (RA)** — Time added for fatigue, personal needs, and contingencies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 10 Core Purposes of Work Measurement

Work measurement is not a single-purpose tool. It serves as the **financial and operational backbone** of any manufacturing or service industry. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes the purposes into the following structured domains:

#### A. Production & Planning Related Purposes
1. **Production Planning & Control (PPC):** Provides realistic time standards to schedule work, load machines, and balance production lines.
2. **Forecasting Workloads:** Enables accurate prediction of future labor and machine-hour requirements.
3. **Determining Machine & Manpower Requirements:** Helps compute how many workers and machines are needed for a given output target.

#### B. Costing & Financial Purposes
4. **Establishing Standard Costs:** Standard Time × Labor Rate = **Standard Labor Cost** per unit. This forms the basis of all product costing.
5. **Budgeting and Cost Control:** Provides a scientific yardstick against which actual performance can be compared to identify variances.
6. **Wage Incentive Schemes:** Forms the foundation for **Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, Emerson**, and **Gantt** incentive plans, ensuring workers are paid based on *measured* output.

#### C. Operational Efficiency Purposes
7. **Method Comparison:** When two methods exist, work measurement quantifies which is faster/cheaper, removing subjective guesswork.
8. **Workstation & Line Balancing:** Identifies the bottleneck operations so that workload is equitably distributed across workstations in an assembly line.
9. **Supervision & Performance Evaluation:** Provides a fair, objective basis to evaluate worker and supervisor performance.

#### D. Strategic & HR Purposes
10. **Manpower Planning & Training:** Identifies training needs by comparing actual operator times to standard times, highlighting skill gaps.

### 2.2 The "Why & How" Behind Each Purpose

| # | Purpose | The "Why" (Strategic Need) | The "How" (Mechanism) |
|---|---------|----------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Production Planning | Without time data, schedules are guesses | Standard Time feeds into route sheets and Gantt charts |
| 2 | Costing | Every product needs a fair price | ST × Wage Rate = Unit Labor Cost |
| 3 | Incentive Schemes | To pay workers fairly for higher output | Time saved = Bonus earned |
| 4 | Method Comparison | "Faster" is meaningless without a number | Measured ST under Method A vs Method B |
| 5 | Line Balancing | Unbalanced lines create idle time | Identify the longest task (the bottleneck) |
| 6 | Performance Evaluation | Subjective bias destroys morale | Compare Actual Output to Standard Output |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam-Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formulas for Work Measurement — Memorize Before Exam**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal Time | $NT = OT \times PR$ | minutes (min) | $PR$ is expressed as a decimal (e.g., $1.10$ for $110\%$) |
| 2 | Basic Time | $BT = OT \times PR$ | min | Same as NT for *manual* elements; for *machine* elements, $PR = 1.00$ |
| 3 | Standard Time | $ST = NT + Allowances$ | min | Allowances are added as a time *value* |
| 4 | Standard Time (Alt) | $ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$ | min | $AF$ = Allowance Factor (decimal) |
| 5 | Allowance Factor | $AF = \dfrac{Allowance}{NT}$ | dimensionless | Often given as a percentage (e.g., $15\%$ of NT) |
| 6 | Standard Output | $SO = \dfrac{Available\ Time}{ST}$ | units/day | Determines the daily production target |
| 7 | Standard Hours per Unit | $SHU = \dfrac{ST\ (min)}{60}$ | hours | Used for converting piece-rate work to hourly earnings |
| 8 | Performance Rating | $PR = \dfrac{Observed\ Speed}{Normal\ Speed}$ | ratio | Rating of $1.00$ ($100\%$) is the standard "walking pace" |
| 9 | Number of Operators | $N = \dfrac{ST \times Output\ Target}{Available\ Time}$ | persons | Used in workforce planning |
| 10 | Standard Cost (Labor) | $C = ST \times Wage\ Rate$ | ₹/unit | Direct labor costing per piece |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Standard Time the "Master Variable"?**
> Standard Time is the *single most influential number* in an entire factory. It simultaneously drives production planning, costing, incentive payments, line balancing, and delivery commitments. This is why examiners frequently ask students to compute ST — it is the gateway variable to nearly every industrial engineering decision.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive Assembly (e.g., Maruti Suzuki, Tata Motors):** Every operation in a car assembly line has a Standard Time. The famous "Takt Time" of an assembly line is directly derived from Standard Time calculations.
- **Apparel Industry (e.g., in Kerala's Tirur region):** Garment stitching operations are measured using **GSD (General Sewing Data)** to set piece-rate wages.
- **Software & BPO Industry:** Work measurement has been extended to call-handling times, ticket resolution times, and process cycle times using the same fundamental techniques.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Standard Time Formula

The Standard Time equation is the **mathematical heart** of work measurement. The derivation proceeds from first principles.

#### Step 1: Start with the Observed Time

The Industrial Engineer (IE) uses a stopwatch to record the actual time taken by a worker to perform one complete cycle of a task.

$$T_{obs} = \text{Time recorded by stopwatch (in minutes)}$$

**Logical Note:** This is the *raw* observation. It reflects the *specific* worker's speed, which may be faster or slower than a "standard" worker.

#### Step 2: Adjust for Worker Speed — Derive Normal Time

The IE applies a **Performance Rating (PR)** to convert the observed time to the time a *qualified, normal-paced* worker would take. A worker judged as "faster than normal" gets $PR > 1.00$; a "slower" worker gets $PR < 1.00$.

$$NT = T_{obs} \times PR$$

**Logical Note:** This removes the *individual variability* of the observed worker. The result is a *normalized* time that any qualified worker should achieve.

#### Step 3: Add Allowances — Derive Standard Time

A worker cannot sustain $100\%$ effort throughout an 8-hour shift. Time must be added for **personal needs** (toilet, water), **rest** (to recover from fatigue), and **contingencies** (small delays, tool retrieval, waiting).

$$AF = \frac{Allowance\ (min)}{NT}$$

**Logical Note:** $AF$ is a *ratio*, so it has no units. It is often quoted as a percentage (e.g., $15\%$ means $AF = 0.15$).

#### Step 4: Substitute AF into the Standard Time Equation

Allowances are typically applied as a *percentage of Normal Time*:

$$ST = NT + Allowances$$

Converting the allowance to its fractional form:

$$Allowances = NT \times AF$$

Substituting back:

$$ST = NT + (NT \times AF)$$

#### Step 5: Factor Out the Normal Time

$$ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$$

**Logical Note:** This is the *final, exam-ready* form. It is the most compact expression and the one KTU examiners expect to see.

#### Step 6: Combine All Three Steps into the Master Equation

Substituting $NT = T_{obs} \times PR$ into $ST$:

$$ST = (T_{obs} \times PR) \times (1 + AF)$$

**Logical Note:** This is the **single most important equation** in the entire Module 3 syllabus. It collapses the three-stage process (observe → rate → add allowance) into one line.

#### Step 7: Special Case — Pure Machine Elements

For *machine-controlled* elements (e.g., a CNC milling cycle), the machine runs at its own fixed speed; the operator's *skill does not affect the cycle time*. Therefore, the rating factor is bypassed:

$$ST_{machine} = T_{obs} \times (1 + AF_{machine})$$

where $PR$ is omitted because the machine *itself* sets the pace.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Linking Each Purpose to a Specific Formula

The following matrix maps **every** purpose of work measurement to the **specific formula** that supports it. This is a high-yield KTU exam resource.

| Purpose of Work Measurement | Primary Formula Used | Practical Application |
|------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| Determine Standard Cost | $C = ST \times Wage\ Rate$ | Per-unit labor cost for a quotation |
| Set Daily Output Target | $SO = \dfrac{Time\ Available}{ST}$ | How many pieces to produce per shift |
| Compute Bonus (Halsey Plan) | $Bonus = 0.5 \times Time\ Saved \times Rate$ | Incentive payment calculation |
| Compute Bonus (Rowan Plan) | $Bonus = \dfrac{Time\ Saved}{ST} \times Time\ Saved \times Rate$ | Proportional incentive |
| Line Balancing | Identify $\max(ST_i)$ per station | Determines the line's cycle time (bottleneck) |
| Manpower Planning | $N = \dfrac{ST \times Demand}{Available\ Hours}$ | Number of workers to hire |
| Compare Two Methods | $ST_A$ vs $ST_B$ | Choose the method with lower ST |
| Machine Utilization | $Util = \dfrac{Loading\ Time}{Available\ Time}$ | Load machines up to their ST capacity |

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Exam-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An IE observes a worker performing a task. The average observed time per cycle is **$1.20$ minutes**. The IE rates the worker at **$110\%$** of normal. The relaxation allowance is **$15\%$** of the normal time. Compute the **Standard Time** per cycle.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Compute Normal Time.

$$NT = T_{obs} \times PR = 1.20 \times 1.10$$

$$NT = 1.32 \text{ minutes}$$

Step 2: Identify Allowance Factor.

$$AF = 15\% = 0.15$$

Step 3: Compute Standard Time.

$$ST = NT \times (1 + AF) = 1.32 \times (1 + 0.15)$$

$$ST = 1.32 \times 1.15$$

$$ST = 1.518 \text{ minutes per cycle}$$

**Step 4: Convert to standard output per 8-hour shift (480 minutes):**

$$SO = \frac{480}{1.518} \approx 316.2 \text{ units/shift}$$

**Conclusion:** The standard time per cycle is **$1.518$ minutes**, and the worker should produce approximately **$316$ pieces per shift**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart: From Observation to Standard Time

The diagram below shows the **three-stage transformation** that every work measurement study follows — from raw stopwatch reading to the final Standard Time used in industry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Worker Performs Task"] --> B["IE Records Time with Stopwatch"]
    B --> C["Observed Time T obs in minutes"]
    C --> D["IE Applies Performance Rating PR"]
    D --> E["Normal Time NT = T obs times PR"]
    E --> F["Add Allowances Rest, Personal, Contingency"]
    F --> G["Standard Time ST = NT times 1 plus AF"]
    G --> H["ST Feeds into Production Planning"]
    H --> I["ST Feeds into Costing and Incentives"]
    I --> J["ST Feeds into Line Balancing"]
    J --> K["Continuous Improvement Cycle"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: 10 Purposes of Work Measurement

The schematic below organizes the **ten principal purposes** of work measurement into four functional domains, as required by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WM["Work Measurement Engine"] --> P1["1. Production Planning"]
    WM --> P2["2. Costing and Budgeting"]
    WM --> P3["3. Method Comparison"]
    WM --> P4["4. Incentive Wage Schemes"]
    WM --> P5["5. Line Balancing"]
    WM --> P6["6. Manpower Planning"]
    WM --> P7["7. Machine Loading"]
    WM --> P8["8. Performance Evaluation"]
    WM --> P9["9. Workload Forecasting"]
    WM --> P10["10. Training Need Identification"]

    P1 --> D1["Domain: PPC and Operations"]
    P2 --> D2["Domain: Finance and Costing"]
    P3 --> D3["Domain: Methods Engineering"]
    P4 --> D3
    P5 --> D1
    P6 --> D1
    P7 --> D1
    P8 --> D4["Domain: Human Resources"]
    P9 --> D1
    P10 --> D4
```

### 4.3 Architecture Diagram: How Standard Time Powers an Entire Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs["Inputs to Work Measurement"]
        I1["Stopwatch Time Study"]
        I2["Predetermined Motion Time Systems PMTS"]
        I3["Work Sampling"]
        I4["Standard Data Systems"]
    end

    subgraph Core["Core Computation Engine"]
        C1["Observed Time Tobs"]
        C2["Performance Rating PR"]
        C3["Allowance Factor AF"]
        C4["Standard Time ST = Tobs x PR x 1 + AF"]
    end

    subgraph Outputs["Organizational Outputs Powered by ST"]
        O1["Production Schedules and Takt Time"]
        O2["Standard Costs and Budgets"]
        O3["Incentive Bonus Calculations"]
        O4["Balanced Assembly Lines"]
        O5["Manpower and Machine Plans"]
        O6["Performance Appraisals"]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C1
    I4 --> C1
    C1 --> C4
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> O1
    C4 --> O2
    C4 --> O3
    C4 --> O4
    C4 --> O5
    C4 --> O6
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> *"Define Work Measurement. List any four purposes of Work Measurement."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Work Measurement is the application of techniques designed to establish the time required for a qualified worker, working at a defined level of performance, to carry out a specified task under specified conditions. *(KTU expects this exact BSI-style phrasing.)*
>
> **Four Purposes (0.25 Mark each = 1 Mark):**
> 1. To determine standard times for production planning and control.
> 2. To establish standard labor costs for each product.
> 3. To compare alternative methods of doing a task and select the most economical one.
> 4. To provide a basis for wage incentive schemes such as Halsey and Rowan.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
> *"Distinguish between Normal Time and Standard Time with suitable formulas."*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Normal Time (1.5 Marks):** It is the time a *qualified worker* would take to perform the task at a *normal pace*. It is computed as $NT = OT \times PR$, where $OT$ is the observed time and $PR$ is the performance rating expressed as a decimal.
>
> **Standard Time (1.5 Marks):** It is the time a worker is *allowed* to complete the task, which includes Normal Time plus all applicable allowances (for rest, personal needs, and contingencies). It is computed as $ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$, where $AF$ is the allowance factor.
>
> **Key Difference:** Normal Time excludes allowances; Standard Time includes them. Standard Time is the *operational* value used in planning, costing, and incentives.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]**

> **a) [7 Marks]** *"Explain in detail the various purposes of Work Measurement in an industrial organization. Categorize them under Production, Costing, and Human Resource domains."*
>
> **b) [7 Marks]** *"During a stopwatch time study, the average observed time for an operation is 0.85 minutes. The worker was rated at 90% of normal performance. The company provides a relaxation allowance of 12% of the normal time. Calculate (i) Normal Time, (ii) Standard Time per cycle, and (iii) the expected standard output per 8-hour shift. If the worker is paid ₹25 per hour, determine the standard labor cost per piece."*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Detailed Purposes (7 Marks):**

1. **Production Planning & Control (2 Marks):** Work measurement provides realistic time standards that form the basis of route sheets, Gantt charts, machine loading, and delivery commitments. Without ST, schedules are based on guesswork.
2. **Costing & Budgeting (2 Marks):** $C = ST \times Wage\ Rate$ gives the standard labor cost per unit, used in product pricing, quotation preparation, and budgetary control.
3. **Wage Incentive Schemes (1.5 Marks):** Time saved beyond standard is the basis of bonus calculation in Halsey, Rowan, Bedaux, and Emerson incentive plans.
4. **Human Resource Functions (1.5 Marks):** ST is used for performance evaluation, identifying training needs, manpower planning, and determining the number of workers required for a given production target.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Working (Examiner-Ready):**

Given Data:

$$T_{obs} = 0.85 \text{ min}, \quad PR = 0.90, \quad AF = 0.12, \quad Wage\ Rate = ₹25/hr$$

**(i) Normal Time (1 Mark):**

$$NT = T_{obs} \times PR = 0.85 \times 0.90 = 0.765 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Standard Time (1.5 Marks):**

$$ST = NT \times (1 + AF) = 0.765 \times (1 + 0.12)$$

$$ST = 0.765 \times 1.12 = 0.8568 \text{ min per piece}$$

**(iii) Standard Output per 8-hour shift (1.5 Marks):**

$$Available\ Time = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ min}$$

$$SO = \frac{480}{0.8568} \approx 560.22 \text{ pieces per shift}$$

**(iv) Standard Labor Cost per piece (2 Marks):**

$$ST\ in\ hours = \frac{0.8568}{60} = 0.01428 \text{ hours}$$

$$C = ST \times Wage\ Rate = 0.01428 \times 25 = ₹0.357 \text{ per piece}$$

> **Final Answer:** $NT = 0.765$ min, $ST = 0.8568$ min, $SO \approx 560$ pieces, $C = ₹0.357$/piece.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

> **a) [7 Marks]** *"With a neat block diagram, explain the step-by-step procedure to compute Standard Time from observed time using a stopwatch study. State clearly the role of Performance Rating and Allowances."*
>
> **b) [7 Marks]** *"A company has recorded the following data for a manual assembly task: Observed time = 1.50 min, Performance rating = 105%, Allowance = 20% of basic time. (i) Find the Normal Time and Standard Time. (ii) If the company has 25 workers working 8 hours per day, find the total standard output per day. (iii) If the demand is 4000 units per week (5 working days), determine how many additional workers are required. Assume 1 week = 5 days."*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Step 1: Record Observed Time (1 Mark):** Use a stopwatch to record the time for each element of the task across multiple cycles. Compute the average.
2. **Step 2: Apply Performance Rating (2 Marks):** The IE rates the operator's pace against a standard (e.g., $100\%$ = normal walking pace). $PR > 1$ means faster than normal; $PR < 1$ means slower. $NT = OT \times PR$.
3. **Step 3: Determine Allowances (2 Marks):** Add relaxation allowance for personal needs, rest, and contingency. Common values: $10\%$–$20\%$ of NT.
4. **Step 4: Compute Standard Time (2 Marks):** $ST = NT \times (1 + AF)$. This is the final value used by the organization for all downstream applications.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

Given:

$$T_{obs} = 1.50 \text{ min}, \quad PR = 1.05, \quad AF = 0.20, \quad Workers = 25, \quad Shift = 8 \text{ hr}$$

**(i) Normal Time & Standard Time (2 Marks):**

$$NT = 1.50 \times 1.05 = 1.575 \text{ min}$$

$$ST = 1.575 \times (1 + 0.20) = 1.575 \times 1.20 = 1.89 \text{ min per piece}$$

**(ii) Daily Output with 25 Workers (2 Marks):**

$$Available\ Time\ per\ Worker = 480 \text{ min}$$

$$Output\ per\ Worker = \frac{480}{1.89} \approx 254.0 \text{ pieces/day}$$

$$Total\ Daily\ Output = 25 \times 254.0 = 6350 \text{ pieces/day}$$

**(iii) Additional Workers Needed (3 Marks):**

$$Weekly\ Demand = 4000 \text{ units in 5 days} \Rightarrow Daily\ Demand = 800 \text{ units/day}$$

$$Workers\ Required = \frac{800}{254.0} \approx 3.15 \Rightarrow 4 \text{ workers (rounded up)}$$

> **Note:** $3.15$ must be rounded *up* to the next whole worker (you cannot employ a fraction of a worker). So, $4$ workers are sufficient.

$$Additional\ Workers = 4 - 25 = -21$$

**Conclusion:** The company has *excess* capacity. It does not need any additional workers — in fact, 21 workers are surplus and can be redeployed to other tasks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Work Measurement Questions:**
> 1. **Performance Rating Error ($-1$ to $-2$ Marks):** Students frequently forget to *multiply* by PR or treat it as a percentage when it should be a decimal. Always write $PR = 1.10$ (not $110\%$) inside the formula.
> 2. **Allowance Unit Confusion ($-1$ Mark):** Allowances are added to **Normal Time**, not to Observed Time. A common mistake is $ST = OT \times (1 + AF)$ — this is **wrong**. The correct sequence is: $OT \to NT$ (apply PR) $\to ST$ (apply Allowances).
> 3. **Rounding Error in Workforce Planning ($-1$ Mark):** When computing the number of workers, always **round up** to the next whole number. Hiring $3.15$ workers is impossible.
> 4. **Omitting Units ($-0.5$ Mark each):** KTU strictly penalizes answers without units. Always write "min," "₹/piece," "pieces/day."
> 5. **Confusing "Basic Time" with "Normal Time":** For *manual* elements, $BT = NT$. For *machine* elements, $BT = OT$ (no PR applied). This is a frequent trick question.
> 6. **Skipping the Rating Step:** Examiners will not award full marks if you jump directly from $OT$ to $ST$ without showing $NT$ as an intermediate step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Purpose of Work Measurement**

- **Definition:** BSI definition is mandatory — *"the application of techniques designed to establish the time required for a qualified worker at a defined level of performance to carry out a specified task."*
- **Central Equation:** $ST = OT \times PR \times (1 + AF)$ — memorize this as one line.
- **Two-stage Conversion:** Observed Time → (multiply by PR) → Normal Time → (multiply by $1+AF$) → Standard Time.
- **Allowances** are *always* applied to **Normal Time**, never to Observed Time.
- **Performance Rating** is the IE's *judgment* of the worker's pace relative to a standard. $PR = 1.00$ is the benchmark.
- **Standard Output:** $SO = \dfrac{Available\ Time}{ST}$ — used to set daily production targets.
- **Standard Cost:** $C = ST \times Wage\ Rate$ — used in product costing and pricing.
- **10 Core Purposes:** Production planning, costing, method comparison, incentives, line balancing, manpower planning, machine loading, performance evaluation, forecasting, training needs.
- **Incentive Formulas to Know:** Halsey ($0.5 \times TS \times R$) and Rowan ($\frac{TS}{ST} \times TS \times R$), where $TS$ = Time Saved = $ST - Time\ Taken$.
- **Line Balancing:** The bottleneck workstation has the highest $ST$; cycle time of line $\geq \max(ST_i)$.
- **Work Sampling vs Time Study:** Work sampling uses *random observations* (statistical), while time study uses *continuous observation* (stopwatch). Both feed into the same ST equation.
- **PMTS** (e.g., MTM, MOST) are *synthetic* work measurement systems that build ST from basic motion times — useful when stopwatch study is impractical.
- **Examiner's Pet Trick:** Watch out for the question *"Is Standard Time inclusive of allowances?"* — Answer: **Yes**, by definition.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Basic Procedure # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Basic Procedure (of Method Study)** refers to the standardized, universally accepted sequence of logical steps prescribed by the International Labour Organization (ILO) for conducting a systematic Method Study investigation. It is the spine of every method improvement project and forms the backbone of KTU Module 3. The **Basic Procedure of Method Study** consists of **seven (7) sequential steps** that an Industrial Engineer follows to investigate, analyze, and improve a work method: | Step No. | Stage Name | Nature of Activity | |----------|-----------|-------------------| | 1 | **Select** | Identify the problem/process to be studied | | 2 | **Record** | Gather data using process charts and recording techniques | | 3 | **Examine** | Analyze the recorded facts critically | | 4 | **Develop** | Formulate the improved/new method | | 5 | **Define** | Specify the proposed method in detail | | 6 | **Install** | Implement the new method in practice | | 7 | **Maintain** | Monitor, sustain, and improve further | > [!NOTE] > The 7-step procedure is sometimes grouped into **3 phases**: > - **Pre-investigation phase:** Select, Record > - **Investigation phase:** Examine, Develop, Define > - **Implementation phase:** Install, Maintain ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "House Renovation" Intuition Imagine you want to renovate your old kitchen to make cooking faster and easier. - **Select** 🏠 → You first decide: *"Yes, the kitchen needs a redesign."* - **Record** 📹 → You take photographs, videos, and measure the current layout. - **Examine** 🔍 → You watch the videos and ask: *"Why am I walking 10 steps just to get a plate?"* - **Develop** 💡 → You sketch a new layout where the stove is next to the shelf. - **Define** 📐 → You write a precise blueprint with measurements and materials. - **Install** 🔨 → You actually build and shift the stove, shelves, and counter. - **Maintain** 🔧 → You clean daily, oil the hinges, and keep tweaking for comfort. The kitchen is the **workplace**, the cooking routine is the **process**, and you are the **Industrial Engineer**. The Basic Procedure ensures no step is skipped — otherwise the renovation (or process improvement) will fail. ## 1.3 Key Principles & Governing Body - **Authoritative Source:** International Labour Organization (ILO), Geneva. - **Standard Reference:** ILO *"Introduction to Work Study"*, Geneva (latest edition). - **Core Aim:** To find the **"One Best Way"** (OBW) of doing a job — a principle originally championed by **Frederick Winslow Taylor** and later refined by the ILO. - **Universal Applicability:** Manufacturing, services, healthcare, banking, and government offices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For end-semester answers, always mention that the Basic Procedure is an **ILO-recommended standardized procedure** used universally for systematic method improvement. --- # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Step-Wise Theoretical Breakdown ### 🔹 Step 1 — SELECT **The "Why":** You cannot improve everything at once. Resources (time, money, manpower) are limited. **The "How":** - Identify the work area/process with the greatest scope for savings. - Use criteria like **economic impact**, **bottleneck severity**, **frequency of occurrence**, and **management priority**. - Apply **Pareto Analysis (80/20 rule)**: 20% of operations often cause 80% of the problems. - Obtain **management approval** before proceeding. > [!TIP] > **Selection Criteria Mnemonic — "E-F-B-M":** Economic impact, Frequency, Bottleneck severity, Management priority. --- ### 🔹 Step 2 — RECORD **The "Why":** To analyze facts, you first need accurate facts. Intuition and memory are unreliable. **The "How":** - Gather information about the existing method using the **5 W's and 1 H**: - **What** is being done? - **Why** is it being done? - **When** is it being done? - **Where** is it being done? - **Who** is doing it? - **How** is it being done? - Use **Recording Techniques** (Tools of Method Study): | Recording Tool | Best Used For | KTU Module 3 Reference | |---------------|--------------|------------------------| | Outline Process Chart | Overall process (5–50 steps) | Module 3 | | Flow Process Chart (Man/Material) | Detailed sequence with movement | Module 3 | | Two-Handed Process Chart | Micro-motion study of both hands | Module 3 | | Multiple Activity Chart | Team/operator-machine coordination | Module 3 | | Travel Chart | Spatial movement between departments | Module 3 | | Flow Diagram | Path of subject on shop floor | Module 3 | | String Diagram | Spatially accurate movement patterns | Module 3 | --- ### 🔹 Step 3 — EXAMINE **The "Why":** Simply recording is not enough — the data must be challenged. **The "How":** - Apply the **Questioning Technique** for every activity: 1. **Purpose** — What is achieved? 2. **Place** — Where is it done? 3. **Sequence** — When is it done? 4. **Person** — Who does it? 5. **Means** — How is it done? - Eliminate unnecessary motions using **therbligs** analysis. - Identify and classify each activity as **Operation, Inspection, Transport, Delay, or Storage**. --- ### 🔹 Step 4 — DEVELOP **The "Why":** Based on examination, practical alternatives are generated. **The "How":** - Apply **Principles of Motion Economy**: - Both hands should begin & end motions simultaneously. - Motions should be **symmetrical and simultaneous**. - Use **minimum distance** for movements. - Tools/materials should be **pre-positioned**. - Apply **Principles of Work Design** (eliminate, combine, rearrange, simplify). - Generate multiple alternatives and use **creativity techniques** (brainstorming). --- ### 🔹 Step 5 — DEFINE **The "Why":** A method that exists only in the head cannot be installed. **The "How":** - Specify the proposed method using: - **Drawings** (layout, flow diagram). - **Charts** (updated process chart). - **Written instructions** (Standard Operating Procedure - SOP). - Calculate **cost-benefit analysis** and **savings projections**. --- ### 🔹 Step 6 — INSTALL **The "Why":** Plans without execution have zero value. **The "How":** - Get **acceptance** from supervisors, workers, and trade unions. - Train the workforce on the new method. - Phase the implementation (pilot → full rollout). - Address resistance by involving operators in the design phase. --- ### 🔹 Step 7 — MAINTAIN **The "Why":** Without follow-up, people drift back to old habits (backsliding). **The "How":** - Conduct **periodic audits**. - Standardize the new method through **Standing Instructions**. - Reward compliance and continuous improvement. - Watch for **regression to the mean**. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > While the "Basic Procedure" itself is procedural, certain **derived quantitative metrics** frequently appear in KTU numerical problems based on this topic. | Metric / Symbol | Formula | Unit | Description | |----------------|---------|------|-------------| | **Total Activity Time (TAT)** | $TAT = \sum T_{O} + \sum T_{I} + \sum T_{Tr} + \sum T_{D} + \sum T_{S}$ | seconds (s) | Sum of all operation/inspection/transport/delay/storage times | | **Value-Adding Time (VAT)** | $VAT = \sum T_{O}$ | seconds (s) | Time spent on operations only | | **Non-Value-Adding Time (NVAT)** | $NVAT = TAT - VAT$ | seconds (s) | All non-productive time | | **Value-Added Ratio (VAR)** | $VAR = \dfrac{VAT}{TAT} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Higher is better | | **Activity Ratio** | $AR = \dfrac{\text{Productive Activities}}{\text{Total Activities}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | KPI of method efficiency | | **Number of Activities Reduction** | $\Delta N = N_{old} - N_{new}$ | count | Direct savings in steps | | **Time Saving Percentage** | $\% S = \dfrac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Used in process improvement | | **Cycle Time (CT)** | $CT = \dfrac{\text{Available Time}}{\text{Demand}}$ | minutes | Used post-improvement | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Industrial Utility - 🏭 **Toyota Production System (TPS):** Standardized work procedures are direct descendants of the ILO 7-step method. - 🏥 **Healthcare:** Used to redesign patient flow in hospitals, reducing waiting time. - 🏦 **Banking:** ATM process re-engineering uses flowcharting from Step 2. - 💻 **Software:** UX (User Experience) design follows a similar select→record→examine flow. - 🛒 **E-commerce Warehouses:** Order-picking paths are optimized using Travel Charts & Flow Diagrams. --- # 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix of the Basic Procedure The 7-step Basic Procedure is analyzed below with respect to **inputs, tools used, outputs, and key questions answered** at each stage. $$ \text{Stage}_{\text{Output}} = f(\text{Stage}_{\text{Input}}, \text{Stage}_{\text{Tool}}) $$ | Step | Stage | Input Required | Tools Used | Output / Deliverable | Key Question Answered | |------|-------|---------------|------------|----------------------|------------------------| | 1 | Select | Management priorities, data | Pareto chart, flowcharts | Defined scope of study | *"Where should we focus?"* | | 2 | Record | Observed facts | Process charts, diagrams | Objective data set | *"What is currently happening?"* | | 3 | Examine | Recorded data | Questioning technique, charts | List of inefficiencies | *"Why is it happening?"* | | 4 | Develop | Examined facts | Motion economy, creativity | New method proposals | *"What can be done?"* | | 5 | Define | Proposed method | Layouts, SOPs, cost data | Final blueprint | *"How exactly should it be done?"* | | 6 | Install | Defined method | Training, scheduling | Implemented new method | *"How do we make it happen?"* | | 7 | Maintain | Installed method | Audits, feedback | Sustained improvement | *"How do we keep it working?"* | --- ## 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Procedure (with derivation logic) ### 🔹 STEP 1 — SELECT (Scope Identification Logic) $$ \text{Score}_{i} = w_{1}E_{i} + w_{2}F_{i} + w_{3}B_{i} + w_{4}M_{i} $$ where: - $E_{i}$ = Economic impact score of activity $i$ - $F_{i}$ = Frequency of occurrence score of activity $i$ - $B_{i}$ = Bottleneck severity score of activity $i$ - $M_{i}$ = Management priority score of activity $i$ -
