$w_{1}, w_{2}, w_{3}, w_{4}$ = weights assigned ($w_{1} + w_{2} + w_{3} + w_{4} = 1$) - $i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n$ (number of activities) **Logic:** The activity with the highest weighted score is selected first because it offers the maximum return on the engineer's time. --- ### 🔹 STEP 2 — RECORD (Time-Activity Decomposition) The total time of a process is decomposed as: $$ TAT = T_{Op} + T_{In} + T_{Tr} + T_{Dl} + T_{St} $$ where: - $T_{Op}$ = Total time spent on **Operations** (the only value-adding activities) - $T_{In}$ = Total time spent on **Inspections** (necessary but non-value-adding) - $T_{Tr}$ = Total time spent on **Transportation** (movement of materials) - $T_{Dl}$ = Total time spent on **Delays** (waiting, idle time) - $T_{St}$ = Total time spent on **Storage** (holdings in inventory) **Logic:** Each activity is classified using the standardized **process chart symbols**: | Symbol | Name | Meaning | Counted in $TAT$? | |--------|------|---------|-------------------| | $\bigcirc$ | Operation | Modifying a workpiece / creating value | Yes | | $\square$ | Inspection | Verifying quality/quantity | Yes | | $\rightarrow$ | Transport | Moving material from place to place | Yes | | $\text{D}$ | Delay | Workpiece waiting/idle | Yes | | $\nabla$ | Storage | Stocked for later use | Yes | **Derived Metric — Value-Added Ratio:** $$ VAR = \frac{T_{Op}}{T_{Op} + T_{In} + T_{Tr} + T_{Dl} + T_{St}} \times 100 $$ > [!TIP] > A **healthy manufacturing process** typically has $VAR \geq 40\%$. Anything below 25% indicates severe process waste. --- ### 🔹 STEP 3 — EXAMINE (Critical Questioning Framework) For each recorded activity, the engineer applies the **dual-question technique**: $$ Q_{activity} = \bigcup_{k=1}^{5} \{Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person, Means\} $$ The questioning hierarchy is: $$ \text{Eliminate} \rightarrow \text{Combine} \rightarrow \text{Re-arrange} \rightarrow \text{Simplify} $$ (ECRS — the famous Japanese Kaizen principle) **Logic:** If an activity fails to contribute to the customer or to the next operation, it is a candidate for **elimination**. --- ### 🔹 STEP 4 — DEVELOP (Alternative Generation) The number of method alternatives generated can be modeled as: $$ N_{alt} = \prod_{j=1}^{m} c_{j} $$ where: - $N_{alt}$ = Number of possible alternative methods - $c_{j}$ = Number of choices available for design variable $j$ - $m$ = Number of design variables **Example:** If you have 4 layout choices ($c_{1}=4$), 3 tool options ($c_{2}=3$), and 2 worker assignments ($c_{3}=2$): $$ N_{alt} = 4 \times 3 \times 2 = 24 \text{ possible methods} $$ The engineer then evaluates each alternative on three criteria: $$ \text{Effectiveness} = f(\text{Cost}, \text{Time}, \text{Quality}, \text{Safety}) $$ --- ### 🔹 STEP 5 — DEFINE (Quantification of Savings) $$ \% \text{Saving in Time} = \frac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100 $$ $$ \% \text{Saving in Cost} = \frac{C_{old} - C_{new}}{C_{old}} \times 100 $$ **Logic:** These two metrics must be balanced — a time saving that increases cost is unacceptable. $$ \text{Payback Period} = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} $$ --- ### 🔹 STEP 6 — INSTALL (Adoption Rate Model) $$ A(t) = A_{max} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right) $$ where: - $A(t)$ = Adoption rate at time $t$ - $A_{max}$ = Maximum possible adoption (typically 100%) - $k$ = Adoption rate constant (depends on training, management support) - $t$ = Time elapsed since installation **Logic:** This is the classical **Bass Diffusion Model** (simplified). It tells us that adoption grows rapidly at first, then saturates. --- ### 🔹 STEP 7 — MAINTAIN (Compliance Index) $$ CI = \frac{N_{compliant}}{N_{audited}} \times 100 $$ where: - $N_{compliant}$ = Number of work cycles found compliant with SOP - $N_{audited}$ = Total number of cycles audited - $CI$ = Compliance Index (target $\geq 95\%$) **Sustainability check:** $$ \text{If } CI_{t+1} < CI_{t} \rightarrow \text{Backsliding detected, intervention required.} $$ --- # 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The 7-Step Basic Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD A["STEP 1 SELECT"]:::stage --> B["STEP 2 RECORD"]:::stage B --> C["STEP 3 EXAMINE"]:::stage C --> D["STEP 4 DEVELOP"]:::stage D --> E["STEP 5 DEFINE"]:::stage E --> F["STEP 6 INSTALL"]:::stage F --> G["STEP 7 MAINTAIN"]:::stage G --> H{"CI less than 95%?"}:::decision H -- YES --> C H -- NO --> I["SUSTAINED IMPROVEMENT"]:::output A -. Uses .-> A1["Pareto Chart, EFB Matrix"]:::tool B -. Uses .-> B1["Process Charts, Flow Diagrams"]:::tool C -. Uses .-> C1["Questioning, ECRS, Therbligs"]:::tool D -. Uses .-> D1["Motion Economy, Creativity"]:::tool E -. Uses .-> E1["Layouts, SOP, Cost Data"]:::tool F -. Uses .-> F1["Training, Pilot Run"]:::tool G -. Uses .-> G1["Audits, Feedback Loop"]:::tool classDef stage fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef tool fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Method Study ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["INPUT DOMAIN"] I1["Existing Process"]:::inp I2["Management Goal"]:::inp I3["Operator Knowledge"]:::inp end subgraph Process["METHOD STUDY ENGINE"] S1["1 SELECT"]:::proc S2["2 RECORD"]:::proc S3["3 EXAMINE"]:::proc S4["4 DEVELOP"]:::proc S5["5 DEFINE"]:::proc S6["6 INSTALL"]:::proc S7["7 MAINTAIN"]:::proc end subgraph Output["OUTPUT DOMAIN"] O1["Improved Process"]:::out O2["Standard SOPs"]:::out O3["Quantified Savings"]:::out O4["Trained Workforce"]:::out end I1 --> S1 I2 --> S1 I3 --> S2 S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 S5 --> O1 S5 --> O2 S5 --> O3 S6 --> O4 S7 -. Feedback .-> S3 classDef inp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Process Chart Symbols — Visual Quick Reference ```mermaid flowchart LR A1(("OPERATION
○")):::op B2["INSPECTION
□"]:::ins C3(("TRANSPORT
→")):::tr D4[/"DELAY
D"/]:::dl E5[["STORAGE
▽"]]:::st A1 --- B2 --- C3 --- D4 --- E5 classDef op fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ins fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tr fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dl fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef st fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- # 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]** ### Q1. List and briefly explain any six steps of the Basic Procedure of Method Study. **Model Answer:** The Basic Procedure of Method Study consists of seven standardized steps recommended by the International Labour Organization (ILO): 1. **Select** the work to be studied. 2. **Record** all relevant facts using process charts. 3. **Examine** the recorded facts critically. 4. **Develop** a better method. 5. **Define** the new method precisely. 6. **Install** the new method as a standard practice. 7. **Maintain** the new method through regular audits. *(3 marks: 1 for list + 1 for brief description + 1 for ILO reference)* --- ### Q2. State the five symbols used in process chart recording along with their meanings. **Model Answer:** | Symbol | Name | Meaning | |--------|------|---------| | $\bigcirc$ | Operation | Modifying a workpiece / adding value | | $\square$ | Inspection | Verifying quality or quantity | | $\rightarrow$ | Transportation | Movement of material | | $\text{D}$ | Delay | Workpiece waiting or idle | | $\nabla$ | Storage | Workpiece held for future use | *(3 marks: 1.5 for symbols + 1.5 for meanings)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Pattern]** --- ### ✍️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### **(a)** Explain in detail the seven-step Basic Procedure of Method Study as recommended by the ILO. *(7 marks)* #### **(b)** An operator in a manufacturing cell performs the following sequence of activities in a cycle: | Step | Activity | Symbol | Time (s) | |------|----------|--------|---------| | 1 | Pick raw material from bin | Operation | 5 | | 2 | Inspect diameter | Inspection | 8 | | 3 | Move to lathe | Transport | 10 | | 4 | Wait for machine | Delay | 12 | | 5 | Machine turning | Operation | 30 | | 6 | Inspect surface finish | Inspection | 6 | | 7 | Move to packing | Transport | 8 | | 8 | Place in storage bin | Storage | 5 | Draw the **man-type flow process chart** and calculate: (i) Total cycle time (ii) Value-Added Ratio (VAR) (iii) If the engineer redesigns the process to eliminate 1 transport, 1 delay, and reduce the operation time from 30 s to 20 s, find the **new VAR** and the **% time saving**. *(7 marks)* --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A #### **(a) Seven-Step Basic Procedure (7 marks):** **Step 1 — SELECT (1 mark):** Identify the work area to be studied using criteria such as economic impact, frequency, bottleneck severity, and management priority. Use Pareto analysis to focus on the vital few problems. **Step 2 — RECORD (1 mark):** Collect objective facts about the present method using recording techniques such as outline process chart, flow process chart, two-handed process chart, multiple activity chart, travel chart, and flow diagram. Apply the 5W1H framework. **Step 3 — EXAMINE (1 mark):** Subject each recorded activity to critical scrutiny using the questioning technique — Purpose, Place, Sequence, Person, Means. Apply ECRS (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify). **Step 4 — DEVELOP (1 mark):** Generate improved method alternatives by applying the principles of motion economy and work design. **Step 5 — DEFINE (1 mark):** Precisely describe the proposed method through drawings, updated charts, SOPs, and quantify cost-benefit. **Step 6 — INSTALL (1 mark):** Implement the new method with management approval, worker training, and pilot trials. **Step 7 — MAINTAIN (1 mark):** Sustain the new method through audits, compliance checks, and continuous improvement (Kaizen) loops. --- #### **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Classify Activities:** | Step | Symbol | Type | Time (s) | |------|--------|------|---------| | 1 | $\bigcirc$ | Operation | 5 | | 2 | $\square$ | Inspection | 8 | | 3 | $\rightarrow$ | Transport | 10 | | 4 | $\text{D}$ | Delay | 12 | | 5 | $\bigcirc$ | Operation | 30 | | 6 | $\square$ | Inspection | 6 | | 7 | $\rightarrow$ | Transport | 8 | | 8 | $\nabla$ | Storage | 5 | **Step 2 — Compute Total Cycle Time (1.5 marks):** $$ TAT = 5 + 8 + 10 + 12 + 30 + 6 + 8 + 5 = 84 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Original VAR (1.5 marks):** $$ T_{Op} = 5 + 30 = 35 \text{ s} $$ $$ VAR_{old} = \frac{35}{84} \times 100 = 41.67\% $$ **Step 4 — Redesigned Method:** - Eliminated 1 transport: save 8 s - Eliminated 1 delay: save 12 s - Operation time reduced from 30 s to 20 s: save 10 s $$ TAT_{new} = 84 - 8 - 12 - 10 = 54 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 5 — New VAR (1.5 marks):** $$ T_{Op,new} = 5 + 20 = 25 \text{ s} $$ $$ VAR_{new} = \frac{25}{54} \times 100 = 46.30\% $$ **Step 6 — Percentage Time Saving (1.5 marks):** $$ \% S = \frac{84 - 54}{84} \times 100 = 35.71\% $$ **Conclusion:** The redesigned method achieves a **35.71% reduction in cycle time** and improves the value-added ratio by ~4.6 percentage points. --- ### ✍️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### **(a)** Explain the significance of the "Examine" step in Method Study. How does the ECRS principle help here? *(7 marks)* #### **(b)** The following data was collected for a parcel delivery warehouse over 8 hours. Compare the productivity of two workers using: | Worker | Packages Handled | Idle Time (min) | |--------|-----------------|-----------------| | A | 220 | 95 | | B | 260 | 60 | Calculate and compare their **Activity Ratio** and comment on who is more productive according to the Basic Procedure framework. *(7 marks)* --- ### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B #### **(a) Significance of the Examine Step (7 marks):** The "Examine" step is the analytical heart of Method Study. After recording facts objectively in Step 2, the engineer must critically analyze them. This is achieved through: **The Questioning Technique (3 marks):** For every activity, five sub-questions are asked: - **Purpose** — Why is this done? - **Place** — Why is it done here? - **Sequence** — Why is it done at this time? - **Person** — Why is this person doing it? - **Means** — Why is it done this way? **ECRS Principle (4 marks):** Based on the questions, four actions are taken in order: $$ \text{Eliminate} \rightarrow \text{Combine} \rightarrow \text{Re-arrange} \rightarrow \text{Simplify} $$ 1. **Eliminate** activities that add no value (delays, redundant transport). 2. **Combine** activities where possible (e.g., combine two inspections into one). 3. **Rearrange** the sequence for better flow (e.g., group inspection at one location). 4. **Simplify** the remaining operations using motion economy. ECRS converts raw observations into actionable improvements. --- #### **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute Total Available Time (1 mark):** $$ T_{available} = 8 \times 60 = 480 \text{ minutes} $$ **Step 2 — Productive Time:** For Worker A: $$ T_{prod,A} = 480 - 95 = 385 \text{ min} $$ For Worker B: $$ T_{prod,B} = 480 - 60 = 420 \text{ min} $$ **Step 3 — Activity Ratio (AR) (3 marks):** For Worker A: $$ AR_{A} = \frac{385}{480} \times 100 = 80.21\% $$ For Worker B: $$ AR_{B} = \frac{420}{480} \times 100 = 87.50\% $$ **Step 4 — Productivity per Productive Minute (3 marks):** For Worker A: $$ P_{A} = \frac{220}{385} = 0.571 \text{ packages/min} $$ For Worker B: $$ P_{B} = \frac{260}{420} = 0.619 \text{ packages/min} $$ **Conclusion:** - Worker B has a higher **Activity Ratio** (87.50% vs 80.21%) — better time utilization. - Worker B also has higher **productivity per productive minute** (0.619 vs 0.571). - Therefore, **Worker B is more productive** under both metrics. From a Basic Procedure perspective, Worker A's process would be selected for Step 3 (Examine) to identify and eliminate the cause of the 95 idle minutes. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Errors in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Skipping the ILO reference** — Always mention the **International Labour Organization (ILO)** as the recommending body. (-1 mark if missed). > 2. **Writing "record" and "examine" together** — These are TWO distinct steps; do not merge them. > 3. **Confusing TAT with VAR** — $TAT$ is total time, $VAR$ is a ratio (%). > 4. **Forgetting units** — Always write "seconds" or "minutes" with numeric answers. > 5. **Process chart symbol errors** — Storage is a **triangle ($\nabla$)**, not a square. > 6. **In Numerical:** Not converting hours to minutes before computing the Activity Ratio — a frequent mistake. > 7. **Missing the feedback loop** — Mentioning that Step 7 (Maintain) **feeds back** to Step 3 (Examine) for continuous improvement earns a **bonus mark** in KTU grading. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **📌 Last-Minute Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3):** - ✅ Basic Procedure = **7 steps**: Select, Record, Examine, Develop, Define, Install, Maintain. - ✅ Recommended by the **ILO** (Geneva). - ✅ Phase grouping: **Pre-investigation (1,2)**, **Investigation (3,4,5)**, **Implementation (6,7)**. - ✅ **5W1H** used in Step 2 (Record). - ✅ **Questioning Technique (5Ws of Examination)** used in Step 3. - ✅ **ECRS** (Eliminate, Combine, Rearrange, Simplify) — key tool of Step 3. - ✅ **5 Process Chart Symbols**: $\bigcirc, \square, \rightarrow, \text{D}, \nabla$. - ✅ **Value-Added Ratio** = Operations time / Total time. - ✅ **Activity Ratio** = Productive activities / Total activities. - ✅ **% Time Saving** = $(T_{old} - T_{new}) / T_{old} \times 100$. - ✅ Step 7 (Maintain) feeds back to Step 3 (Examine) — continuous improvement. - ✅ Always quote **practical industrial examples** (Toyota, Hospital, Banking) to gain examiner goodwill. - ✅ Use **structured tables** in answers — KTU examiners reward clarity. - ✅ Remember the **Principle of "One Best Way"** by **F.W. Taylor**. - ✅ Mention **Pareto Analysis** in Step 1 for extra depth. ---

topics.title: Performance Rating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Rating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 3 | PERFORMANCE RATING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance Rating** is the **technique by which an industrial engineer (or time study observer)** evaluates and expresses the **skill and effort effectiveness of a worker** performing a task, expressed as a **percentage of the observer's concept of "Normal Performance."**

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

$$\text{Performance Rating} = \frac{\text{Rated Performance of Worker}}{\text{Normal Performance (Standard)}} \times 100$$

Where:
- **Rated Performance** = The pace, skill, and effort actually demonstrated by the worker during observation.
- **Normal Performance** = A standard benchmark defined as the **pace of a qualified worker working at a normal effort under normal working conditions** (often symbolised as **100% on the BSI scale** or **100 BPM on the pace rating scale**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> Performance Rating is a **sub-stage of Time Study** (part of Work Measurement). It is applied **after the stopwatch time study** but **before computing Normal Time and Standard Time**. It bridges raw observation and the engineered "Standard."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **cricket umpire** and a bowler delivers a ball. You rate the speed of delivery on a scale where a "normal professional pace" is 100%. If the bowler is unusually fast (Shaun Tait in his prime), you may rate him 130%. If the bowler is a club-level spinner, you may rate him 80%.

**Performance Rating in industry works exactly the same way:**
- The **Industrial Engineer is the "Umpire"** → judging pace/effort.
- The **Worker is the "Bowler"** → delivering the actual work task.
- The **"Normal Performance"** is the **umpire's internal benchmark of 100% pace.**

> [!TIP]
> Think of rating as the **"Calibration of the Worker's Effort"** — without rating, every worker's raw time would be treated as identical, which is unfair because a fast worker would be set unachievable standards.

### Key Components of the Concept

- **Standard Rating (100%)**: A worker moving with **average skill** and **average effort** under **average conditions**.
- **Under-Rating**: When the worker is actually faster/effortful but rated low. *(Unfair to worker)*
- **Over-Rating**: When the worker is actually slower but rated high. *(Unfair to organization)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Examiner's Note:** Performance Rating is the **most subjective step** in Time Study. It relies on the **judgement, training, and consistency** of the rater. Hence the question of accuracy is critical in the exam.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Rating Process

The industrial engineer follows a structured logic when rating:

1. **Observe the Worker** continuously during stopwatch time study (using fly-back timing device or cumulative timing).
2. **Compare** the observed worker's pace with the mentally held **concept of "Normal Pace"**.
3. **Identify Skill Level** (does the worker use smooth, automatic motions or hesitating motions?).
4. **Identify Effort Level** (is the worker hustling or coasting?).
5. **Note Conditions** (lighting, temperature, noise — better/worse than normal).
6. **Assign a Numerical Rating** on a chosen scale (100-point scale or 60–80 BPM scale).
7. **Apply the Rating to Obtain Normal Time.**

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU University Exam question on Time Study.**

| **S. No.** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Description / Units** |
|------------|--------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Performance Rating Factor (PRF) | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{\text{Worker's Observed Rate}}{\text{Normal Rate (100\%)}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | Normal Time (NT) | $\text{NT} = \text{Observed Time (OT)} \times \text{PRF}$ | Minutes or seconds |
| 3 | Basic Time (BT) | $\text{BT} = \dfrac{\text{Normal Time}}{1 - \text{Rest Allowance fraction}}$ | After subtracting relaxation allowance |
| 4 | Standard Time (ST) | $\text{ST} = \text{Normal Time} \times (1 + \text{Total Allowance fraction})$ | Final allowed time per cycle |
| 5 | Allowance Factor Addition | $\text{ST} = \text{NT} + (\text{NT} \times \text{Allowance \%})$ | Inclusive form |
| 6 | Westinghouse Multiplier | $\text{Rating} = 1 + S + E + C + I$ | Sum of four factor adjustments |
| 7 | Rating on 60-BPM Scale | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{\text{Observed Pace in BPM}}{60}$ | Barnes 60-80 Scale |
| 8 | Rating on 100-BPM Scale | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{\text{Observed Pace in BPM}}{100}$ | BSI Standard |
| 9 | Overall Rating (Synthesis) | $\text{Rating} = \dfrac{\sum (\text{Skill} + \text{Effort} + \text{Conditions} + \text{Consistency})}{n}$ | Average rater approach |
| 10 | Average Observed Time | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}{n}$ | Arithmetic mean of cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> In all of the above, **Rating values are expressed as decimals when plugged into formulas.** For example, **120% rating** is used as **1.20** in computation, **NOT** as 120.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manpower Planning:** Standard Time × Number of units = Total man-hours required to plan workforce size.
- **Cost Estimation:** Wage cost per unit = Standard Time × Wage rate.
- **Incentive Wage Systems (Halsey, Rowan, Bedeaux):** All incentive plans require an engineered Standard Time as the basis for bonus calculation.
- **Lean Manufacturing:** Standard Time is the foundation for takt time computation and line balancing.
- **Ergonomics & Method Study:** Rating reveals where the **method is poor** (forcing the worker to over-exert).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Normal Time from Observed Time

We start from the **fundamental definition** of the rating concept:

By definition, a worker who **matches the normal pace** should produce a time **identical to the engineer's expected "normal time."** Hence, we may write the proportion:

$$
\frac{\text{Worker's Actual Pace}}{\text{Normal Pace}} = \frac{\text{Expected Time (Normal)}}{\text{Observed Time}}
$$

This is because **a faster worker (high pace) completes the same job in less time**, hence the time relationship is **inversely proportional** to the pace.

Rearranging the proportion:

$$
\text{Normal Time} \times \text{Worker's Actual Pace} = \text{Observed Time} \times \text{Normal Pace}
$$

Dividing both sides by the **Normal Pace** term:

$$
\text{Normal Time} = \text{Observed Time} \times \frac{\text{Worker's Actual Pace}}{\text{Normal Pace}}
$$

The right-hand side fraction is precisely the **Performance Rating Factor (PRF)**:

$$
\boxed{\text{Normal Time} = \text{Observed Time} \times \text{PRF}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Standard Time from Normal Time (Allowance Application)

The worker cannot sustain **100% effort indefinitely.** Allowances are added for:
- **Personal Needs** (~5%)
- **Rest / Fatigue** (~10–15%)
- **Contingencies / Delays** (~5%)

Let $A$ denote the **Total Allowance expressed as a decimal fraction** (e.g., 0.15 for 15%).

By definition:

$$
\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} + \text{Allowance Time}
$$

The allowance time is a **fraction of the normal time** itself:

$$
\text{Allowance Time} = \text{Normal Time} \times A
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} + (\text{Normal Time} \times A)
$$

Factoring out Normal Time:

$$
\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} \times (1 + A)
$$

Substituting the earlier expression for Normal Time:

$$
\boxed{\text{Standard Time} = (\text{Observed Time} \times \text{PRF}) \times (1 + A)}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practice this exact type of question — it is a guaranteed KTU University Exam question.**

**Problem:** During a stopwatch time study on a drilling operation, **5 cycles** were timed: 0.42 min, 0.45 min, 0.40 min, 0.43 min, 0.45 min. The worker was rated at **115%** by the engineer. **Total Allowance** is **18%** of normal time. Compute the **Standard Time**.

### Step 1 — Compute the Average (Mean) Observed Time

$$
\bar{T} = \frac{0.42 + 0.45 + 0.40 + 0.43 + 0.45}{5}
$$

Summing the numerator:

$$
\sum T_i = 0.42 + 0.45 + 0.40 + 0.43 + 0.45 = 2.15 \text{ min}
$$

Dividing by 5:

$$
\bar{T} = \frac{2.15}{5} = 0.43 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 1 Result: Average Observed Time = 0.43 min**

---

### Step 2 — Apply Performance Rating (Convert Percentage to Decimal)

The rating is **115%**. When used in formulas, this is **1.15** (NOT 115).

$$
\text{PRF} = 1.15
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute the Normal Time

Using the derived formula:

$$
\text{NT} = \text{OT} \times \text{PRF}
$$

$$
\text{NT} = 0.43 \times 1.15
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
\text{NT} = 0.4945 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 3 Result: Normal Time ≈ 0.4945 min**

---

### Step 4 — Compute the Standard Time with Allowance

Total Allowance $A = 18\% = 0.18$.

$$
\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times (1 + A)
$$

$$
\text{ST} = 0.4945 \times (1 + 0.18)
$$

$$
\text{ST} = 0.4945 \times 1.18
$$

Multiplying:

$$
\text{ST} = 0.58351 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 4 Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Standard Time} \approx 0.5835 \text{ min/cycle} \approx 35.01 \text{ seconds/cycle}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Computation Flow

| **Stage** | **Input** | **Operation** | **Output** |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|------------|
| Raw data | 5 cycle times (min) | $\bar{T} = \frac{\sum T_i}{n}$ | Average Observed Time |
| Performance | Worker's pace assessment | $\text{PRF} = \dfrac{\text{Rated Pace}}{100}$ | Rating factor (decimal) |
| Normalisation | $\bar{T}$ × PRF | Multiplication | Normal Time |
| Allowance | NT × (1 + A) | Addition of fractional buffer | **Standard Time** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Position of Performance Rating in the Work Measurement Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **complete logical flow** of time study, highlighting where Performance Rating sits:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define the Job to be Studied] --> B[Select the Worker to be Timed]
    B --> C[Conduct Stopwatch Time Study - Observe Cycles]
    C --> D[Record Observed Time OT]
    D --> E[Calculate Average Observed Time]
    E --> F[PERFORMANCE RATING - Judge Worker Pace vs Normal]
    F --> G[Compute Normal Time NT = OT x PRF]
    G --> H[Determine Allowances - Rest, Personal, Contingency]
    H --> I[Compute Standard Time ST = NT x 1 + A]
    I --> J[Issue Engineered Standard to Production]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:3px
    style G fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> The **yellow-highlighted block "F"** represents **Performance Rating**, the pivot between raw observation and engineered standard. **Master this block — every KTU question on time study traces back to this step.**

## 4.3 Three Methods of Performance Rating — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> C[2. Effort Rating Method]
    A --> D[3. Synthetic / Westinghouse Method]

    B --> B2[Used when worker repeats rhythmic cycle]
    B --> B3[Formula: PRF equals Observed BPM divided by 100]

    C --> C1[Effort judged on scale of 0 to 100 percent]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Performance Rating. Explain the concept of "Normal Performance."**

#### Model Answer:

**Performance Rating** is the **technique used in time study** by which an **industrial engineer** evaluates and expresses the **skill and effort effectiveness of a worker** in comparison to a defined standard, called **Normal Performance**.

**Normal Performance** is the **standard benchmark pace** defined as the rate of working of a **qualified worker** who possesses the **required skill** and works at a **normal effort** under **normal working conditions**. On the universally accepted **100-point scale**, normal performance is rated as **100%** or **1.00**.

The **rating is expressed as a percentage** and is used to convert **Observed Time into Normal Time** using the formula:

$$
\text{Normal Time} = \text{Observed Time} \times \frac{\text{Rating}}{100}
$$

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**List any THREE rating methods. Explain the Westinghouse System in brief.**

#### Model Answer:

The **three main methods of performance rating** are:

1. **Speed Rating Method** — Pace is judged relative to a standard speed (e.g., 100 BPM = normal).
2. **Effort Rating Method** — Effort is judged subjectively on a 0–100% scale (BS 3138).
3. **Synthetic / Westinghouse Method** — Combines four independent factors.

**Westinghouse System:** Developed by **Westinghouse Electric Company**, this method rates a worker on **four independent factors**, each carrying its own adjustment multiplier:

- **Skill** (from Super Skill A1 = +0.15 to Unsatisfactory D2 = -0.22)
- **Effort** (from Super Effort A1 = +0.13 to Unsatisfactory D2 = -0.12)
- **Working Conditions** (from Ideal A = +0.06 to Poor F = -0.07)
- **Consistency** (from Perfect A = +0.04 to Poor F = -0.04)

The **overall rating** is:

$$
\text{Rating} = 1 + (\Delta S + \Delta E + \Delta C + \Delta I)
$$

This makes the Westinghouse System the **most objective and structured** method.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Westinghouse System of Rating** in detail. Discuss each of its **four factors** with their **rating values and adjustment factors**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A worker is observed to complete **6 cycles** with times (in minutes): **1.20, 1.10, 1.15, 1.18, 1.12, 1.14**. The engineer rated the worker at **110%**. **Personal allowance is 7%, rest allowance is 12%, and contingency allowance is 5%**. Determine the **Standard Time per cycle**. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question A (a)

The **Westinghouse System** is the **most systematic and widely used performance rating method**, originally developed to overcome the subjectivity of single-factor rating.

It evaluates **four independent attributes** of a worker, each on its own descriptive scale with **positive or negative numerical adjustment factors**. These factors are **algebraically added** to a base of 1.00 to yield the overall rating.

**The Four Factors:**

**1. SKILL** — Knowledge, training, and dexterity.
- **A1 Super Skill** = +0.15
- **A2 Excellent** = +0.13
- **B1 Good** = +0.11
- **B2 Average** = +0.08
- **C1 Fair** = +0.05
- **C2 Poor** = 0.00
- **D1 Very Poor** = -0.05
- **D2 Unsatisfactory** = -0.22

**2. EFFORT** — Willingness to apply skill effectively.
- **A1 Super Effort** = +0.13
- **A2 Excellent** = +0.12
- **B1 Good** = +0.10
- **B2 Average** = +0.08
- **C1 Fair** = +0.05
- **C2 Poor** = 0.00
- **D1 Very Poor** = -0.04
- **D2 Unsatisfactory** = -0.12

**3. CONDITIONS** — Physical environment — heat, light, ventilation.
- **A Ideal** = +0.06
- **B Excellent** = +0.04
- **C Good** = +0.02
- **D Average** = 0.00
- **E Fair** = -0.03
- **F Poor** = -0.07

**4. CONSISTENCY** — Uniformity of speed during cycle (measured by standard deviation of observed times).
- **A Perfect** = +0.04
- **B Excellent** = +0.03
- **C Good** = +0.01
- **D Average** = 0.00
- **E Fair** = -0.02
- **F Poor** = -0.04

**Final Rating Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Rating} = 1.00 + \Delta S + \Delta E + \Delta C + \Delta I}
$$

**Merits:** Reduces subjectivity; each factor is judged independently; provides a numerical defence in incentive disputes. **Limitations:** Skill and Effort can still overlap in judgement; requires extensive training of the rater.

---

### Model Solution — Question A (b)

**Given:**
- 6 cycle times: 1.20, 1.10, 1.15, 1.18, 1.12, 1.14 min
- Rating = 110% = **1.10 (decimal)**
- Personal Allowance = 7%
- Rest Allowance = 12%
- Contingency Allowance = 5%

**Step 1: Compute the Average Observed Time**

Summing all observed cycle times:

$$
\sum T_i = 1.20 + 1.10 + 1.15 + 1.18 + 1.12 + 1.14 = 6.89 \text{ min}
$$

Dividing by 6:

$$
\bar{T} = \frac{6.89}{6} = 1.1483 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Total Allowance**

$$
A = 7\% + 12\% + 5\% = 24\% = 0.24
$$

**Step 3: Compute Normal Time**

$$
\text{NT} = \bar{T} \times \text{PRF} = 1.1483 \times 1.10
$$

$$
\text{NT} = 1.2632 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Standard Time**

$$
\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times (1 + A)
$$

$$
\text{ST} = 1.2632 \times (1 + 0.24)
$$

$$
\text{ST} = 1.2632 \times 1.24
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
\text{ST} = 1.5663 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Standard Time per cycle} \approx 1.566 \text{ min} \approx 93.98 \text{ seconds}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Distinguish between **Speed Rating and Effort Rating**. Explain the procedure of **Performance Rating** with its merits and demerits. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The observed times for a machining operation over **10 cycles** are: **2.4, 2.6, 2.3, 2.5, 2.4, 2.7, 2.3, 2.5, 2.4, 2.6 minutes**. The worker's pace is **rated 125%**. If the **total allowance is 20%**, compute the **Standard Time per cycle** and the **total standard hours for a 100-piece production lot**. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution — Question B (a)

**Distinction Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Speed Rating** | **Effort Rating** |
|------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Basis | Judged by comparing worker's **pace (speed of motion)** with the standard Normal Pace | Judged by assessing the **effort (will-power) exerted** by the worker |
| Scale | 100 BPM = 100% (BSI Standard) | 0 to 100% subjective scale |
| Reference | Originally Barnes' 60-BPM scale (later standardised to 100 BPM) | Defined by BS 3138 (British Standards) |
| Best Use | Repetitive, **rhythmic operations** (e.g., assembly line) | **Non-rhythmic, varied work** (e.g., tool making, machine set-up) |
| Subjectivity | Moderate | Higher |

**Procedure of Performance Rating:**

1. **Preparation:** Define the job and break it into elements.
2. **Observation:** Time the worker using stopwatch; record cycle times.
3. **Rating:** Compare the observed pace/effort with the standard "Normal" benchmark.
4. **Computation:** Convert observed time to normal time using PRF.
5. **Documentation:** Issue a rating sheet signed by the engineer and supervisor.

**Merits:**
- Converts raw observed time to a fair, comparable "normal" benchmark.
- Essential for incentive wage calculations.
- Helps identify workers needing training or workers exceeding standard.

**Demerits:**
- Highly **subjective**; depends on the rater's training and consistency.
- Different raters may give **different ratings** to the same worker.
- Time-consuming and requires **expensive training of raters**.
- **Rater bias** (personal favouritism) can creep in.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (b)

**Given:**
- 10 cycle times: 2.4, 2.6, 2.3, 2.5, 2.4, 2.7, 2.3, 2.5, 2.4, 2.6 min
- Rating = 125% = **1.25 (decimal)**
- Total Allowance = 20% = **0.20**
- Lot size = 100 pieces

**Step 1: Average Observed Time**

Summing the cycle times:

$$
\sum T_i = 2.4 + 2.6 + 2.3 + 2.5 + 2.4 + 2.7 + 2.3 + 2.5 + 2.4 + 2.6 = 24.7 \text{ min}
$$

Dividing by 10:

$$
\bar{T} = \frac{24.7}{10} = 2.47 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 2: Normal Time**

$$
\text{NT} = \bar{T} \times \text{PRF}
$$

$$
\text{NT} = 2.47 \times 1.25
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
\text{NT} = 3.0875 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 3: Standard Time per Cycle**

$$
\text{ST} = \text{NT} \times (1 + A)
$$

$$
\text{ST} = 3.0875 \times (1 + 0.20)
$$

$$
\text{ST} = 3.0875 \times 1.20
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
\text{ST} = 3.705 \text{ min/cycle}
$$

**Step 4: Total Standard Hours for 100 Pieces**

Converting per-cycle standard time into total time:

$$
\text{Total ST} = 3.705 \times 100 = 370.5 \text{ min}
$$

Converting to hours:

$$
\text{Total ST} = \frac{370.5}{60} = 6.175 \text{ hours}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Standard Time per cycle} = 3.705 \text{ min} \quad ; \quad \text{Total for 100 pieces} = 6.175 \text{ hours}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Using 125 instead of 1.25 in formula** → Loses 1 mark. Always convert percentage rating to **decimal** before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting to add allowances** → Standard Time becomes Normal Time → Wrong answer.
> 3. **Averaging the wrong quantity** → If the question gives **each element's time + each element's rating**, you must compute **Normal Time element-wise and then sum** — do NOT average the normal times of elements with different ratings.
> 4. **Unit mismatch in final answer** → Convert minutes to hours only if asked. Otherwise, retain min/cycle.
> 5. **Confusing Basic Time with Normal Time** → Basic Time = Normal Time / (1 - Rest Allowance). Standard Time uses Total Allowance (Personal + Rest + Contingency).
> 6. **Mixing up Westinghouse grades (B1 vs B2, C1 vs C2)** → Memorise the table carefully; these are distinct grades with different multipliers.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

### Core Conceptual Points

- **Performance Rating** is the **judgement-based step** in time study that converts **Observed Time → Normal Time**.
- **Normal Performance** is the **universal benchmark** — a qualified worker, normal skill, normal effort, normal conditions.
- **The 100% rating (1.00 decimal)** is the **global convention** for Normal Pace.

### Formula Stack (Most Important)

- **Normal Time** = Observed Time × PRF
- **Standard Time** = Normal Time × (1 + Total Allowance)
- **Combined Formula:** Standard Time = (Observed Time × PRF) × (1 + A)
- **Westinghouse Rating** = 1 + ΔSkill + ΔEffort + ΔCondition + ΔConsistency

### Three Methods of Rating

- **Speed Rating** → Compare pace against the standard pace (100 BPM).
- **Effort Rating** → Judge mental effort and willpower (BS 3138).
- **Westinghouse / Synthetic** → Combines Skill, Effort, Conditions, Consistency.

### Westinghouse Quick Grades (Remember These at Minimum)

- **Skill:** A1(+0.15), B2(+0.08), D2(-0.22) — extreme ends.
- **Effort:** A1(+0.13), B2(+0.08), D2(-0.12).
- **Conditions:** A(+0.06), D(0.00), F(-0.07).
- **Consistency:** A(+0.04), D(0.00), F(-0.04).

### Common Numerical Pitfalls

- **Convert percentage to decimal before multiplying.**
- **Allowances are added to Normal Time, NOT to Observed Time.**
- **For element-wise studies with different ratings**, never average Normal Times with a single global PRF.
- **Total Allowance = Personal + Rest/Relaxation + Contingency/Process.**

### Conceptual Pitfalls

- **Don't confuse Rating with Efficiency.** Rating is judged at the time of study; efficiency is measured against an existing standard.
- **Don't confuse Standard Time with Normal Time.** Standard includes allowances; Normal does not.
- **Performance Rating is NOT a measurement — it is a JUDGEMENT.** It is the only human-dependent step in time study.

### One-Line Exam-Ready Definition

> *"Performance Rating is the technique used in time study by which an industrial engineer evaluates and records the skill and effort effectiveness of a worker relative to the observer's concept of normal performance, expressed as a percentage or decimal ratio."*

### Quick Mnemonic — **"SECC"** for Westinghouse Four Factors

- **S**kill
- **E**ffort
- **C**onditions
- **C**onsistency

> "A good rater checks the worker's **SECC-urity** level before stamping the rating."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Normal Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Concept of Normal Time

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Normal Time (NT)** is the time required by a **qualified, trained, and experienced worker** working at a **normal (standard) pace**, under **standard operating conditions**, to perform a specified job or task using the prescribed method. It is a time value **adjusted for worker performance** (i.e., pace rating), but **excluding any allowances** for rest, personal needs, or contingencies.

In formal Industrial Engineering terminology (aligned with KTU 2024 / ILO / IS 8989 standards):

$$
\text{Normal Time} = \text{Average Observed Time} \times \text{Performance Rating Factor}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCPET501 – Module 3):**
> Normal Time is the cornerstone concept bridging raw observed work measurement and the final **Standard Time**. Without Normal Time, industries cannot establish fair production targets, determine wages under incentive schemes, or balance assembly lines.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ideal Marathoner" Intuition

Imagine you are timing runners in a marathon using a stopwatch:

- Some runners are **fast**, some are **slow**.
- The **raw time** you record is just the clock reading — biased by the specific runner's speed.
- Now you **mentally normalize** every runner to a "standard athlete" running at a steady, average competitive pace (the *normal pace*).
- If a runner finishes 20% faster than standard → Rating = **120%**.
- If a runner finishes 10% slower than standard → Rating = **90%**.

**Normal Time** is exactly this idea — it converts *what actually happened on the floor* into *what a standard worker would do under standard conditions*. It strips away individual worker speed variations so that engineering standards become **fair, repeatable, and universally applicable**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Convention (as per KTU / ILO) |
|---|---|
| **Normal Pace (100% Rating)** | **100%** or **Rating Factor = 1.00** |
| Typical Performance Rating Range | **80% to 120%** |
| Standard Working Day | **8 hours** or **480 minutes** |
| SI Unit of Normal Time | **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)** |
| Performance Rating Notation | **PR**, **R**, or **RF** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Normal Time Exists:**
> Two different workers observed on the same job will record **different observed times** purely because of personal pace differences. Normal Time **removes this variability** so that the resulting time is a property of the **work** — not the **worker**.

---

## 1.4 Position of Normal Time in the Work-Measurement Pipeline

Normal Time sits in the **middle** of the classical time-study pipeline. It is the output of the **rating step** and the input to the **allowances step**.

$$
\boxed{\text{Observed Time} \xrightarrow{\times\, \text{Performance Rating}} \text{Normal Time} \xrightarrow{+\, \text{Allowances}} \text{Standard Time}}
$$

This three-stage transformation is fundamental to Method Study, Time Study, and Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS) such as **MTM** and **Work Factor**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — How Normal Time is Established

Normal Time is computed through the following structured logic chain:

- **Step 1 — Job Selection & Method Standardization**
  A specific, well-defined job is selected. The **method** to perform it must be standardized (Best Practiced Method) **before** any time measurement begins. *Why?* Because you cannot establish a fair time for a method that is still varying.

- **Step 2 — Worker Selection**
  A **qualified, trained, experienced worker** is selected. The worker should be neither exceptionally fast nor unusually slow — ideally representative of the "average" workforce. *Why?* This minimizes the magnitude of the rating adjustment.

- **Step 3 — Recording Observed Time (Stopwatch Time Study)**
  Using a stopwatch (or electronic timing device), the time for each **element** (sub-task) is recorded across **multiple cycles** (typically 10 to 30 cycles for statistical reliability).

- **Step 4 — Average Observed Time Calculation**
  The arithmetic mean of recorded times is computed:

$$
\bar{T}_{obs} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i
$$

- **Step 5 — Performance Rating (Pace Rating)**
  The trained time-study analyst mentally compares the worker's actual pace against a **standard (normal) pace** and assigns a **rating factor** (commonly 60% – 140%, with 100% = normal).

- **Step 6 — Normal Time Computation**
  Adjust the average observed time by multiplying with the rating factor.

- **Step 7 — Summing Elemental Normal Times**
  The job-level Normal Time is the sum of all element-level Normal Times:

$$
NT_{job} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} NT_j
$$

where $m$ is the number of elements comprising the job.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Code)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Complete Formula Reference for Normal Time & Related Concepts**
> *Use \vert and \mid instead of vertical pipes to preserve table syntax.*

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NT = \bar{T}_{obs} \times R$ | Normal Time from average observed time & rating | seconds (s) or minutes (min) |
| 2 | $NT = OT \times R$ | Alternative notation (Observed Time = OT) | $R$ is **dimensionless** |
| 3 | $\bar{T}_{obs} = \dfrac{\sum T_i}{n}$ | Arithmetic mean of observed times | $n$ = number of cycles |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{\text{Worker's Pace}}{\text{Normal Pace}}$ | Performance Rating Factor | $R = 1.0$ at normal pace |
| 5 | $R(\%) = R \times 100$ | Rating expressed as percentage | e.g., $R = 1.10 \Rightarrow$ 110% |
| 6 | $NT = \sum_{j=1}^{m} ( \bar{T}_{j} \times R_j )$ | Job-level NT by summing element NTs | $m$ = number of elements |
| 7 | $NT = RT \times R$ | When **Rated Time (RT)** is given directly | RT is pre-rated average |
| 8 | $ST = NT + \text{Allowances}$ | Standard Time = NT + total allowances | $ST \geq NT$ always |
| 9 | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | Standard Time using allowance factor $A$ | $A$ as decimal (e.g., 0.15) |
| 10 | $A = A_p + A_r + A_c + A_s$ | Total allowance = Personal + Rest + Contingency + Special | expressed as fraction of NT |

**Boundary Conditions:**
- $0.6 \leq R \leq 1.4$ (typical realistic range).
- $NT > 0$ always.
- $ST > NT$ always (allowances are non-negative).
- If $A = 0$, then $ST = NT$ (theoretical, never in practice).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Normal Time

Normal Time underpins nearly every industrial-engineering decision:

- **Wage Setting (Incentive Wage Systems):** Halsey, Rowan, and Bedeaux plans all pay incentive on time **saved below** Normal Time.
- **Production Planning:** Daily output targets = $\dfrac{\text{Available Time per day}}{NT}$.
- **Line Balancing:** Workstation times for assembly-line balancing are Normal Times.
- **Cost Estimation & Quoting:** Labor cost = $NT \times$ labor rate per unit time.
- **Capacity Planning:** Machine and manpower requirements derived from accumulated Normal Times.
- **Worker Performance Feedback:** Comparing actual pace to NT identifies training needs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> Without a correctly established **Normal Time**, every downstream decision — incentive payouts, machine loading, delivery promises, even hiring levels — becomes **biased** by the worker's individual pace. This is why every **KTU examiner** treats the NT calculation step as a **high-weightage, marks-defining** question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Derivation of Normal Time

Starting from the fundamental definition of performance rating:

$$
R = \frac{\text{Worker's Observed Pace}}{\text{Normal (Standard) Pace}}
$$

By definition of pace (inverse of time per unit work), a worker performing the same job faster has a **shorter** observed time. Hence, for a fixed quantity of work $W$:

$$
\text{Pace} \propto \frac{1}{\text{Time}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
R = \frac{\text{Normal Pace}}{\text{Worker's Pace}} = \frac{1 / T_{normal}}{1 / T_{observed}} = \frac{T_{observed}}{T_{normal}}
$$

Rearranging to solve for the normal time required for the same amount of work:

$$
T_{normal} = \frac{T_{observed}}{R}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $R$ to recover the working formula:

$$
\boxed{\,NT = T_{observed} \times R\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the subtlety: $R > 1$ (faster worker) **decreases** time if computed as $T_{obs}/R$, but the **practical working formula** $NT = T_{obs} \times R$ assumes the rating is **applied** as a normalization, not a division. The KTU convention uses **$NT = OT \times R$** with $R > 1$ meaning the worker was *faster than normal*, so we **inflate** the observed fast time back up to normal. This is the **synthetic rating** correction logic.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example — KTU Style

### Problem Statement
A time-study analyst observed a worker for **8 cycles** of a manual assembly operation. The recorded times (in minutes) are:

$$
T = \{1.45,\ 1.50,\ 1.42,\ 1.48,\ 1.55,\ 1.43,\ 1.47,\ 1.50\}
$$

The analyst rated the worker's pace at **115%** throughout. Calculate:

1. Average Observed Time ($\bar{T}_{obs}$)
2. Normal Time (NT)
3. Standard Time (ST) given: Personal Allowance = 5%, Rest Allowance = 10%, Contingency Allowance = 3%.

---

### Solution — Step 1: Average Observed Time

Summing the observed times:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{8} T_i = 1.45 + 1.50 + 1.42 + 1.48 + 1.55 + 1.43 + 1.47 + 1.50
$$

Computing sequentially:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.45 + 1.50 &= 2.95 \\
2.95 + 1.42 &= 4.37 \\
4.37 + 1.48 &= 5.85 \\
5.85 + 1.55 &= 7.40 \\
7.40 + 1.43 &= 8.83 \\
8.83 + 1.47 &= 10.30 \\
10.30 + 1.50 &= 11.80
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence:

$$
\sum T_i = 11.80 \text{ minutes}
$$

The arithmetic mean:

$$
\bar{T}_{obs} = \frac{11.80}{8} = 1.475 \text{ minutes}
$$

---

### Solution — Step 2: Normal Time

Performance rating as a decimal:

$$
R = \frac{115}{100} = 1.15
$$

Apply the rating:

$$
NT = \bar{T}_{obs} \times R = 1.475 \times 1.15
$$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.475 \times 1.15 &= 1.475 \times \left(1 + 0.15\right) \\
&= 1.475 + (1.475 \times 0.15) \\
&= 1.475 + 0.22125 \\
&= 1.69625 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{NT = 1.6963 \text{ minutes (per cycle)}}
$$

---

### Solution — Step 3: Standard Time

Total allowance fraction:

$$
A = A_p + A_r + A_c = 0.05 + 0.10 + 0.03 = 0.18
$$

Using the multiplicative form:

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 1.69625 \times (1 + 0.18) = 1.69625 \times 1.18
$$

Computing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.69625 \times 1.18 &= 1.69625 + (1.69625 \times 0.18) \\
&= 1.69625 + 0.305325 \\
&= 2.001575 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{ST \approx 2.0016 \text{ minutes per cycle}}
$$

---

### Solution — Step 4: Production Rate & Sanity Checks

If a worker works for a full **8-hour shift** (480 minutes):

$$
\text{Daily output (standard)} = \frac{480}{ST} = \frac{480}{2.0016} \approx 239.81 \text{ units/day}
$$

Sanity checks:
- $NT > \bar{T}_{obs}$ because the worker was rated **above** 100% (faster than normal). ✓
- $ST > NT$ because allowances add time. ✓
- $ST \approx 2$ min/unit is reasonable for manual assembly. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1 — Decision Matrix for Normal Time Problems**

| Given Data | Required Output | Formula Path |
|---|---|---|
| $\sum T_i$, $n$, $R$ | $NT$ | $NT = (\sum T_i / n) \times R$ |
| $\sum T_i$, $n$, $R$, allowances | $ST$ | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ |
| $\bar{T}_{obs}$, $R$, $ST$ | Allowance Factor | $A = (ST / NT) - 1$ |
| $NT$, $ST$, 8-hr shift | Daily Standard Output | $N_{units} = 480 / ST$ |
| Element-wise $(T_{ij}, R_j)$ | Job-level $NT$ | $NT_{job} = \sum_j (\bar{T}_{ij} \cdot R_j)$ |

---

## 3.4 Variants & Special Cases Encountered in KTU Papers

- **Average Pace Rating (Multi-Element Jobs):** Different elements may be rated differently; use a **weighted average rating** only when justified.
- **Foreign Element Exclusion:** Times recorded for non-cyclic or irregular elements are excluded from $NT$ calculation but flagged separately.
- **Standard Data Approach:** Pre-established Normal Times of standard elements are looked up from a **Standard Data Table** and summed — no fresh time study needed.
- **Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS):** $NT$ is computed from fundamental motion time tables (e.g., MTM) without any actual timing of workers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes the **complete transformation pipeline** of work measurement, with **Normal Time** clearly highlighted as the central pivot.

## 4.2 Relationship Hierarchy — Time Concepts Block Diagram

This block diagram clarifies **how the various "times" in Industrial Engineering relate to each other** and where **Normal Time** fits.


## 4.3 Element-Wise NT Computation — Schematic

For multi-element jobs (typical in KTU problems), each element $j$ has its own average time and rating.


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define Normal Time. State the formula used to compute it from a time study.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Normal Time (NT)** is the time required by a qualified, trained worker, working at a **normal (standard) pace**, under standard working conditions, to perform a specified job using the prescribed method.

It is computed as:

$$
\boxed{NT = \text{Average Observed Time} \times \text{Performance Rating Factor}}
$$

$$
NT = \bar{T}_{obs} \times R
$$

where $R$ is the dimensionless rating factor (e.g., 1.10 for 110% rated pace). Normal Time **excludes** allowances for rest, personal needs, or contingencies. *(3 marks — Definition: 2, Formula: 1)*

---

### **Q2. What is Performance Rating? Why is it applied while computing Normal Time?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Performance Rating** is the act of mentally comparing the **actual pace** of the observed worker against a **standard (normal) pace**, expressed as a percentage (e.g., 90%, 100%, 120%) or decimal (0.90, 1.00, 1.20).

**Why it is applied:**

1. To **normalize** the observed time against individual worker variability (fast/slow).
2. To convert the recorded time into a time **representative of a standard worker**.
3. To ensure that the resulting time standard reflects the **job**, not the **worker**.
4. Without rating, two equally capable workers at different paces would yield **different observed times**, making standards inconsistent. *(3 marks — Definition: 1, Reasons: 2)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Never** confuse **Observed Time** with **Normal Time** — they differ by the rating factor. Skipping the multiplication with $R$ costs **up to 50% marks** in the calculation sub-part.
> - **Always** express the rating as a **decimal** ($R$) before multiplying. Writing "$NT = 1.475 \times 115$" instead of "$NT = 1.475 \times 1.15$" is a **common error** that yields a 100× wrong answer.
> - When the problem says **"rated time"** (RT), it means the **observed time already includes** rating — do **not** rate it again.
> - In element-wise problems, **sum the Normal Times**, not the observed times, before adding allowances.
> - Always state **units** explicitly in the final answer; examiners routinely deduct marks for missing units.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### (a) Explain the concept of Normal Time in detail. Discuss its significance in industrial engineering applications. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Normal Time (NT) is the time required by a qualified worker, working at a **normal pace** under standard conditions, to perform a specified task using the standard method.

**Computation:**

$$
NT = \bar{T}_{obs} \times R
$$

**Significance in Industrial Engineering:**

1. **Foundation for Standard Time:** NT is the starting point for computing Standard Time, which is essential for production planning and control.
2. **Fair Wage Determination:** Incentive wage schemes (Halsey, Rowan, Bedeaux) base incentives on time saved **relative to Normal Time**.
3. **Production Standards:** Daily/hourly output targets = Available Time / NT.
4. **Cost Estimation:** Direct labor cost per unit = NT × labor rate.
5. **Line Balancing:** Workstation assignments in assembly lines use element-level NTs.
6. **Worker Evaluation:** Comparing actual output with NT-based targets identifies training needs.
7. **Capacity Planning:** Manpower and machine requirements are derived from accumulated NTs across products.
8. **Performance Benchmarking:** Provides a **fair, objective** benchmark that removes individual worker variability.

Thus, Normal Time acts as the **anchor concept** linking observed work activity to all downstream managerial and economic decisions. *(7 marks — Concept: 2, Formula: 1, Applications: 4)*

---

#### (b) A worker is observed for **12 cycles** of an operation. The recorded times (in seconds) are: **52, 55, 50, 54, 53, 51, 56, 52, 54, 53, 51, 55**. The analyst's performance rating is **108%**. If the total allowance is **20%** of Normal Time, compute the **Average Observed Time**, **Normal Time**, and **Standard Time** for the operation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sum of observed times:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum T_i &= 52 + 55 + 50 + 54 + 53 + 51 + 56 + 52 + 54 + 53 + 51 + 55 \\
&= 636 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(Detail: 52+55=107; +50=157; +54=211; +53=264; +51=315; +56=371; +52=423; +54=477; +53=530; +51=581; +55=636.)*

**Step 2 — Average Observed Time:**

$$
\bar{T}_{obs} = \frac{636}{12} = 53.00 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 3 — Normal Time:**

Convert rating to decimal: $R = 108 / 100 = 1.08$

$$
NT = 53.00 \times 1.08 = 57.24 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Standard Time:**

Allowance factor $A = 0.20$

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 57.24 \times 1.20 = 68.688 \text{ seconds}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{\bar{T}_{obs} = 53.00 \text{ s}, \quad NT = 57.24 \text{ s}, \quad ST = 68.69 \text{ s}}
$$

*(7 marks — Sum: 1, Avg: 1, NT: 2, ST: 2, Units: 1)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) Explain the various types of **allowances** used in computing Standard Time from Normal Time. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Allowances are **additions to Normal Time** to account for unavoidable non-productive elements and worker needs. They convert NT (pure work time) into ST (realistic, achievable time).

**Types of Allowances:**

1. **Personal Time Allowance (A_p):**
   - For personal needs like drinking water, using washrooms, washing hands.
   - Typically **2% – 5%** of NT.
   - Higher in hot/humid conditions.

2. **Rest / Fatigue Allowance (A_r):**
   - To recover from physical and mental fatigue.
   - Typically **5% – 15%** of NT.
   - Depends on job heaviness, posture, monotony, and environmental factors.

3. **Contingency Allowance (A_c):**
   - For minor, unpredictable delays (e.g., tool adjustment, brief machine stoppage).
   - Typically **2% – 5%** of NT.

4. **Special / Process Allowance (A_s):**
   - For unavoidable delays specific to the process (e.g., waiting for material to dry, cooling time).
   - Varies widely based on industry and operation.

**Total Allowance:**

$$
A = A_p + A_r + A_c + A_s
$$

**Standard Time:**

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A)
$$

> **Significance:** Allowances ensure the time standard is **achievable, humane, and sustainable**, avoiding worker burnout while maintaining realistic production targets. *(7 marks — Four allowances × 1.5 = 6, Total: 1)*

---

#### (b) The **average observed time** for a manual operation is **2.40 minutes**. The worker's performance was rated at **90%**. If the personal, rest, and contingency allowances are **5%, 12%, and 3%** of Normal Time respectively, determine the **Normal Time** and **Standard Time** for the operation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert rating to decimal:**

$$
R = \frac{90}{100} = 0.90
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Normal Time:**

$$
NT = \bar{T}_{obs} \times R = 2.40 \times 0.90 = 2.16 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Total Allowance:**

$$
A = 0.05 + 0.12 + 0.03 = 0.20
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Standard Time:**

$$
ST = NT \times (1 + A) = 2.16 \times 1.20 = 2.592 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Final Answers:**

$$
\boxed{NT = 2.16 \text{ min}, \quad ST = 2.592 \text{ min}}
$$

**Sanity Check:** Since rating is below 100%, NT < Observed Time. ✓ ST > NT because of positive allowances. ✓

*(7 marks — Rating decimal: 1, NT: 2, Allowance total: 1, ST: 2, Verification: 1)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Summary — Read Before Every KTU Exam**

- **Normal Time (NT)** = time of a **standard worker** at **standard pace** for the job using the standard method.
- **Working formula:** $NT = \bar{T}_{obs} \times R$, where $R$ is the **decimal** performance rating.
- **Performance Rating** compares worker's pace to a **normal pace** ($R=1.00$ means normal).
- **Typical rating range:** 80% – 120%.
- **NT excludes allowances**; allowances are added **only** when computing Standard Time.
- **Standard Time:** $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$, with $A = A_p + A_r + A_c + A_s$.
- **Typical allowance buckets:** Personal 2–5%, Rest 5–15%, Contingency 2–5%, Special variable.
- **Element-wise NT:** Compute NT for each element, then **sum** to get job NT.
- **Job-level ST:** Apply allowance **once** on the **summed NT**, not element by element.
- **Daily output (standard):** $\dfrac{480 \text{ min}}{ST \text{ (min/unit)}}$.
- **Rated Time (RT)** in problems = already-normalized; do **not** apply rating twice.
- **Without NT, no Standard Time**; without Standard Time, no fair wages, no proper production planning.
- **Standardization precedes timing** — always fix the method before measuring.
- **Units matter:** Always carry units (s, min, hr) explicitly through every calculation step.
- **KTU hot topics:** Element-wise NT calculation, allowance addition logic, and conversion between rating percentage and decimal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Allowances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Allowances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the discipline of **Work Study** and **Time Study**, an **Allowance** is a formally quantified, standardized addition of time granted to the **Normal Time (NT)** of a job, intended to compensate the operator for non-productive, yet legitimate and unavoidable, elements of work that cannot be efficiently eliminated through improved method study. Mathematically, allowances are expressed either as a **percentage of Normal Time** or as a **fixed time increment**, and they form the critical mathematical bridge that converts theoretical work content into a realistic, achievable, and humane **Standard Time (ST)**.

$$ST = NT \times (1 + A)$$

where the **Allowance Factor (A)** is the decimal equivalent of the allowance percentage (e.g., 15% → A = 0.15).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Standard:** An allowance is *"the time added to the basic time (or normal time) to allow for legitimate personal needs, rest, and unavoidable delays that are an integral part of carrying out a task under standard conditions."*

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student preparing for a 3-hour KTU university exam.

- The **observed time** you take to answer is, say, 2 hours and 40 minutes.
- The **normal time** is the time an average, fully trained, alert student would take — let's say 3 hours.
- However, no student can write continuously for 3 hours. You need:
  - Time to **drink water** (personal need).
  - Time to **stretch your back** (rest).
  - Time when the **clock runs out of battery**, or your pen **runs dry** (unavoidable delay).

So, the university **allows** you an extra 15 minutes. That 15 minutes is the **"Allowance"** added to the normal time to compute the **Standard Time** of the exam. Without this allowance, the standard would be impossible, frustrating, and ultimately meaningless.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Highlight:** Allowances do **NOT** compensate for poor method, bad management, or machine breakdowns. They only compensate for *worker-related* and *operationally unavoidable* delays under **standard working conditions**.

---

### Classification of Allowances (KTU 2024 Module 3 Core Framework)

| **Category** | **Type of Allowance** | **Purpose / What it Compensates For** |
|---|---|---|
| **Direct Worker Allowances** | Rest Allowance | Fatigue, physical & mental recovery |
| | Personal Allowance | Human physiological needs (water, restroom) |
| | Contingency Allowance | Minor, unpredictable delays (jamming, tool slip) |
| | Special / Process Allowance | Process-mandated delays (cooling, drying, setting) |
| **Indirect Worker Allowances** | Interference Allowance | Lost time due to multi-machine tending |
| | **Total Allowance** | Sum of all applicable allowances |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Reminder:** The **Rest Allowance** is the **largest** and most critical component in most industrial settings, typically ranging from **5% to 20%** depending on the physical and mental severity of the job.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Time Study Cascade — Where Allowances Fit

The complete KTU 2024 formula pipeline for computing Standard Time is:

$$
\text{Observed Time (OT)} \xrightarrow{\times \text{Performance Rating (R)}} \text{Normal Time (NT)} \xrightarrow{+ \text{Allowances}} \text{Standard Time (ST)}
$$

Mathematically expressed:

$$NT = \text{OT} \times R$$

$$ST = NT + \text{Allowances (in minutes)} = NT \times (1 + A)$$

Combining the two:

$$ST = \text{OT} \times R \times (1 + A)$$

where:
- $\text{OT}$ = Observed Time (measured during stopwatch time study, in **minutes or seconds**).
- $R$ = Performance Rating (e.g., 1.10 for a fast worker, 0.95 for a slow worker; ideal is 1.00).
- $A$ = Allowance Factor (decimal form of allowance percentage).

---

### 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Allowance Type

#### **A. Rest Allowance (Fatigue Allowance)**

Rest allowance compensates the worker for physiological and psychological fatigue accumulated during continuous productive work. It is the **single largest component** of the total allowance.

- **Physical Fatigue**: Due to muscular effort, posture, awkward movements, heat, noise, vibration.
- **Mental Fatigue**: Due to concentration, monotony, responsibility, visual strain.
- **Range**: Typically **5% to 20%** of normal time, depending on:
  - **Light Work (seated, light assembly)** → ~5–7%
  - **Medium Work (standing, moderate lifting)** → ~10–12%
  - **Heavy Work (heavy lifting, hot environment)** → ~15–20%
  - **Very Heavy Work (mining, forging)** → ~20–25%

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** If the problem statement says "light work," do **not** assume the textbook 12%. Read carefully. KTU often tests whether students can apply the correct category.

---

#### **B. Personal Allowance**

This compensates for legitimate human needs that occur during work hours and cannot be avoided.

- Drinking water.
- Visiting the restroom.
- Washing hands and face.
- Wiping sweat (in hot environments).
- Standard value: Typically **5% to 7%** of normal time (≈ **2 to 5 minutes per hour**).
- **Often combined with Rest Allowance** and reported as a single combined figure, called **"Rest and Personal Allowance"** (R&P Allowance).

---

#### **C. Contingency Allowance (or Delay Allowance)**

This compensates for **minor, unpredictable, and irregular delays** that are inherent to the work itself and not under the worker's control.

- Example: A worker's tool slips, a bolt is dropped, a minor jam in the assembly, brief consultation with supervisor.
- **Range**: Usually **3% to 5%** of normal time.
- **Critical Distinction**: This is for delays the **operator cannot control** but the **method study cannot eliminate** entirely.

---

#### **D. Special Allowance (Process or Unavoidable Delay Allowance)**

This compensates for **process-mandated delays** that are built into the production method.

- Example: A chemical reaction needs **30 minutes to cure**.
- A casting needs to **cool down** before removal.
- A paint coat needs to **dry** before the next layer.
- **Range**: **Highly variable**, depends on the process.
- **Unique Feature**: This is **not related to the worker** but to the **process** itself.

---

#### **E. Interference Allowance (For Multi-Machine Operators)**

When one operator tends **multiple machines** simultaneously, the operator's attention is divided. When one machine is running automatically, the operator attends to another. The time the operator is **prevented from attending** a machine because of duty on another is called **Interference Time**, and an **Interference Allowance** is given.

- Common in **CNC tending, textile spindles, power presses**.
- Calculated using **statistical methods or queuing theory**.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examinable in PCPET501 (KTU 2024). Master this table.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $NT = \text{OT} \times R$ | Normal Time = Observed Time × Rating | minutes |
| 2 | $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ | Standard Time with percentage allowance | minutes |
| 3 | $ST = NT + (\text{Allowance time in min})$ | Standard Time with fixed allowance | minutes |
| 4 | $A_{\text{total}} = A_{\text{rest}} + A_{\text{personal}} + A_{\text{cont}} + A_{\text{special}}$ | Total Allowance = Sum of all allowances | dimensionless |
| 5 | $ST = \text{OT} \times R \times (1 + A_{\text{total}})$ | Master standard time equation | minutes |
| 6 | $\text{Cycle Time} = \text{Standard Time per piece}$ | Time allowed per unit | minutes/unit |
| 7 | $\text{Output per day} = \dfrac{\text{Available time per day}}{\text{Standard Time per unit}}$ | Productivity formula | units/day |
| 8 | $\text{Piece Rate Wages} = \text{Units produced} \times \text{Rate per piece}$ | Wages computation | currency |

> **⚠️ KTU Pipeline Reminder:** $\text{OT} \xrightarrow{\times R} \text{NT} \xrightarrow{\times (1+A)} \text{ST} \xrightarrow{\div \text{day time}} \text{Daily Output}$

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Allowances are the **foundation of modern manufacturing planning**. They are used for:

- **Production Planning**: Setting realistic daily output targets.
- **Wage Incentive Systems**: Piece-rate wage calculation.
- **Cost Estimation**: Standard time × labor rate = direct labor cost.
- **Capacity Planning**: Determining machine load vs. available time.
- **Workforce Scheduling**: Balancing operator load across machines.
- **Lean Manufacturing (Kaizen)**: Identifying and reducing excessive allowances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Excessive allowances** hide inefficiency, inflate standard time, reduce output targets, and make the organization uncompetitive. **Insufficient allowances** cause worker fatigue, accidents, and quality defects. **Optimizing allowances is an art of industrial engineering.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Derivation: From Observed Time to Standard Time

We derive the full KTU 2024 standard time equation step by step.

**Given:**
- A worker's observed cycle time for one unit: $\text{OT} = 0.80 \text{ min/unit}$
- Performance Rating: $R = 110\% = 1.10$
- Allowances:
  - Rest Allowance: 12%
  - Personal Allowance: 5%
  - Contingency Allowance: 4%
  - Special Allowance: 2%

**Goal:** Find the Standard Time (ST) per unit.

---

**Step 1: Compute the Normal Time (NT)**

Normal time is the time required by a *qualified, fully-trained worker working at a normal pace*. We must first remove the performance rating bias from the observed time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If a worker is rated 1.10, they are 10\% faster than normal.} \\
\text{Therefore, the NORMAL time must be INFLATED to bring this fast worker} \\
\text{down to a "normal" benchmark.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= \text{OT} \times R \\
NT &= 0.80 \text{ min} \times 1.10 \\
NT &= 0.88 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2: Compute the Total Allowance Factor (A_total)**

Since all allowances are percentages of normal time, we **add** them directly (this is the KTU 2024 convention for independent, non-overlapping allowances).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{total}} &= A_{\text{rest}} + A_{\text{personal}} + A_{\text{cont}} + A_{\text{special}} \\
A_{\text{total}} &= 12\% + 5\% + 4\% + 2\% \\
A_{\text{total}} &= 23\% = 0.23
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each percentage is divided by 100 to convert into a decimal factor, but we keep them as percentages during summation for clarity.

---

**Step 3: Compute the Standard Time (ST)**

The standard time is the normal time plus the total allowance time. The allowance time itself is the percentage applied to NT.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT \times (1 + A_{\text{total}}) \\
ST &= 0.88 \text{ min} \times (1 + 0.23) \\
ST &= 0.88 \text{ min} \times 1.23 \\
ST &= 1.0824 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** $ST \approx 1.08 \text{ min/unit}$.

---

**Step 4: Convert Allowance to Time (Sanity Check)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowance time} &= NT \times A_{\text{total}} \\
&= 0.88 \times 0.23 = 0.2024 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT + \text{Allowance time} \\
&= 0.88 + 0.2024 = 1.0824 \text{ min/unit} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Each Allowance on Standard Time

Using our example with $NT = 0.88$ min, let us analyze how each allowance individually affects the Standard Time.

| **Allowance Type** | **Percentage** | **Time Added (min)** | **Cumulative ST (min)** | **% Increase from NT** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal Time (baseline)** | 0% | 0 | 0.8800 | 0.00% |
| Rest Allowance | 12% | 0.1056 | 0.9856 | 12.00% |
| Personal Allowance | 5% | 0.0440 | 1.0296 | 17.00% |
| Contingency Allowance | 4% | 0.0352 | 1.0648 | 21.00% |
| Special Allowance | 2% | 0.0176 | 1.0824 | 23.00% |
| **Standard Time (final)** | **23%** | **0.2024** | **1.0824** | **23.00%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The **Rest Allowance (12%) alone** accounts for **52%** of the total allowance time (0.1056 / 0.2024 ≈ 52%). This is why proper rest allowance estimation is critical in KTU exam problems.

---

### 3.3 Practical Engineering Derivation: Daily Output Calculation

**Problem Context:** A factory operates **8 hours per day** (480 minutes). With $ST = 1.0824$ min/unit, how many units can be produced per day?

**Step 1: Identify Available Production Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available time} &= 8 \text{ hours/day} \times 60 \text{ min/hour} = 480 \text{ min/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Daily Output**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily Output} &= \frac{\text{Available time per day}}{ST \text{ per unit}} \\
&= \frac{480 \text{ min}}{1.0824 \text{ min/unit}} \\
&\approx 443.47 \text{ units/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Round Down to Whole Units (Industrial Convention)**

$$
\text{Daily Output} = 443 \text{ units/day (practical standard)}
$$

> **Rounding Logic:** We round **down** because producing 0.47 of a unit is impossible. This is the **achievable, KTU-board-accepted** answer.

---

### 3.4 Inverse Problem: Computing Required Allowance

**Problem Context:** Management wants a Standard Time of exactly **1.00 min/unit**. Observed Time is 0.75 min, Performance Rating is 1.00. What total allowance should be applied?

**Step 1: Compute Normal Time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= \text{OT} \times R = 0.75 \times 1.00 = 0.75 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for A_total from the Standard Time Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT \times (1 + A) \\
1.00 &= 0.75 \times (1 + A) \\
\frac{1.00}{0.75} &= 1 + A \\
1.3333 &= 1 + A \\
A &= 0.3333 = 33.33\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** A total allowance of **33.33%** would yield a standard time of 1.00 min/unit. This is unusually high, suggesting the observed time is artificially constrained or the work is very heavy.

---

### 3.5 Comprehensive Master Equation Summary

$$
\boxed{
ST = \text{OT} \times R \times (1 + A_{\text{rest}} + A_{\text{personal}} + A_{\text{cont}} + A_{\text{special}})
}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mandate:** When solving PCPET501 problems, students **must** show all three computational steps:
> 1. $NT = \text{OT} \times R$
> 2. $A_{\text{total}} = \text{sum of all allowances}$
> 3. $ST = NT \times (1 + A_{\text{total}})$
> Skipping any step will cost marks even if the final answer is correct.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Allowance Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Time OT] --> B[Multiply by Performance Rating R]
    B --> C[Normal Time NT]
    C --> D[Add Rest Allowance 12%]
    C --> E[Add Personal Allowance 5%]
    C --> F[Add Contingency Allowance 4%]
    C --> G[Add Special Allowance 2%]
    D --> H[Sum all Allowances]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Total Allowance A total = 23%]
    I --> J[Multiply NT by 1 + A]
    J --> K[Standard Time ST per unit]
    K --> L[Daily Output Planning]
    K --> M[Wage Incentive Calculation]
    K --> N[Cost Estimation]

    style A fill:#FFE5B4
    style K fill:#90EE90
    style L fill:#87CEEB
    style M fill:#87CEEB
    style N fill:#87CEEB
```

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Architecture: Categories of Allowances

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Allowances] --> B[Direct Worker Allowances]
    A --> C[Indirect Worker Allowances]

    B --> B1[Rest Allowance]
    B --> B2[Personal Allowance]
    B --> B3[Contingency Allowance]
    B --> B4[Special Allowance]

    C --> C1[Interference Allowance]

    B1 --> B1a[Physical Fatigue]
    B1 --> B1b[Mental Fatigue]

    B2 --> B2a[Water, Restroom]
    B2 --> B2b[Hygiene]

    B3 --> B3a[Tool Slip, Minor Jams]

    B4 --> B4a[Cooling, Drying, Curing]

    C1 --> C1a[Multi-Machine Tending]

    style A fill:#FFD700
    style B fill:#FFA500
    style C fill:#FFA500
```

---
