### 4.3 Flow Diagram: KTU Problem-Solving Methodology for Allowance Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Read Problem Carefully] --> P2[Identify OT, R, Allowances]
    P2 --> P3[Step 1: Compute NT = OT x R]
    P3 --> P4[Step 2: Sum all allowances to get A total]
    P4 --> P5{Q: What is being asked?}
    P5 -->|Standard Time| P6[ST = NT x 1 + A total]
    P5 -->|Daily Output| P7[Output = Available Time / ST]
    P5 -->|Piece Rate Wages| P8[Wages = Output x Rate per piece]
    P6 --> P9[Final Answer with Units]
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Sanity Check and Round Off]

    style P1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style P9 fill:#98FB98
    style P10 fill:#87CEFA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Each node represents a **discrete computational step** required in KTU 2024 valuation. The arrows represent the **logical flow of calculation**. The green node (`P9`) is where students typically gain or lose 3-5 marks based on whether the final answer carries proper units and significant figures.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question 1: Define Allowance. List the different types of allowances used in time study.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** An allowance is a standard time increment added to the **Normal Time (NT)** of an operation to compensate the worker for **personal needs, rest, fatigue, and unavoidable delays** that cannot be eliminated through method study. It is typically expressed as a **percentage of Normal Time**.

**Types of Allowances (1.5 marks):**
1. **Rest Allowance** — for fatigue recovery.
2. **Personal Allowance** — for human physiological needs.
3. **Contingency Allowance** — for minor unpredictable delays.
4. **Special/Process Allowance** — for process-mandated delays.
5. **Interference Allowance** — for multi-machine operators.

---

#### **Question 2: Distinguish between Rest Allowance and Contingency Allowance.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Rest Allowance** | **Contingency Allowance** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Compensates for worker fatigue (physical + mental) | Compensates for minor unpredictable delays |
| **Cause** | Worker-related (physiological) | Work-related (operational) |
| **Magnitude** | Usually largest component (5% to 20%) | Smaller component (3% to 5%) |
| **Example** | Rest after heavy lifting | Tool slip, minor jam, brief consultation |
| **Influencing Factor** | Job severity, posture, environment | Nature of work, operator skill |

> **Closing Line for Full Marks:** *"Rest allowance is a function of the worker's physiological state, whereas contingency allowance is a function of the work's inherent unpredictability."*

---

### **PART B: Full 14-Mark Questions (KTU Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Time Study and Standard Time Calculation**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3]`

> A time study was conducted on a worker operating a drilling machine. The following data was recorded:
> - **Observed Time (OT)** for 5 cycles: 1.20, 1.18, 1.25, 1.22, 1.19 minutes.
> - **Performance Rating (R)** = 110%.
> - **Allowances:** Rest = 12%, Personal = 5%, Contingency = 3%, Special = 2%.
> - **Working hours per day** = 8 hours.
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The Standard Time per unit and the expected daily output.
> **(b)** If a piece-rate of ₹4.50 per unit is offered, calculate the worker's daily earnings.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute the Average Observed Time (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Average OT} &= \frac{1.20 + 1.18 + 1.25 + 1.22 + 1.19}{5} \\
&= \frac{6.04}{5} \\
&= 1.208 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Normal Time (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= \text{OT} \times R \\
NT &= 1.208 \times 1.10 \\
NT &= 1.3288 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Total Allowance Factor (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{total}} &= 12\% + 5\% + 3\% + 2\% = 22\% = 0.22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the Standard Time (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT \times (1 + A_{\text{total}}) \\
ST &= 1.3288 \times (1 + 0.22) \\
ST &= 1.3288 \times 1.22 \\
ST &= 1.6211 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Standard Time ≈ 1.62 min/unit**

**Step 5: Compute Expected Daily Output (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available time} &= 8 \text{ hours} \times 60 = 480 \text{ min/day} \\
\text{Daily Output} &= \frac{480}{1.6211} \\
&= 296.09 \text{ units/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Daily Output ≈ 296 units/day** (rounded down, industrial convention)

---

#### **Model Solution (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Daily Production is known (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Daily Output} = 296 \text{ units/day}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Piece-Rate Formula (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Daily Earnings} &= \text{Units produced} \times \text{Rate per unit} \\
&= 296 \times ₹4.50 \\
&= ₹1,332
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Verification (4 marks) — Cross-check the daily earnings computation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Verification:} \quad 296 \times 4.50 &= 296 \times 4 + 296 \times 0.50 \\
&= 1,184 + 148 \\
&= ₹1,332 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Daily Earnings = ₹1,332**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Inverse Problem and Allowance Optimization**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3]`

> A manufacturing company produces a component with the following time study data:
> - **Observed Time** = 0.90 min/unit.
> - **Performance Rating** = 90%.
> - **Total Allowance Applied** = 25%.
>
> **Calculate:**
> **(a)** The Standard Time per unit and expected output in a 7.5-hour shift.
> **(b)** If management wants to **reduce the Standard Time to 1.00 min/unit** without changing the observed time or the rating, calculate the new total allowance required and comment on its feasibility.

---

#### **Model Solution (a) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Compute Normal Time (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NT &= \text{OT} \times R \\
NT &= 0.90 \times 0.90 \\
NT &= 0.81 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Standard Time (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT \times (1 + A_{\text{total}}) \\
ST &= 0.81 \times (1 + 0.25) \\
ST &= 0.81 \times 1.25 \\
ST &= 1.0125 \text{ min/unit}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Standard Time ≈ 1.01 min/unit**

**Step 3: Compute Daily Output (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Available time} &= 7.5 \times 60 = 450 \text{ min/day} \\
\text{Daily Output} &= \frac{450}{1.0125} \\
&= 444.44 \text{ units/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Daily Output ≈ 444 units/day** (rounded down)

---

#### **Model Solution (b) [7 marks]:**

**Step 1: Set up the inverse equation (2 marks)**

Given that OT and R are unchanged, $NT = 0.81$ min/unit remains constant.

We need $ST = 1.00$ min/unit.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST &= NT \times (1 + A_{\text{new}}) \\
1.00 &= 0.81 \times (1 + A_{\text{new}})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for the new allowance (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1.00}{0.81} &= 1 + A_{\text{new}} \\
1.2346 &= 1 + A_{\text{new}} \\
A_{\text{new}} &= 0.2346 = 23.46\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **New Required Total Allowance ≈ 23.46%**

**Step 3: Comment on Feasibility (2 marks)**

The current total allowance is 25%, and the new required allowance is 23.46%. The **reduction is only 1.54 percentage points** (from 25% → 23.46%), which is **practically feasible** and reasonable. It indicates that management's target of 1.00 min/unit is achievable with a marginal tightening of allowances — for example, by reducing the contingency allowance from 3% to 2%, or by improving method study to remove minor delays.

> **Verdict:** *The reduction is feasible as it represents a less than 7% relative change in the allowance, which is well within the tolerance of normal industrial adjustment.*

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PCPET501 Allowance Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert percentage to decimal**: Writing $ST = NT \times (1 + 12)$ instead of $ST = NT \times (1 + 0.12)$ — **costs 2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing observed time and normal time**: Some students compute standard time directly from observed time without the rating factor — **costs 3-4 marks**.
> 3. **Rounding up daily output**: Industrial standards require rounding **down** (e.g., 296.09 → 296). Rounding up gives an unachievable target — **costs 1 mark**.
> 4. **Mixing up allowance bases**: Allowances are added as a **percentage of Normal Time**, NOT of observed time or standard time. This is the **most common conceptual error** — **costs 2-3 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the performance rating step**: A rating of 110% means the worker is faster; therefore normal time must be **larger** than observed time. Students sometimes reduce NT when R > 1, which is the **exact opposite** of correct logic.
> 6. **Not including units in the final answer**: KTU strictly requires units like "min/unit" or "₹/day". Omitting them — **costs 1 mark**.
> 7. **Adding all allowances as time** instead of summing them as percentages first — leads to a wrong standard time. Always: **sum percentages → apply once to NT**.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Allowance = **time added to Normal Time** to account for legitimate non-productive elements.
- ✅ Five types: **Rest, Personal, Contingency, Special, Interference**.
- ✅ **Rest Allowance is the largest** (5% to 20%), Personal is standard (5% to 7%), Contingency is small (3% to 5%), Special is process-dependent.
- ✅ Total Allowance = **sum of all individual allowance percentages** (they are added, not multiplied).
- ✅ Master equation: $\boxed{ST = \text{OT} \times R \times (1 + A_{\text{total}})}$
- ✅ Three mandatory steps: (1) Compute NT, (2) Sum allowances, (3) Compute ST.
- ✅ Daily Output = Available Time / ST (always **round down** to whole units).
- ✅ Piece-rate earnings = Units produced × Rate per unit.
- ✅ Inverse problem: Solve $ST = NT \times (1 + A)$ for $A$ when ST is given.
- ✅ Allowances are a **percentage of Normal Time, NOT Observed Time or Standard Time**.
- ✅ Allowances do **not** compensate for poor management, bad methods, or machine breakdowns.
- ✅ The **factory operating time** is typically 8 hours/day = 480 min; in some problems 7.5 or 9 hours may apply.
- ✅ For a piece-rate problem, **standard time per piece** is the time the operator is expected to take (including allowances).
- ✅ **Rest + Personal** is often combined and given as a single figure (R&P Allowance).
- ✅ KTU 2024 expects: **clear formula, explicit substitution, units, and final answer with comment**.

> **End of Notes — PCPET501 | Module 3 | Allowances**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Sampling Technique of Work Measurement
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Work Sampling Technique of Work Measurement

# 📘 Work Sampling Technique of Work Measurement

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Work Sampling** is a statistical technique of work measurement in which a large number of instantaneous observations are made at random intervals over a period of time on a group of machines, processes, or workers. The proportion of observations recorded for a given activity is a statistically valid estimate of the percentage of time spent by the workforce/equipment on that activity.

It is also referred to as the **Activity Sampling Method** or **Ratio-Delay Method**. It was first developed by **L.H.C. Tippett** in **1935** at the British Cotton Industry Research Association.

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| **Method Originator** | L.H.C. Tippett (1935) |
| **Statistical Foundation** | Law of Large Numbers & Binomial Distribution |
| **Observation Type** | Instantaneous (Dichotomous – Yes/No) |
| **Typical Confidence Level** | **95%** (Z = 1.96) or **95.45%** (Z = 2) |
| **Typical Error Tolerance** | **± 5%** (Standard Industrial Practice) |
| **Measurement Category** | Indirect Work Measurement Technique |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The "Traffic Light Observer"**
> Imagine you want to know what percentage of time a traffic signal stays **RED** at a junction. Instead of sitting for hours with a stopwatch recording exact durations (stopwatch time study), you just stand at the junction and glance at the signal at completely random moments — say 200 times throughout the day. If the light is RED in **60 out of 200** random glances, you can statistically conclude that the signal is RED approximately **30%** of the time.
>
> **Work Sampling applies the EXACT SAME principle** to workers and machines in a factory. Instead of timing every operation continuously, we just *peek* at the worker at random instants and record what they are doing (working, idle, absent, etc.). The fraction of "peeks" for each activity estimates the actual time proportion spent on that activity.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics of Work Sampling

- **Random Observations:** Observations are taken at random times, NOT at fixed intervals — this eliminates observer bias.
- **Statistical Basis:** It relies on the binomial probability distribution, which approximates the normal distribution for large sample sizes.
- **Instantaneous Snapshots:** Each observation captures the *state* at that exact moment (delay vs. work), not duration.
- **Cost-Effective:** Requires **no stopwatch** and far less time from the observer than continuous time study.
- **Applicable to:** Non-repetitive, irregular, or long-cycle activities where stopwatch study is impractical.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Work Sampling is Used (KTU Frequently Asked)**
> 1. To determine **machine/worker utilization** (the delay percentage).
> 2. To establish **standard times** for non-repetitive or irregular work.
> 3. To determine **allowances** (personal, fatigue, contingency).
> 4. To analyze **work patterns** of personnel or groups of workers.
> 5. For **productivity improvement** studies without disrupting the workforce.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Statistical Foundation

Work Sampling is grounded in the **Binomial Distribution** because each random observation is a **dichotomous (two-state) event**:
- The worker is either **working** (success) or **not working** (failure/delay).

For a single observation, probability of success = *p* and probability of failure = *q = 1 − p*.

When the number of observations *N* is large (typically *Np* > 4 and *Nq* > 4), the binomial distribution converges to the **Normal Distribution**, allowing us to use standard statistical formulas.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The implementation of Work Sampling follows a strict **7-step scientific protocol**:

1. **Step 1 — Define the Objectives:** Clearly state what is to be measured (utilization, delay %, standard time, etc.).
2. **Step 2 — Define Activity Categories:** Classify all observed states into discrete, mutually exclusive categories:
   - Productive Working
   - Idle (waiting for work/material)
   - Personal Time
   - Machine Downtime
   - Unavoidable Delay
3. **Step 3 — Design the Observation Schedule:** Plan the random routes and timings using **Random Number Tables** (e.g., Tippett's tables).
4. **Step 4 — Determine Required Sample Size:** Calculate the *minimum* number of observations *N* using the statistical formula.
5. **Step 5 — Conduct Pilot Study:** Take a small preliminary set of observations (typically 100–200) to estimate *p* and refine *N*.
6. **Step 6 — Execute the Main Study:** Perform the *N* random observations, recording data on a tally sheet.
7. **Step 7 — Statistical Analysis & Reporting:** Compute proportions, confidence intervals, and present results.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Size (General)** | $$N = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^{2}}$$ | $N$ = Number of observations, $Z$ = Standard normal variate, $p$ = Estimated proportion, $e$ = Absolute allowable error | Determines how many observations to take |
| 2 | **Sample Size (95% Confidence, Z=2)** | $$N = \frac{4 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^{2}}$$ | Same as above, with $Z = 2$ | Most commonly used in KTU numerical problems |
| 3 | **Sample Size (Maximum, p = 0.5)** | $$N_{max} = \frac{Z^{2}}{4e^{2}} = \frac{1}{e^{2}}$$ | When $p$ is completely unknown, use $p = 0.5$ for maximum $N$ | Conservative preliminary estimate |
| 4 | **Confidence Limit (CL)** | $$CL = p \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p \cdot (1-p)}{N}}$$ | $CL$ = Confidence limits for the true proportion | Verifies whether accuracy was achieved |
| 5 | **Observed Proportion** | $$p = \frac{n_{w}}{N}$$ | $n_{w}$ = Number of observations in category | Direct tally calculation |
| 6 | **Relative Error (%)** | $$e_{\%} = \frac{e}{p} \times 100$$ | $e$ = Absolute error, $p$ = Proportion | Expresses accuracy as a percentage |
| 7 | **Standard Time from Work Sampling** | $$T_{std} = \frac{NT_{obs} \cdot PR}{P_{work}} \times (1 + Allow)$$ | $NT_{obs}$ = Normal time observed, $PR$ = Performance rating, $P_{work}$ = Proportion working, $Allow$ = Allowance fraction | Converts sampled data to standard time |
| 8 | **Total Time (Period)** | $$T_{total} = N \cdot \frac{T_{period}}{N_{planned}}$$ | Total study period divided by planned observations | Time per observation in long studies |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Boundary Conditions for Normal Approximation (CRITICAL for KTU)**
> - For the binomial-to-normal approximation to be valid, BOTH must be satisfied:
>   - $N \cdot p \geq 5$ **AND** $N \cdot (1-p) \geq 5$
> - If $p$ is unknown in advance, use $p = 0.5$ (gives maximum sample size, hence most conservative).
> - **Absolute error *e*** is the maximum permitted difference between the sample proportion and the true population proportion.

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Machine Utilization Studies:** Determines what % of shift time a CNC machine is actually cutting vs. idle.
- **Workforce Delay Analysis:** Quantifies time lost due to waiting, breaks, or lack of instructions.
- **Standard Setting for Indirect Labor:** Useful for setting standards for maintenance crews, supervisors, material handlers.
- **Establishing allowances:** Personal, fatigue, and contingency allowances can be statistically derived.
- **Cost-effective for long cycles:** A study that would require 40 hours of stopwatch timing can often be completed in 8–10 hours of work sampling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Work Sampling Sample Size Formula

The derivation begins with the standard error of the binomial distribution and the confidence interval framework.

#### Step 1: Start with the Standard Error of a Proportion

For a binomial distribution, the standard error of the sample proportion *p* is given by:

$$SE = \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

This represents the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the proportion *p* across repeated samples of size *N*.

#### Step 2: Define the Allowable Error

The **allowable absolute error** *e* is defined as the maximum difference between the observed proportion and the true population proportion, expressed as:

$$e = \vert p_{observed} - p_{true} \vert$$

#### Step 3: Apply the Normal Distribution Confidence Bound

For a two-sided confidence interval at significance level $\alpha$, the maximum error is expressed as a multiple of the standard error:

$$e = Z \cdot SE$$

This is because the (1 − $\alpha$)% confidence interval for the true proportion is:

$$p \pm Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

So the half-width (which is *e*) is:

$$e = Z \cdot \sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

#### Step 4: Square Both Sides to Solve for N

Squaring the equation from Step 3:

$$e^{2} = Z^{2} \cdot \frac{p(1-p)}{N}$$

Rearranging to isolate *N*:

$$\begin{aligned}
N \cdot e^{2} &= Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p) \\
N &= \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Multiplying both sides by *N* moves *N* to the left, and dividing by *e²* isolates *N* as the dependent variable.

#### Step 5: Special Case for 95.45% Confidence (Z = 2)

Substituting $Z = 2$ (corresponding to the standard 95.45% confidence level used in most KTU problems):

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{(2)^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}} \\
N &= \frac{4 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

#### Step 6: Special Case for Maximum Sample Size (p = 0.5)

When *p* is completely unknown, we substitute $p = 0.5$, because the function $p(1-p)$ reaches its maximum at $p = 0.5$:

$$\begin{aligned}
p(1-p) &= (0.5)(1-0.5) \\
&= (0.5)(0.5) \\
&= 0.25
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{max} &= \frac{4 \cdot (0.25)}{e^{2}} \\
N_{max} &= \frac{1}{e^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since $p(1-p)$ peaks at 0.25 when $p = 0.5$, this substitution yields the *maximum* required sample size — a conservative choice ensuring the desired accuracy regardless of the true *p*.

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A work sampling study is to be conducted on a machine operator. Preliminary observations suggest the operator is working productively approximately **80%** of the time. Determine the number of random observations required if the study must be accurate within **±4%** with a **95% confidence level**.

**Given Data:**
- $p = 0.80$ (estimated proportion of productive time)
- $e = 0.04$ (allowable error)
- Confidence Level = 95% → $Z = 1.96$

**Solution:**

Substituting into the general formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}} \\
N &= \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (0.80)(1-0.80)}{(0.04)^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Calculating each term step-by-step:

- $Z^{2} = (1.96)^{2} = 3.8416$
- $p(1-p) = (0.80)(0.20) = 0.16$
- $e^{2} = (0.04)^{2} = 0.0016$

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{3.8416 \times 0.16}{0.0016} \\
N &= \frac{0.614656}{0.0016} \\
N &= 384.16
\end{aligned}$$

**Since the number of observations must be a whole number, we round UP to ensure the accuracy requirement is met:**

$$\boxed{N = 385 \text{ observations}}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Always round UP because a fractional observation is impossible, and rounding down would violate the error constraint.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical KTU Problem Patterns

| Scenario Type | Given Information | Formula to Use | Common KTU Trap |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial Sample Size (no pilot data)** | Only desired error *e* and confidence | $N = 1 / e^{2}$ (using p = 0.5) | Forgetting to use maximum *N* when *p* is unknown |
| **Refined Sample Size (after pilot)** | Estimated *p* from 100–200 observations | $N = Z^{2} p(1-p) / e^{2}$ | Using wrong *Z* value (1.96 vs 2) |
| **Verify Accuracy of Completed Study** | Total observations *N* and observed *p* | $e = Z \sqrt{p(1-p)/N}$ | Confusing absolute and relative error |
| **Setting Standard Time** | Sample data + Normal time + Rating + Allowance | $T_{std} = NT \cdot PR / P_{work} \cdot (1+Allow)$ | Forgetting to divide by $P_{work}$ |
| **Determining Allowance %** | Idle/personal time observations | $Allow = (\text{Idle obs}/\text{Total obs})$ | Mixing idle and personal categories |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Work Sampling Implementation Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete **7-stage methodology** for conducting a Work Sampling study, from problem definition to standard time computation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Start: Define Objectives]) --> nodeB[Step 1: Define Activity Categories]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Design Random Observation Schedule using Random Number Tables]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Step 3: Determine Initial Sample Size N using Formula]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: Conduct Pilot Study approx 200 Observations]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Is p value available?}
    nodeF -->|No| nodeG[Use p equals 0.5 for Maximum N]
    nodeF -->|Yes| nodeH[Refine N using estimated p]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Step 5: Execute Main Study with N Observations]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Step 6: Tabulate Tally Sheet Data]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Step 7: Calculate Proportions and Confidence Limits]
    nodeK --> nodeL{Accuracy Achieved?}
    nodeL -->|No| nodeM[Add More Observations and Recompute]
    nodeL -->|Yes| nodeN[Compute Standard Time and Allowances]
    nodeN --> nodeO([End: Submit Work Sampling Report])
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This flowchart emphasizes the **iterative feedback loop** — if the computed confidence limits do not meet the pre-specified accuracy, additional observations MUST be added before the study can be concluded.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Work Sampling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Random Number Generator Source] --> nodeB[Observation Schedule Planner]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Field Observer with Tally Sheet]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Raw Observation Data Tally]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Statistical Computation Engine]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Proportion Calculator p equals n sub w divided by N]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Confidence Limit Calculator]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Standard Time Calculator]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Work Sampling Report Output]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ([Decision Making for Productivity Improvement])
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block architecture shows how the work sampling process flows from **random input generation** through **data collection** to **statistical analysis**, ultimately producing an **industrial decision-support report**.

### 4.3 Activity Classification Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Total Working Time of Operator]) --> nodeB[Productive Work Time]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Non Productive Time]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Non Working Time]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Actual Value Adding Operations]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Setup and Adjustment]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Waiting for Material or Instructions]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Machine Downtime]
    nodeD --> nodeI[Personal Time]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Fatigue Breaks]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Unavoidable Delays]
```

> **Diagram Interpretation:** This taxonomy shows how a worker's shift is decomposed into mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive activity categories — a prerequisite for any valid work sampling study.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define Work Sampling. Mention any four of its advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Work Sampling is a statistical technique used for determining the proportion of time spent by workers or machines on various activities by taking a large number of instantaneous, random observations over a study period. It was introduced by **L.H.C. Tippett in 1935**.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Economical:** Less time and manpower are required compared to continuous stopwatch time study.
2. **No Stopwatch Needed:** Observations are instantaneous dichotomous recordings.
3. **Suitable for Non-Repetitive Work:** Can be applied to irregular, long-cycle, or non-standardized tasks where time study is impractical.
4. **Minimal Worker Disruption:** Because observations are random and brief, workers do not alter their natural work pace (the *Hawthorne effect* is reduced).

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **State and explain the statistical formula used for determining the number of observations in work sampling.**

**Model Answer:**

The number of observations *N* required in a work sampling study is determined by the formula:

$$N = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}}$$

**Where:**
- $Z$ = Standard normal variate corresponding to the desired confidence level (e.g., $Z = 1.96$ for 95% confidence).
- $p$ = Estimated proportion of time spent on the activity of interest.
- $(1-p)$ = Proportion of time NOT spent on that activity.
- $e$ = Allowable absolute error (accuracy tolerance).

For the commonly used **95.45% confidence level** where $Z = 2$:

$$N = \frac{4 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}}$$

When *p* is unknown, **p = 0.5** is used (because $p(1-p)$ is maximized at 0.5), giving the most conservative (largest) sample size:

$$N_{max} = \frac{1}{e^{2}}$$

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer ANY ONE of the following questions. Each carries **14 marks** with internal choice between sub-parts as shown.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the step-by-step procedure for conducting a Work Sampling study in an industry. Include the formulas used.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define Objectives and Scope**
The study's purpose is clearly defined — e.g., determining machine utilization, establishing worker delay percentage, or computing standard times.

**Step 2: Classify Activities into Discrete Categories**
All possible worker/machine states are categorized into mutually exclusive groups such as:
- Productive working
- Idle (waiting)
- Personal time
- Machine breakdown
- Setup/Adjustment

**Step 3: Design the Random Observation Schedule**
Random observation times are generated using **Random Number Tables** (e.g., Tippett's Tables). The observer walks a pre-planned randomized route through the work area at randomized instants.

**Step 4: Determine Required Sample Size (N)**
Using the formula:

$$N = \frac{Z^{2} \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}}$$

For 95% confidence with $Z = 1.96$:

$$N = \frac{3.8416 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^{2}}$$

**Step 5: Conduct Pilot Study (200 observations)**
A preliminary set of ~200 observations is taken to estimate *p* and verify the formula assumption. The pilot *p* is then used to refine the final *N*.

**Step 6: Execute Main Study**
The full *N* observations are taken over multiple days, at random intervals, ensuring no pattern is observable to the workers.

**Step 7: Tabulate and Analyze**
Calculate proportions:

$$p = \frac{\text{Number of observations in category}}{\text{Total observations } N}$$

Verify accuracy using confidence limits:

$$CL = p \pm Z\sqrt{\frac{p(1-p)}{N}}$$

**Step 8: Compute Standard Times and Allowances**
If delay (non-productive) observations form *p_del*, then the allowance percentage is $p_{del} \times 100$.

---

> **(b)** [7 Marks] In a work sampling study, preliminary observations showed that a worker is engaged in productive work 75% of the time. Determine the number of observations required for:
> - (i) ±4% accuracy at 95% confidence (Z = 1.96)
> - (ii) ±3% accuracy at 95% confidence (Z = 1.96)
> Also verify the normal approximation validity.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Given:** $p = 0.75$, $1-p = 0.25$

**Part (i):** $e = 0.04$, $Z = 1.96$

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (0.75)(0.25)}{(0.04)^{2}} \\
N &= \frac{3.8416 \times 0.1875}{0.0016} \\
N &= \frac{0.7203}{0.0016} \\
N &= 450.19
\end{aligned}$$

**Round up: $N = 451$ observations**

**Part (ii):** $e = 0.03$, $Z = 1.96$

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{(1.96)^{2} \cdot (0.75)(0.25)}{(0.03)^{2}} \\
N &= \frac{3.8416 \times 0.1875}{0.0009} \\
N &= \frac{0.7203}{0.0009} \\
N &= 800.33
\end{aligned}$$

**Round up: $N = 801$ observations**

**Verification of Normal Approximation:**
- $Np = 801 \times 0.75 = 600.75 \geq 5$ ✓
- $N(1-p) = 801 \times 0.25 = 200.25 \geq 5$ ✓

Both conditions are satisfied, so the normal approximation is **valid**.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Lose Up to 2 Marks Here**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often use $Z = 2$ instead of $Z = 1.96$ when the problem specifies 95% confidence. Always check the exact confidence value stated in the question.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students round *down* instead of *up*. You MUST round UP because a fractional observation is impossible and rounding down violates the error guarantee.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students forget to verify $Np \geq 5$ and $N(1-p) \geq 5$ — this is a frequently tested 1-mark sub-part in KTU papers.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the statistical principles underlying Work Sampling. Explain why it is considered superior to continuous time study for certain applications.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Statistical Principles:**

**1. Law of Large Numbers:** As the number of random observations *N* approaches infinity, the sample proportion *p* converges to the true population proportion. Hence, a sufficiently large *N* yields a statistically accurate estimate.

**2. Binomial Distribution:** Each observation is a dichotomous event (working/not working). For *N* observations with true proportion *p*:

$$P(X = k) = \binom{N}{k} p^{k} (1-p)^{N-k}$$

**3. Normal Approximation:** For large *N* (when $Np \geq 5$ and $N(1-p) \geq 5$), the binomial distribution converges to the normal distribution:

$$\mu = Np, \quad \sigma = \sqrt{Np(1-p)}$$

**4. Central Limit Theorem:** The sampling distribution of the sample proportion *p* is approximately normal for large samples, regardless of the parent population distribution.

**Why Work Sampling is Superior to Continuous Time Study:**

| Aspect | Work Sampling | Continuous Time Study |
|---|---|---|
| **Observation Duration** | Instantaneous | Continuous over long periods |
| **Observer Fatigue** | Minimal | High (after 2 hours) |
| **Cost** | Low (no stopwatch) | High |
| **Effect on Worker** | Negligible (random peeks) | Significant (extended observation) |
| **Suitable for Non-Repetitive Tasks** | Yes | No |
| **Statistical Confidence** | Quantifiable via Z and e | Less quantifiable |
| **Group Studies** | Easily handles 5–10 workers | Difficult |

---

> **(b)** [7 Marks] A work sampling study was conducted on 5 machines over 10 days. A total of 600 observations were recorded, of which 96 observations showed the machines in an idle state. The plant works for 8 hours per day with one shift. Calculate:
> - (i) The percentage of machine idle time.
> - (ii) The standard time per piece if the normal time observed is 4.2 minutes per piece and the personal, fatigue, and contingency allowance is 12%.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Total observations $N = 600$
- Idle observations = 96
- Normal time per piece = 4.2 minutes
- Allowance = 12% = 0.12

**Part (i): Percentage of Machine Idle Time**

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{idle} &= \frac{96}{600} = 0.16 \\
\text{Percentage} &= 0.16 \times 100 = \mathbf{16\%}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, machine idle time = **16%** of total available time.

**Part (ii): Standard Time per Piece**

The standard time formula incorporating allowance is:

$$T_{std} = NT \times (1 + Allow)$$

Where:
- $NT$ = Normal Time = 4.2 minutes
- $Allow$ = Allowance fraction = 0.12

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{std} &= 4.2 \times (1 + 0.12) \\
T_{std} &= 4.2 \times 1.12 \\
T_{std} &= \mathbf{4.704 \text{ minutes}}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Note:** The idle time percentage from work sampling informs the allowance figure. In this problem, the allowance (12%) is already provided, so direct substitution into the standard time formula is sufficient.

**Additional Verification:** The 16% idle time observed suggests that if the allowance were *derived* from work sampling alone, the allowance fraction could be interpreted as 16% — meaning the actual allowance should account for this delay. However, since the problem explicitly states allowance = 12%, we use that value.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Lose Up to 3 Marks Here**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students confuse **idle time %** with **allowance %**. They are NOT necessarily the same — idle time is what you observe; allowance is the *provision* added to normal time to obtain standard time.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Students forget the formula $T_{std} = NT \times (1 + Allow)$ and incorrectly divide by idle proportion or add them linearly.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students omit units (minutes vs. hours) — always state the unit explicitly in the final answer.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- ⭐ **Originator:** L.H.C. Tippett (1935)
- ⭐ **Also Called:** Activity Sampling / Ratio-Delay Method
- ⭐ **Statistical Basis:** Binomial → Normal Distribution (for large *N*)
- ⭐ **Key Assumption:** Observations must be **random** and **independent**

### ✅ Must-Memorize Formulas
- ⭐ Sample Size: $N = Z^{2} p(1-p) / e^{2}$
- ⭐ 95% Confidence Sample Size: $N = 4p(1-p)/e^{2}$
- ⭐ Maximum Sample Size (p=0.5): $N_{max} = 1/e^{2}$
- ⭐ Confidence Limit: $CL = p \pm Z\sqrt{p(1-p)/N}$
- ⭐ Standard Time: $T_{std} = NT \times (1 + Allow)$

### ✅ Critical Numerical Conventions
- ⭐ Always use **Z = 1.96** for 95% confidence; **Z = 2** for 95.45% confidence
- ⭐ **Always round UP** the final *N* to the next whole number
- ⭐ Normal approximation requires **both** $Np \geq 5$ AND $N(1-p) \geq 5$
- ⭐ Use **p = 0.5** when no preliminary data is available

### ✅ Common Application Areas
- ⭐ Machine utilization studies
- ⭐ Workforce delay analysis
- ⭐ Setting standards for indirect labor
- ⭐ Establishing allowances
- ⭐ Productivity improvement projects

### ✅ Frequent KTU Exam Traps
- ⚠️ Don't confuse idle % with allowance %
- ⚠️ Don't use Z = 2 when 95% (not 95.45%) is specified
- ⚠️ Don't round *down* the sample size
- ⚠️ Don't forget to verify normal approximation validity
- ⚠️ Don't forget to specify UNITS in final numerical answers

### ✅ Comparison with Other Methods
- ⭐ **vs. Time Study:** Cheaper, no stopwatch, less Hawthorne effect
- ⭐ **vs. Predetermined Motion Time Systems (PMTS):** Less detailed, more statistical, faster
- ⭐ **vs. Analytical Estimation:** More objective, data-driven

> 🎓 **Final Tip for KTU 2024 Success:** In numerical problems, always SHOW ALL STEPS of substitution clearly — KTU examiners award step-marks even if the final numerical answer is incorrect. Always end with a clearly boxed final answer in the correct units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Predetermined Motion Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Predetermined Motion Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS)** is a standardized work measurement technique that assigns **predetermined time values** to basic human motions (such as reach, grasp, move, position, release, etc.) based on extensive research into the factors influencing motion performance. These time values are derived from laboratory analysis of thousands of motion picture films and are presented in published tables, eliminating the need for a stopwatch on the shop floor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** PMTS is a **synthetic (or synthetic-estimating)** method of determining standard time. It is classified under *Indirect Work Measurement Techniques* along with Standard Data and Work Factor systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Motion Dictionary"

Imagine a child learning the English language. Instead of inventing every sentence from scratch, the child uses a **dictionary** containing thousands of pre-built words, each with a defined meaning and length. The child combines these words to construct sentences quickly and consistently.

PMTS works exactly the same way for industrial work measurement:

- **The Dictionary** → PMTS motion tables (e.g., Reach, Grasp, Move, Position, Release)
- **The Words** → Basic human motions with pre-assigned time values
- **The Sentences** → Complete work cycles analyzed by combining these basic motions

Just as a dictionary ensures every English speaker uses words in the same consistent way, PMTS ensures every industrial engineer analyzes a job using **identical, scientifically validated time standards**, irrespective of which plant, country, or engineer performs the analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Idea:** The time for **any** manual task is determined *before* the task is performed, by summing the time values of its constituent basic motions as catalogued in published standard tables.

## 1.3 Standard Time Units Used in PMTS

PMTS systems use specialized time units to maintain decimal precision:

- **TMU (Time Measurement Unit)** = **0.00001 hour** = **0.0006 minute** = **0.036 second**
- **1 second = 27.8 TMU** (approximately)
- **1 minute = 1667 TMU** (approximately)
- **1 hour = 100,000 TMU**

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the conversion: **1 hour = 100,000 TMU**. This is the single most-used conversion in KTU PMTS numerical problems.

## 1.4 Origins & Historical Context

The concept of PMTS was pioneered in the 1920s–1940s to overcome the **rating variability** and **skill-based inconsistency** of stopwatch time study. The first widely accepted PMTS was **Motion Economy** (developed by Gilbreth), followed by **Methods Time Measurement (MTM)**, **Work Factor**, and **Basic Motion Timestudy**. These systems enabled engineers to determine standard times *before* the work was performed and were extensively used during **World War II** for rapid production planning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles of PMTS

Every PMTS is built on **four foundational axioms** that must be understood thoroughly for KTU examinations:

1. **Motion Decomposition Principle**
   - Every manual task, no matter how complex, can be broken down into a finite sequence of **basic human motions** (reach, grasp, move, position, release, disengage, apply pressure, crouch, etc.).
   - These motions are universally performed across all industrial work.

2. **Time Value Invariance Principle**
   - Each basic motion has a **fixed, predetermined time value** that is independent of the workplace, the operator, or the industry.
   - Time values depend only on the **motion type** and the **distance or difficulty variable**.

3. **Additivity Principle**
   - The total time for a job is the **algebraic sum** of the time values of all its constituent basic motions.
   - This allows the analyst to compute standard times by *synthesis* rather than observation.

4. **Consistency Principle**
   - Two qualified analysts, using the same PMTS, will arrive at **essentially the same standard time** for the same job, ensuring uniformity in industrial engineering practice.

## 2.2 Classification of Major PMTS Systems

The major PMTS systems studied under the KTU 2024 syllabus are summarized below:

| **PMTS System** | **Full Name** | **Year Developed** | **Basic Motion Unit** | **Primary Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MTM** | Methods Time Measurement | 1948 | TMU (1 TMU = 0.036 s) | General manufacturing, clerical |
| **WF** | Work Factor | 1938 | WF Unit (1 WF = 0.00001 hr) | Heavy industrial work |
| **BMT** | Basic Motion Timestudy | 1930s | TMU | General manufacturing |
| **MOST** | Maynard Operation Sequence Technique | 1960s | TS (Time Scale) Unit | High-level operations |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master formula sheet** for PMTS-based numerical problems in KTU exams. Memorize every line.

| **Formula / Conversion** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Time Computation | $T_{std} = \sum T_{basic\ motions} \times (1 + Allowances)$ | Time in TMU, converted to minutes/hours |
| Allowance Factor Conversion | $T_{std\ (min)} = T_{normal\ (min)} \times (1 + A)$ | $A$ is decimal allowance (e.g., 0.15 for 15%) |
| TMU to Seconds | $T_{sec} = T_{TMU} \times 0.036$ | Standard PMTS conversion |
| TMU to Minutes | $T_{min} = T_{TMU} \times 0.0006$ | Derived from above |
| TMU to Hours | $T_{hr} = T_{TMU} \times 0.00001$ | **Most-used conversion in KTU** |
| 1 Hour in TMU | $1\ hr = 100{,}000\ TMU$ | Exact conversion constant |
| 1 Minute in TMU | $1\ min \approx 1666.67\ TMU$ | Use 1667 for KTU problems |
| 1 Second in TMU | $1\ s \approx 27.78\ TMU$ | Use 27.8 for KTU problems |
| Standard Daily Hours | $8\ hr = 480\ min = 800{,}000\ TMU$ | KTU standard working day |
| Standard Weekly Hours | $48\ hr = 2{,}880\ min = 4{,}800{,}000\ TMU$ | Six-day work week assumed by KTU |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Exam Rule:** When converting TMU back to standard time, always apply the **personal, fatigue, and delay (PFD) allowance** *after* summing the motion TMUs. A common error is to add allowance to individual motions.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of PMTS

PMTS is widely deployed in:

- **Pre-production planning**: Setting fair day-work standards before the line is operational
- **Lean manufacturing and 5S audits**: Quantifying waste in human motion
- **Ergonomics and method improvement**: Comparing alternate methods without stopwatches
- **Indirect work standards**: Setting standards for maintenance, clerical, and supervisory work where stopwatch study is impractical
- **Cost estimation and quotation engineering**: Quoting piece-price rates with defensible scientific backing
- **Worker training and incentive systems**: Establishing fair "100% performance" benchmarks

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Master Conversion Chain for PMTS Time Units

The PMTS conversion system is anchored on the definition of **1 TMU = 0.00001 hour**. All other conversions are derived from this single anchor.

> [!NOTE]
> Each algebraic step is shown explicitly. KTU valuation key requires students to show the intermediate conversion logic to earn full marks.

### Step 1: Start with the Defining Equation

$$1\ TMU = 0.00001\ hour$$

### Step 2: Convert Hours to Minutes

Since 1 hour equals 60 minutes, multiply the right-hand side by 60:

$$1\ TMU = 0.00001 \times 60\ min$$

$$1\ TMU = 0.0006\ min$$

### Step 3: Convert Minutes to Seconds

Since 1 minute equals 60 seconds, multiply again by 60:

$$1\ TMU = 0.0006 \times 60\ s$$

$$1\ TMU = 0.036\ s$$

### Step 4: Derive the Inverse Conversions

Inverting each of the above equations gives us the standard KTU conversions:

$$1\ s = \frac{1}{0.036}\ TMU = 27.78\ TMU \approx 27.8\ TMU$$

$$1\ min = 60 \times 27.78\ TMU = 1666.67\ TMU \approx 1667\ TMU$$

$$1\ hr = 60 \times 1666.67\ TMU = 100{,}000\ TMU$$

### Step 5: Build the Master Multiplication Table

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{sec} &= T_{TMU} \times 0.036 \\
T_{min} &= T_{TMU} \times 0.0006 \\
T_{hr} &= T_{TMU} \times 0.00001 \\
T_{TMU} &= T_{sec} \times 27.8 \\
T_{TMU} &= T_{min} \times 1667 \\
T_{TMU} &= T_{hr} \times 100{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of PMTS with Stopwatch Time Study

This comparison matrix is a **high-yield KTU topic** that appears almost every year in either Part A or Part B.

| **Parameter** | **Stopwatch Time Study** | **Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Method Type** | Direct measurement (stopwatch on shop floor) | Indirect measurement (synthetic, table-based) |
| **Standard Set** | *After* the job is in production | *Before* the job is in production |
| **Operator Skill** | Must use a trained, experienced operator | Skill of operator is irrelevant; based on standard motions |
| **Performance Rating** | Required (subjective, leads to disputes) | Not required (time values are already normal) |
| **Job Cycle Time** | Suitable for short, repetitive cycles | Suitable for both short and long cycles |
| **Use of Motion Pictures** | Used only for motion analysis | Used to *build* the basic motion time tables |
| **Consistency** | Varies between observers | Highly consistent across observers |
| **Indirect Work** | Difficult to measure (e.g., maintenance) | Easily measured using motion tables |
| **Cost** | Low initial cost; ongoing labor cost | High initial training cost; minimal ongoing cost |
| **Skill Required by Analyst** | Moderate (rating skill) | High (motion decomposition skill) |
| **Effect on Method** | Cannot help improve method | Can compare alternate methods on paper |
| **Time to Set Standard** | Relatively short (1–2 days) | Relatively long (2–4 weeks for complex jobs) |
| **Commonly Used In** | Production departments, machine shops | Methods engineering, IE offices, pre-production planning |

## 3.3 Derivation: Standard Time from PMTS Motion Data

**Worked Example (KTU-Style):**

A manual assembly operation is analyzed using MTM. The basic motion TMU values are:

- Reach to bolt (20 cm distance): 12.0 TMU
- Grasp bolt: 6.0 TMU
- Move bolt to fixture: 10.0 TMU
- Position bolt into hole: 23.0 TMU
- Release bolt: 2.0 TMU
- Apply pressure (press-fit): 15.0 TMU
- Release pressure: 2.0 TMU

The PFD allowance is **15%**. Calculate the standard time per cycle in **seconds**.

**Step 1: Sum all basic motion TMU values**

$$T_{normal} = 12.0 + 6.0 + 10.0 + 23.0 + 2.0 + 15.0 + 2.0 = 70.0\ TMU$$

**Step 2: Convert TMU to seconds (1 TMU = 0.036 s)**

$$T_{sec} = 70.0 \times 0.036 = 2.52\ s$$

**Step 3: Apply PFD allowance of 15%**

$$T_{std} = 2.52 \times (1 + 0.15)$$

$$T_{std} = 2.52 \times 1.15 = 2.898\ s \approx 2.90\ s$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Round off only at the final step. Showing intermediate unrounded values demonstrates analytic depth and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PMTS Application Workflow — High-Yield Diagram

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **end-to-end PMTS application procedure** as examined in KTU. Every box represents a stage an Industrial Engineer must complete when using PMTS to set a standard.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Job Selected for Standardization"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Conduct Methods Analysis"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Decompose Job into Basic Human Motions"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Identify Motion Type and Distance Variable"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Look Up TMU Value from PMTS Tables"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Step 5: Record TMU for Each Motion"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Step 6: Sum All TMUs for Normal Time"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Step 7: Apply PFD Allowance Multiplier"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Step 8: Compute Standard Time per Cycle"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Step 9: Convert to Standard Daily or Hourly Output"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"Step 10: Compare Alternate Methods"}
    nodeK -- Yes --> nodeB
    nodeK -- No --> nodeL["END: Document Standard and Publish"]
```

## 4.2 Classification Architecture of PMTS Systems

This Mermaid block diagram maps the **family tree of major PMTS systems** and their primary application domains.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Predetermined Motion Time Systems Family"]
    nodeA --> nodeB["MTM - Methods Time Measurement"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["WF - Work Factor System"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["BMT - Basic Motion Timestudy"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["MOST - Maynard Operation Sequence Technique"]
    nodeA --> nodeF["DMT - Dimensional Motion Times"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["General Manufacturing"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Clerical and Office Work"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["Heavy Industrial Assembly"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Mining and Construction"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Older General Manufacturing"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["High-Level Operations"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Logistics and Warehousing"]

    nodeF --> nodeF1["Modern CNC and Semi-Automated Lines"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of PMTS Decision Engine

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of how a PMTS engine ingests a job description and produces a standard time.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["INPUT BLOCK: Job Description and Layout Diagram"] --> nodeB["DECOMPOSITION BLOCK: Motion Sequence Identifier"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["LOOKUP BLOCK: PMTS Motion Table Database"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["SUMMATION BLOCK: TMU Accumulator"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["ALLOWANCE BLOCK: PFD Multiplier Module"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["OUTPUT BLOCK: Standard Time Report and Cost Sheet"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["VALIDATION BLOCK: Compare with Stopwatch Pilot Study"]
    nodeG -- Discrepancy --> nodeB
    nodeG -- Acceptable --> nodeH["FINAL PUBLISHED STANDARD"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q: Define Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS). List any two advantages of PMTS over stopwatch time study.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS)** is a synthetic work measurement technique in which time standards are established by summing the *predetermined time values* of basic human motions catalogued in published tables. The standard time is determined *before* the work is performed, without using a stopwatch on the shop floor.

**Two Advantages over Stopwatch Time Study:**

1. **Performance rating is eliminated** — The time values in PMTS tables are already based on a *normal* or *standard* performance, removing the subjective variability introduced by stopwatch rating.
2. **Standards can be set *before* production begins** — PMTS enables pre-production planning, costing, and quotation engineering without waiting for the operator to reach a trained, steady state.

*(Any other valid advantages accepted: eliminates operator skill dependency, applicable to indirect work, allows comparison of alternate methods on paper.)* **[3 Marks]**

---

### Question 2: Unit Conversion
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q: A basic motion in MTM is recorded as 250 TMU. Convert this value to (a) seconds, and (b) hours.**

**Model Answer:**

Given: $T_{TMU} = 250\ TMU$

**Part (a): Convert to Seconds**

Using the conversion $1\ TMU = 0.036\ s$:

$$T_{sec} = 250 \times 0.036 = 9.0\ s$$

**Part (b): Convert to Hours**

Using the conversion $1\ TMU = 0.00001\ hr$:

$$T_{hr} = 250 \times 0.00001 = 0.0025\ hr = 9.0\ s\ (\text{verified})$$

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### Question A: Full Standard Time Calculation

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the fundamental principles underlying any Predetermined Motion Time System. List the basic motions used in the MTM system with their TMU values for a 20 cm reach and a 5 cm move. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A manual drilling operation consists of the following basic motions (in TMU). The PFD allowance is 20%. Compute the **standard time per piece** in **seconds** and the **standard daily output** for an 8-hour day.

| **Motion** | **TMU** |
|---|---|
| Reach for drill handle (20 cm) | 12.0 |
| Grasp drill handle | 6.0 |
| Move drill to workpiece (15 cm) | 11.0 |
| Position drill on hole | 23.0 |
| Apply pressure (drill) | 29.0 |
| Withdraw drill | 10.0 |
| Move drill back to rest (15 cm) | 11.0 |
| Release drill | 2.0 |

**[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Fundamental Principles of PMTS**

Any PMTS is built on **four pillars**:

1. **Motion Decomposition:** Every job can be broken down into a finite set of *basic human motions* (reach, grasp, move, position, release, etc.).
2. **Time Value Invariance:** Each basic motion has a *predetermined* time value that depends only on the *type* of motion and the *distance/difficulty* variable — not on the operator or workplace.
3. **Additivity:** The total normal time for a job is the *algebraic sum* of the time values of all its basic motions.
4. **Consistency:** Trained analysts using the same PMTS will arrive at the same standard time, ensuring cross-plant uniformity.

**MTM Reference Values (approximate, 1948 MTM-1):**

- Reach 20 cm (Type A) ≈ 12.0 TMU
- Move 5 cm (Type A) ≈ 6.0 TMU

*(Acceptable values from any standard MTM-1 table earn full marks.)* **[7 Marks]**

**Part (b): Standard Time Computation**

**Step 1: Sum the basic motion TMUs**

$$T_{normal} = 12.0 + 6.0 + 11.0 + 23.0 + 29.0 + 10.0 + 11.0 + 2.0$$

$$T_{normal} = 104.0\ TMU$$

**Step 2: Convert TMU to seconds**

$$T_{sec} = 104.0 \times 0.036 = 3.744\ s$$

**Step 3: Apply PFD allowance of 20%**

$$T_{std} = 3.744 \times (1 + 0.20)$$

$$T_{std} = 3.744 \times 1.20 = 4.4928\ s \approx 4.49\ s\ /\ \text{piece}$$

**Step 4: Compute Standard Daily Output**

Convert the 8-hour day to seconds:

$$T_{day} = 8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800\ s$$

$$N_{pieces} = \frac{28{,}800}{4.49} = 6414.25 \approx 6414\ \text{pieces per day}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification using TMU:** $8\ hr = 800{,}000\ TMU$; $\frac{800{,}000}{104.0 \times 1.20} = 6410\ pieces$. The small discrepancy (6414 vs 6410) is due to rounding of 0.036 s/TMU. Either answer is accepted by KTU examiners. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B: Alternative Choice — Comparative Analysis and Application

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q: (a)** Compare Predetermined Motion Time System (PMTS) with Stopwatch Time Study under any **six** technical parameters. Explain *why* PMTS is preferred for setting standards of indirect work such as maintenance. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A worker performs a job consisting of: Reach (15 cm) = 10 TMU, Grasp = 5 TMU, Move (10 cm) = 8 TMU, Position = 15 TMU, Release = 2 TMU. If the worker repeats this job **120 times per hour** and the PFD allowance is **18%**, verify whether the worker is performing **above**, **at**, or **below** the standard. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a): Six-Parameter Comparison** *(6 parameters × 1 mark each = 6 marks, plus 1 mark for indirect work explanation)*

| **Parameter** | **PMTS** | **Stopwatch Time Study** |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Set | Before production | After production |
| Performance Rating | Not required | Required (subjective) |
| Operator Skill | Irrelevant | Operator must be trained |
| Indirect Work | Easily measured | Difficult to measure |
| Consistency Across Analysts | Very high | Low to moderate |
| Initial Cost | High (training) | Low |

**Why PMTS is Preferred for Indirect Work:**

Indirect work such as maintenance, inspection, or clerical tasks involves **long, irregular, and infrequent cycles** that are impractical to capture using a stopwatch. PMTS uses the *basic motion tables* to compute standard times *synthetically*, allowing the engineer to set defensible standards for one-off maintenance jobs simply by decomposing them into standard motions. **[7 Marks]**

**Part (b): Performance Verification**

**Step 1: Sum the basic motion TMUs for one cycle**

$$T_{normal} = 10 + 5 + 8 + 15 + 2 = 40\ TMU$$

**Step 2: Convert to seconds per cycle**

$$T_{sec} = 40 \times 0.036 = 1.44\ s\ /\ \text{cycle}$$

**Step 3: Apply PFD allowance of 18% to get the standard time per cycle**

$$T_{std} = 1.44 \times 1.18 = 1.6992\ s\ /\ \text{cycle}$$

**Step 4: Convert to standard cycles per hour**

Since 1 hour = 3600 s:

$$N_{std} = \frac{3600}{1.6992} = 2118.64\ \text{cycles per hour}$$

**Step 5: Compare with actual performance**

Actual cycles per hour = 120. Standard cycles per hour = 2118.64.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The worker's actual output (120 cycles/hr) is drastically *below* the standard (2119 cycles/hr).**
>
> **Efficiency** = $\frac{120}{2118.64} \times 100 = 5.66\%$
>
> This indicates the worker is performing at only ~5.66% of standard — almost certainly indicating either a misinterpretation of the job description or a serious process bottleneck. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on PMTS Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the PFD Allowance (–2 to –3 marks):** Students often compute the *normal* time and forget to apply the allowance multiplier. Always re-read the problem statement to check if allowance is given as a *percentage* (e.g., 15%) or as a *decimal* (e.g., 0.15).
> 2. **Wrong TMU-to-Time Conversion Direction (–2 marks):** Confusing $1\ TMU = 0.036\ s$ with $1\ s = 0.036\ TMU$. Memorize: **TMU is the smaller unit, so one TMU equals a small fraction of a second.**
> 3. **Confusing PMTS with Standard Data (–1 to –2 marks):** PMTS is based on *basic human motions*; Standard Data is based on *elements of work* (larger aggregates). Examiners deduct marks when students use the two terms interchangeably.
> 4. **Mixing MTM and WF Values in the Same Problem (–1 mark):** Always use values from **one** PMTS system consistently throughout a single computation. MTM uses TMU; WF uses WF units.
> 5. **Not Showing the Conversion of Standard Daily Output (–1 mark):** The final answer to standard daily output must be in *pieces per day*, not TMU per day. Convert at the end.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall.**

- **Definition:** PMTS = Synthetic work measurement using predetermined time values for basic human motions.
- **Core Anchor:** $1\ TMU = 0.00001\ hr = 0.0006\ min = 0.036\ s$.
- **Three Pillars of PMTS:** Decomposition, Invariance, Additivity.
- **Major PMTS Family:** MTM (1948), WF (1938), BMT (1930s), MOST (1960s).
- **Key Advantage:** Standards set *before* production; *no performance rating* required; *applicable to indirect work*.
- **Key Limitation:** Requires *highly trained analysts*; not suitable for very short cycle jobs (over-decomposition risk); high initial training cost.
- **Standard Time Formula:** $T_{std} = \sum T_{TMU} \times 0.036 \times (1 + A)\ s$.
- **Standard Daily Output Formula:** $N = \frac{8 \times 3600}{T_{std\ (s)}}$ pieces per day.
- **Typical PFD Allowance Range in KTU Problems:** 10% to 25%.
- **MTM Basic Motions (must memorize):** Reach, Move, Turn, Grasp, Position, Release, Disengage, Apply Pressure, Crouch, Kneel, Sit, Stand, Walk, Step.
- **PMTS vs Stopwatch:** PMTS is *indirect* and *synthetic*; Stopwatch is *direct* and *observational*.
- **Common Conversion Trap:** $1\ s = 27.8\ TMU$ (NOT 36 TMU).
- **KTU Favourite Question Pattern:** "Given a sequence of TMU values and a PFD allowance, compute standard time per piece in seconds and standard daily output."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Study: Memo Motion and Micro Motion Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion Study: Memo Motion and Micro Motion Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motion Study — The Parent Concept

**Motion Study** is the systematic, scientific analysis of the bodily movements involved in performing a task, with the objective of eliminating wasted movements, simplifying the work, establishing the **best possible sequence** of motions, and standardizing the method. It was pioneered by **Frank B. Gilbreth** (and later refined with his wife **Lillian Gilbreth**) in the early 1900s. Gilbreth's lifelong dictum was: *"There is always a better way."*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus Verbatim):** Motion Study is a technique for analyzing the body motions used in performing a given task with the object of eliminating unnecessary motions and establishing the best possible sequence and method.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — How a Surgeon Operates vs. How a Robot Sorts

Think of motion study like watching a master chef fillet a fish. The chef's hand moves in **a dozen tiny, fluid motions** — some absolutely essential (cutting along the bone) and many that are *habitual waste* (re-gripping, lifting the knife between cuts, looking away). A motion study analyst acts like a high-speed camera that not only records the chef's hands but also **tags every micro-motion with a label** (like "search," "grasp," "position"). After the study, the analyst redesigns the workstation so the chef does only the *essential motions* — saving time, energy, and RSI (Repetitive Strain Injury). Memo Motion and Micro Motion Study are the two *camera technologies* used to perform this scrutiny.

## 1.3 Memo Motion Study — The "Slow-Speed Snapshot" Technique

**Memo Motion Study** is a technique of motion study in which a **movie camera** is used to take **exposures at fixed time intervals** (typically **1 frame every second**, but it can vary from 0.5 to several seconds depending on the cycle length) over an extended period (often **20–40 minutes** of a repetitive cycle). The resulting film is then projected at a normal projection speed, producing a **"fast-forward" effect** that condenses the long recording into a few minutes and reveals the **pattern, rhythm, and idle periods** of the worker.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight — The "Memo" Prefix:** The word *Memo* (from Latin *memorandum* = "to be remembered") was coined because the original purpose of the technique was to **record jobs that the analyst could not continuously observe** — a kind of "memory aid" for the engineer reviewing the work later.

### 1.3.1 Equipment — The Pravail / Memo Motion Camera

The instrument is a **16 mm spring-driven movie camera fitted with a timing device (intervalometer)** and a **revolving disc shutter** controlled by a clockwork mechanism. The camera is fixed on a tripod about **1.5 m to 2 m** from the work area. The intervalometer can be set to expose **1, 2, 5, 10, or 20 frames per minute** (or per second in some models).

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Frame interval | 0.5 s, 1 s, 2 s, 5 s (selectable) |
| Total shooting time | 15–45 minutes |
| Projection speed | 16 frames/second (standard cinema) |
| Recording-to-projection time ratio | 60:1 to 600:1 (compression) |
| Camera position | Tripod-mounted, 1.5–2 m from operator |

## 1.4 Micro Motion Study — The "High-Speed Microscope" Technique

**Micro Motion Study** is a technique of motion study in which a **movie camera** is used to take exposures at a **normal or near-normal speed** (typically **16 frames per second** for a 16 mm camera, or even higher with 35 mm or digital high-speed cameras — up to **1000+ fps** in modern practice). The film is then projected **at slower-than-recording speed** (often 1–4 frames per second) to "stretch" the motion and allow frame-by-frame analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Micro Motion Study is the analysis of motion elements by means of a movie camera taking a large number of frames per second, with the film subsequently studied frame-by-frame at a much slower projection speed to detect small, fast, or imperceptible motions.

### 1.4.1 Equipment — The Gilbreth Chronocyclegraph Camera

The original Gilbreth micro motion camera used a **fixed-lens 16 mm camera** synchronized with a **cyclograph (a clock dial mounted in the field of view)** to display elapsed time directly on each frame. Modern digital equivalents (e.g., **Qualisys, Vicon, OptiTrack, or smartphone slow-motion at 240/480 fps**) extend the principle to motion-capture systems.

## 1.5 Why Both Techniques? — A Side-by-Side Comparison

| Feature | Memo Motion Study | Micro Motion Study |
|---|---|---|
| Frame rate | Very low (1 frame / 1–5 s) | High (16–1000+ fps) |
| Purpose | Identify **macro** patterns, idle times, cycle rhythm | Analyze **micro** motions, therbligs, fast events |
| Operator burden | None (worker often unaware) | None (worker can be unaware) |
| Film viewing | Projected fast (compressed time) | Projected slow (expanded time) |
| Best for | Long repetitive cycles, multi-worker flow | Short, fast, intricate operations |
| Cost | Lower film usage, less analysis time | More film, more lab analysis time |
| Output tool | Worker activity chart, idle-time chart | **SIMO chart** (Simultaneous Motion Chart) |
| Inventor | M. Eugene H. Pravail (extension of Gilbreth) | Frank & Lillian Gilbreth (1912–1920s) |

## 1.6 The Building Block: Therbligs

Both memo and micro motion studies rely on breaking human motion into **17 fundamental elements called *Therbligs*** (a near-anagram of *Gilbreth*). A therblig is a *basic, indivisible, classifiable* unit of human motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **18th therblig — "Rest"** (R) — is sometimes added in the classification, although Frank Gilbreth originally treated rest as an *absence* of motion. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know **17 fundamental therbligs plus the concept of rest**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Therbligs:** Just as *chemical elements* combine to form molecules, *therbligs* combine to form a *complete operation*. Each therblig has a **symbol, a colour, and a code letter** so it can be drawn on a **SIMO (Simultaneous Motion) chart** by hand or by analysis software.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 17 Fundamental Therbligs — Complete Reference

Frank Gilbreth identified **17 fundamental therbligs** that, in combination, describe every possible purposeful hand/eye/body motion in any manual task. Each is assigned a **3-letter code**, a **symbol**, and a **colour** (black, red, or green) for SIMO chart drawing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Colour-Coding Convention (high-yield for KTU):**
> - **Black (or hatched) symbols** = *Effective* (productive) therbligs
> - **Red symbols** = *Ineffective* (waste / non-productive) therbligs
> - **Green symbols** = *Preparatory / Semi-effective* therbligs
> - **Blue or dashed symbols** = *Rest* (if 18th is included)

### 2.1.1 Master Table of the 17 Therbligs

| # | Therblig Name | Symbol | Code | Colour | Category | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Transport (TL)** | An arrow → | TL | Black | Effective | Move an object from one place to another (load, carry) |
| 2 | **Grasp (G)** | A cup/G-shape | G | Black | Effective | Closing the fingers around an object |
| 3 | **Release (RL)** | A cup upside-down | RL | Black | Effective | Letting go of an object |
| 4 | **Position (P)** | A peg on a hole | P | Black | Effective | Aligning, orienting an object for assembly |
| 5 | **Assemble (A)** | Two parts joining | A | Black | Effective | Joining two parts together |
| 6 | **Disassemble (DA)** | Two parts separating | DA | Black | Effective | Taking apart two joined parts |
| 7 | **Use (U)** | A man-with-tool | U | Black | Effective | Using a tool or device |
| 8 | **Inspect (I)** | A magnifier/eye | I | Black | Effective | Verifying quality/dimensions |
| 9 | **Pre-position (PP)** | A peg near a hole | PP | Black | Effective | Positioning object in advance (e.g., on jig) |
| 10 | **Search (SH)** | Eye with squiggles | SH | Red | Ineffective | Trying to find an object |
| 11 | **Find (F)** | Eye with rings | F | Red | Ineffective | A momentary search result (mental reaction) |
| 12 | **Select (ST)** | A hand sifting | ST | Red | Ineffective | Choosing among several objects |
| 13 | **Plan (PN)** | A head with thought lines | PN | Green | Semi-effective | Mental delay — deciding next move |
| 14 | **Hold (H)** | A magnet on iron | H | Green | Semi-effective | Holding an object (no useful work) |
| 15 | **Rest (R)** | A seated figure | R | Green/Blue | Ineffective | Resting to overcome fatigue |
| 16 | **Unavoidable Delay (UD)** | A man with crossed tools | UD | Red | Ineffective | Delay beyond operator control |
| 17 | **Avoidable Delay (AD)** | A man leaning | AD | Red | Ineffective | Delay within operator control |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common KTU Trap:** Some textbooks swap the *names* of **Hold** and **Pre-position** in their symbols. Always verify against the original Gilbreth chart shown in your module material. KTU examiners award partial marks even if you describe the *meaning* correctly.

## 2.2 The SIMO Chart (Simultaneous Motion Chart)

A **SIMO Chart** is the principal *output* of a micro motion study. It is a multi-column time-vs-motion diagram that records **what the LEFT HAND, the RIGHT HAND, and the EYES are doing simultaneously** at each instant of the cycle.

### 2.2.1 Structural Anatomy of a SIMO Chart

A SIMO chart has **three or more vertical columns** (Left Hand, Right Hand, Eyes — sometimes feet or both feet are added). Time is plotted on the **horizontal axis**, divided into equal small intervals (typically 0.01 s, 0.05 s, or one frame = 1/16 s ≈ 0.0625 s for 16 fps film).

Within each column, the analyst draws the **therblig symbols** stacked in sequence, with the **length of the symbol proportional to the time the therblig lasted**. Two horizontal lines below the chart show the **cycle time** and the **elapsed time clock**.

### 2.2.2 What the SIMO Chart Reveals

- **Both hands working simultaneously** vs. one hand waiting for the other (an indicator of poor workstation balance)
- **Idle micro-periods** that are too short to see with the naked eye
- **Search / Find** events that point to poor kitting, lighting, or labelling
- **Rearrangement of work** so that left and right hand *cycle together* (a key Gilbreth principle of **"two hands should not be idle at the same time"**)

## 2.3 Procedure for Conducting a Motion Study

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Examination Pattern:** When asked to "explain the procedure for motion study," examiners expect **8–10 sequential steps** as follows:

1. **Select the job** to be studied (one with the highest labour cost / highest volume / most fatigue complaints).
2. **Obtain management approval** and inform the operator.
3. **Record the present method** using a motion picture camera (memo motion or micro motion) and a **chronometer** (decimal-minute stopwatch).
4. **Project and analyse the film**, identifying therbligs for both hands.
5. **Draw the SIMO chart** and list each therblig with its duration.
6. **Question the sequence** — for every therblig ask: *Is it necessary? Can it be eliminated? Can it be combined with another? Can it be done by a different hand?*
7. **Develop the improved method** — redesign workplace layout, tools, fixtures, materials feed.
8. **Test the new method** — record a fresh film of the improved cycle.
9. **Standardize the method** — write a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) and train the operator.
10. **Maintain the method** — periodic re-checks to ensure no regression.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable for KTU 2024. Memorize them with units.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} + \text{Allowances}$ | Total time to complete one cycle under standard conditions | **minutes** (or **seconds**) |
| 2 | $\text{Normal Time} = \text{Observed Time} \times \text{Performance Rating}$ | Time taken by a *rated-average* operator | **minutes** |
| 3 | $\text{Allowance Factor} = \dfrac{\text{Allowance Time}}{\text{Normal Time}}$ | Dimensionless ratio | **%** |
| 4 | $\text{Standard Time} = \text{Normal Time} \times \left( 1 + \text{Allowance Factor} \right)$ | Compact form | **minutes** |
| 5 | $\text{Element Cycle Time} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i$ | Sum of all therblig times in one hand's column | **seconds** |
| 6 | $\text{Number of Frames} = \text{Frame Rate} \times \text{Recording Time}$ | Total exposures on the film | **frames** |
| 7 | $\text{Time per Frame} = \dfrac{1}{\text{Frame Rate}}$ | Duration of one exposure | **seconds/frame** |
| 8 | $\text{Compression Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Recording Time}}{\text{Projection Time}}$ | Memo motion expansion factor | **dimensionless** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trap — Performance Rating Scales:** A common error is to confuse the **100% = Standard Pace** (Westinghouse / Bedaux / ILO) with the **60/80/100 scale (Methods Time Measurement – MTM)**. In *motion study*-based rating, **100%** is the standard for a *qualified, motivated* operator at *normal pace*. Always state the rating system used.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | How Motion Study is Used |
|---|---|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Standardizing engine assembly lines; reducing micro-delays; designing carousels for both-hand symmetry |
| **Surgical Ergonomics** | Studying surgeon hand motions in laparoscopic procedures; redesigning instrument trays |
| **Apparel Manufacturing** | Sewing line balancing using SIMO charts; reducing idle hand-time |
| **Aerospace Wiring** | Bundle-routing and connector-insertion training using micro motion analysis |
| **Pharmaceutical Packaging** | Memo motion studies of long filling-line cycles to find hidden idle time |
| **Warehouse Picking** | Memo motion of picker cycles across multi-aisle warehouses |
| **Sports Biomechanics** | Therblig analysis of golf swings, cricket strokes, swimming strokes |

## 2.6 Advantages and Limitations — For Examination Purposes

### 2.6.1 Advantages of Motion Study
- **Permanent record** — the film can be re-analysed by different experts years later.
- **Frame-by-frame precision** impossible to achieve with naked-eye observation.
- **Reveals micro-delays** (0.01–0.1 s) that stopwatches cannot resolve.
- **Both hands and eyes** can be studied *simultaneously* on the SIMO chart.
- **Worker-friendly** — the camera is non-intrusive; no instrumented glove required.
- **Useful as a training aid** for new operators.

### 2.6.2 Limitations
- **Cost** of camera, film (or digital storage), projector, and analyst time.
- **Skill required** to interpret films and assign therbligs reliably (high inter-analyst variability).
- **One-off study** — every job redesign needs a fresh film.
- **Limited to visible motion** — cannot directly capture **mental** therbligs (Plan, Find) which must be inferred.
- **Not suitable for very fast or very large motions** (e.g., robotic arms) without specialised high-speed cameras.
