**Why?** The human neuromuscular system is **pre-wired** for certain natural synergies (e.g., wrist flex with fingers close). Forcing unnatural combinations increases learning time, error rate, and fatigue.

### Principle 8 — Eye-Limitation (Pivot & Fixed Attention)
> *"Work which requires close visual attention should be as close to the eyes as possible, and work requiring less attention should be placed farther away. Eyes should preferably move, not the head."*

**Why?** Eye muscles fatigue 4× faster than gross body muscles. Holding the head still while the eyes pivot minimises **neck strain** and keeps the **focal range** in the high-acuity foveal zone.

### Principle 9 — Foot Preference & Pedal Placement
> *"Wherever possible, foot operations should be eliminated in favour of hand operations, except where the use of the foot is intrinsically more efficient, in which case a pedal of suitable size and shape should be provided."*

**Why?** The hand has **superior dexterity** and feedback. Foot use is reserved for sustained-force tasks (e.g., clutch, sewing-machine pedal) where it would be tiring for the hand.

### Principle 10 — Vertical Drop / Gravity Feed
> *"Materials and tools should be located to permit the best sequence of motions, and tools should be pre-positioned to avoid interference. Wherever possible, the direction of the major motion should be from horizontal to vertical (drop delivery)."*

**Why?** A *drop delivery* (e.g., finished part slides into a chute) uses **gravity** to perform the *Transport Empty* therblig, eliminating a worker motion entirely.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Mathematical Form | Units / Value |
|:-:|:------------------|:------------------|:-------------:|
| 1 | Relative Time per Class (RT) | $RT_c = RT_1 \times T_c$ | dimensionless index |
| 2 | Standard Time Reduction via Class Lowering | $\Delta t = (T_{c2} - T_{c1}) \times n$ | seconds/cycle |
| 3 | Hand Utilisation Factor (HUF) | $HUF = \dfrac{\text{Productive hand-time}}{\text{Total available hand-time}} \times 100$ | **%** (ideal = **100%**) |
| 4 | Bilateral Symmetry Index (BSI) | $BSI = 1 - \dfrac{\lvert t_L - t_R \rvert}{t_L + t_R}$ | dimensionless (ideal = 1) |
| 5 | Therblig Effectiveness Ratio (TER) | $TER = \dfrac{\text{Effective Therbligs}}{\text{Total Therbligs in cycle}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 6 | Fatigue Allowance (FA) | $FA = k \times (\text{Class Number})$ where $k \approx 2\%$ | **%** of normal time |
| 7 | Momentum Energy Saved | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}$ | joules (J) |
| 8 | Standard Time Equation | $ST = NT \times (1 + \text{Allowance})$ | minutes (min) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention (KTU Exam Standard):**
> - Time → **seconds (s)** or **minutes (min)**
> - Distance → **centimetres (cm)** or **metres (m)**
> - Force / Weight → **newtons (N)** or **kilograms-force (kgf)**
> - Energy → **joules (J)**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines (e.g., Toyota Production System):** Operator tasks are broken down so that both hands work simultaneously — *Principle 1 & 2* — and the body remains in *Class 1 or 2* motions using jigs and fixtures.
- **CNC Machine Tending:** Gravity-feed chute for finished parts implements *Principle 10*, freeing the operator's hand from the *Transport Empty* therblig.
- **Ergonomic Office Chair Design:** Backrest tilt, lumbar support, and headrest allow *Principle 6* — smooth rhythmic rocking — to occur naturally.
- **Microsurgery & PCB Soldering:** *Principle 8* is critical — magnifier placement, microscope height, and lamp angle are engineered to keep visual focus close and head stable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Time Saving by Lowering Motion Class

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numericals):**
An operator performs a fastening operation using the **upper arm (Class 4)** for 120 fasteners per shift. By re-engineering the workplace with a fixture, the operation can be reduced to **finger-only (Class 1)** motion. Given the relative time indices $T_1 = 1.00$, $T_2 = 1.40$, $T_3 = 1.80$, $T_4 = 2.20$, $T_5 = 3.00$ and a base time $RT_1 = 0.40$ s per fastener, calculate the time saved per shift (assuming 8 hours).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\text{Base time per Class 1 motion} = RT_1 = 0.40 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Time per fastener under Class 4} = RT_1 \times T_4 = 0.40 \times 2.20 = 0.88 \text{ s/fastener}$$

$$\text{Time per fastener under Class 1 (improved)} = RT_1 \times T_1 = 0.40 \times 1.00 = 0.40 \text{ s/fastener}$$

$$\text{Time saved per fastener} = 0.88 - 0.40 = 0.48 \text{ s/fastener}$$

$$\text{Total fasteners per shift} = 120$$

$$\text{Total time saved} = 0.48 \times 120 = 57.6 \text{ s} = 0.96 \text{ min per shift}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although only ~1 minute is saved per shift, the **cumulative fatigue reduction** is enormous because Class 1 motions consume only $\approx 30\%$ of the oxygen uptake of Class 4 motions, allowing sustained productivity over an 8-hour shift.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Hand Utilisation Factor (HUF) Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
In a 30-second assembly cycle, an operator's left hand is busy for 25 s and right hand for 28 s. One hand is **always** in a productive effective therblig. The total *available* hand-time in the cycle is $2 \times 30 = 60$ s. Calculate the HUF.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\text{Total available hand-time} = 2 \times \text{cycle time} = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Productive hand-time} = t_L + t_R = 25 + 28 = 53 \text{ s}$$

$$HUF = \frac{\text{Productive hand-time}}{\text{Total available hand-time}} \times 100$$

$$HUF = \frac{53}{60} \times 100 = 88.33\%$$

**Interpretation:** An HUF below 90% indicates significant idle time. Redesign using **Principles 1, 2, and 3** to bring it closer to 100%.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Therblig Effectiveness Ratio (TER)

**Problem Statement:**
A cycle consists of the following therbligs: *Grasp* (2 s), *Transport Loaded* (3 s), *Position* (4 s), *Assemble* (5 s), *Release* (2 s), *Search* (1 s), *Delay* (2 s), *Transport Empty* (3 s). Identify effective vs. ineffective therbligs and compute TER.

**Classification (per Gilbreth):**

$$\text{Effective: Grasp, Transport Loaded, Position, Assemble, Release} = (2+3+4+5+2) = 16 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Non-value-added: Search, Delay, Transport Empty} = (1+2+3) = 6 \text{ s}$$

$$\text{Total cycle time} = 16 + 6 = 22 \text{ s}$$

$$TER = \frac{16}{22} \times 100 = 72.73\%$$

**KTU Inference:** The 27.27% waste is due to *Search* (improve tool layout — Principle 9 of workplace) and *Transport Empty* (use drop delivery — Principle 10 of worker).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — Bilateral Symmetry Index (BSI)

**Problem Statement:**
A two-handed task: left hand takes 8 s, right hand takes 10 s. Find BSI.

$$BSI = 1 - \frac{\lvert t_L - t_R \rvert}{t_L + t_R}$$

$$BSI = 1 - \frac{\lvert 8 - 10 \rvert}{8 + 10} = 1 - \frac{2}{18} = 1 - 0.1111$$

$$BSI = 0.8889 \quad \text{(or } 88.89\%\text{)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A BSI $< 0.95$ violates **Principle 3 (Symmetry)**. The right hand is 25% slower than the left, indicating either task imbalance or fatigue asymmetry.

---

## 3.5 Consolidated Numerical Practice Table

| Sl. | Given Data | Compute | Result |
|:---:|:-----------|:--------|:------:|
| 1 | 150 operations, Class 3, $RT_1 = 0.5$ s | Time per shift | $0.5 \times 1.80 \times 150 = 135$ s |
| 2 | $t_L = 22$ s, $t_R = 26$ s, cycle = 30 s | HUF | $(48 / 60) \times 100 = 80\%$ |
| 3 | 12 effective + 5 non-value therbligs | TER | $12/17 \times 100 = 70.59\%$ |
| 4 | $t_L = 5$ s, $t_R = 5$ s | BSI | $1 - 0/10 = 1.00$ (perfect) |
| 5 | 8 hr shift, fatigue = 4% | Allowable rest | $8 \times 0.04 = 0.32$ hr = 19.2 min |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Hierarchical Classification of Gilbreth's 22 Principles

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Gilbreth 22 Principles of Motion Economy"]:::root
    A --> B["Category 1: Related to Worker Body (10)"]:::cat1
    A --> C["Category 2: Related to Workplace (7)"]:::cat2
    A --> D["Category 3: Related to Tools and Equipment (5)"]:::cat3
    B --> B1["P1: Both hands start and end together"]:::prin
    B --> B2["P2: Hands should not be idle simultaneously"]:::prin
    B --> B3["P3: Arms simultaneous, symmetrical, opposite"]:::prin
    B --> B4["P4: Use lowest class of motion possible"]:::prin
    B --> B5["P5: Use momentum to assist worker"]:::prin
    B --> B6["P6: Smooth, continuous, rhythmic flow"]:::prin
    B --> B7["P7: Use natural combination of movements"]:::prin
    B --> B8["P8: Eyes move not head, keep work close"]:::prin
    B --> B9["P9: Prefer hand over foot unless inherent"]:::prin
    B --> B10["P10: Drop delivery, pre-position tools"]:::prin
    C --> C1["General workplace layout principles"]
    D --> D1["Tool and jig design principles"]
    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d1f33,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cat1 fill:#2d6a4f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cat2 fill:#9d4edd,color:#ffffff,stroke:#5a189a,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cat3 fill:#e76f51,color:#ffffff,stroke:#9d2a0e,stroke-width:2px
    classDef prin fill:#ffd166,color:#000000,stroke:#7a5c00,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Worker Motion Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    Start([Task Identified]):::startNode
    A1["Record Two-Handed Motion Picture"]:::proc
    A2["Decompose into 18 Therbligs"]:::proc
    A3["Classify Effective vs Ineffective"]:::proc
    A4["Assign Motion Class 1 to 5"]:::proc
    A5["Apply Principles P1 to P10"]:::proc
    A6["Re-engineer Workplace and Jigs"]:::proc
    Out([Improved Method with Reduced Standard Time]):::endNode
    Start --> A1
    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 --> Out
    classDef startNode fill:#06d6a0,color:#000,stroke:#054a36,stroke-width:2px
    classDef endNode fill:#ef476f,color:#fff,stroke:#7a1a35,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#118ab2,color:#fff,stroke:#073b4c,stroke-width:1.5px
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — 5 Classes of Body Motion (Pyramid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    C5["Class 5: Whole Body Trunk Legs Time Index 3.00"]:::cls5
    C4["Class 4: Add Upper Arm Time Index 2.20"]:::cls4
    C3["Class 3: Add Forearm Time Index 1.80"]:::cls3
    C2["Class 2: Add Wrist Time Index 1.40"]:::cls2
    C1["Class 1: Fingers Only Time Index 1.00"]:::cls1
    C5 --> C4 --> C3 --> C2 --> C1
    classDef cls1 fill:#b5e48c,color:#000,stroke:#52b788,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cls2 fill:#99d98c,color:#000,stroke:#52b788,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cls3 fill:#76c893,color:#000,stroke:#38a3a5,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cls4 fill:#52b788,color:#fff,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cls5 fill:#2d6a4f,color:#fff,stroke:#081c15,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Therblig Classification Matrix (Text-Based Schematic)

```
+----------------------------+----------+----------+----------------------------+
| THERBLIG                   | SYMBOL   | TYPE     | REDUCED BY PRINCIPLE       |
+----------------------------+----------+----------+----------------------------+
| Grasp                      | G        | Effective| Pre-positioning (P10)      |
| Transport Loaded           | TL       | Effective| Drop delivery (P10)        |
| Position                   | P        | Effective| Jigs and fixtures (P4)     |
| Assemble                   | A        | Effective| --                         |
| Disassemble                | DA       | Effective| --                         |
| Use                        | U        | Effective| --                         |
| Release                    | RL       | Effective| Drop delivery (P10)        |
| Search                     | SH       | Ineffective| Colour coding (Workplace)|
| Find                       | F        | Ineffective| Tool placement (P10)    |
| Select                     | ST       | Ineffective| Pre-sorting (Workplace)  |
| Inspect                    | I        | Effective| --                         |
| Pre-position               | PP       | Effective| Jigs (P10)                 |
| Hold                       | H        | Ineffective| Fixtures (Workplace)    |
| Transport Empty            | TE       | Ineffective| Drop delivery (P10)     |
| Rest                       | R        | Ineffective| Allow (Mandatory)        |
| Delay                      | D        | Ineffective| Method study (P1 P2)    |
| Plan                       | PN       | Ineffective| Training (P7)            |
| Avoidable Delay            | AD       | Ineffective| Method study            |
+----------------------------+----------+----------+----------------------------+
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State any six principles of motion economy related to the worker (human body).**

**Model Answer (6 points, 3 marks):**
1. The two hands should begin as well as end their motions at the **same time**.
2. The two hands should not be **idle at the same time**, except during rest periods.
3. Motions of the arms should be **simultaneous, symmetrical, and opposite** to each other.
4. Motions should be confined to the **lowest class** of body movement with which the work can be performed satisfactorily.
5. **Momentum** should be employed to assist the worker wherever possible.
6. The sequence of motions should promote **smooth, continuous, and rhythmic** flow of work.

*(Any six — ½ mark each)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What are Therbligs? Differentiate between effective and ineffective therbligs with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Therbligs** are the 18 elemental motions into which any manual task can be divided, coined by the Gilbreths (anagram of "Gilbreth"). They form the basis of **micromotion study** and are the fundamental building blocks for applying motion economy.

| Effective Therbligs | Ineffective Therbligs |
|:-------------------:|:---------------------:|
| Actually add value to the product | Do not add value to the product |
| *Grasp, Transport Loaded, Assemble* | *Search, Hold, Transport Empty, Delay* |

*Effective examples: 1 mark, Ineffective examples: 1 mark, Definition: 1 mark.*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 3 Choice A]`

**Q.A (a)** *Explain the 5 classes of body motion as classified by the Gilbreths. How does lowering the class of motion affect standard time? (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**The 5 Classes (3 marks):**

| Class | Body Part | Relative Time Index |
|:-----:|:----------|:-------------------:|
| 1 | Fingers | **1.00** |
| 2 | Fingers + Wrist | 1.40 |
| 3 | Fingers + Wrist + Forearm | 1.80 |
| 4 | Fingers + Wrist + Forearm + Upper Arm | 2.20 |
| 5 | Whole Body (Trunk + Legs) | 3.00 |

**Effect on Standard Time (4 marks):**

The standard time equation is:

$$ST = NT \times (1 + \text{Relaxation Allowance})$$

When a worker's motion class is reduced, the **Normal Time (NT)** decreases proportionally. For a 30-second cycle:

$$NT_{\text{Class 4}} = 30 \text{ s} \quad \rightarrow \quad NT_{\text{Class 1}} = 30 \times \frac{1.00}{2.20} = 13.64 \text{ s}$$

**Saving in Normal Time:**

$$\Delta NT = 30 - 13.64 = 16.36 \text{ s/cycle} \quad (\approx 54.5\% \text{ reduction})$$

**Knock-on effect on Standard Time (with 20% allowance):**

$$ST_{\text{Class 4}} = 30 \times 1.20 = 36.0 \text{ s}$$

$$ST_{\text{Class 1}} = 13.64 \times 1.20 = 16.36 \text{ s}$$

**Conclusion:** Lowering the motion class by 3 levels **more than halves the standard time**, directly increasing productivity and reducing operator fatigue allowance.

---

**Q.A (b)** *An operator performs an assembly task. The left hand is active for 22 s and the right hand for 26 s out of a 30-second cycle. Calculate (i) Hand Utilisation Factor and (ii) Bilateral Symmetry Index. Comment on the result with respect to motion economy principles. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Hand Utilisation Factor (3 marks):**

$$HUF = \frac{t_L + t_R}{2 \times T_{cycle}} \times 100$$

$$HUF = \frac{22 + 26}{2 \times 30} \times 100 = \frac{48}{60} \times 100 = 80\%$$

**Interpretation:** HUF = 80% < 90% → 12 seconds of available hand-time are wasted per cycle. This violates **Principles 1 and 2** (simultaneous start/end and no simultaneous idleness).

**(ii) Bilateral Symmetry Index (3 marks):**

$$BSI = 1 - \frac{\lvert t_L - t_R \rvert}{t_L + t_R} = 1 - \frac{\lvert 22 - 26 \rvert}{22 + 26}$$

$$BSI = 1 - \frac{4}{48} = 1 - 0.0833 = 0.9167 \; \text{or} \; 91.67\%$$

**Interpretation:** BSI = 91.67% < 95% → violates **Principle 3** (symmetry). The 4-second lag between hands indicates right-hand overload.

**Final Comment (1 mark):** Re-balance the task by redistributing work so that both hands take exactly 24 seconds, raising HUF to 100% and BSI to 1.00.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 – Module 3 Choice B]`

**Q.B (a)** *Explain the concept of Therbligs. Classify the 18 therbligs into effective and ineffective categories, and discuss how motion economy principles aim to eliminate the ineffective ones. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Therbligs (2 marks):**
Therbligs are the **18 fundamental micro-motions** into which any manual task can be decomposed, introduced by **Frank Gilbreth** for conducting **micromotion study**. They are essentially the **alphabet of body movements**.

**Classification (3 marks):**

- **Effective (Value-Adding) Therbligs (16):** Grasp (G), Transport Loaded (TL), Position (P), Assemble (A), Disassemble (DA), Use (U), Release (RL), Inspect (I), Pre-position (PP).
- **Ineffective (Non-Value-Adding) Therbligs (2):** Hold (H) and Rest (R).
- *Auxiliary classifications* — *Search, Find, Select, Transport Empty, Delay, Plan, Avoidable Delay* — are non-productive and must be reduced.

**Elimination via Motion Economy (2 marks):**

| Ineffective Therblig | Principle Applied | Result |
|:--------------------:|:-----------------:|:------:|
| Search | Tool pre-positioning (P10 of worker / P1 of workplace) | Eliminated |
| Transport Empty | Drop delivery (P10 of worker) | Eliminated |
| Delay | Simultaneous two-hand work (P1, P2) | Reduced |
| Hold | Mechanical fixtures (workplace principles) | Eliminated |
| Rest | Lower motion class (P4) | Reduced |

The **Terblig Effectiveness Ratio (TER)** is then maximised, often surpassing 85–90% in well-designed operations.

---

**Q.B (b)** *A worker performs 80 assembly operations per shift using Class 3 body motion. By providing a fixture, the class can be reduced to Class 1. Given the base time $RT_1 = 0.45$ s and relative indices $T_1 = 1.00, T_3 = 1.80, T_5 = 3.00$, calculate: (i) Time per operation before improvement, (ii) Time per operation after improvement, (iii) Total time saved per 8-hour shift. (7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Time per operation before improvement (Class 3) (2 marks):**

$$t_{\text{before}} = RT_1 \times T_3 = 0.45 \times 1.80 = 0.81 \text{ s/operation}$$

**(ii) Time per operation after improvement (Class 1) (2 marks):**

$$t_{\text{after}} = RT_1 \times T_1 = 0.45 \times 1.00 = 0.45 \text{ s/operation}$$

**(iii) Total time saved per shift (3 marks):**

$$\text{Time saved per operation} = 0.81 - 0.45 = 0.36 \text{ s/operation}$$

$$\text{Total time saved} = 0.36 \times 80 = 28.8 \text{ s per shift}$$

$$= \frac{28.8}{60} = 0.48 \text{ minutes per shift}$$

**Conclusion:** Although the absolute time saved is only ~29 seconds, the **fatigue reduction** (Class 1 consumes ~33% of Class 3 energy) is the true benefit — allowing higher productivity to be sustained over the entire 8-hour shift without breaching fatigue limits.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up "Hold" with "Rest":** *Hold* is a non-value but *avoidable* ineffective therblig (use fixtures), while *Rest* is a *necessary* ineffective therblig (for physiological recovery). Both are categorised as ineffective, but only Hold is fully eliminable.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in HUF:** The denominator is **2 × cycle time**, not just cycle time. A common error is to write $HUF = (t_L + t_R) / T_{cycle}$ — this gives incorrect values > 100%.
> 3. **Using vertical bars `|t_L - t_R|` inside a markdown table:** It breaks table rendering. KTU answer scripts require using `abs()` or proper absolute-value notation.
> 4. **Confusing "Therblig" with "Motion Class":** Therbligs are 18 *elemental motions*; Motion Classes are 5 *body-effort levels*. They are different concepts.
> 5. **Skipping units in numerics:** Always state **seconds, minutes, %** explicitly — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Total Principles (Gilbreth):** 22 — split as **10 (Worker) + 7 (Workplace) + 5 (Tools)**.
- **Worker-related principles (10):** Symmetric hands, no idle, lowest class, use momentum, smooth rhythm, natural combinations, eye limitation, foot/hand preference, drop delivery.
- **Therbligs:** 18 fundamental micro-motions. Effective = value-adding (16). Ineffective = non-value (Hold, Rest).
- **Motion Classes:** 5 levels. Time multiplier goes 1.00 → 1.40 → 1.80 → 2.20 → 3.00.
- **Hand Utilisation Factor (HUF) ideal = 100%; Bilateral Symmetry Index (BSI) ideal = 1.00.**
- **Terblig Effectiveness Ratio (TER):** Aim for > 85% in well-designed workstations.
- **Drop delivery** uses gravity to eliminate *Transport Empty* therblig.
- **Eye muscles fatigue 4× faster** than gross body muscles → keep visual work close (Principle 8).
- **Momentum is double-edged** — use it to assist, never to fight against.
- **Numericals to master:** Class-time calculation, HUF, BSI, TER, Standard Time with allowance.
- **Always state units.** KTU penalises missing units in numerical answers.
- **Relationship with Time Study:** Motion economy principles feed into *method study* → which sets the *standard method* → which is then timed in *time study* to derive the *standard time*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Workplace and Tools
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Workplace and Tools

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Workplace — Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Engineering**, a **Workplace** (also called a *workstation* or *work center*) refers to the carefully engineered physical and environmental area assigned to an operator or a machine for the execution of a specific task or a group of related tasks within a production system.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus**, workplace design integrates:
- **Ergonomic principles** (human factors engineering)
- **Motion economy principles**
- **Tool and equipment placement**
- **Anthropometric data** (body measurements of the working population)
- **Environmental factors** (lighting, ventilation, noise, temperature)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workplace Design Objective:** To maximize operator productivity, minimize fatigue, ensure safety, and reduce the overall cycle time of the operation by optimizing the man–machine–environment interface.

### 1.2 Tools — Formal Definition

**Tools** in Industrial Engineering refer to any implement, instrument, or device used by an operator to perform a specific function on a workpiece. Tools are classified into:

| Category | Description | Example |
|----------|-------------|---------|
| **Hand Tools** | Manually operated devices | Screwdriver, hammer, wrench |
| **Power Tools** | Powered by external energy | Drill, grinder, pneumatic wrench |
| **Machine Tools** | Stationary production equipment | Lathe, milling machine, CNC |
| **Automated Tools** | Programmed / robotic | Robotic arms, PLC-controlled actuators |
| **Jigs & Fixtures** | Work-holding/guiding devices | Drill jig, milling fixture |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍳 Real-World Analogy — The Chef's Kitchen:**
> Imagine a professional chef's kitchen. Every knife, pan, spice jar, and cutting board has a *designated location* — frequently used items are placed within easy arm's reach, while rarely used items go on higher shelves. The height of the chopping counter matches the chef's elbow height to avoid back strain. This is exactly what Industrial Engineers do when designing a **workplace**: they analyze the *task*, the *human operator's body dimensions*, and the *tools used* to design a station that minimizes wasted motion, energy, and time.

A well-designed workplace is to an industrial worker what a well-organized kitchen is to a chef — **everything is in its right place for effortless, efficient, and safe execution**.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics

The effectiveness of a workplace design is measured by:

- **Output per unit time** (productivity)
- **Fatigue index** (energy expenditure per cycle)
- **Defect/rejection rate** (quality)
- **Safety incident rate** (occupational health)
- **Worker satisfaction score** (human factors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy:** *"The workplace should adapt to the worker — NOT the worker to the workplace."* This is the central tenet of ergonomics-guided workplace design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principles of Workplace Design

A workplace must be designed considering the following structured layers:

#### Layer 1 — Anthropometric Considerations
- The workstation dimensions must accommodate the **5th to 95th percentile** of the user population (inclusive design).
- Critical dimensions: standing elbow height, eye height, shoulder breadth, forward reach, grip diameter.

#### Layer 2 — Motion Economy (Therbligs)
Based on **Frank Gilbreth's Therbligs**, motions are classified into **17 fundamental elements**. Workplace design aims to **eliminate, combine, simplify, or rearrange** these motions.

The **Principles of Motion Economy** (Gilbreth's 10+ principles) relevant to workplace:

| # | Principle | Application |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Both hands should begin/finish motion simultaneously | Two-handed operation |
| 2 | Both hands should not be idle simultaneously | Avoid one-handed waiting |
| 3 | Motions should be symmetric and simultaneous | Mirror-image assembly |
| 4 | Hand/foot motions should be simultaneously initiated | Co-ordinated control |
| 5 | Momentum should assist the operator | Use of gravity in drop delivery |
| 6 | Smooth curved motions are preferred over straight jerky ones | Natural arc motions |
| 7 | Ballistic (free) motions are faster than controlled | Avoid constraints where possible |
| 8 | Eye focus should be minimized | Use of fixed gaze points |
| 9 | Tools/materials should be pre-positioned | Pre-located jigs/fixtures |
| 10 | Materials/tools should be located within the normal work area | "Pie-chart" zone placement |

#### Layer 3 — Tool Selection Criteria
Tools must satisfy:
- **Functional suitability** for the task
- **Anthropometric compatibility** with the operator's hand
- **Force/torque adequacy**
- **Durability and maintainability**
- **Safety and comfort**

#### Layer 4 — Environmental Integration
- **Lighting**: 300–500 lux for general tasks; 1000+ lux for fine assembly.
- **Noise**: < **85 dB(A)** (8-hour exposure limit per OSHA standards).
- **Temperature**: 20–24 °C for sedentary work.
- **Vibration**: Minimized per ISO 2631.

### 2.2 Classification of Tools by Application

```
Tools
├── Perishable Tools (consumed in use)
│   ├── Cutting inserts, grinding wheels
├── Non-perishable Hand Tools
│   ├── Striking (hammers)
│   ├── Turning (wrenches)
│   ├── Cutting (pliers, cutters)
│   └── Holding (vices, clamps)
├── Power Tools
│   ├── Electric, pneumatic, hydraulic
└── Automatic / Programmed Tools
    ├── Robots, CNC, PLC-controlled
```

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Examinations**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Normal Working Area (semicircle reach) | $A_n = \dfrac{\pi \cdot R_n^2}{2}$ | $R_n$ = normal reach (m); $A_n$ in m² | Maximum reach for seated work |
| 2 | Maximum Working Area (full circle) | $A_m = \pi \cdot R_m^2$ | $R_m$ = maximum reach (m) | Total reach envelope |
| 3 | Preferred Work Zone (arcs swept by forearm) | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R_2^2 - R_1^2)$ | $R_1, R_2$ = inner/outer arc radii | Frequent-use zone |
| 4 | Force Exertion Limit (elbow height) | $F_{max} \approx 220 \text{ N}$ (male avg.) | Force in Newtons | Static push/pull |
| 5 | Vertical Work Surface Height | $H_{opt} = H_{eye} - (d_{task} + \text{clearance})$ | Heights in m | Standing inspection |
| 6 | Horizontal Work Surface Height (standing) | $H_{work} = H_{elbow} - (5 \text{ to } 10) \text{ cm}$ | Heights in cm | Bench work |
| 7 | Horizontal Work Surface Height (seated) | $H_{work} = H_{elbow} + (0 \text{ to } 5) \text{ cm}$ | Heights in cm | Seated precision work |
| 8 | Recommended Illumination Level | $E = \dfrac{\Phi}{A}$ | $\Phi$ = luminous flux (lm); $A$ = area (m²); $E$ in lux | Lighting design |
| 9 | Cycle Time Reduction | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100\%$ | $T$ = cycle time (s); $\eta$ = % | Workplace improvement |
| 10 | Operator Efficiency | $E_{op} = \dfrac{\text{Standard Time}}{\text{Actual Time}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio × 100 | Performance rating |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Workplace and Tool design is foundational to:
- **Lean Manufacturing** (elimination of *muda* — waste)
- **Six Sigma** (reduction of variation)
- **Assembly Line Balancing**
- **Occupational Health & Safety (OHS)** compliance
- **Industry 4.0** (smart human–machine collaboration)

The proper design of workplace and tools can yield **15–30% improvement in productivity** and significant reduction in musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Work Surface Height (Standing Position)

**Problem Context:** Design the optimal height of a standing workstation for an operator performing light assembly tasks.

**Given:**
- Eye height of operator (5th–95th percentile): $H_{eye} = 150 \text{ cm}$ to $175 \text{ cm}$
- Elbow height: $H_{elbow} = 100 \text{ cm}$ to $115 \text{ cm}$
- Task viewing distance required: $d_{task} = 30 \text{ cm}$
- Clearance for hands/material: $c = 10 \text{ cm}$

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

For standing precision work, the work surface should be **5 cm below elbow height** for light assembly.

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{work,min} &= H_{elbow,5th} - 5 \text{ cm} \\
H_{work,min} &= 100 - 5 \\
H_{work,min} &= 95 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{work,max} &= H_{elbow,95th} - 5 \text{ cm} \\
H_{work,max} &= 115 - 5 \\
H_{work,max} &= 110 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, an adjustable workstation range of **95 cm to 110 cm** accommodates the 5th–95th percentile population.

**Alternative — Compromise Height (mean):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{compromise} &= \dfrac{H_{work,min} + H_{work,max}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{95 + 110}{2} \\
&= 102.5 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Working Area Calculations (Normal vs Maximum Zones)

**Problem Context:** An operator performs seated work with arm reach of 50 cm (normal) and 65 cm (maximum). Calculate the working areas.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Normal Working Area** — semicircular region in front of operator:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_n &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot R_n^2}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \cdot (0.50)^2}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.25}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{0.7854}{2} \\
&= 0.3927 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Maximum Working Area** — full circular region around operator:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_m &= \pi \cdot R_m^2 \\
&= \pi \cdot (0.65)^2 \\
&= 3.1416 \times 0.4225 \\
&= 1.3270 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Preferred (frequent-use) Zone** — annular region between inner arc (R₁ = 25 cm) and outer arc (R₂ = 40 cm):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_p &= \dfrac{\pi}{2}(R_2^2 - R_1^2) \\
&= \dfrac{\pi}{2}\left((0.40)^2 - (0.25)^2\right) \\
&= \dfrac{3.1416}{2}\left(0.16 - 0.0625\right) \\
&= \dfrac{3.1416}{2}(0.0975) \\
&= 1.5708 \times 0.0975 \\
&= 0.1532 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation for KTU Exam:**
- **Frequent-use tools** must be placed within $A_p$.
- **Occasional-use tools** within $A_n$.
- **Emergency/rare-use** within $A_m$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cycle Time Improvement Calculation

**Problem Context:** A workplace redesign reduced the operator's cycle time from 120 seconds to 90 seconds. Calculate the productivity improvement percentage.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{120 - 90}{120} \times 100\% \\
&= \dfrac{30}{120} \times 100\% \\
&= 0.25 \times 100\% \\
&= 25\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Productivity increase:** 25% — this validates the ergonomic investment.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Recommended Illumination for a Workstation

**Problem Context:** A workshop of area $50 \text{ m}^2$ uses 20 fluorescent lamps, each delivering 2000 lumens. Compute the average illumination level.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi_{total} &= n \times \Phi_{lamp} \\
&= 20 \times 2000 \\
&= 40{,}000 \text{ lumens}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E &= \dfrac{\Phi_{total}}{A} \\
&= \dfrac{40{,}000}{50} \\
&= 800 \text{ lux}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** This illumination level is suitable for **fine assembly** (recommended: 500–1000 lux per IS 3646).

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Workplace Design

| Design Parameter | Light Work | Precision Work | Heavy Work |
|------------------|------------|---------------|------------|
| Work surface height | Elbow − 5 cm | Elbow + 0 to 5 cm | Elbow − 15 to 20 cm |
| Reach envelope | Normal (50 cm) | Reduced (30 cm) | Maximum (65 cm) |
| Force exertion | < 100 N | < 50 N | 100–220 N |
| Illumination | 300 lux | 1000 lux | 200 lux |
| Posture | Standing/Sitting | Seated | Standing |
| Tool weight | < 1 kg | < 0.5 kg | > 2.5 kg |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Workplace Design Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[TASK ANALYSIS] --> A1[Identify operations and sequence]
    A1 --> A2[Determine motion elements and Therbligs]
    A2 --> B0[ANTHROPOMETRIC DATA INPUT]
    B0 --> B1[5th percentile dimensions]
    B0 --> B2[95th percentile dimensions]
    B0 --> B3[Mean compromise values]
    B1 --> C0[WORKPLACE LAYOUT SYNTHESIS]
    B2 --> C0
    B3 --> C0
    C0 --> C1[Normal working area placement]
    C0 --> C2[Maximum working area placement]
    C0 --> C3[Preferred frequent-use zone]
    C1 --> D0[TOOL POSITIONING]
    C2 --> D0
    C3 --> D0
    D0 --> D1[Frequent tools in preferred zone]
    D0 --> D2[Rare tools in max zone]
    D0 --> D3[Tool pre-positioning by jig fixture]
    D1 --> E0[ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS]
    D2 --> E0
    D3 --> E0
    E0 --> E1[Illumination level design]
    E0 --> E2[Noise control under 85 dB]
    E0 --> E3[Temperature 20 to 24 C]
    E0 --> E4[Vibration isolation]
    E1 --> F0[VALIDATION AND ITERATION]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    E4 --> F0
    F0 --> F1[Cycle time measurement]
    F0 --> F2[Fatigue index check]
    F0 --> F3[Safety compliance audit]
    F1 --> G0[OPTIMIZED WORKPLACE OUTPUT]
    F2 --> G0
    F3 --> G0
```

### 4.2 Tool Classification Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TOOLS[INDUSTRIAL TOOLS] --> HT[Hand Tools]
    TOOLS --> PT[Power Tools]
    TOOLS --> MT[Machine Tools]
    TOOLS --> AT[Automated Tools]
    HT --> HT1[Striking Tools]
    HT --> HT2[Cutting Tools]
    HT --> HT3[Holding Tools]
    HT --> HT4[Measuring Tools]
    PT --> PT1[Electric Powered]
    PT --> PT2[Pneumatic Powered]
    PT --> PT3[Hydraulic Powered]
    MT --> MT1[Conventional Lathe]
    MT --> MT2[Milling Machine]
    MT --> MT3[CNC Machines]
    AT --> AT1[Industrial Robots]
    AT --> AT2[PLC Based Actuators]
    AT --> AT3[AGV Systems]
```

### 4.3 Working Zone (Pie-Chart) Representation — Conceptual Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ZONE[OPERATOR WORKING ZONES] --> Z1[Zone A: Preferred]
    ZONE --> Z2[Zone B: Normal]
    ZONE --> Z3[Zone C: Maximum]
    Z1 --> Z1A[Frequently used tools]
    Z1 --> Z1B[Primary materials]
    Z1 --> Z1C[Within forearm sweep]
    Z2 --> Z2A[Occasionally used items]
    Z2 --> Z2B[Secondary fixtures]
    Z3 --> Z3A[Rarely used items]
    Z3 --> Z3B[Emergency controls]
```

### 4.4 Motion Economy Flow — Gilbreth's Principle Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MOTION[WORKER MOTION DURING TASK] --> M1[Symmetric Both Hands]
    MOTION --> M2[Simultaneous Start and End]
    MOTION --> M3[Minimum Idle Time]
    MOTION --> M4[Curved Smooth Paths]
    MOTION --> M5[Ballistic Free Motions]
    MOTION --> M6[Reduced Eye Movements]
    M1 --> OUT[ENHANCED PRODUCTIVITY]
    M2 --> OUT
    M3 --> OUT
    M4 --> OUT
    M5 --> OUT
    M6 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Exams:** In questions involving diagrammatic representation of workplace zones, students must clearly label the **three concentric zones** (Preferred, Normal, Maximum) and indicate tool placement within each. Diagrams fetch **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Workplace Design. List any four principles of motion economy.**

**Model Answer:**

**Workplace Design** is the systematic process of arranging the work area, equipment, tools, and environmental conditions to optimize human performance, safety, and comfort during task execution.

**Four Principles of Motion Economy (Gilbreth):**

1. **Both hands should begin and finish their motions simultaneously** to avoid one-handed idle time.
2. **Motions should be symmetric and simultaneous** — both hands performing identical motions in opposite directions.
3. **Hand and foot motions** should be initiated simultaneously wherever possible.
4. **Tools and materials** should be pre-positioned to eliminate search and selection motions.

*(Additional valid principles: curved motions preferred over straight jerky ones; ballistic motions faster than controlled; eye focus minimized.)*

---

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Jigs and Fixtures.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Jig | Fixture |
|---------|-----|---------|
| **Primary Function** | Guides the cutting tool | Holds and locates the workpiece |
| **Contact** | With tool | With workpiece |
| **Use Case** | Drilling, reaming, tapping | Milling, turning, grinding |
| **Accuracy** | Higher (controls tool position) | Lower (only workpiece stability) |
| **Cost** | Generally higher | Generally lower |
| **Examples** | Drill jig, boring jig | Milling fixture, welding fixture |

Jigs are used when **tool guidance precision** is critical; fixtures when only **workpiece stability** is required.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer **ONE full question** of 14 marks from a Module. Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** is provided below.

---

#### 🔷 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Principles of Motion Economy** as proposed by Frank Gilbreth. Discuss their application in the design of a workplace for an **electronic PCB assembly station**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, describe the **normal working area** and **maximum working area** for a seated operator. An operator works at a seated workstation with a normal reach of 55 cm and maximum reach of 70 cm. Compute:
- (i) Normal working area
- (ii) Maximum working area
- (iii) Percentage reduction in area from maximum to normal zone. **(7 Marks)**

##### ✅ Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Principles of Motion Economy (7 Marks):**

Frank Gilbreth classified all work motions into **17 fundamental units called Therbligs** and established principles to minimize unnecessary motion:

**Core Principles:**

1. **Two-handed principle**: Both hands should operate simultaneously and symmetrically to maximize output. *Example:* Holding a PCB with the left hand while soldering with the right hand.

2. **Elimination of idle time**: Both hands must remain productive — no hand should wait for the other.

3. **Smooth curved motions**: Curved natural-arm motions consume less energy than zig-zag straight-line motions.

4. **Ballistic motions**: Free-swinging motions are faster and consume less energy than tightly controlled motions.

5. **Pre-positioning of tools and materials**: All frequently used components, soldering irons, and flux must be placed within the **preferred (Zone A)** of the operator.

6. **Use of jigs and fixtures**: For PCB assembly, use component holders that pre-position resistors, ICs, and capacitors so the operator only retrieves — not locates — components.

7. **Gravity drop delivery**: Components can slide down inclined chutes directly into the operator's preferred zone, eliminating transport motions.

8. **Reduced eye movement**: Use of **fixed reference markings** and illumination focused on the assembly area reduces eye fatigue.

**Application in PCB Assembly Station:**

- Use **anti-static mats** and pre-positioned **component trays** at 15°–30° from straight ahead.
- Install **bin separators** so the most-frequently-used components (resistors, capacitors) are within 30 cm.
- Use **soldering fume extractors** integrated into the workstation for environmental safety.
- Maintain **work surface at seated elbow height** to avoid back strain.

---

**(b) Working Area Calculations (7 Marks):**

**Diagram (description):**
- *Normal working area*: A semicircle in front of the operator on the horizontal plane, swept by the **forearm from elbow pivot**, typically **0 to 90°** in the sagittal plane.
- *Maximum working area*: A complete circle around the operator, swept by the **fully extended arm**, including sideways reach.

**Given:**
- Normal reach $R_n = 55 \text{ cm} = 0.55 \text{ m}$
- Maximum reach $R_m = 70 \text{ cm} = 0.70 \text{ m}$

**(i) Normal Working Area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_n &= \dfrac{\pi \cdot R_n^2}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{\pi \times (0.55)^2}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.3025}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{0.9503}{2} \\
&= 0.4752 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Maximum Working Area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_m &= \pi \cdot R_m^2 \\
&= 3.1416 \times (0.70)^2 \\
&= 3.1416 \times 0.49 \\
&= 1.5394 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Percentage Reduction:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Reduction} &= \dfrac{A_m - A_n}{A_m} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1.5394 - 0.4752}{1.5394} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{1.0642}{1.5394} \times 100 \\
&= 69.13\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The normal working area is only **30.87%** of the maximum area, hence critical tools should be placed in this region to minimize operator motion.

---

#### 🔷 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **factors affecting workplace design**. Explain the role of **anthropometry** in designing a workstation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A standing workstation is to be designed for operators whose **elbow heights range from 100 cm (5th percentile) to 115 cm (95th percentile)**. Calculate:
- (i) The minimum and maximum recommended work surface heights.
- (ii) The compromise height for a single-height workstation.
- (iii) The recommended range if the workstation is **adjustable**. **(7 Marks)**

##### ✅ Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Factors Affecting Workplace Design (7 Marks):**

**1. Anthropometric Factors:**
- Operator body dimensions (5th–95th percentile).
- Standing/sitting height, reach distances, grip diameter.
- Determines table height, seat dimensions, reach envelopes.

**2. Task-Related Factors:**
- Nature of operation (precision vs heavy work).
- Cycle time and frequency.
- Posture required (standing, seated, mixed).

**3. Tool-Related Factors:**
- Weight, shape, handle diameter.
- Vibration and noise emission.
- Pre-positioning requirements.

**4. Environmental Factors:**
- **Lighting** (lux level per IS 3646).
- **Temperature** (20–24 °C optimum).
- **Noise** (< 85 dB(A) OSHA limit).
- **Humidity and ventilation**.

**5. Safety Factors:**
- Emergency stop accessibility.
- Guarding and barriers.
- Material handling safety.

**6. Ergonomic Factors:**
- Posture, fatigue, Repetitive Strain Injury (RSI) prevention.
- Display and control layout.

**7. Economic Factors:**
- Cost of tools, jigs, fixtures.
- ROI on ergonomic improvements.

**Role of Anthropometry:**
- Anthropometry provides the **body measurement database** essential for inclusive workplace design.
- For an inclusive design, the workstation must accommodate **90% of the population** (5th–95th percentile).
- Critical dimensions used: stature, eye height, elbow height, shoulder breadth, sitting height, popliteal height, buttock-knee length, hand grip diameter.
- *Example:* Chair seat height = popliteal height + shoe heel height + clearance (2.5 cm).

---

**(b) Standing Workstation Height Calculations (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $H_{elbow,5th} = 100 \text{ cm}$
- $H_{elbow,95th} = 115 \text{ cm}$
- Light-to-medium assembly work (recommended offset: 5 cm below elbow).

**(i) Minimum and Maximum Recommended Heights:**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{min} &= H_{elbow,5th} - 5 \\
&= 100 - 5 \\
&= 95 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{max} &= H_{elbow,95th} - 5 \\
&= 115 - 5 \\
&= 110 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Compromise Height (Single Height Workstation):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{compromise} &= \dfrac{H_{min} + H_{max}}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{95 + 110}{2} \\
&= 102.5 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Recommended Range for Adjustable Workstation:**

$$\boxed{H_{adjustable} = 95 \text{ cm to } 110 \text{ cm}}$$

**Engineering Recommendation:** An **adjustable-height workstation** is preferred over a fixed compromise height, because it accommodates the full 5th–95th percentile range, reduces fatigue, and prevents musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) of the back, neck, and shoulders.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common mistakes students make in this topic (and where marks are lost):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in working-area calculations — $A_n$ must always be expressed in **m²** or **cm²**, with clear conversion shown. *(-1 mark)*
> 2. **Confusing normal vs maximum area** — Normal area is **semicircular**; Maximum area is **full circular**. Writing $A_n = \pi R_n^2$ instead of $\frac{\pi R_n^2}{2}$ loses **2 marks** in Part B.
> 3. **Not stating percentile basis** — When mentioning anthropometric data, always specify whether **5th, 50th, or 95th percentile** is being used.
> 4. **Mixing Jigs and Fixtures** — A *jig guides the tool*; a *fixture holds the workpiece*. Reversing this definition is a frequent error.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams** — KTU examiners allocate **2–3 marks** for neat sketches of working zones (pie chart) and workplace layouts. *Always draw the diagram even if not explicitly asked.*
> 6. **Forcing formulas** — Workplace design involves **principles + recommendations**, not just formulas. Students must explain the **concept first, then compute**.
> 7. **Ignoring environmental factors** — A workplace design answer that does not mention **lighting, noise, temperature** is considered incomplete. *(-2 marks)*

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Workplace and Tools**

- ☐ **Workplace design** integrates ergonomics, motion economy, anthropometry, and environmental factors.
- ☐ **Three working zones**: Preferred (Zone A), Normal (Zone B), Maximum (Zone C).
- ☐ **Normal working area** = $\dfrac{\pi R_n^2}{2}$ (semicircle).
- ☐ **Maximum working area** = $\pi R_m^2$ (full circle).
- ☐ **Preferred zone (frequent use)** = $\dfrac{\pi}{2}(R_2^2 - R_1^2)$.
- ☐ Standing work surface height = **Elbow height − 5 cm** (light work) or **− 15 to 20 cm** (heavy work).
- ☐ Seated work surface height = **Elbow height + 0 to 5 cm** (precision work).
- ☐ Inclusive design range = **5th to 95th percentile** of operator population.
- ☐ **Gilbreth's Therbligs** = 17 fundamental motion elements.
- ☐ **Jig** guides the tool; **Fixture** holds the workpiece.
- ☐ Recommended noise limit = **< 85 dB(A)** (8-hour OSHA exposure).
- ☐ Recommended illumination = **300 lux (general), 1000 lux (fine precision)**.
- ☐ Recommended temperature = **20–24 °C** for sedentary work.
- ☐ **Productivity gain** through ergonomic redesign = typically **15–30%**.
- ☐ **Cycle time reduction** $\eta = \dfrac{T_{old} - T_{new}}{T_{old}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ **Operator efficiency** $E_{op} = \dfrac{\text{Standard Time}}{\text{Actual Time}} \times 100\%$.
- ☐ Always remember to **draw diagrams** (pie-chart zones, workplace layout) — fetch **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions.
- ☐ Always include **units** in numerical answers and specify **percentile basis** for anthropometric data.
- ☐ Workplace design principle: ***"Adapt the workplace to the worker, NOT the worker to the workplace."***

---

**End of Module 3 — Workplace and Tools | KTU 2024 Scheme | PCPET501 — Industrial Engineering**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Engineering (IE)**, the term **"Objectives"** refers to the **predetermined, measurable goals** that the discipline seeks to achieve within an integrated production–management system. These objectives form the strategic blueprint of any industrial enterprise and are aligned with maximizing **productivity**, optimizing **resource utilization**, ensuring **quality assurance**, and maintaining **human welfare**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Standard):**
> *Industrial Engineering objectives are the quantitative and qualitative targets established to convert given inputs (Men, Machines, Materials, Methods, Money — the 5M's) into maximum output with optimal efficiency, minimum waste, and maximum human satisfaction.*

### 1.1.1 Classification of IE Objectives (Hierarchical View)

| Tier | Objective Category | Focus Area |
|------|-------------------|------------|
| **Tier 1 (Primary)** | Productivity & Profitability | Economic Growth |
| **Tier 2 (Secondary)** | Quality & Cost Reduction | Process Optimization |
| **Tier 3 (Tertiary)** | Worker Welfare & Safety | Human Resource |
| **Tier 4 (Long-term)** | Innovation & Sustainability | Strategic Development |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Kitchen Chef"**
>
> Imagine you are a **head chef** in a restaurant kitchen. Your **inputs** are raw vegetables, gas, knives, and your cooks. Your **outputs** are the finished dishes served to customers. Now, your **IE objectives** are like the chef's daily targets:
>
> - **Cook the maximum number of dishes** (Productivity ↑)
> - **Waste the least vegetables** (Cost ↓)
> - **Make every dish taste the same** (Quality ↑)
> - **Ensure cooks don't burn their hands or get exhausted** (Welfare ↑)
> - **Try a new recipe next month** (Innovation ↑)
>
> Just as the chef cannot focus on speed alone without taste, an Industrial Engineer cannot pursue profit alone without worker satisfaction. The **"Objectives"** of IE are therefore a **balanced scorecard** of organizational success.

---

## 1.3 The 5M's of Industrial Engineering (Key Constants)

The objectives of IE are achieved by balancing the **5M's** — these are *standard industrial metrics* used universally:

- **Men** — Human resources / workforce
- **Machines** — Equipment & technology
- **Materials** — Raw inputs
- **Methods** — Procedures & processes
- **Money** — Capital & finance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> Every IE objective must be traced back to its impact on **at least one of the 5M's**. If a goal does not affect any M, it is not an IE objective.

---

## 1.4 Primary vs. Secondary Objectives — A Quick View

**Primary Objectives (Economic):**
1. Increase Productivity
2. Reduce Production Cost
3. Improve Quality
4. Ensure Better Customer Satisfaction

**Secondary Objectives (Human):**
1. Improve Worker Safety
2. Enhance Job Satisfaction
3. Reduce Monotony & Fatigue
4. Develop Human Skills
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Major Objective

### 🎯 Objective 1: Productivity Enhancement

**Why it matters:** Productivity is the *king metric* of any industrial economy. It measures how efficiently inputs are converted to outputs.

**How it is achieved:**
- Method Study (process simplification)
- Time Study (standardization)
- Work Measurement (performance evaluation)
- Ergonomics (human factor optimization)

**Productivity Formula:**

$$P = \frac{O}{I}$$

Where $P$ = Productivity, $O$ = Output produced, $I$ = Input consumed.

> Three flavors of Productivity exist in IE:
> - **Labor Productivity:** $P_L = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Labor Hours}}$
> - **Machine Productivity:** $P_M = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{Machine Hours}}$
> - **Total Factor Productivity (TFP):** $P_{TFP} = \frac{\text{Output}}{\text{All Inputs Combined}}$

---

### 🎯 Objective 2: Cost Reduction

**Why it matters:** Lower cost per unit means higher competitiveness in the market.

**How it is achieved:**
- Eliminating non-value-added activities
- Reducing waste (Material, Time, Motion)
- Improving layout and workflow
- Standardizing operations

**Cost Reduction Formula:**

$$C_u = \frac{C_T}{N}$$

Where $C_u$ = Cost per unit, $C_T$ = Total production cost, $N$ = Number of units produced.

> [!NOTE]
> A reduction in $C_T$ **or** an increase in $N$ (without compromising quality) leads to cost reduction — this is a *dual-action* objective.

---

### 🎯 Objective 3: Quality Improvement

**Why it matters:** Quality is non-negotiable; poor quality erodes brand value and increases rework costs.

**How it is achieved:**
- Statistical Quality Control (SQC)
- Total Quality Management (TQM)
- Six Sigma methodology
- ISO certification compliance

**Quality Metric — First Pass Yield (FPY):**

$$FPY = \frac{N_{good}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

Where $N_{good}$ = Defect-free units, $N_{total}$ = Total units produced.

---

### 🎯 Objective 4: Worker Welfare & Safety

**Why it matters:** A safe, satisfied worker is a productive worker. Industrial Engineering is the only engineering discipline that places *human welfare* as a co-equal goal to profit.

**How it is achieved:**
- Ergonomic workplace design
- Safety protocols and PPE
- Work-rest cycles
- Job enrichment and rotation

**Safety Metric — Injury Frequency Rate (IFR):**

$$IFR = \frac{N_{injuries} \times 10^6}{\text{Total Person-Hours Worked}}$$

---

### 🎯 Objective 5: Innovation & System Improvement

**Why it matters:** Static systems die. Continuous improvement (Kaizen) is the lifeblood of modern industry.

**How it is achieved:**
- Research & Development
- Adoption of Industry 4.0 (IoT, AI, Automation)
- Lean Manufacturing
- Value Engineering

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize these formulas. They appear in nearly every KTU IE question paper.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|--------------|------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Productivity | $P = O / I$ | $O$ = output units, $I$ = input units | Overall efficiency |
| 2 | Labor Productivity | $P_L = O / (L \times H)$ | $L$ = workers, $H$ = hours | Workforce output |
| 3 | Cost per Unit | $C_u = C_T / N$ | $C_T$ in ₹, $N$ in units | Cost optimization |
| 4 | First Pass Yield | $FPY = N_{good} / N_{total}$ | Dimensionless (%) | Quality check |
| 5 | Defect Rate | $D = N_{def} / N_{total} \times 100$ | Dimensionless (%) | Quality control |
| 6 | Utilization | $U = (T_{active} / T_{available}) \times 100$ | $T$ in hours | Machine efficiency |
| 7 | Effectiveness (OEE) | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | A, P, Q in % | Overall equipment effectiveness |
| 8 | Injury Frequency | $IFR = N_{inj} \times 10^6 / PH$ | $PH$ = person-hours | Safety metric |
| 9 | Efficiency | $\eta = (S_{actual} / S_{standard}) \times 100$ | $S$ in time units | Performance rating |
| 10 | Value Added Ratio | $VAR = (VA / T_{total}) \times 100$ | $VA$ = value-added time | Lean metric |

> **⚠ Markdown Safety Note:** The `|x|` syntax is replaced with `\vert x \vert` throughout to prevent table-rendering breakage.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application of IE Objectives |
|----------|------------------------------|
| **Automotive (e.g., Maruti Suzuki)** | Reducing assembly line cycle time |
| **Software (e.g., TCS)** | Improving developer productivity (lines per day) |
| **Healthcare (e.g., Apollo Hospitals)** | Reducing patient wait time (queue theory) |
| **Aerospace (e.g., ISRO)** | Quality control of launch vehicles |
| **Manufacturing (e.g., HAL)** | Worker safety in high-risk zones |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Industrial Engineering is the **only engineering discipline** that explicitly treats the human being as a variable in its equations. This is why IE objectives always include a *human* component.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Productivity, Cost, and Profit

Let us derive the **fundamental relationship** that ties together three of the most critical IE objectives: **Productivity, Cost, and Profitability**.

### Given:
- Let $O$ = Total output (units produced per year)
- Let $C_T$ = Total cost of production (₹)
- Let $S$ = Selling price per unit (₹)
- Let $N$ = Total number of units sold
- Let $P_r$ = Total profit (₹)

### Step 1: Express Total Revenue
The total revenue generated from selling the output is the selling price multiplied by the number of units sold.

$$R = S \times N$$

### Step 2: Express Total Profit
Profit is mathematically defined as total revenue minus total cost. This is a fundamental accounting identity.

$$P_r = R - C_T$$

### Step 3: Substitute Revenue
Substituting the expression for $R$ from Step 1 into the profit equation.

$$P_r = (S \times N) - C_T$$

### Step 4: Express Productivity
Productivity is defined as the ratio of output to input. In this context, we use total cost as the total input.

$$P = \frac{O}{C_T}$$

### Step 5: Isolate Cost in terms of Productivity
Rearranging the productivity equation to solve for $C_T$.

$$C_T = \frac{O}{P}$$

### Step 6: Substitute Cost Back Into Profit Equation
Plugging the expression for $C_T$ from Step 5 into the profit equation from Step 3.

$$P_r = (S \times N) - \frac{O}{P}$$

### Step 7: Final Insight
This final equation reveals the **strategic interplay** of IE objectives. If we hold $S$, $N$, and $O$ constant, then:

$$\frac{dP_r}{dP} = \frac{O}{P^2} > 0$$

This means: **A unit increase in productivity directly and positively increases profit**, while a unit increase in cost per unit (lowering productivity) decreases profit quadratically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** The IE objective of *"increasing productivity"* is mathematically equivalent to the IE objective of *"reducing cost"*, which in turn is mathematically equivalent to *"increasing profit"*. **All three objectives converge into a single optimization problem.**

---

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (For KTU Board-Style Questions)

> **Question:** A manufacturing unit produced **5,000 units** in a month, with a total input cost of **₹2,50,000**. Calculate:
> 1. Productivity
> 2. Cost per unit
> 3. If the selling price is ₹80/unit and all units are sold, find the profit.
> 4. If productivity is increased by 20% (cost same), what is the new profit?

### Solution:

**Part (1): Productivity**

$$P = \frac{O}{C_T} = \frac{5000}{250000} = 0.02 \text{ units/₹}$$

**Part (2): Cost per unit**

$$C_u = \frac{C_T}{N} = \frac{250000}{5000} = ₹50/\text{unit}$$

**Part (3): Profit at original parameters**

$$P_r = (S \times N) - C_T = (80 \times 5000) - 250000 = 400000 - 250000 = ₹1,50,000$$

**Part (4): Profit after 20% productivity increase**

New output: $O_{new} = 5000 \times 1.20 = 6000$ units
New cost: $C_T$ remains = ₹2,50,000 (assumption)

$$P_{r,new} = (80 \times 6000) - 250000 = 480000 - 250000 = ₹2,30,000$$

**Profit Increase:**

$$\Delta P_r = 230000 - 150000 = ₹80,000$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Insight:** KTU examiners love questions where you must show a *before-and-after* numerical comparison. Always conclude with the delta (Δ) value to demonstrate analytical clarity.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping IE Objectives to the 5M's

| IE Objective ↓ / 5M's → | Men | Machines | Materials | Methods | Money |
|--------------------------|-----|----------|-----------|---------|-------|
| **Productivity ↑**        | ✓ Training | ✓ Maintenance | ✓ Waste ↓ | ✓ Standardization | ✓ ROI ↑ |
| **Cost ↓**                | ✓ Wages    | ✓ Downtime ↓ | ✓ Scrap ↓ | ✓ Layout ↑        | ✓ Capital ↑ |
| **Quality ↑**             | ✓ Skill    | ✓ Calibration | ✓ Inspection | ✓ SOPs         | ✓ Warranty ↓ |
| **Safety ↑**              | ✓ PPE      | ✓ Guards     | ✓ MSDS     | ✓ Procedures    | ✓ Insurance ↓ |
| **Innovation ↑**          | ✓ R&D      | ✓ Automation | ✓ New materials | ✓ Lean    | ✓ Investment |

> A complete understanding of this matrix is **mandatory** for KTU Module 4 viva and Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram of IE Objectives

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING OBJECTIVES]
    node2[PRIMARY OBJECTIVES]
    node3[Productivity Enhancement]
    node4[Cost Reduction]
    node5[Quality Improvement]
    node6[SECONDARY OBJECTIVES]
    node7[Worker Welfare]
    node8[Workplace Safety]
    node9[Job Satisfaction]
    node10[TERTIARY OBJECTIVES]
    node11[Innovation and R and D]
    node12[Sustainability]
    node13[Continuous Improvement Kaizen]
    node14[OUTCOMES]
    node15[Organizational Profitability]
    node16[Competitive Advantage]
    node17[Long Term Growth]

    node1 --> node2
    node1 --> node6
    node1 --> node10
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5
    node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node6 --> node9
    node10 --> node11
    node10 --> node12
    node10 --> node13
    node3 --> node14
    node4 --> node14
    node5 --> node14
    node7 --> node14
    node8 --> node14
    node9 --> node14
    node11 --> node15
    node12 --> node16
    node13 --> node17
    node15 --> node16
    node16 --> node17
```

---

## 4.2 Input–Process–Output (IPO) Model of IE Objectives

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[INPUTS 5M: Men Machines Materials Methods Money]
    nodeB[IE PROCESS: Method Study, Time Study, Work Measurement, Quality Control, Ergonomics]
    nodeC[OUTPUTS: Improved Productivity, Reduced Cost, Better Quality, Safe Workplace]
    nodeD[FEEDBACK LOOP: Continuous Improvement]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeB
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The **feedback loop** is the essence of IE — objectives are not static; they are continuously refined based on measured outcomes.

---

## 4.3 Flow Chart: How IE Objectives Drive Decisions

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeQ[Identify Industrial Problem]
    nodeR[Define IE Objective]
    nodeS[Select IE Technique]
    nodeT[Apply Technique]
    nodeU[Measure Outcome]
    nodeV[Compare with Objective]
    nodeW[Objective Met]
    nodeX[Reiterate with Improvement]

    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS
    nodeS --> nodeT
    nodeT --> nodeU
    nodeU --> nodeV
    nodeV --> nodeW
    nodeV --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question asks *"How are IE objectives achieved in practice?"* — your answer should mirror this flowchart. The examiner is testing your understanding of the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** in disguise.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Industrial Engineering and list any four of its primary objectives.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Model Answer:**

Industrial Engineering is a branch of engineering that deals with the design, improvement, and installation of integrated systems of men, machines, materials, methods, and money to achieve organizational goals efficiently.

**Four Primary Objectives of IE:**
1. **Productivity Enhancement** — Maximizing output per unit of input.
2. **Cost Reduction** — Minimizing per-unit production cost.
3. **Quality Improvement** — Ensuring defect-free products and services.
4. **Customer Satisfaction** — Meeting or exceeding customer expectations.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the four objectives)*

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between primary and secondary objectives of Industrial Engineering.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Primary Objectives | Secondary Objectives |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Focus** | Economic / Organizational goals | Human / Worker-centric goals |
| **Examples** | Productivity, Cost, Quality | Safety, Welfare, Job Satisfaction |
| **Measured by** | Monetary or output metrics | Surveys, injury rates, absenteeism |
| **Time horizon** | Short to medium term | Long term, strategic |

*(3 marks — full table fetch with 2 valid points earns 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> KTU Regulation: Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **one full question** (either Choice A or Choice B).

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **major objectives of Industrial Engineering** with reference to the **5M's framework**. Discuss how each objective contributes to organizational profitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A firm produces 8,000 units/month with a total cost of ₹4,00,000. The selling price is ₹70/unit. Calculate the **productivity, cost per unit, total profit, and profit per unit**. If productivity improves by 25% (with constant cost), what is the **new profit per unit**? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

The objectives of Industrial Engineering are organized under the 5M's framework — Men, Machines, Materials, Methods, and Money.

**1. Productivity Enhancement:**
The primary goal of IE is to maximize output for a given input. This is achieved by:
- Streamlining methods (Method Study)
- Standardizing work (Time Study)
- Optimizing machine utilization
- Training and upskilling men (workforce)

**2. Cost Reduction:**
By eliminating waste (material, time, motion), IE reduces the cost per unit. Lower cost directly translates to higher profit margin, contributing to organizational profitability.

**3. Quality Improvement:**
Quality objectives ensure that products meet specifications. Higher quality reduces rework, warranty claims, and reputational damage — all of which protect profit.

**4. Worker Safety and Welfare:**
A safe worker is a productive worker. Reducing accidents lowers insurance costs and absenteeism, indirectly boosting profitability.

**5. Innovation and Continuous Improvement:**
IE promotes Kaizen (continuous improvement) and Value Engineering, which keep the organization competitive in the long run.

> **Concluding Statement:** All IE objectives, when properly executed, converge to a single outcome — **organizational profitability and sustainability**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for 5 objectives + 2 marks for conclusion/convergence statement)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- Output $O = 8000$ units/month
- Total cost $C_T = ₹4,00,000$
- Selling price $S = ₹70$/unit
- All units sold: $N = 8000$

**Step 1: Productivity**

$$P = \frac{O}{C_T} = \frac{8000}{400000} = 0.02 \text{ units/₹}$$

**Step 2: Cost per unit**

$$C_u = \frac{C_T}{N} = \frac{400000}{8000} = ₹50/\text{unit}$$

**Step 3: Total Profit**

$$P_r = (S \times N) - C_T = (70 \times 8000) - 400000 = 560000 - 400000 = ₹1,60,000$$

**Step 4: Profit per unit**

$$\text{Profit/unit} = S - C_u = 70 - 50 = ₹20/\text{unit}$$

**Step 5: After 25% productivity increase**
New output: $O_{new} = 8000 \times 1.25 = 10,000$ units/month
Cost remains: $C_T = ₹4,00,000$ (assumption)

**Step 6: New Cost per unit**

$$C_{u,new} = \frac{400000}{10000} = ₹40/\text{unit}$$

**Step 7: New Profit per unit**

$$\text{Profit/unit}_{new} = 70 - 40 = ₹30/\text{unit}$$

**Step 8: Change in profit per unit**

$$\Delta = 30 - 20 = ₹10/\text{unit (an increase of 50%)}$$

*(7 marks: 1 mark for each correct calculation step; final delta gives bonus conceptual credit)*

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of IE objectives in achieving sustainable manufacturing**. How do these objectives align with the **United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)**? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data pertains to a manufacturing plant:
- Output = 6,000 units
- Labor hours = 1,500 hours
- Labor cost = ₹1,80,000
- Material cost = ₹1,20,000
- Overhead cost = ₹60,000

Compute: **(i)** Labor Productivity, **(ii)** Multifactor Productivity, **(iii)** Cost per unit. If a worker was absent for 1 day (8 hours), what is the percentage decrease in labor productivity? **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### **Solution to Part (a):**

**Role of IE Objectives in Sustainable Manufacturing:**

Industrial Engineering objectives are no longer limited to profit; they now incorporate the **triple bottom line** — People, Planet, Profit.

**1. Productivity Enhancement → Resource Conservation (SDG 12: Responsible Consumption)**
Higher productivity means producing more with less — less raw material, less energy, less waste.

**2. Cost Reduction → Economic Sustainability (SDG 8: Decent Work & Economic Growth)**
Lower costs keep the firm competitive, sustaining employment and economic contribution.

**3. Quality Improvement → Waste Minimization (SDG 12.5)**
Defect prevention reduces landfill-bound scrap, supporting circular economy principles.

**4. Worker Welfare → Social Sustainability (SDG 3: Good Health & Well-Being)**
Safe, ergonomic workplaces reduce occupational diseases and accidents.

**5. Innovation → Long-term Resilience (SDG 9: Industry, Innovation & Infrastructure)**
Adoption of Industry 4.0 technologies (IoT, AI) supports sustainable industrial growth.

> **Conclusion:** Modern IE objectives form the **operational backbone** of the UN's 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for 5 alignments + 2 marks for conclusion)*

---

#### **Solution to Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $O = 6000$ units
- Labor hours $LH = 1500$ hours
- Labor cost $LC = ₹1,80,000$
- Material cost $MC = ₹1,20,000$
- Overhead cost $OC = ₹60,000$

**Step 1: Labor Productivity**

$$P_L = \frac{O}{LH} = \frac{6000}{1500} = 4 \text{ units/hour}$$

**Step 2: Total Cost**

$$C_T = LC + MC + OC = 180000 + 120000 + 60000 = ₹3,60,000$$

**Step 3: Multifactor Productivity**

$$P_{MFP} = \frac{O}{C_T} = \frac{6000}{360000} = 0.0167 \text{ units/₹}$$

Or equivalently: $P_{MFP} = 1/60$ units/₹

**Step 4: Cost per unit**

$$C_u = \frac{C_T}{O} = \frac{360000}{6000} = ₹60/\text{unit}$$

**Step 5: After 8-hour worker absence**
New labor hours: $LH_{new} = 1500 - 8 = 1492$ hours
Assuming output remains 6000 units (same production level maintained by other workers):

$$P_{L,new} = \frac{6000}{1492} = 4.021 \text{ units/hour}$$

**Step 6: Percentage change in labor productivity**

$$\%\Delta = \frac{P_{L,new} - P_L}{P_L} \times 100 = \frac{4.021 - 4}{4} \times 100 = 0.535\% \text{ increase}$$

> **Note:** Interestingly, when one worker is absent but the same output is produced, the *per-hour* productivity of the remaining workers **increases** (slightly) because the denominator (labor hours) decreased while output held constant. This is a classic KTU trap question.

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for 6 computational steps + 1 mark for correct interpretation of the trap)*

---

## 5.3 ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing objectives with functions:** Objectives are *goals*; functions are *activities*. Students often write "Method Study" as an objective — WRONG. It is a *technique* used to *achieve* the objective of productivity.
> 2. **Forgetting the human element:** A surprising number of students list only economic objectives. The KTU 2024 syllabus (NEP 2020 aligned) **mandates** the inclusion of worker welfare objectives. Omitting them costs at least 2 marks.
> 3. **Unit errors in productivity:** Always specify units (units/hour, units/₹, etc.). Bare numerical answers are penalized.
> 4. **Skipping the "why":** When asked to "explain objectives," do not just list them. State *why* each objective matters. A bullet list without rationale earns only 50% marks.
> 5. **Formula mis-substitution:** In Part B numericals, students often write $P = I/O$ (inverse). This is a guaranteed zero for that sub-part. Memorize: **P = Output / Input**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Objectives**
>
> - ✅ IE objectives are goals, not techniques (Method Study is a tool, not an objective).
> - ✅ Three tiers: **Primary (economic), Secondary (human), Tertiary (strategic).**
> - ✅ **5M's framework:** Men, Machines, Materials, Methods, Money.
> - ✅ **Core formula:** $P = O / I$. Always carry units.
> - ✅ **Cost per unit:** $C_u = C_T / N$. Lower $C_u$ = higher competitiveness.
> - ✅ **First Pass Yield (FPY)** is the standard quality metric in IE.
> - ✅ **Productivity, Cost, and Profit are mathematically linked** — improving one improves all.
> - ✅ Modern IE objectives align with **UN SDGs** (Sustainability is now an examinable concept).
> - ✅ **PDCA cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** is the operational framework for achieving IE objectives.
> - ✅ **Worker welfare is a co-equal goal** to profit, not a secondary afterthought.
> - ✅ Numerical questions often test the **delta (change)** between two scenarios — always show before-and-after.
> - ✅ Mermaid diagrams use **alphanumeric node IDs only** (e.g., `node1`, `nodeA`) — never use reserved words like `end` or `graph` as node names.
> - ✅ Avoid `|x|` syntax in tables — use `\vert x \vert` to preserve markdown rendering.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors Affecting Industrial Relations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET501 - INDUSTRIAL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Factors Affecting Industrial Relations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Relations (IR)** is the multidisciplinary field that studies the relationship between employers, employees (and their unions), and the government, focusing on the processes, systems, and structures governing employment conditions, workplace policies, conflict resolution, and collective bargaining within an industrial or organizational setting.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET501 - Industrial Engineering)**, the *factors affecting industrial relations* are defined as the **internal and external variables (economic, social, political, legal, psychological, technological, organizational, and cultural)** that shape, modify, or determine the quality, stability, and effectiveness of the employment relationship between management, labour, and trade unions.

The term **"Industrial Relations"** was popularized by **John T. Dunlop** through his *Systems Theory of Industrial Relations* (1958), which proposed that an IR system is shaped by three primary actors — **Management**, **Workers (Unions)**, and **Government** — operating within a specific environmental context (technology, market, and societal/community level).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Family Table"**

Imagine an industrial organization as a **joint family dinner table**:
- **Management** plays the role of the *earning head* (sets the menu, budget, and rules).
- **Workers/Trade Unions** are the *family members* who want their share, respect, and a voice in decisions.
- **Government** is the *respected elder* sitting in the next room, watching the dinner unfold, ready to intervene if arguments escalate.

Now, what affects how smoothly this dinner goes?
- **Economic conditions** = How much money is in the family bank account?
- **Political climate** = Is the family under any external social pressure or scrutiny?
- **Legal framework** = Are there house rules (laws) that must be followed?
- **Social & cultural factors** = The values, traditions, and education of family members.
- **Technological change** = A new gadget that changes how the kitchen works.
- **Managerial style** = The head's leadership personality.
- **Union strength** = How organized and united the family members are.

If the *earning head* is fair, *members* are reasonable, and the *elder* has clear rules — the dinner (industrial relations) is **harmonious**. If even one of these factors is unhealthy, the table is set for **conflict (strikes, lockouts, grievances)**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| **Term** | **Meaning in KTU Context** |
|---|---|
| **Industrial Relations (IR)** | The relationship between employers, employees, and government. |
| **Trade Union** | An organized association of workers formed to protect and promote their collective interests. |
| **Collective Bargaining** | A negotiation process between employers and a group of employees to determine conditions of employment. |
| **Strike** | A collective refusal to work by employees as a protest. |
| **Lockout** | A work stoppage initiated by the employer. |
| **Grievance** | A formal complaint raised by an employee regarding work conditions. |
| **Workplace Harmony** | The state of peaceful, productive industrial relations. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 The Dunlop's Systems Framework (Contextual Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
